Stochastic gradient descent updates $W_l \leftarrow W_l - \eta \nabla_{W_l} \mathcal{L}_{CE}$ can move the weights arbitrarily far from the pre-trained initialization, destroying learned features. **L2-SP Derivation (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Start with cross-entropy on target data: $$ \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_i^{(c)} \log \hat{y}_i^{(c)} $$ **Step 2:** Define the L2-SP penalty as squared Frobenius norm deviation from pre-trained weights $\theta^{pre}$: $$ \mathcal{R}_{L2\text{-}SP} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Vert W_l - W_l^{pre} \Vert_F^2 = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \sum_{i,j} (W_l^{ij} - W_l^{pre,ij})^2 $$ **Step 3:** Combine into composite loss: $$ \mathcal{L}_{ft} = \mathcal{L}_{CE} + \lambda \mathcal{R}_{L2\text{-}SP} $$ $$ \mathcal{L}_{ft} = -\frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{i=1}^{N_t} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_i^{(c)} \log \hat{y}_i^{(c)} + \lambda \sum_{l=1}^{L} \Vert W_l - W_l^{pre} \Vert_F^2 $$ **Step 4:** Gradient w.r.t. $W_l$: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{ft}}{\partial W_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial W_l} + 2\lambda (W_l - W_l^{pre}) $$ **Step 5:** Update rule: $$ W_l^{(t+1)} = W_l^{(t)} - \eta \left[ \nabla_{W_l} \mathcal{L}_{CE} + 2\lambda (W_l^{(t)} - W_l^{pre}) \right] $$ The term $2\lambda(W_l^{(t)} - W_l^{pre})$ acts as an **elastic anchor** — it prevents the fine-tuned weights from drifting far from the pre-trained values, thus preserving source knowledge. --- **(b) Compare Transfer Learning, Multi-Task Learning, and Domain Adaptation. Provide two real-world engineering applications for each with justification.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Comparison (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Transfer Learning** | **Multi-Task Learning** | **Domain Adaptation** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Objective | Improve target task using source | Improve all tasks jointly | Adapt model to new domain | | Tasks | Sequential ($T_s \to T_t$) | Simultaneous | Same task, different domain | | Data | $D_s$ large, $D_t$ small | $D_1, D_2, \ldots$ all available | $D_s$ labeled, $D_t$ unlabeled | | Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{target}$ | $\sum_i \alpha_i \mathcal{L}_i$ | $\mathcal{L}_{task} + \mathcal{L}_{domain}$ | **Real-World Applications (4 marks — 2 per concept):** **Transfer Learning:** 1. **Medical Tumor Detection** — Pre-trained ResNet (ImageNet) → fine-tuned on 500 MRI scans. *Justification:* MRI data scarcity ($N \ll 10^5$) makes direct training infeasible. 2. **Plant Disease Classification** — Pre-trained MobileNet → fine-tuned on 10K plant leaf images. *Justification:* Edge deployment requires lightweight models with strong priors. **Multi-Task Learning:** 1. **Autonomous Driving Perception** — Joint detection + segmentation + depth estimation. *Justification:* Shared features benefit all three tasks. 2. **Facial Analysis** — Joint face recognition + age estimation + emotion recognition. *Justification:* Correlated auxiliary tasks act as regularizers. **Domain Adaptation:** 1. **Sim-to-Real Robotics** — Train in simulator (GTA driving), adapt to real-world. *Justification:* Cheaper simulation labels, real data unlabeled. 2. **Cross-Language NLP** — Train sentiment model on English reviews, adapt to Hindi. *Justification:* Annotation cost prohibitive for low-resource languages. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Transfer Learning Answers:** > > 1. **Forgetting the TL Trigger Condition (–2 marks):** Students often *define* Transfer Learning but forget to state the condition $D_s \neq D_t$ or $T_s \neq T_t$. Always include this inequality — it is the examiner's favorite differentiator from standard ML. > > 2. **Confusing Feature Extraction with Fine-Tuning (–1 mark):** These are *not* the same. Feature Extraction = $\nabla W_l = 0$ for *all* layers. Fine-Tuning = $\nabla W_l \neq 0$ for *some/all* layers. Examiners explicitly look for this distinction. > > 3. **No mention of Learning Rate scaling in Fine-Tuning (–1 mark):** Always state $\eta_{ft} = (1/10)\eta_{pre}$ as a practical rule. Omitting it is the #1 reason students lose the "engineering insight" marks. > > 4. **Skipping the Layer-Wise Feature Hierarchy (–1 mark):** Examiners expect you to state that *lower layers = generic features (edges, textures)* and *upper layers = task-specific features (object parts)*. This is the conceptual backbone of WHY transfer learning works. > > 5. **Writing Generic CNN explanations instead of TL-specific (–2 marks):** Do not explain what a CNN is — explain what is *transferred* and *how*. The examiner wants domain/task formalism, not convolutional arithmetic. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Transfer Learning:** - ✅ **Formal Definition:** TL leverages $\{D_s, T_s\}$ knowledge to improve $\{D_t, T_t\}$ where $D_s \neq D_t$ or $T_s \neq T_t$. - ✅ **Domain:** $D = \{\mathcal{X}, P(X)\}$ — feature space + marginal distribution. - ✅ **Task:** $T = \{\mathcal{Y}, P(Y \mid X)\}$ — label space + conditional distribution. - ✅ **Why TL Works:** Lower layers learn *generic* (universal) features; upper layers learn *task-specific* features. - ✅ **Two Main Strategies:** **Feature Extraction** (freeze all) and **Fine-Tuning** (unfreeze some/all with low $\eta$). - ✅ **Standard Pre-trained Models:** VGG-16, VGG-19, ResNet-50, ResNet-101, Inception-V3, EfficientNet-B0, MobileNet, DenseNet-121. - ✅ **Fine-Tuning Learning Rate Rule:** $\eta_{ft} = (1/10) \cdot \eta_{pre}$ (typically $10^{-4}$). - ✅ **Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation:** L2-SP regularizer: $\mathcal{L}_{ft} = \mathcal{L}_{CE} + \lambda \Vert \theta - \theta^{pre} \Vert_F^2$. - ✅ **Discriminative Fine-Tuning:** $\eta_l = \eta_0 \cdot \gamma^{L-l}$ where $\gamma \in (0,1)$. - ✅ **Progressive Unfreezing Order:** Unfreeze from top (task-specific) to bottom (generic) layers. - ✅ **PAC-Bayes Justification:** Freezing reduces $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$, tightening the generalization bound by $\sqrt{k \cdot \overline{\text{rank}} / 2N_t}$. - ✅ **Common Pitfall:** TL ≠ Multi-Task Learning (MTL is simultaneous; TL is sequential). - ✅ **TL ≠ Domain Adaptation** (DA focuses on same task, different domain; TL is broader). - ✅ **Real-World Use Cases:** Medical imaging, defect detection, NLP (BERT/GPT), speech recognition, autonomous driving. - ✅ **Data-Scarcity Trigger:** TL is the *de facto* standard when $N < 10^5$ labeled samples. - ✅ **KTU 2024 High-Weight Topics:** Layer-wise freezing strategy, L2-SP derivation, VGG-16/ResNet adaptation procedure, comparative analysis with MTL & DA.

topics.title: Deep CNN Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Deep CNN Architectures # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Deep CNN Architectures** refer to a specialized family of deep Convolutional Neural Networks engineered with carefully designed, multi-layered topologies — featuring innovations in *layer depth*, *convolutional kernel sizing*, *parameter sharing*, *skip connections*, and *computational block modularization* — to achieve state-of-the-art performance on large-scale visual recognition tasks (most notably the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge, ILSVRC). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT746)**, deep CNN architectures are studied as the evolution pipeline from the foundational **LeNet-5** (1998) to modern architectures like **ResNet**, **GoogLeNet**, **VGG**, and **DenseNet**. Each architecture introduces a unique engineering solution to overcome specific bottlenecks — vanishing gradients, overfitting, parameter explosion, or computational cost. The defining taxonomy of deep CNN architectures is built upon four engineering pillars: | Pillar | Purpose | |---|---| | **Depth** | More layers = richer hierarchical feature extraction | | **Width** | Multi-scale feature capture via parallel kernels | | **Connectivity** | Skip/dense connections to ease gradient flow | | **Efficiency** | Factorized convolutions & depthwise separable convolutions | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Layered Cake Factory > [!NOTE] > **Analogy**: Imagine a cake factory where raw flour (raw pixels) enters and finished cakes (recognized objects) exit. > - The **first few rooms** (early convolutional layers) wash and sieve the flour — they detect *edges and textures*. > - The **middle rooms** (middle layers) shape the dough — they detect *motifs and parts* (eyes, wheels). > - The **final rooms** (deep layers) decorate and assemble — they detect *full objects* (face, car). > - **AlexNet** = A small bakery with 8 rooms. > - **VGGNet** = A massive bakery with 16–19 nearly identical rooms (uniformity). > - **GoogLeNet** = A factory where each room does multiple parallel tasks at once (Inception module). > - **ResNet** = A factory with *elevators* (skip connections) that allow flour to skip broken rooms — solving the "deeper is worse" problem. ## 1.3 Why Deep CNN Architectures Matter The breakthrough from shallow to deep CNNs (post-2012) marked the start of the modern AI revolution. Three core metrics drive every architectural innovation: - **Top-5 Error Rate** on ImageNet (lower is better). - **Parameter Count** (lower = memory efficient). - **FLOPs / Computational Cost** (lower = faster inference). > [!IMPORTANT] > The 2012 ImageNet competition win by **AlexNet** (Top-5 error ≈ **15.3%**) triggered a paradigm shift. By 2015, **ResNet-152** achieved a Top-5 error of just **3.57%** — surpassing human-level performance (~5.1%). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Deep CNNs A deep CNN architecture is fundamentally a function approximator of the form: $$f: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C} \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}^{K}$$ where the input is an image tensor of height $H$, width $W$, and $C$ input channels, and the output is a vector of $K$ class probabilities. The function $f$ is composed of stacked layers, each performing one of three operations: 1. **Convolution + Nonlinearity** — Feature extraction. 2. **Pooling** — Spatial downsampling. 3. **Fully Connected (Dense)** — Classification. ### 2.1.1 The Output Dimension Formula (KERNEL-LEVEL) For a single convolution operation with input feature map of size $W_{in}$, kernel size $k$, padding $p$, and stride $s$: $$W_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{in} - k + 2p}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$ ### 2.1.2 Parameter Count Formula (CONVOLUTIONAL LAYER) For a convolutional layer with $C_{in}$ input channels, $C_{out}$ output channels, and kernel size $k \times k$: $$P_{conv} = (k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$$ The "+1" term represents the bias per output channel. ### 2.1.3 Parameter Count Formula (FULLY CONNECTED LAYER) $$P_{fc} = (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out}$$ ### 2.1.4 Receptive Field (Recursive) $$RF_{l} = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i$$ where $k_l$ is the kernel size and $s_i$ is the stride of layer $i$. ## 2.2 The Four Engineering Problems Deep CNNs Solve | Problem | Mathematical Symptom | Architectural Solution | |---|---|---| | **Vanishing Gradient** | $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W \to 0$ in deep nets | Skip connections (ResNet), Better init (He init) | | **Overfitting** | Train accuracy ≫ Test accuracy | Dropout, Data augmentation, BatchNorm | | **Parameter Explosion** | $P_{conv}$ grows quadratically with $k$ | $1 \times 1$ convolutions (bottleneck), Depthwise separable convs | | **Computational Cost** | FLOPs scale as $H \cdot W \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} \cdot k^2$ | Inception modules, Grouped convolutions | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA REFERENCE — KTU EXAM VIVA & WRITTEN** | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Use Case | |---|---|---| | Convolution Output Size | $W_{out} = \lfloor(W_{in} - k + 2p)/s\rfloor + 1$ | Compute feature map dimensions | | Conv Parameter Count | $P = (k^2 \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ | Memory estimation | | Receptive Field | $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1)\prod s_i$ | Determine effective input region | | FLOPs (per conv layer) | $2 \cdot H_{out} \cdot W_{out} \cdot k^2 \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out}$ | Computational cost estimation | | Depthwise Separable Saving | $1/C_{out} + 1/k^2$ factor vs standard conv | Compare MobileNet vs VGG efficiency | | ResNet Residual Mapping | $H(x) = F(x) + x$ | Skip connection identity | | Bottleneck Compression | $C \to C/r \to C$ (with $r \geq 4$) | Parameter reduction | | Inception Naive FLOPs | $4 \times$ more than $3 \times 3$ with $5 \times 5$ | Justifies $1 \times 1$ bottleneck | | BatchNorm Normalization | $\hat{x} = (x - \mu)/\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \epsilon}$ | Stabilize activations | | Softmax Output | $P(y_i) = e^{z_i}/\sum_j e^{z_j}$ | Final classification | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL PITFALL — KTU valuation**: When computing parameter counts, *never forget the bias term* $(+1)$ per filter. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for omitting it. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AlexNet (2012)** — Pioneered GPU-based training; foundation for all modern vision systems. - **VGGNet (2014)** — Used as feature extractor in object detection (Faster R-CNN backbone). - **GoogLeNet (2014)** — Inspired NAS (Neural Architecture Search) and modern efficient backbones. - **ResNet (2015)** — Backbone of nearly every production-grade CV system (segmentation, detection, pose estimation). - **MobileNet / EfficientNet** — Deployed on edge devices (smartphones, IoT, autonomous drones). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Analysis of Landmark Architectures ### 3.1.1 AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) **Engineering Innovations:** 1. ReLU activation (solves vanishing gradient in shallow layers). 2. Dropout ($p = 0.5$) in fully connected layers. 3. Overlapping max pooling ($3 \times 3$ stride 2). 4. Local Response Normalization (LRN). 5. Dual-GPU training (model parallelism). **Layer-by-Layer Architecture:** ``` Input: 224 × 224 × 3 Conv1: 11×11, 96 filters, stride 4, padding 0 → Output: 55×55×96 MaxPool1: 3×3, stride 2 → Output: 27×27×96 Conv2: 5×5, 256 filters, padding 2, grouped (2 GPUs) → Output: 27×27×256 MaxPool2: 3×3, stride 2 → Output: 13×13×256 Conv3: 3×3, 384 filters, padding 1 → Output: 13×13×384 Conv4: 3×3, 384 filters, padding 1 → Output: 13×13×384 Conv5: 3×3, 256 filters, padding 1 → Output: 13×13×256 MaxPool3: 3×3, stride 2 → Output: 6×6×256 FC1: 4096 neurons (with Dropout 0.5) FC2: 4096 neurons (with Dropout 0.5) FC3: 1000 neurons (Softmax) ``` **Parameter Calculation (Step-by-Step) — KTU Frequently Asked:** Let us compute parameters for **Conv1** of AlexNet: - $k = 11$, $C_{in} = 3$, $C_{out} = 96$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{Conv1} &= (k \cdot k \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out} \\ &= (11 \cdot 11 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 96 \\ &= (363 + 1) \cdot 96 \\ &= 364 \cdot 96 \\ &= 34{,}944 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ Now **Conv2** of AlexNet (note: was split across 2 GPUs originally, but mathematically): - $k = 5$, $C_{in} = 96$, $C_{out} = 256$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{Conv2} &= (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 96 + 1) \cdot 256 \\ &= (2400 + 1) \cdot 256 \\ &= 2401 \cdot 256 \\ &= 614{,}656 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **FC1 Parameter Count:** - Input = $6 \times 6 \times 256 = 9216$ - Output = $4096$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{FC1} &= (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out} \\ &= (9216 + 1) \cdot 4096 \\ &= 9217 \cdot 4096 \\ &= 37{,}752{,}832 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation**: FC layers in AlexNet account for ≈ **95%** of total parameters (~58M out of ~62M total). This is why VGG and later architectures reduced FC layers. ### 3.1.2 VGGNet (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2014) **Core Design Principle**: *Uniform architecture — only 3×3 convolutions and 2×2 max pooling.* **Why 3×3 stacked?** Two stacked $3 \times 3$ convs have an effective receptive field of $5 \times 5$ but with **fewer parameters** and **more nonlinearities**: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{5 \times 5} &= (5^2 \cdot C^2 + C) \cdot \text{(single layer)} = 25C^2 + C \\ P_{3 \times 3 \times 2} &= 2 \times (3^2 \cdot C^2 + C) = 18C^2 + 2C \end{aligned} $$ The savings ratio is: $$ \frac{18C^2}{25C^2} = 0.72 $$ So stacked $3 \times 3$ saves **28%** parameters while increasing depth and representational power. ### 3.1.3 GoogLeNet / Inception (Szegedy et al., 2014) **Inception Module (Naive Version)**: Concatenates outputs of $1 \times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$ convolutions and $3 \times 3$ pooling — all in parallel. **Computational Problem of Naive Inception:** For an input of size $14 \times 14 \times 256$, and $5 \times 5$ conv with 32 filters: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FLOPs}_{5 \times 5} &= 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 32 \\ &= 196 \cdot 25 \cdot 256 \cdot 32 \\ &= 40{,}140{,}800 \text{ FLOPs} \end{aligned} $$ **Solution — Inception with $1 \times 1$ Bottleneck:** Insert a $1 \times 1$ conv (32 filters) before the $5 \times 5$ conv to reduce channel depth: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FLOPs}_{bottleneck} &= 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 1^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 32 + 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \\ &= 1{,}605{,}632 + 12{,}554{,}240 \\ &= 14{,}159{,}872 \text{ FLOPs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Reduction Ratio} &= \frac{14{,}159{,}872}{40{,}140{,}800} \\ &\approx 0.353 \end{aligned} $$ **≈ 64.7% reduction in computation** — this is the *single most important engineering insight* behind GoogLeNet. ### 3.1.4 ResNet (He et al., 2015) — The Identity Revolution **The Degradation Problem**: Naively stacking more layers caused *higher training error* — not overfitting, but an optimization difficulty. **Solution — Residual Learning:** Instead of learning $H(x)$ directly, learn the residual $F(x) = H(x) - x$: $$ H(x) = F(x) + x $$ This is implemented as a **skip connection**: $$ y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x $$ where $\mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) = W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 \cdot x)$ represents the residual mapping. **Gradient Flow Derivation (Why It Works):** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \frac{\partial x_L}{\partial x_l} \\ &= \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_l} \sum_{i=l}^{L-1} \mathcal{F}(x_i)\right) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The "+1" term in the gradient ensures that even if $\partial \mathcal{F}/\partial x \to 0$, the gradient **never vanishes** — it is propagated directly via the identity path. **ResNet-50 Bottleneck Block:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Block Output} &= W_3 \cdot \sigma(BN(W_2 \cdot \sigma(BN(W_1 \cdot x)))) + x \\ \text{where: } W_1 &: 1 \times 1 \text{ conv (compression by factor } r=4) \\ W_2 &: 3 \times 3 \text{ conv} \\ W_3 &: 1 \times 1 \text{ conv (expansion back to original)} \end{aligned} $$ For ResNet-50 with $C = 256$ channels and bottleneck $r = 4$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{bottleneck} &= (1^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 64) + 64 + (3^2 \cdot 64 \cdot 64) + 64 + (1^2 \cdot 64 \cdot 256) + 256 \\ &= 16384 + 64 + 36864 + 64 + 16384 + 256 \\ &= 69{,}016 \text{ parameters per block} \end{aligned} $$ Compared to a plain $3 \times 3 \times 3$ block at 256 channels: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{plain} &= 3 \times ((3^2 \cdot 256^2) + 256) \\ &= 3 \times (589{,}824 + 256) \\ &= 1{,}770{,}240 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **The bottleneck reduces parameters by ≈ 96.1% per block!** ## 3.2 Comparative Performance Matrix (ImageNet ILSVRC) | Architecture | Year | Layers | Top-5 Error | Parameters (M) | Key Innovation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | LeNet-5 | 1998 | 7 | N/A | 0.06 | First CNN (digit recognition) | | AlexNet | 2012 | 8 | **15.3%** | 62 | ReLU, Dropout, GPU training | | VGG-16 | 2014 | 16 | 7.3% | 138 | Uniform 3×3 kernels | | GoogLeNet | 2014 | 22 | 6.7% | 5 | Inception module | | ResNet-152 | 2015 | 152 | **3.57%** | 60 | Skip connections | | DenseNet-201 | 2016 | 201 | 5.54% | 20 | Dense connectivity | | MobileNetV2 | 2018 | 53 | 4.6% | 3.5 | Depthwise separable convs | | EfficientNet-B7 | 2019 | 438 | 2.97% | 66 | Compound scaling | > [!NOTE] > **ResNet-152 has only 60M parameters** vs VGG-16's 138M — yet achieves a *dramatically lower error rate*. This proves **architectural innovation > raw depth**. ## 3.3 Analytical Summary — Why Each Architecture Was Revolutionary $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AlexNet: } & \text{Broke the linearity barrier with ReLU} \\ \text{VGGNet: } & \text{Showed depth matters; small kernels are efficient} \\ \text{GoogLeNet: } & \text{Showed width AND depth together} \\ \text{ResNet: } & \text{Solved the vanishing gradient at extreme depth} \\ \text{DenseNet: } & \text{Maximized feature reuse via dense connectivity} \\ \text{EfficientNet: } & \text{Optimized scaling across depth, width, resolution} \end{aligned} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Architecture Comparison — Functional Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AlexNet_Block["ALEXNET 2012 — 8 LAYERS"] A1[Input 224x224x3] --> A2[Conv 11x11 stride 4 ReLU] A2 --> A3[MaxPool 3x3 stride 2] A3 --> A4[Conv 5x5 ReLU] A4 --> A5[MaxPool 3x3] A5 --> A6[Conv 3x3 x3 ReLU] A6 --> A7[MaxPool 3x3] A7 --> A8[FC 4096 Dropout] A8 --> A9[FC 4096 Dropout] A9 --> A10[Softmax 1000] end subgraph VGGNet_Block["VGGNET 2014 — 16/19 LAYERS"] V1[Input 224x224x3] --> V2[Conv 3x3x2 ReLU 64] V2 --> V3[MaxPool 2x2] V3 --> V4[Conv 3x3x2 ReLU 128] V4 --> V5[MaxPool 2x2] V5 --> V6[Conv 3x3x3 ReLU 256] V6 --> V7[MaxPool 2x2] V7 --> V8[Conv 3x3x3 ReLU 512] V8 --> V9[MaxPool 2x2] V9 --> V10[FC 4096] V10 --> V11[FC 4096] V11 --> V12[Softmax 1000] end subgraph GoogLeNet_Block["GOOGLENET 2014 — 22 LAYERS INC MODULES"] G1[Input] --> G2[Conv 7x7 stride 2] G2 --> G3[MaxPool 3x3] G3 --> G4[Inception 3a 256 filters] G4 --> G5[Inception 3b 480] G5 --> G6[MaxPool] G6 --> G7[Inception 4a 512] G7 --> G8[Aux Classifier 1] G7 --> G9[Inception 4b 512] G9 --> G10[Inception 4c 512] G10 --> G11[Inception 4d 528] G11 --> G12[Aux Classifier 2] G11 --> G13[Inception 4e 832] G13 --> G14[MaxPool] G14 --> G15[Inception 5a/b 1024] G15 --> G16[AvgPool 7x7] G16 --> G17[FC Softmax] end subgraph ResNet_Block["RESNET 2015 — 50/101/152 LAYERS"] R1[Input] --> R2[Conv 7x7 stride 2 BN ReLU] R2 --> R3[MaxPool 3x3] R3 --> R4[Residual Stage 1 3 blocks 256] R4 --> R5[Residual Stage 2 4 blocks 512] R5 --> R6[Residual Stage 3 6 blocks 1024] R6 --> R7[Residual Stage 4 3 blocks 2048] R7 --> R8[AvgPool] R8 --> R9[FC Softmax] end style AlexNet_Block fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style VGGNet_Block fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style GoogLeNet_Block fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ResNet_Block fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 ResNet Skip Connection — Detailed Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR InputX["Input x"] --> Conv1["Conv 1x1
Compression"] Conv1 --> BN1["BatchNorm + ReLU"] BN1 --> Conv2["Conv 3x3
Bottleneck"] Conv2 --> BN2["BatchNorm + ReLU"] BN2 --> Conv3["Conv 1x1
Restoration"] Conv3 --> AddNode["Addition Node +"] InputX -->|Identity Skip| AddNode AddNode --> ReLUFinal["ReLU Activation"] ReLUFinal --> OutputY["Output y"] style InputX fill:#FFD700 style OutputY fill:#90EE90 style AddNode fill:#FF6347 style ReLUFinal fill:#87CEEB ``` ## 4.3 Inception Module with 1×1 Bottleneck — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB In["Input Feature Map
H x W x C"] --> BranchA["Conv 1x1
64 filters"] In --> BranchB["Conv 1x1
reduce 64"] BranchB --> Conv3["Conv 3x3
128 filters"] In --> BranchC["Conv 1x1
reduce 64"] BranchC --> Conv5["Conv 5x5
32 filters"] In --> BranchD["MaxPool 3x3"] BranchD --> Conv1After["Conv 1x1
32 filters"] BranchA --> Concat["Concatenation
Depth-wise"] Conv3 --> Concat Conv5 --> Concat Conv1After --> Concat Concat --> Out["Output
H x W x 256"] style In fill:#FFD700 style Out fill:#90EE90 style Concat fill:#FF6347 style BranchA fill:#FFA07A style BranchB fill:#FFA07A style BranchC fill:#FFA07A style BranchD fill:#FFA07A ``` ## 4.4 Evolution of CNN Architectures — Timeline & Motivation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR LeNet["LeNet-5 1998
7 layers
Digit Recognition"] --> Alex["AlexNet 2012
8 layers
ReLU Dropout GPU"] Alex --> VGG["VGGNet 2014
16-19 layers
Uniform 3x3 Kernels"] VGG --> Google["GoogLeNet 2014
22 layers
Inception 1x1 Bottleneck"] Google --> ResNet["ResNet 2015
152 layers
Skip Connections"] ResNet --> DenseNet["DenseNet 2016
201 layers
Dense Connectivity"] ResNet --> Mobile["MobileNet 2017
53 layers
Depthwise Separable"] DenseNet --> Eff["EfficientNet 2019
Compound Scaling"] style LeNet fill:#E0FFFF style Alex fill:#FFE4B5 style VGG fill:#B0E0E6 style Google fill:#98FB98 style ResNet fill:#FFB6C1 style DenseNet fill:#DDA0DD style Mobile fill:#F0E68C style Eff fill:#FFA07A ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **List any four deep CNN architectures used for image classification with their years of introduction.** **Model Answer:** The four landmark deep CNN architectures are: 1. **AlexNet (2012)** — Pioneered ReLU activation, dropout, and GPU training. Won ImageNet ILSVRC with 15.3% Top-5 error. 2. **VGGNet (2014)** — Used uniform 3×3 convolutions stacked to 16/19 layers. Achieved 7.3% Top-5 error. 3. **GoogLeNet (2014)** — Introduced Inception modules with 1×1 bottleneck. 22 layers with only 5M parameters. 4. **ResNet (2015)** — Introduced residual/skip connections enabling 152+ layers. Achieved 3.57% Top-5 error. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Explain the vanishing gradient problem and how ResNet solves it.** **Model Answer:** The **vanishing gradient problem** occurs during backpropagation in deep networks where gradients become exponentially small as they propagate from output to input layers, causing early layers to stop learning. **ResNet's Solution:** ResNet introduces **identity skip connections** that allow the gradient to flow directly through the identity path: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_l} \sum_{i=l}^{L-1} \mathcal{F}(x_i)\right) $$ The "+1" term ensures the gradient never vanishes, even when $\partial \mathcal{F}/\partial x \to 0$. This enables training of networks with **152+ layers**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of AlexNet in detail. List its key contributions and explain how it differs from LeNet-5.** **(b) [7 Marks] Compute the total number of parameters in AlexNet's first three convolutional layers and the first fully connected layer. Show all calculations.** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (a)** **AlexNet Architecture (8 learnable layers):** | Layer | Type | Kernel | Filters | Stride | Padding | Output Size | Activation | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | Input | — | — | — | — | 224×224×3 | — | | 1 | Conv1 | 11×11 | 96 | 4 | — | 55×55×96 | ReLU | | 2 | MaxPool | 3×3 | — | 2 | — | 27×27×96 | — | | 3 | Conv2 | 5×5 | 256 | 1 | 2 | 27×27×256 | ReLU | | 4 | MaxPool | 3×3 | — | 2 | — | 13×13×256 | — | | 5 | Conv3 | 3×3 | 384 | 1 | 1 | 13×13×384 | ReLU | | 6 | Conv4 | 3×3 | 384 | 1 | 1 | 13×13×384 | ReLU | | 7 | Conv5 | 3×3 | 256 | 1 | 1 | 13×13×256 | ReLU | | 8 | MaxPool | 3×3 | — | 2 | — | 6×6×256 | — | | 9 | FC1 | — | 4096 | — | — | 4096 | ReLU + Dropout | | 10 | FC2 | — | 4096 | — | — | 4096 | ReLU + Dropout | | 11 | FC3 | — | 1000 | — | — | 1000 | Softmax | **Key Contributions of AlexNet over LeNet-5:** 1. **ReLU Activation** — Replaces sigmoid/tanh; solves vanishing gradient in shallow layers. 2. **Dropout Regularization** — Applied to FC layers with p=0.5; reduces overfitting. 3. **GPU Training** — Trained on two NVIDIA GTX 580 GPUs using model parallelism. 4. **Overlapping Max Pooling** — 3×3 with stride 2 instead of 2×2 stride 2. 5. **Local Response Normalization (LRN)** — Biologically inspired normalization. 6. **Data Augmentation** — Image translations, horizontal flips, and RGB color augmentation. **Differences from LeNet-5:** | Aspect | LeNet-5 (1998) | AlexNet (2012) | |---|---|---| | Layers | 7 | 8 | | Activation | Sigmoid/Tanh | ReLU | | Regularization | None | Dropout | | Training | CPU | GPU | | Input Size | 32×32 | 224×224 | | Classes | 10 (digits) | 1000 (ImageNet) | | Parameters | ~60K | ~62M | --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-A (b)** **Calculation of Parameters — AlexNet:** **Layer 1 (Conv1):** $k=11, C_{in}=3, C_{out}=96$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{Conv1} &= (11 \cdot 11 \cdot 3 + 1) \cdot 96 \\ &= (363 + 1) \cdot 96 \\ &= 364 \cdot 96 \\ &= 34{,}944 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Layer 2 (Conv2):** $k=5, C_{in}=96, C_{out}=256$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{Conv2} &= (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 96 + 1) \cdot 256 \\ &= (2400 + 1) \cdot 256 \\ &= 2401 \cdot 256 \\ &= 614{,}656 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Layer 3 (Conv3):** $k=3, C_{in}=256, C_{out}=384$ (Note: In original, Conv2 output was 256 split as 48 per GPU; mathematically = 256) $$ \begin{aligned} P_{Conv3} &= (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 256 + 1) \cdot 384 \\ &= (2304 + 1) \cdot 384 \\ &= 2305 \cdot 384 \\ &= 885{,}120 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Total Parameters in First Three Conv Layers:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{sum} &= P_{Conv1} + P_{Conv2} + P_{Conv3} \\ &= 34{,}944 + 614{,}656 + 885{,}120 \\ &= 1{,}534{,}720 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Layer 4 (FC1):** Input = $6 \times 6 \times 256 = 9216$, Output = $4096$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_{FC1} &= (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out} \\ &= (9216 + 1) \cdot 4096 \\ &= 9217 \cdot 4096 \\ &= 37{,}752{,}832 \text{ parameters} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** - First three conv layers: **1,534,720 parameters** - FC1: **37,752,832 parameters** - Note that FC1 alone has ~24× more parameters than all three conv layers combined. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of the Inception module as used in GoogLeNet. How does the use of 1×1 convolutions reduce computational cost?** **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of residual learning in ResNet. Derive how skip connections help in mitigating the vanishing gradient problem during backpropagation.** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (a)** **GoogLeNet Inception Module:** The Inception module applies multiple convolution operations **in parallel** on the same input and concatenates their outputs depth-wise. The naive version uses $1\times 1$, $3\times 3$, $5\times 5$ convolutions and a $3\times 3$ max pooling operation — all in parallel. **Naive Inception Computational Cost:** For input of size $14 \times 14 \times 256$: - $1 \times 1$ conv with 64 filters: $$FLOPs_{1\times 1} = 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 1^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 64 = 3{,}211{,}264$$ - $3 \times 3$ conv with 128 filters: $$FLOPs_{3\times 3} = 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 128 = 57{,}802{,}752$$ - $5 \times 5$ conv with 32 filters: $$FLOPs_{5\times 5} = 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 256 \cdot 32 = 40{,}140{,}800$$ **Total Naive FLOPs:** $$FLOPs_{naive} = 3{,}211{,}264 + 57{,}802{,}752 + 40{,}140{,}800 + 12{,}845{,}056 = 113{,}999{,}872$$ **Inception with $1\times 1$ Bottleneck:** Insert a $1 \times 1$ conv before the $3 \times 3$ and $5 \times 5$ ops to reduce channel depth: - $1 \times 1$ reduce (64 filters) → $3 \times 3$ (128 filters): $$FLOPs = 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 256 \cdot 64 + 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 64 \cdot 128 \cdot 9 = 3{,}211{,}264 + 23{,}068{,}672 = 26{,}279{,}936$$ - $1 \times 1$ reduce (32 filters) → $5 \times 5$ (32 filters): $$FLOPs = 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 256 \cdot 32 + 14 \cdot 14 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 25 = 1{,}605{,}632 + 5{,}013{,}760 = 6{,}619{,}392$$ **Total Bottleneck FLOPs:** $$FLOPs_{bottleneck} \approx 37{,}000{,}000$$ **Reduction:** $$\frac{FLOPs_{bottleneck}}{FLOPs_{naive}} \approx 0.32$$ So the bottleneck version achieves **≈ 68% reduction** in computation. The $1 \times 1$ convolution acts as a *channel-wise dimensionality reduction* layer, drastically cutting the input channel depth before expensive spatial convolutions. --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q-B (b)** **Residual Learning in ResNet:** **Concept:** Instead of learning the direct mapping $H(x)$ from input to output, ResNet reformulates the layers to learn a **residual function** $F(x) = H(x) - x$. The original mapping becomes: $$H(x) = F(x) + x$$ This is implemented by adding an **identity shortcut connection** that skips one or more layers: $$y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$$ where $x$ is the input to the block and $\mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\})$ is the residual mapping to be learned (typically two or three convolutional layers). **Backpropagation Derivation:** Let $x_l$ be the input to the $l$-th residual block and $x_{l+1}$ its output. Then: $$ x_{l+1} = x_l + \mathcal{F}(x_l, W_l) $$ Recursively applying this for $L - l$ blocks: $$ x_L = x_l + \sum_{i=l}^{L-1} \mathcal{F}(x_i, W_i) $$ Computing the gradient of loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $x_l$ using the chain rule: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \frac{\partial x_L}{\partial x_l} $$ $$ \frac{\partial x_L}{\partial x_l} = 1 + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_l} \sum_{i=l}^{L-1} \mathcal{F}(x_i, W_i) $$ Therefore: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_L} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_l} \sum_{i=l}^{L-1} \mathcal{F}(x_i, W_i)\right) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The "+1" term in the parenthesis ensures that the gradient $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x_l$ **never vanishes**, even if the residual term $\partial \mathcal{F}/\partial x$ becomes very small. This is because the identity mapping $x$ provides a direct gradient pathway from $\mathcal{L}$ to $x_l$, completely bypassing the chain of nonlinear transformations. **Practical Implication:** This mathematical property allows ResNet to successfully train networks with 50, 101, 152, and even 1000+ layers — a feat that was impossible with traditional CNN architectures due to vanishing gradients. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL ALERT — COMMON MARK DEDUCTION ZONES:** > > 1. **Parameter Count Errors**: Forgetting the **bias term (+1)** per filter costs **1 mark**. Always write $P = (k^2 \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$. > > 2. **Receptive Field Calculation**: Students often forget to account for **stride multiplication** across layers. The formula is $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod s_i$, NOT just $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1)$. > > 3. **ResNet Gradient Derivation**: Showing only the forward equation $H(x) = F(x) + x$ without deriving $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x_l$ results in **partial marking**. The gradient flow proof is the *core answer* for residual learning questions. > > 4. **Inception Module FLOPs**: Many students compute only one branch instead of summing all four parallel branches. Always include $1\times 1$, $3\times 3$, $5\times 5$, and pooling branch FLOPs. > > 5. **Architecture Year Confusion**: Examiners *strictly* mark AlexNet=2012, VGG/GoogLeNet=2014, ResNet=2015. Confusing these loses 1 mark. > > 6. **FC Layer Dominance**: When asked "where are most parameters in AlexNet?", students often say "convolutional layers." Wrong — **FC layers hold ~95%** of AlexNet's parameters. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — DEEP CNN ARCHITECTURES** **Core Architectures:** - ☐ **AlexNet (2012)** — 8 layers, ReLU, Dropout, GPU training, 62M params, Top-5 = 15.3% - ☐ **VGGNet (2014)** — 16/19 layers, uniform 3×3 convs, 138M params, Top-5 = 7.3% - ☐ **GoogLeNet (2014)** — 22 layers, Inception modules, 1×1 bottleneck, only 5M params - ☐ **ResNet (2015)** — 50/101/152 layers, skip connections, residual mapping, Top-5 = 3.57% - ☐ **DenseNet (2016)** — Dense connectivity, feature reuse, 20M params - ☐ **MobileNet (2017)** — Depthwise separable convolutions for edge devices - ☐ **EfficientNet (2019)** — Compound scaling (depth × width × resolution) **Key Formulas:** - ☐ Output size: $W_{out} = \lfloor (W_{in} - k + 2p)/s \rfloor + 1$ - ☐ Conv params: $P = (k^2 \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ - ☐ FC params: $P = (N_{in} + 1) \cdot N_{out}$ - ☐ Receptive field: $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) \cdot \prod s_i$ - ☐ ResNet identity: $H(x) = F(x) + x$ - ☐ Gradient with skip: $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x_l = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial x_L \cdot (1 + \partial \sum \mathcal{F}/\partial x_l)$ **Engineering Solutions:** - ☐ **Vanishing gradient** → ResNet skip connections + BatchNorm + He initialization - ☐ **Overfitting** → Dropout + Data Augmentation + BatchNorm - ☐ **Parameter explosion** → 1×1 bottleneck + Depthwise separable convs - ☐ **Computational cost** → Inception modules + Grouped convolutions **Viva-Favorite Points:** - ☐ Stacked 3×3 convs have effective RF of 5×5 but **fewer parameters** - ☐ GoogLeNet's 1×1 bottleneck saves **~64% FLOPs** in naive Inception - ☐ ResNet's "+1" in gradient is the *mathematical key* to training 150+ layer nets - ☐ VGG-16's 138M params vs ResNet-152's 60M params proves **architecture > depth** - ☐ AlexNet's FC layers dominate (≈95% of total parameters)

topics.title: Feedforward Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feedforward Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Feedforward Neural Network (FNN)** is a fundamental class of artificial neural networks in which information propagates strictly in one direction—from the input layer, through one or more **hidden layers**, to the output layer—without forming any cycles, loops, or recurrent connections. Mathematically, an FNN defines a parameterized non-linear mapping $f_{\theta} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$, where $\theta$ represents the set of learnable weights and biases optimized via gradient-based methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEEBT746, Module 1):**
> Feedforward networks form the *foundational architecture* upon which all deep learning models (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) are built. Mastery of forward propagation, activation functions, and the backpropagation algorithm is **mandatory** for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Assembly Line of Decisions"

Imagine a manufacturing assembly line in a factory:
- **Raw materials** (input features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$) enter at one end.
- **Worker stations** (artificial neurons) at each layer inspect, weigh, and transform the parts.
- Each worker uses a **decision rule** (activation function) to either pass the part along or modify it.
- The **finished product** (output $\hat{y}$) emerges at the final station.

Just as no worker can send a part *backwards* to a previous station, in a feedforward network, **signals travel only forward**—there are no backward arrows. The "experience" of the workers (the weights) is updated based on the quality of the final product (the loss), which is the essence of **learning**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Input Layer** | Receives raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$; performs no computation. |
| **Hidden Layer** | Intermediate layer(s) where non-linear feature transformations occur. |
| **Output Layer** | Produces final prediction $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$. |
| **Neuron / Unit** | Computational node computing a weighted sum followed by an activation. |
| **Weight ($\mathbf{W}$)** | Learnable parameter matrix of dimension (output\_dim $\times$ input\_dim). |
| **Bias ($\mathbf{b}$)** | Learnable scalar/vector offset added to the pre-activation. |
| **Activation Function** | Non-linear function $\phi(\cdot)$ applied to the pre-activation. |
| **Loss Function** | Scalar measure of prediction error, denoted $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$. |
| **Epoch** | One complete pass through the entire training dataset. |

> [!NOTE]
> A network with a single hidden layer is termed a **shallow network**; one with multiple hidden layers is a **deep network**. The universal approximation theorem guarantees that a feedforward network with a *single* sufficiently wide hidden layer can approximate any continuous function on a compact domain—although deep networks often achieve this with exponentially fewer parameters.

## 1.4 Historical Context

The modern FNN is the culmination of the **McCulloch–Pitts neuron (1943)**, the **Rosenblatt Perceptron (1958)**, and the landmark **backpropagation paper by Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)**. KTU examiners frequently reference this evolution in 10-mark essay questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Artificial Neuron

A single neuron performs two sequential operations:

**Step 1 — Linear Pre-activation (Weighted Sum):**
$$z = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

**Step 2 — Non-linear Activation:**
$$a = \phi(z)$$

- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ : weight vector
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ : bias scalar
- $\phi : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ : activation function
- $a$ : the neuron's output (also called *activation*)

> [!NOTE]
> The **bias term** $b$ is crucial—it shifts the activation function left or right, enabling the network to fit data that is not centered at the origin. Omitting it severely limits representational power.

## 2.2 Layer-Wise Vectorized Formulation

For layer $\ell$ with input $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell-1}}$ and output $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$:

**Pre-activation vector:**
$$\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$

**Activation vector:**
$$\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \phi^{(\ell)}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)}\right)$$

where $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell} \times n_{\ell-1}}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell}}$.

## 2.3 Standard Activation Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Function | Formula | Output Range | Derivative | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ | Binary classification output |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centered) |
| **ReLU** | $\max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\mathbf{1}_{z > 0}$ | Default hidden layer activation |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $\max(\alpha z, z)$, $\alpha \approx 0.01$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\mathbf{1}_{z > 0} + \alpha \cdot \mathbf{1}_{z \le 0}$ | Mitigates "dying ReLU" |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | $\text{diag}(\mathbf{p}) - \mathbf{p}\mathbf{p}^{\top}$ | Multi-class output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vanishing Gradient Problem:** Sigmoid and tanh saturate (gradient $\rightarrow 0$) for large $\vert z \vert$, causing slow training in deep networks. **ReLU is the default choice** for hidden layers in modern KTU-aligned deep learning practice.

## 2.4 Loss Functions

| Loss | Formula | Task |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\mathcal{L} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$ | Regression |
| **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | Binary classification |
| **Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE)** | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y}_c$ | Multi-class classification |
| **Hinge Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$ | SVM-style classification |

## 2.5 The Backpropagation Algorithm — Conceptual Foundation

Backpropagation efficiently computes gradients of the loss with respect to every weight by applying the **chain rule of calculus** in *reverse* (from output back to input). It has two phases:

1. **Forward Pass:** Compute activations layer-by-layer and store them.
2. **Backward Pass:** Propagate the error signal $\delta^{(\ell)}$ from output to input, computing partial derivatives.

For output layer $L$ with squared error loss:
$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \phi^{\prime}(z^{(L)})$$

For hidden layer $\ell$:
$$\delta^{(\ell)} = \left( \mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)} \right) \odot \phi^{\prime}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)}\right)$$

Gradient w.r.t. weights and biases:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)\top}, \qquad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Dimension |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}$ | Weight matrix of layer $\ell$ | $n_{\ell} \times n_{\ell-1}$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$ | Bias vector of layer $\ell$ | $n_{\ell} \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)}$ | Pre-activation of layer $\ell$ | $n_{\ell} \times 1$ |
| $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)}$ | Post-activation of layer $\ell$ | $n_{\ell} \times 1$ |
| $\delta^{(\ell)}$ | Error signal at layer $\ell$ | $n_{\ell} \times 1$ |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$) | scalar |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Loss / cost function | scalar |

**Universal Update Rule (Gradient Descent):**
$$\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}}$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computer Vision:** Pixel classification in medical imaging.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Bag-of-words text classifiers.
- **Finance:** Credit scoring, fraud detection.
- **Control Systems:** System identification and inverse dynamics modelling.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG classification (a popular KTU project domain).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Propagation Through a 2-Layer Network

**Setup:** Input $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$, one hidden layer with 3 neurons, output $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$. Activations: tanh (hidden), linear (output). Loss: MSE.

Let
$$\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} & w_{12} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} \\ w_{31} & w_{32} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 & w_2 & w_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b^{(2)} = b_4$$

### Step 1 — Hidden Layer Pre-activation

We compute the dot product of the weight matrix with the input vector and add the bias.

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

Expanding the matrix multiplication explicitly row by row:

$$z_1^{(1)} = w_{11} x_1 + w_{12} x_2 + b_1$$
$$z_2^{(1)} = w_{21} x_1 + w_{22} x_2 + b_2$$
$$z_3^{(1)} = w_{31} x_1 + w_{32} x_2 + b_3$$

### Step 2 — Hidden Layer Activation (tanh)

Apply the tanh function element-wise to each pre-activation value.

$$a_1^{(1)} = \tanh(z_1^{(1)}), \quad a_2^{(1)} = \tanh(z_2^{(1)}), \quad a_3^{(1)} = \tanh(z_3^{(1)})$$

### Step 3 — Output Layer Pre-activation

The output neuron receives the 3-dimensional hidden activation vector and produces a single scalar pre-activation.

$$z^{(2)} = w_1 a_1^{(1)} + w_2 a_2^{(1)} + w_3 a_3^{(1)} + b_4$$

This can be written compactly as $z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$.

### Step 4 — Output Activation (Linear)

Since this is a regression problem, we use a linear (identity) output activation.

$$\hat{y} = z^{(2)}$$

### Step 5 — Loss Computation (MSE for a single sample)

The mean squared error is half the squared difference between the prediction and the true target (the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a convention that simplifies differentiation).

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Backpropagation Gradients for the Same Network

We now compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}}$, $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}}$, $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}}$, and $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}}$.

### Step 6 — Gradient at the Output

Differentiate the loss with respect to the network output $\hat{y}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y$$

Since $\hat{y} = z^{(2)}$, the chain rule gives the error signal at the output layer.

$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot 1 = \hat{y} - y$$

### Step 7 — Gradients for $\mathbf{W}^{(2)}$ and $b^{(2)}$

Differentiate with respect to the output weight matrix and the output bias.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot \mathbf{a}^{(1)\top} = (\hat{y} - y) \begin{bmatrix} a_1^{(1)} & a_2^{(1)} & a_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = \hat{y} - y$$

### Step 8 — Error Signal at Hidden Layer

We propagate the error backward using the chain rule, multiplying by the derivative of the activation function evaluated at the pre-activation.

$$\delta^{(1)} = \left( \mathbf{W}^{(2)\top} \delta^{(2)} \right) \odot \phi^{\prime}\!\left(\mathbf{z}^{(1)}\right)$$

Concretely, since $\phi = \tanh$ and $\tanh^{\prime}(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$:

$$\delta_i^{(1)} = w_i \, \delta^{(2)} \cdot \left(1 - \left(a_i^{(1)}\right)^{2}\right), \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

### Step 9 — Gradients for $\mathbf{W}^{(1)}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(1)}$

Finally, the gradients with respect to the first-layer weights and biases are obtained by outer product of the error signal and the input.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} \\ \delta_2^{(1)} \\ \delta_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} x_1 & \delta_1^{(1)} x_2 \\ \delta_2^{(1)} x_1 & \delta_2^{(1)} x_2 \\ \delta_3^{(1)} x_1 & \delta_3^{(1)} x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} \delta_1^{(1)} \\ \delta_2^{(1)} \\ \delta_3^{(1)} \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 10 — Parameter Update (Vanilla Gradient Descent)

With learning rate $\eta$:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}}$$

for $\ell = 1, 2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient of the Sigmoid Activation

This is a frequent **3-mark sub-part** in KTU exams.

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -\frac{1}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

Rewriting to express in terms of $\sigma(z)$ itself:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

$$\boxed{\sigma^{\prime}(z) = \sigma(z)\bigl(1 - \sigma(z)\bigr)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Softmax + Cross-Entropy Combined Gradient

A frequently tested result. Let $\mathbf{p} = \text{softmax}(\mathbf{z})$ and $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k} y_k \log p_k$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = p_i - y_i$$

This elegant simplification is why **softmax + cross-entropy is the canonical pair** for multi-class classification output layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The elegance $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial z_i = p_i - y_i$ means the gradient is simply the *difference between the predicted probability and the true one-hot label*. Memorize this—it is worth **3 easy marks** in the ESE.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix (Forces To Remember)

| Forward Pass | Backward Pass |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$ | $\delta^{(L)} = \nabla_{\hat{y}} \mathcal{L} \odot \phi^{\prime}(\mathbf{z}^{(L)})$ |
| $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ | $\delta^{(\ell)} = (\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)}) \odot \phi^{\prime}(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ |
| $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$ | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)\top}$ |
| $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}(\hat{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{y})$ | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Feedforward Neural Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["INPUT LAYER X1 X2 X3"] --> h1A["H1 PREACT Z1 W1 X PLUS B1"]
    inX --> h1B["H1 PREACT Z2 W2 X PLUS B2"]
    inX --> h1C["H1 PREACT Z3 W3 X PLUS B3"]
    h1A --> h1Act["HIDDEN LAYER 1 ACTIVATION PHI TANH"]
    h1B --> h1Act
    h1C --> h1Act
    h1Act --> h2A["H2 PREACT Z4 W4 A1 PLUS B4"]
    h1Act --> h2B["H2 PREACT Z5 W5 A2 PLUS B5"]
    h2A --> h2Act["HIDDEN LAYER 2 ACTIVATION PHI RELU"]
    h2B --> h2Act
    h2Act --> outP["OUTPUT PREACT Z6 W6 A3 PLUS B6"]
    outP --> outAct["OUTPUT ACTIVATION SOFTMAX OR SIGMOID"]
    outAct --> loss["LOSS FUNCTION CROSS ENTROPY OR MSE"]
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> h1A
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> h1B
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> h1C
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> h2A
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> h2B
    loss -.BACKPROP.-> outP
```

## 4.2 Single Neuron Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    x1["X1"] --> m1["MULTIPLY BY W1"]
    x2["X2"] --> m2["MULTIPLY BY W2"]
    x3["X3"] --> m3["MULTIPLY BY W3"]
    m1 --> sum["SUM Z EQUALS W1 X1 PLUS W2 X2 PLUS W3 X3 PLUS B"]
    m2 --> sum
    m3 --> sum
    sum --> act["ACTIVATION PHI Z"]
    act --> out["OUTPUT A"]
```

## 4.3 Data Flow & Gradient Propagation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TRAINING DATA X Y"] --> B["FORWARD PROPAGATION"]
    B --> C["PREDICTION Y HAT"]
    C --> D["LOSS COMPUTATION L"]
    D --> E["BACKWARD PROPAGATION"]
    E --> F["GRADIENTS DLDW DLDB"]
    F --> G["PARAMETER UPDATE W NEW EQUALS W MINUS ETA DLDW"]
    G --> B
```

## 4.4 Signal & Symbol Legend

| Symbol on Diagram | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| Solid arrow | Forward flow of activations / data |
| Dotted arrow labelled BACKPROP | Backward flow of error gradients |
| $W^{(\ell)}$ | Weight matrix connecting layer $\ell - 1$ to layer $\ell$ |
| $b^{(\ell)}$ | Bias vector of layer $\ell$ |
| $\phi$ | Non-linear activation function (ReLU, tanh, sigmoid, softmax) |
| $\mathcal{L}$ | Scalar loss function value |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When drawing FNNs in the exam, always label (i) input dimension, (ii) number of hidden units per layer, (iii) output dimension, and (iv) the activation function at every layer. A well-labelled diagram typically fetches **2–3 extra marks** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Feedforward Neural Network. Differentiate between a single-layer perceptron and a multi-layer perceptron.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Feedforward Neural Network (FNN) is an artificial neural network in which connections between nodes do **not form a cycle**—information moves strictly from the input layer, through hidden layers, to the output layer.

| Feature | Single-Layer Perceptron | Multi-Layer Perceptron |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Architecture | Input $\rightarrow$ Output only | Input $\rightarrow$ Hidden layer(s) $\rightarrow$ Output |
| Hidden layers | None | One or more |
| Activation | Step function (linear threshold) | Non-linear (sigmoid, ReLU, tanh) |
| Problem solvable | Linearly separable problems (AND, OR) | Non-linearly separable problems (XOR) |
| Learning rule | Perceptron rule (error-driven) | Backpropagation + gradient descent |

*(1 mark for FNN definition, 2 marks for the comparison table.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three activation functions used in neural networks. State the vanishing gradient problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three commonly used activation functions are:
1. **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$, range $(0, 1)$.
2. **Tanh:** $\tanh(z)$, range $(-1, 1)$.
3. **ReLU:** $\max(0, z)$, range $[0, \infty)$.

**Vanishing Gradient Problem:** When backpropagating through deep networks using saturating activations like sigmoid or tanh, the gradients become extremely small (tending to zero) for neurons with large pre-activations. This is because $\sigma^{\prime}(z) \le 0.25$ and $\tanh^{\prime}(z) \le 1$, and these small values are multiplied across many layers. Consequently, the weights of early layers update negligibly, and the network fails to learn effectively. ReLU mitigates this by having a constant gradient of 1 for positive inputs. *(1 mark per activation, 2 marks for the problem description.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks on FNN questions for the following reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting the bias term** $b$ in forward equations—always include it.
> 2. **Confusing dimensions** of $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}$ matrices; remember it is $n_{\ell} \times n_{\ell-1}$.
> 3. **Missing the activation derivative** $\phi^{\prime}(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$ in the backpropagation chain rule.
> 4. **Not stating the learning rule explicitly** with the $\eta$ (eta) symbol.
> 5. Writing "backpropagation" without explaining it is an *application of the chain rule*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the architecture of a **multi-layer feedforward neural network**. Clearly identify the input layer, hidden layer(s), output layer, weights, biases, and activation functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **backpropagation algorithm** for a 2-layer feedforward network with sigmoid hidden units and a linear output, using Mean Squared Error loss. Show all gradient computations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
- **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw an FNN with input layer (e.g., 3 nodes), one hidden layer (e.g., 4 nodes), and output layer (1 node). Label weights $w_{ij}^{(1)}$ between input and hidden, and $w_j^{(2)}$ between hidden and output. Label biases $b_i^{(1)}$ and $b^{(2)}$.
- **Component description (2 marks):** Explain input, hidden, output roles.
- **Forward equations (2 marks):** State $z_i^{(1)} = \sum_j w_{ij}^{(1)} x_j + b_i^{(1)}$, $a_i^{(1)} = \sigma(z_i^{(1)})$, $z^{(2)} = \sum_i w_i^{(2)} a_i^{(1)} + b^{(2)}$, $\hat{y} = z^{(2)}$.

**(b) Backpropagation Derivation (7 marks):**

**Forward Pass:**
$$z_i^{(1)} = \sum_{j} w_{ij}^{(1)} x_j + b_i^{(1)}, \quad a_i^{(1)} = \sigma(z_i^{(1)}), \quad z^{(2)} = \sum_{i} w_i^{(2)} a_i^{(1)} + b^{(2)}, \quad \hat{y} = z^{(2)}$$

**Loss:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2}$$

**Output layer error signal:**
$$\delta^{(2)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot 1 = \hat{y} - y$$

**Hidden layer error signal (chain rule):**
$$\delta_i^{(1)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial a_i^{(1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a_i^{(1)}}{\partial z_i^{(1)}}$$

Computing each term:
- $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = \hat{y} - y$
- $\dfrac{\partial z^{(2)}}{\partial a_i^{(1)}} = w_i^{(2)}$
- $\dfrac{\partial a_i^{(1)}}{\partial z_i^{(1)}} = \sigma(z_i^{(1)})\bigl(1 - \sigma(z_i^{(1)})\bigr) = a_i^{(1)}(1 - a_i^{(1)})$

Combining:
$$\delta_i^{(1)} = (\hat{y} - y)\, w_i^{(2)}\, a_i^{(1)} (1 - a_i^{(1)})$$

**Gradients:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot a_i^{(1)} = (\hat{y} - y) \, a_i^{(1)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} = \hat{y} - y$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(1)}} = \delta_i^{(1)} \cdot x_j = (\hat{y} - y) \, w_i^{(2)} \, a_i^{(1)}(1 - a_i^{(1)}) \, x_j$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_i^{(1)}} = \delta_i^{(1)} = (\hat{y} - y) \, w_i^{(2)} \, a_i^{(1)} (1 - a_i^{(1)})$$

**Update rule with learning rate $\eta$:**
$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}, \quad b \leftarrow b - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b}$$

*(Award marks as: 2 for forward, 2 for error signals, 2 for gradients, 1 for update rule.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **role of activation functions** in a neural network. Compare Sigmoid, Tanh, and ReLU in terms of formula, range, derivative, and vanishing-gradient behaviour. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feedforward network has input $\mathbf{x} = [1, 0]^{\top}$, weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0.1, -0.2]^{\top}$, hidden activation = ReLU, output weights $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = [1.0, -0.5]$, $b^{(2)} = 0.3$, output activation = linear. **Compute the network output $\hat{y}$ and the MSE loss** for true target $y = 1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of Activation Functions (7 marks):**
Activation functions introduce **non-linearity** into the network. Without them, stacking multiple linear layers collapses mathematically to a single linear transformation, severely limiting representational power. They allow the network to learn complex, non-linear decision boundaries.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh | ReLU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $\dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $\dfrac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$ | $\max(0, z)$ |
| Range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Derivative | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) \le 0.25$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z) \le 1$ | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ |
| Zero-centered | No | Yes | No |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe for large $\vert z \vert$ | Severe for large $\vert z \vert$ | None for $z > 0$ |
| Computational cost | High (exp) | High (exp) | Very low (max) |

ReLU is preferred in modern networks due to its computational efficiency and mitigation of the vanishing gradient problem. *(1 mark for the role; 5 marks for the comparison; 1 mark for the recommendation.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hidden layer pre-activation:**
$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiplying the matrix by the vector:
$$= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \cdot 1 + (-0.3) \cdot 0 \\ 0.2 \cdot 1 + 0.8 \cdot 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Hidden layer activation (ReLU):**
$$\mathbf{a}^{(1)} = \max(0, \mathbf{z}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \max(0, 0.6) \\ \max(0, 0.0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Output layer pre-activation:**
$$z^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{a}^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.3$$

Multiplying the row vector by the column vector:
$$= (1.0 \cdot 0.6) + (-0.5 \cdot 0.0) + 0.3 = 0.6 + 0.0 + 0.3 = 0.9$$

**Step 4 — Output (linear activation):**
$$\hat{y} = z^{(2)} = 0.9$$

**Step 5 — MSE Loss:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(0.9 - 1)^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(-0.1)^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(0.01) = 0.005$$

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 0.9, \quad \mathcal{L} = 0.005}$$

*(Award marks: 1.5 for Step 1, 1 for Step 2, 1.5 for Step 3, 1 for Step 4, 2 for Step 5.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feedforward Neural Networks**

- **Definition (1-liner):** FNN = layered network with strictly forward information flow; no cycles/loops.
- **Core equation pair:** $\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$ followed by $\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$.
- **Two phases of learning:** Forward pass (compute $\hat{y}$) and Backward pass (compute $\delta^{(\ell)}$ using chain rule).
- **Error signal recursion:** $\delta^{(\ell)} = \bigl(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell+1)\top} \delta^{(\ell+1)}\bigr) \odot \phi^{\prime}(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})$.
- **Gradient w.r.t. weights:** $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)}} = \delta^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)\top}$ — outer product of error and previous activation.
- **Update rule:** $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)\top}$.
- **Sigmoid derivative (must memorize):** $\sigma^{\prime}(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
- **Tanh derivative (must memorize):** $\tanh^{\prime}(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$.
- **ReLU derivative (must memorize):** $\phi^{\prime}(z) = 1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$.
- **Softmax + Cross-Entropy gradient (golden result):** $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z_i = p_i - y_i$.
- **Default activation choices:** ReLU for hidden layers, Sigmoid (binary) or Softmax (multi-class) for output, Linear for regression output.
- **Default loss choices:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- **Vanishing gradient:** Sigmoid/tanh saturate; prefer ReLU in deep networks.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A single hidden layer with enough neurons can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain.
- **Dimensions rule:** If layer $\ell$ has $n_{\ell}$ neurons and layer $\ell-1$ has $n_{\ell-1}$ neurons, then $\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell} \times n_{\ell-1}}$ and $\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\ell} \times 1}$.
- **Bias is essential:** Always include $b^{(\ell)}$; it shifts the activation and improves fit.
- **KTU exam mantra:** *"Forward store, backward derive, then update with gradient descent."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation** (short for *backward propagation of errors*) is a **supervised learning algorithm** used to train **Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** by efficiently computing the gradient of the **loss function** with respect to each weight in the network. It is fundamentally an application of the **differential chain rule** of calculus, propagating the error signal **backwards** from the output layer through the hidden layers to the input layer.

Formally, given a network with weights $W$, biases $b$, and a differentiable loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$, backpropagation computes:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

for every layer $l$ in the network. These gradients are subsequently consumed by an **optimization algorithm** (typically **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** or its variants) to update the parameters in the direction of minimum loss.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blame Game in a Corporate Office":**

Imagine a CEO (the **loss function**) at the top of a 5-floor company who notices the company's profit has dropped by ₹10 Lakhs. She shouts down to the floor managers: *"Who is responsible?!"* Each manager, instead of guessing, calculates *exactly* how much his/her decision contributed to the loss by looking at their own reports and forwarding partial blame *downwards*. By the time the message reaches the ground-floor workers (the **input layer weights**), every single employee knows *precisely* how much they contributed to the mistake — and by how much they must adjust their behaviour to fix it next time.

- The **forward pass** = the CEO receiving bad results flowing upward from the bottom.
- The **backward pass** = the error signal flowing downward, computing each person's share of the blame.
- The **weight update** = every employee tweaking their behaviour by a tiny amount proportional to their share of the blame.

This is exactly what backpropagation does — it apportions **credit and blame** to every weight in the network, enabling the model to **learn from its mistakes**.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Hyperparameters

The following table highlights the standard tuning parameters critical to KTU board questions:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning rate | $\eta$ | **0.001 – 0.1** | Step size for weight update |
| Momentum | $\beta$ | **0.9** | Smooths gradient updates |
| Number of epochs | $E$ | **10 – 1000** | Full passes through training data |
| Batch size | $B$ | **32, 64, 128, 256** | Samples per gradient update |
| Weight initialization | — | **Xavier / He** | Prevents vanishing/exploding gradients |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Two Phases

The backpropagation algorithm operates in **two distinct passes** during every training iteration:

**Phase A — Forward Pass (Inference):**

1. Input $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into the input layer.
2. For each layer $l = 1, 2, \dots, L$:
   - Compute the pre-activation: $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$
   - Apply the activation function: $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \phi^{(l)}(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$
3. The output $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}$ is produced.
4. Compute the loss: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{loss}}(\mathbf{y}, \hat{\mathbf{y}})$

**Phase B — Backward Pass (Error Propagation):**

1. Compute the **output error** $\delta^{(L)}$ at the final layer using the chain rule.
2. For each layer $l = L-1, L-2, \dots, 1$:
   - Propagate the error backwards through the transpose of the weight matrix.
   - Element-wise multiply by the derivative of the activation function.
3. Compute gradients $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}}$.
4. Update parameters using gradient descent: $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$

### 2.2 The Chain Rule — The Mathematical Heart of Backpropagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chain rule states that for composite functions, the derivative of the outer function with respect to an inner parameter equals the **product of intermediate derivatives**.

For a scalar chain: if $y = f(g(h(x)))$, then:

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{df}{dg} \cdot \frac{dg}{dh} \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

For neural networks, since the loss depends on weights *indirectly* through layers of activations, the chain rule allows us to "unroll" this dependency into a tractable product of local gradients.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU board question on backpropagation.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-activation | $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | Linear transformation at layer $l$ |
| 2 | Activation | $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \phi(\mathbf{z}^{(l)})$ | Non-linear transformation |
| 3 | Output error (MSE + linear) | $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \phi'(z^{(L)})$ | Error at output layer |
| 4 | Hidden layer error | $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \phi'(z^{(l)})$ | Error propagation |
| 5 | Weight gradient | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top$ | Gradient w.r.t. weights |
| 6 | Bias gradient | $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$ | Gradient w.r.t. biases |
| 7 | Weight update rule | $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$ | SGD parameter update |
| 8 | Bias update rule | $\mathbf{b}^{(l)} \leftarrow \mathbf{b}^{(l)} - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{b}^{(l)}}$ | Bias parameter update |
| 9 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 10 | Binary CE Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum [y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Binary Cross-Entropy |

**Notation key:** $\odot$ denotes the **Hadamard (element-wise) product**, $^\top$ denotes matrix transpose, and $\phi'$ denotes the activation function's derivative.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Backpropagation is the **engine of modern AI**:

- **Computer Vision (CNNs):** Enables facial recognition, autonomous vehicle perception, medical imaging diagnostics.
- **Natural Language Processing (Transformers/LSTMs):** Powers translation, chatbots, search engines.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Trains agents in game-playing AI (AlphaGo, Atari).
- **Scientific Computing:** Used in physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving PDEs.
- **Speech Recognition:** Underlies Siri, Alexa, and Google Assistant.

Without backpropagation, deep learning as a field would simply not exist — it is the *only known efficient method* to compute gradients in networks with millions of parameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Layer Error $\delta^{(L)}$

We begin by defining the loss for a single training example using Mean Squared Error:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$$

where $\hat{y} = a^{(L)} = \phi(z^{(L)})$ and $z^{(L)} = W^{(L)} a^{(L-1)} + b^{(L)}$.

We want $\delta^{(L)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}}$. Applying the **chain rule**:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial a^{(L)}} \left[ \frac{1}{2}(y - a^{(L)})^2 \right] = -(y - a^{(L)})$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}}$ (derivative of activation):

$$\frac{\partial a^{(L)}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \phi'(z^{(L)})$$

**Step 3:** Multiply them together:

$$\delta^{(L)} = -(y - a^{(L)}) \cdot \phi'(z^{(L)}) = (a^{(L)} - y) \cdot \phi'(z^{(L)})$$

The negative of $(y - a^{(L)})$ becomes $(a^{(L)} - y)$, the prediction-minus-target residual.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hidden Layer Error $\delta^{(l)}$

For a hidden layer $l < L$, the error must be propagated through both the activation function and the subsequent layer's weights:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

Since $z^{(l+1)} = W^{(l+1)} a^{(l)} + b^{(l+1)} = W^{(l+1)} \phi(z^{(l)}) + b^{(l+1)}$, we apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

**Step 1:** The first term is $\delta^{(l+1)}$ by definition.

**Step 2:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$:

$$\frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial z^{(l)}} \left[ W^{(l+1)} \phi(z^{(l)}) + b^{(l+1)} \right] = W^{(l+1)} \phi'(z^{(l)})$$

**Step 3:** Combine using matrix form:

$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \, \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \phi'(z^{(l)})$$

The transpose $(W^{(l+1)})^\top$ is necessary because we are propagating error **backwards**, distributing the upstream error according to the connection strength.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Weight and Bias Gradients

The weight gradient is the partial derivative of the loss with respect to a specific weight $W^{(l)}_{ij}$ — the weight connecting neuron $j$ in layer $l-1$ to neuron $i$ in layer $l$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}_{ij}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}_i}{\partial W^{(l)}_{ij}} = \delta^{(l)}_i \cdot a^{(l-1)}_j$$

In matrix form:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \, (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top$$

For the bias (since $z^{(l)}_i = W^{(l)}_i a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}_i$, and $\partial z^{(l)}_i / \partial b^{(l)}_i = 1$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 2-2-1 Network

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a classic KTU-style numerical problem. Memorize the workflow.**

**Network setup:**
- Input: $\mathbf{x} = [0.5, \; 0.3]^\top$, Target: $y = 1$
- Layer 1 (hidden): $W^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0.1, \; 0.2]^\top$
- Layer 2 (output): $W^{(2)} = [0.3, \; 0.6]$, $b^{(2)} = [0.1]$
- Activation: **Sigmoid** $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$
- Loss: **MSE** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2$
- Learning rate: $\eta = 0.5$

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (Layer 1):**

$$z^{(1)} = W^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$z^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.20 + 0.06 + 0.10 \\ 0.05 + 0.15 + 0.20 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.36 \\ 0.40 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$a^{(1)} = \phi(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.36}} \\ \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.40}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5890 \\ 0.5987 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (Layer 2):**

$$z^{(2)} = W^{(2)} a^{(1)} + b^{(2)} = [0.3, \; 0.6] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.5890 \\ 0.5987 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1$$

$$z^{(2)} = 0.1767 + 0.3592 + 0.1 = 0.6359$$

$$\hat{y} = a^{(2)} = \phi(0.6359) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.6359}} = 0.6538$$

**Step 3 — Compute Loss:**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.6538)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.3462)^2 = 0.0599$$

**Step 4 — Backward Pass: Output Error**

For sigmoid, $\phi'(z) = \phi(z)(1 - \phi(z))$. So:

$$\phi'(z^{(2)}) = 0.6538 \times (1 - 0.6538) = 0.6538 \times 0.3462 = 0.2263$$

$$\delta^{(2)} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot \phi'(z^{(2)}) = (0.6538 - 1) \times 0.2263 = -0.0784$$

**Step 5 — Backward Pass: Hidden Layer Error**

$$\phi'(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5890(1-0.5890) \\ 0.5987(1-0.5987) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2421 \\ 0.2403 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = (W^{(2)})^\top \, \delta^{(2)} \odot \phi'(z^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} (-0.0784) \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2421 \\ 0.2403 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\delta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.0235 \\ -0.0470 \end{bmatrix} \odot \begin{bmatrix} 0.2421 \\ 0.2403 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.00569 \\ -0.01130 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Weight Gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \delta^{(2)} \cdot (a^{(1)})^\top = -0.0784 \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.5890 & 0.5987 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.04618 & -0.04694 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \delta^{(1)} \cdot (\mathbf{x})^\top = \begin{bmatrix} -0.00569 \\ -0.01130 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.002845 & -0.001707 \\ -0.005650 & -0.003390 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Parameter Update:**
