$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = W^{(2)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(2)}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} - 0.5 \times \begin{bmatrix} -0.04618 & -0.04694 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$W^{(2)}_{\text{new}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.32309 & 0.62347 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$W^{(1)}_{\text{new}} = W^{(1)} - 0.5 \times \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.40142 & 0.20085 \\ 0.10283 & 0.50170 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Activation Functions

| Activation | $\phi(z)$ | $\phi'(z)$ | Used In | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | $\frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $\phi(z)(1-\phi(z))$ | Output (binary) | Suffers vanishing gradient |
| Tanh | $\frac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ | $1 - \tanh^2(z)$ | Hidden layers | Zero-centered |
| ReLU | $\max(0, z)$ | $0$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | Hidden (default) | Solves vanishing gradient partially |
| Leaky ReLU | $\max(0.01z, z)$ | $0.01$ if $z<0$, else $1$ | Hidden | Prevents dead neurons |
| Softmax | $\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | $\phi_i(\delta_{ij} - \phi_j)$ | Output (multi-class) | Probabilistic outputs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Computational Graph — Forward and Backward Flow

The following mermaid diagram illustrates the complete flow of a backpropagation pass through a generic 3-layer network:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x"] --> Z1["z1 = W1 x + b1"]
    Z1 --> A1["a1 = phi z1"]
    A1 --> Z2["z2 = W2 a1 + b2"]
    Z2 --> A2["a2 = phi z2"]
    A2 --> Z3["z3 = W3 a2 + b3"]
    Z3 --> Yhat["yhat = phi z3"]
    Yhat --> L["Loss L y yhat"]
    Y["Target y"] --> L

    L -.->|delta3| Z3
    Z3 -.->|delta2| Z2
    Z2 -.->|delta1| Z1

    Z1 -.->|grad W1| W1["W1 updated"]
    Z2 -.->|grad W2| W2["W2 updated"]
    Z3 -.->|grad W3| W3["W3 updated"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram legend:** Solid arrows represent the forward pass (data flowing up). Dotted arrows represent the backward pass (gradients flowing down). Each $\delta^{(l)}$ represents the local error term at layer $l$.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FWD["FORWARD PASS"]
        F1["Load minibatch X"] --> F2["Compute activations a layer by layer"]
        F2 --> F3["Compute prediction yhat"]
        F3 --> F4["Compute Loss L"]
    end

    subgraph BWD["BACKWARD PASS"]
        F4 --> B1["Compute output error deltaL"]
        B1 --> B2["Backpropagate errors to hidden layers"]
        B2 --> B3["Compute weight and bias gradients"]
    end

    subgraph UPD["PARAMETER UPDATE"]
        B3 --> U1["Apply SGD or variant Adam RMSprop"]
        U1 --> U2["Update W and b in place"]
        U2 --> U3{"Convergence check"}
    end

    U3 -->|not converged| F1
    U3 -->|converged| END["Trained Model"]
```

### 4.3 Visualization of the Chain Rule Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["L = Loss"] -->|"dL/dyhat"| Y["yhat = aL"]
    Y -->|"dyhat/dzL"| Z["zL = W aL-1 + b"]
    Z -->|"dzL/dW"| W["W weight matrix"]
    Z -->|"dzL/daL-1"| A["aL-1 previous activations"]
```

The total gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$ is the **product** of all three factors along the path — exactly what the chain rule demands.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Define backpropagation. Why is it considered the backbone of deep learning?

**Model Answer:**  
Backpropagation is a supervised learning algorithm that computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to each weight in a neural network by recursively applying the **chain rule of calculus** from the output layer back to the input layer. It is considered the backbone of deep learning because it provides an *efficient* (polynomial-time) method to compute gradients in networks with millions of parameters. Without backpropagation, training deep networks via gradient descent would be computationally infeasible. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is the role of the **learning rate** $\eta$ in backpropagation? What happens if $\eta$ is too small or too large?

**Model Answer:**  
The learning rate $\eta$ controls the **step size** taken during gradient descent updates: $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$.

- If $\eta$ is **too small**, convergence becomes very slow, and the model may get stuck in suboptimal local minima.
- If $\eta$ is **too large**, the loss may oscillate or diverge — the model can fail to converge entirely.

A well-tuned $\eta$ (typically **0.001–0.1**) balances speed and stability. Techniques like **learning rate scheduling**, **Adam optimizer**, and **cyclical learning rates** are used to adaptively control $\eta$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **transpose** the weight matrix when propagating error backwards — this is the single most common deduction (loss of 2–3 marks).
> - In numerical problems, students often confuse $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z}$ with $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$. Always compute the **local error $\delta$** first, then derive gradients.
> - Do **not** apply the activation derivative to the wrong layer — it must be applied at the layer where the activation was computed.
> - State the **chain rule explicitly** in your derivation — examiners reward clarity.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the backpropagation equations for a multi-layer feedforward neural network using the chain rule. Clearly state the expressions for: (i) output layer error $\delta^{(L)}$, (ii) hidden layer error $\delta^{(l)}$, and (iii) weight gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}}$. Assume Mean Squared Error loss and sigmoid activation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-2-1 network with input $\mathbf{x} = [0.4, 0.6]^\top$, target $y = 0.8$. Given weights and biases:

$$W^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.5 \\ 0.4 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = [0.1, 0.3]^\top$$
$$W^{(2)} = [0.6, 0.7], \quad b^{(2)} = 0.2$$

Using sigmoid activation and learning rate $\eta = 0.5$, perform **one complete iteration** of backpropagation (forward pass, loss computation, backward pass, weight update). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3** of these notes. The derivation yields:

$$\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - y) \odot \phi'(z^{(L)})$$

$$\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \phi'(z^{(l)})$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \, (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top$$

**Part (b):** Numerical solution follows the exact 7-step procedure demonstrated in **Section 3.4**. Students must show:

1. Pre-activations $z^{(1)}$ and $z^{(2)}$ — (2 marks)
2. Activations $a^{(1)}$ and $\hat{y}$ — (2 marks)
3. Loss value and output error $\delta^{(2)}$ — (1 mark)
4. Hidden error $\delta^{(1)}$ — (1 mark)
5. Updated weights $W^{(1)}_{\text{new}}$ and $W^{(2)}_{\text{new}}$ — (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** in deep neural networks. How do the following mitigate it: (i) ReLU activation, (ii) Batch Normalization, (iii) careful weight initialization (Xavier/He)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)**, **Mini-batch SGD**, and **Adam optimizer** in the context of backpropagation. Provide the update rule for each and discuss when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a):**  
- **Vanishing gradient:** When $\vert \phi'(z) \vert < 1$ (e.g., sigmoid: max derivative is 0.25), errors shrink exponentially as they propagate backwards, causing early layers to learn negligibly slowly.  
- **Exploding gradient:** When $\vert \phi'(z) \vert > 1$ or weights are large, gradients grow exponentially, causing unstable updates and divergence.

**Mitigations:**
- **ReLU:** $\phi'(z) = 1$ for $z > 0$ — gradient does not shrink for active neurons. (2 marks)
- **Batch Normalization:** Normalizes layer inputs to zero mean and unit variance per minibatch, keeping gradients in a stable range. (2 marks)
- **Xavier/He initialization:** Sets initial weights as $W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sqrt{2/n_{\text{in}}})$ for ReLU (He) or $\sqrt{1/n_{\text{in}}}$ for tanh (Xavier), keeping variance of activations stable across layers. (3 marks)

**Part (b):**

| Optimizer | Update Rule | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **SGD (Pure)** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W \mathcal{L}(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Online learning, streaming data |
| **Mini-batch SGD** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{1}{B}\sum_{i=1}^{B} \nabla_W \mathcal{L}(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Standard training, GPUs |
| **Adam** | $W \leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\hat{m}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \epsilon}$ | Default choice, adaptive, fast convergence |

SGD is slow but generalizes well. Adam adapts learning rates per-parameter using first and second moment estimates (mean and variance of gradients). **(7 marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Backpropagation**

- ✅ Backpropagation = **forward pass + backward pass + parameter update**.
- ✅ The **chain rule** is the mathematical foundation — never forget to apply it sequentially.
- ✅ **Local error** $\delta^{(l)} = \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$ is the *anchor* of all backprop derivations.
- ✅ Output error: $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - y) \odot \phi'(z^{(L)})$.
- ✅ Hidden error: $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \phi'(z^{(l)})$.
- ✅ Weight gradient: $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})^\top$.
- ✅ Bias gradient: $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$.
- ✅ Update rule (SGD): $W \leftarrow W - \eta \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}$.
- ✅ Activation choice critically affects gradient flow — prefer **ReLU** in hidden layers.
- ✅ Vanishing/exploding gradients are the *primary failure mode* of deep networks.
- ✅ **Hadamard product** $\odot$ is required (not regular matrix multiplication) when combining $\delta$ with $\phi'(z)$.
- ✅ Numerical problems in KTU typically use **2-2-1** or **2-3-1** networks with **sigmoid** activation and **MSE** loss — practice these!
- ✅ Memorize the **order of operations**: Forward → Loss → $\delta^{(L)}$ → $\delta^{(l)}$ → Gradients → Update.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gradient Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gradient Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gradient Descent** is a first-order, iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable multivariable function. In the context of Deep Learning, it is the foundational mechanism used to minimize the **loss function** (also called cost function) $J(\theta)$ by iteratively updating the model parameters $\theta$ in the direction opposite to the gradient of the function evaluated at the current point.

Mathematically, the canonical update rule is expressed as:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ represents the parameter vector at iteration $t$
- $\eta$ (eta) is the **learning rate**, a positive scalar hyperparameter typically in the range $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$
- $\nabla J(\theta_t)$ is the gradient (vector of partial derivatives) of the cost function with respect to the parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Gradient Descent is the *primary* optimization engine of virtually every neural network. Mastery of its mathematical foundation, variants (Batch, Mini-batch, Stochastic), and convergence behavior is **mandatory** for all Part A and Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain and you want to reach the lowest valley. Because of the fog, you cannot see the valley. However, you *can* feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet.

**The Rule You Follow:**
1. Feel the ground — measure the slope (the gradient) where you are standing.
2. Take a small step in the direction where the ground slopes **downward** most steeply.
3. Repeat until the ground feels flat (gradient ≈ 0).

**Mapping the Analogy to the Mathematics:**

| Real-World Mountain | Mathematical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your position on the mountain | Parameter vector $\theta_t$ |
| Altitude of the mountain | Loss function $J(\theta)$ |
| Steepness of the slope | Gradient $\nabla J(\theta_t)$ |
| Length of your step | Learning rate $\eta$ |
| Reaching the valley floor | Convergence to minimum |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The *negative* sign in the update rule is critical. Since the gradient points in the direction of *steepest ascent* (uphill), we move in the *negative* gradient direction to descend toward the minimum.

## 1.3 Role in Deep Learning Pipelines

Gradient Descent is the engine that powers **backpropagation** in neural networks. During training:
- The **forward pass** computes predictions and the loss $J(\theta)$.
- The **backward pass** computes $\nabla J(\theta)$ via the chain rule.
- The **optimizer** (e.g., SGD, Adam) applies the gradient descent update.

Without gradient descent, deep neural networks could not learn from data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Gradient Descent

The algorithm rests on three foundational pillars. Understanding *why* each exists is essential for KTU examination answers.

### Pillar 1: The Gradient (Direction of Steepest Ascent)
For a function $J(\theta)$ where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the gradient is the vector of all partial derivatives:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} \\ \vdots \\ \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_n} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Why this direction?** By the directional derivative theorem, the gradient points in the direction that maximizes the rate of increase of $J$. Therefore, $-\nabla J(\theta)$ points in the direction of **steepest descent**.

### Pillar 2: The Learning Rate (Step Size)
The learning rate $\eta$ controls the magnitude of each parameter update.

- If $\eta$ is **too small** $\Rightarrow$ convergence is painfully slow; algorithm may get stuck in plateaus.
- If $\eta$ is **too large** $\Rightarrow$ updates overshoot the minimum; algorithm may **diverge** or oscillate.
- The optimal $\eta$ is problem-dependent and is typically tuned via grid search or learning rate schedules.

### Pillar 3: Convergence Criterion
The algorithm terminates when one of the following conditions is met:
1. Gradient norm falls below tolerance: $\vert\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert\vert < \epsilon$
2. Maximum number of iterations $T$ is reached
3. Change in loss between iterations is negligible: $\vert J(\theta_{t+1}) - J(\theta_t) \vert < \delta$

## 2.2 Variants of Gradient Descent — Critical for KTU

### 2.2.1 Batch Gradient Descent (BGD)
Uses the **entire training dataset** to compute the gradient at each iteration.

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathcal{D}_{\text{full}})$$

- **Pros:** Deterministic, stable convergence, smooth trajectory.
- **Cons:** Very slow for large datasets; requires all data in memory.

### 2.2.2 Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
Uses a **single randomly selected training example** $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ per iteration.

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$$

- **Pros:** Fast per-iteration; introduces noise that can help escape shallow local minima; enables online learning.
- **Cons:** Noisy convergence path; may never settle at the exact minimum.

### 2.2.3 Mini-Batch Gradient Descent (MBGD) — *The Industry Standard*
Uses a small random batch $\mathcal{B}_t$ of size $m$ (typically $m = 32, 64, 128, 256$).

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathcal{B}_t)$$

- **Pros:** Balances BGD stability and SGD speed; leverages hardware parallelism (GPUs).
- **Cons:** Introduces the mini-batch size as an additional hyperparameter.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$ | Canonical update rule | **Must memorize** — appears in 90% of derivations |
| $\nabla J(\theta) = \left[\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_n}\right]^T$ | Gradient vector | Definition questions |
| $\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)$ | Objective: find optimal parameters | Conceptual |
| $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ | Mean loss across $m$ examples | Used in BGD/MBGD |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate, $\eta > 0$ | Hyperparameter question |
| $\epsilon$ | Convergence tolerance | Convergence criteria |
| $T$ | Maximum iterations | Termination |
| $\vert\vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \vert\vert < \epsilon$ | Convergence criterion | Theory |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Gradient descent (and its modern descendants — Adam, RMSProp, AdaGrad) trains virtually every production deep learning system, from image classifiers (CNNs) to large language models (Transformers). Mastering its mechanics is non-negotiable for any AI/ML engineer.

## 2.4 Challenges in Gradient Descent

1. **Local Minima:** Non-convex loss landscapes (deep networks) contain many local minima; gradient descent may converge to a suboptimal one.
2. **Saddle Points:** Points where gradient is zero but the function is neither a max nor a min. Common in high-dimensional spaces.
3. **Vanishing / Exploding Gradients:** In deep networks, gradients may shrink to near-zero or grow uncontrollably across layers.
4. **Ill-Conditioning:** The loss surface may be much steeper in some directions than others, causing zig-zag trajectories.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule from First-Order Taylor Expansion

This derivation is a **high-probability KTU 14-mark question**. We derive *why* the update rule $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$ is mathematically optimal for local descent.

### Step 1 — Start with the Optimization Goal
We wish to find $\theta^*$ such that $J(\theta^*)$ is a local minimum. Consider a small perturbation $\Delta \theta$ applied to the current parameter $\theta_t$. We want the new loss $J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta)$ to be smaller than the current loss $J(\theta_t)$.

$$J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta) < J(\theta_t)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the First-Order Taylor Series Expansion
The first-order Taylor expansion of $J(\theta)$ around the point $\theta_t$ is:

$$J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta$$

This approximation is valid when $\Delta \theta$ is sufficiently small (a key assumption of gradient descent).

### Step 3 — Impose the Descent Condition
Substitute the expansion into the descent requirement:

$$J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta < J(\theta_t)$$

Cancel $J(\theta_t)$ from both sides:

$$\nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta < 0$$

This inequality says: **the perturbation $\Delta \theta$ must have a negative inner product with the gradient**.

### Step 4 — Choose $\Delta \theta$ to Make the Inner Product Most Negative
The inner product $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b}$ is minimized (most negative) when $\mathbf{b} = -\eta \mathbf{a}$ for any $\eta > 0$. Therefore, to make $\nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta$ as negative as possible, we set:

$$\Delta \theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)$$

where $\eta > 0$ is a small scalar (the learning rate).

### Step 5 — Form the Update Rule
Substitute $\Delta \theta$ back into the perturbation equation $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + (-\eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t))$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)}$$

This is the **canonical gradient descent update rule** — derived rigorously from first principles. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note that the inequality $\nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta < 0$ is satisfied strictly only when $\nabla J(\theta_t) \neq 0$. This is why the algorithm terminates at points where the gradient vanishes (critical points: minima, maxima, or saddle points).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2D Quadratic Loss

A classic KTU-style numerical problem. Consider the loss function:

$$J(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \theta_1^2 + 2\theta_2^2 - 4\theta_1 - 8\theta_2 + 20$$

**Task:** Perform **two iterations** of gradient descent starting from $\theta^{(0)} = (0, 0)$ with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Gradient
The partial derivatives are:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = 2\theta_1 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_2} = 4\theta_2 - 8$$

Therefore the gradient vector is:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 2\theta_1 - 4 \\[4pt] 4\theta_2 - 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Iteration 1 (at $\theta^{(0)} = (0, 0)$)
Evaluate the gradient:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(0)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0) - 4 \\ 4(0) - 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply the update rule:

$$\theta^{(1)} = \theta^{(0)} - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta^{(0)})$$

$$\theta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ -8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -0.4 \\ -0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Iteration 2 (at $\theta^{(1)} = (0.4, 0.8)$)
Evaluate the gradient at the new point:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.4) - 4 \\ 4(0.8) - 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.2 \\ -4.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply the update rule:

$$\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -3.2 \\ -4.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} -0.32 \\ -0.48 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.72 \\ 1.28 \end{bmatrix}}$$

### Step 4 — Verify Convergence
The true minimum (analytically) occurs at $\nabla J = 0$, i.e., $\theta_1^* = 2$ and $\theta_2^* = 2$. After only 2 iterations, we have moved from $(0, 0)$ to $(0.72, 1.28)$ — clearly progressing toward the true minimum of $(2, 2)$, confirming the algorithm is working correctly.

## 3.3 Convergence Rate Analysis (Theoretical)

For a **convex**, **L-smooth** function with $\mu$-strong convexity, gradient descent with constant step size $\eta = \dfrac{1}{L}$ achieves the following linear convergence rate:

$$J(\theta_t) - J(\theta^*) \leq \left( 1 - \frac{\mu}{L} \right)^t \cdot \left( J(\theta_0) - J(\theta^*) \right)$$

The ratio $\kappa = \dfrac{L}{\mu}$ is called the **condition number** of the problem. Smaller $\kappa$ implies faster convergence. This is why **preconditioning** and **normalization** (e.g., batch normalization) accelerate training in practice.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Convergence Comparison of Variants

| Property | BGD | SGD | MBGD |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data per update | $N$ (all) | $1$ | $m$ (batch) |
| Update frequency per epoch | $1$ | $N$ | $N/m$ |
| Per-update cost | High | Very low | Moderate |
| Convergence path | Smooth, stable | Noisy, oscillating | Balanced |
| Memory requirement | Very high | Negligible | Moderate |
| Escapes local minima? | No | Yes (via noise) | Sometimes |
| Real-world usage | Small datasets, theory | Online learning | **Standard for deep learning** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gradient Descent Iteration Loop — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize parameters theta_0 randomly] --> B[Set learning rate eta and max iterations T]
    B --> C[Iteration t = 0]
    C --> D{Shuffle and split data into mini-batches B_t}
    D --> E[Forward pass: compute predictions y_hat]
    E --> F[Compute loss J theta on batch B_t]
    F --> G[Backward pass: compute gradient of J wrt theta]
    G --> H[Update parameters: theta equals theta minus eta times gradient]
    H --> I{Convergence check: gradient norm less than epsilon OR t equals T}
    I -->|Not converged| J[Increment t by 1]
    J --> D
    I -->|Converged| K[Return optimal parameters theta star]
    K --> L[End: Use theta star for inference]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent in a Deep Learning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputA[Training Dataset D with N samples] --> Shuffle[Shuffle and Partition into Mini-Batches of size m]
    Shuffle --> BatchLoop[For each mini-batch B_t in t = 1 to T]

    BatchLoop --> Fwd[Forward Propagation]
    Fwd --> LossComp[Loss Computation: J theta on B_t]
    LossComp --> Bwd[Backward Propagation: Chain Rule]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Gradient Descent. State the update rule and explain the role of the learning rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gradient Descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to minimize a differentiable function $J(\theta)$ by moving parameters in the direction of steepest descent — i.e., the negative of the gradient.

The update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)$$

The **learning rate** $\eta$ is a positive hyperparameter that controls the size of the step taken at each iteration. A small $\eta$ yields slow but stable convergence, while a large $\eta$ risks overshooting the minimum and causing divergence. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare Batch Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, and Mini-Batch Gradient Descent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Variant | Data Used per Update | Speed | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Batch GD | Entire dataset | Slow | Very stable |
| Stochastic GD | 1 sample | Very fast per step | Noisy, oscillates |
| Mini-Batch GD | $m$ samples (32–256) | Fast and parallelizable | Balanced |

Mini-Batch GD is the de facto standard in modern deep learning as it balances convergence stability with computational efficiency, especially on GPUs. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the gradient descent update rule $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$ from the first-order Taylor series expansion. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the loss function $J(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^2 + 5$, perform **three iterations** of gradient descent starting from $\theta^{(0)} = 0$ with learning rate $\eta = 0.2$. Comment on whether the algorithm is converging to the true minimum.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Assumption:** $J(\theta)$ is differentiable and we consider a small perturbation $\Delta \theta$ around $\theta_t$.

**Step 1:** Apply the first-order Taylor expansion of $J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta)$:

$$J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta) \approx J(\theta_t) + \nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 2:** Impose the descent condition $J(\theta_t + \Delta \theta) < J(\theta_t)$, which simplifies to:

$$\nabla J(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta \theta < 0 \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 3:** The inner product is most negative when $\Delta \theta$ is anti-parallel to the gradient. Choosing $\eta > 0$:

$$\Delta \theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t) \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\Delta \theta$ into $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t) \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Justification of Step 3:** By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, the inner product $\nabla J^T \cdot \Delta \theta \geq -\vert\vert \nabla J \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \Delta \theta \vert\vert$ with equality when $\Delta \theta = -\eta \nabla J$. **(1 mark for stating the justification)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The function is $J(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^2 + 5$. The gradient is:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \frac{dJ}{d\theta} = 2(\theta - 3)$$

**Iteration 1:** At $\theta^{(0)} = 0$:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(0)}) = 2(0 - 3) = -6$$

$$\theta^{(1)} = 0 - 0.2 \times (-6) = 0 + 1.2 = 1.2 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Iteration 2:** At $\theta^{(1)} = 1.2$:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(1)}) = 2(1.2 - 3) = 2(-1.8) = -3.6$$

$$\theta^{(2)} = 1.2 - 0.2 \times (-3.6) = 1.2 + 0.72 = 1.92 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Iteration 3:** At $\theta^{(2)} = 1.92$:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(2)}) = 2(1.92 - 3) = 2(-1.08) = -2.16$$

$$\theta^{(3)} = 1.92 - 0.2 \times (-2.16) = 1.92 + 0.432 = 2.352 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Convergence Comment:** The true minimum is at $\theta^* = 3$ (where $\nabla J = 0$ and $J = 5$, the global minimum). The sequence of $\theta$ values — $0, 1.2, 1.92, 2.352$ — is monotonically increasing toward $3$, with the step sizes decreasing each iteration. This is a hallmark of **stable linear convergence** and confirms the algorithm is correctly converging. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of Mini-Batch Gradient Descent. Discuss the trade-offs involved in choosing the mini-batch size $m$. What are the practical implications for GPU-based training?

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a 2D loss function $J(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2$. Starting from $\theta^{(0)} = (4, 4)$ with $\eta = 0.1$, perform two iterations of gradient descent and find the parameters after the second iteration.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept [2 marks]:** Mini-Batch Gradient Descent computes the gradient of the loss function using a small random subset (mini-batch) of size $m$ from the training set, rather than the full set (BGD) or a single sample (SGD). The update rule is:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta J(\theta_t; \mathcal{B}_t)$$

**Trade-offs in choosing $m$ [3 marks]:**

- **Small $m$ (e.g., 16–32):** More frequent updates; noisier gradients that can help escape shallow local minima; poor hardware utilization because GPUs prefer larger matrix operations.
- **Large $m$ (e.g., 512–4096):** Smoother, more accurate gradient estimates; excellent GPU utilization; fewer updates per epoch; may get stuck in sharp minima that hurt generalization.
- **Typical sweet spot:** $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$ depending on the problem and memory budget.

**Practical implications for GPUs [2 marks]:** GPU cores perform best when operating on large contiguous tensor operations. Mini-batches that are too small under-utilize the GPU, while mini-batches that are too large exhaust VRAM. Effective deep-learning engineering balances $m$ to maximize GPU occupancy without triggering out-of-memory errors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The loss function is $J(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2$. The gradient is:

$$\nabla J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 2\theta_1 \\ 2\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:** At $\theta^{(0)} = (4, 4)$:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(0)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(4) \\ 2(4) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 - 0.8 \\ 4 - 0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2 \\ 3.2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**Iteration 2:** At $\theta^{(1)} = (3.2, 3.2)$:

$$\nabla J(\theta^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} 2(3.2) \\ 2(3.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.4 \\ 6.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2 \\ 3.2 \end{bmatrix} - 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 6.4 \\ 6.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.2 - 0.64 \\ 3.2 - 0.64 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\theta^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.56 \\ 2.56 \end{bmatrix}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**Verification [1 mark]:** The true minimum is at the origin $(0, 0)$. The sequence $(4, 4) \to (3.2, 3.2) \to (2.56, 2.56)$ shows smooth linear convergence toward the origin, with each step being $0.8 \times$ the previous position (geometric decay with ratio $1 - 2\eta = 0.8$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing the negative sign** in $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J$ — the most common single error; costs **1–2 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Forgetting to evaluate the gradient at the new $\theta$** at each iteration in numerical problems — using $\theta^{(0)}$ for all steps will get you 0 on the numerical portion.
> 3. **Not stating the Taylor series assumption** that $\Delta \theta$ is "sufficiently small" — examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark for assumptions.
> 4. **Confusing BGD, SGD, MBGD definitions** — writing "SGD uses the full dataset" is a fatal conceptual error.
> 5. **In numerical problems, omitting the convergence commentary** — always end with a 1-line statement comparing your result to the true optimum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Gradient Descent**

- **Core update rule (memorize verbatim):** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)$
- **Three pillars:** Gradient (direction), Learning Rate (magnitude), Convergence Criterion (termination).
- **Derivation origin:** First-order Taylor expansion + descent condition $\nabla J^T \Delta \theta < 0$.
- **Three variants:** Batch (all data, stable/slow), Stochastic (1 sample, fast/noisy), Mini-Batch (small subset, balanced — industry standard).
- **Typical batch sizes for deep learning:** $\mathbf{32, 64, 128, 256}$.
- **Typical learning rate range:** $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$.
- **Convergence criteria:** $\vert\vert \nabla J \vert\vert < \epsilon$, max iterations $T$, or negligible loss change.
- **Convergence rate for convex L-smooth functions:** linear with ratio $(1 - \mu/L)^t$, where $\kappa = L/\mu$ is the condition number.
- **Common challenges:** local minima, saddle points, vanishing/exploding gradients, ill-conditioning.
- **Modern descendants:** SGD with Momentum, AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam — all built atop the core gradient descent update.
- **Role in deep learning:** gradient descent (via backpropagation) is the engine that *trains* every neural network.
- **Numerical problem pattern:** Compute $\nabla J$, then iterate: $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla J(\theta^{(t)})$.
- **Key intuition:** Move opposite to the gradient because the gradient points uphill; we want to go downhill.

**End of Notes — Module 1: Gradient Descent**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loss Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loss Function (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ is a mathematical operator that maps a single prediction–ground truth pair $(\hat{y}, y)$ to a non-negative scalar value quantifying the *discrepancy*, *penalty*, or *divergence* between the model's predicted output $\hat{y}$ and the true target $y$. It serves as the **primary supervisory signal** that drives the parameter updates of a neural network through back-propagation and gradient-based optimization.

In the formal KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework, the loss function is the mathematical embodiment of the learning objective. It is the *only* component of the training pipeline that directly connects the network's output to the learning algorithm. The minimizer of the expected loss is, by definition, the optimal predictor — a result rooted in **statistical decision theory** and the **principle of empirical risk minimization (ERM)** introduced by Vapnik and Chervonenkis.

The expected risk (true loss) is given by:

$$
J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim p_{\text{data}}} \left[ \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x), y) \right]
$$

where $f_\theta$ is the neural network parameterized by weights $\theta$ and $p_{\text{data}}$ is the true (unknown) data distribution. Because $p_{\text{data}}$ is unknown, we approximate $J(\theta)$ with the **empirical risk**:

$$
\hat{J}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber in Fog

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition: Hiking Down a Mountain Blindfolded**
>
> Imagine you are standing on a mountain (the loss landscape) and you want to reach the lowest valley (minimum loss). You are blindfolded. The only feedback you have is **how steep the slope is directly beneath your feet** — this feedback is the **gradient of the loss function**. The loss function is essentially the *altitude meter* strapped to your ankle. Every step you take in the steepest downhill direction is computed using the derivative of this altitude meter. A *good* loss function makes the slope a faithful guide to the valley; a *poor* one gives a misleading reading that sends you off a cliff.

## 1.3 Why Loss Functions Matter in Deep Learning

A neural network with $L$ layers and millions of parameters $\theta = \{W^{(1)}, b^{(1)}, \dots, W^{(L)}, b^{(L)}\}$ is updated at every iteration $t$ via:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, \nabla_\theta \, \hat{J}(\theta_t)
$$

The **entire learning process** hinges on two things: the choice of loss function (which determines the shape of the landscape) and the optimizer (which determines the path). Choosing a bad loss function can lead to:
- **Vanishing gradients** (e.g., saturating sigmoid + MSE)
- **Exploding gradients** (improperly scaled losses)
- **Mode collapse** in GANs
- **Class imbalance blindness** in classification

## 1.4 Terminology Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Distinguish Carefully:**
> - **Loss Function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ → operates on a **single sample**
> - **Cost Function** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i} \mathcal{L}(\hat{y}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ → **average over the dataset**
> - **Objective Function** → most general term; may include **regularization** $R(\theta)$: $\tilde{J}(\theta) = J(\theta) + \lambda R(\theta)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Good Loss Function

A well-designed loss function for deep learning should satisfy the following criteria:

1. **Differentiability** (or sub-differentiability) — to enable gradient-based optimization via back-propagation.
2. **Convexity** (preferred, though deep networks are generally non-convex overall).
3. **Numerical Stability** — should not explode or vanish when outputs approach 0 or 1.
4. **Probabilistic Justification** — ideally derivable from a maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) framework.
5. **Task Alignment** — penalty structure should match the downstream evaluation metric.

## 2.2 Major Categories of Loss Functions

### A. Regression Losses
Used when the target $y \in \mathbb{R}$ is continuous.

### B. Classification Losses
Used when $y \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\}$ is discrete.

### C. Probabilistic / Distributional Losses
Used when comparing two probability distributions.

### D. Embedding / Metric Losses
Used in representation learning, Siamese networks, contrastive setups.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — Comprehensive Loss Function Reference (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

| # | Loss Function | Mathematical Form $\mathcal{L}$ | Domain of $\hat{y}$ | Typical Use Case | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\frac{1}{2}(\hat{y} - y)^2$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Regression | Differentiable, sensitive to outliers (penalizes $\propto e^2$) |
| 2 | **Mean Absolute Error (MAE / L1)** | $\vert \hat{y} - y \vert$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Regression, robust to outliers | Sub-gradient at 0; less sensitive to outliers |
| 3 | **Huber Loss** | $\frac{1}{2}e^2$ if $\vert e \vert \le \delta$, else $\delta(\vert e \vert - \frac{1}{2}\delta)$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | Robust regression | Quadratic near 0, linear in tails |
| 4 | **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** | $-[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | $[0, 1]$ | Binary classification | Log-loss, information-theoretic |
| 5 | **Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE)** | $-\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | Probability simplex | Multi-class classification | Equivalent to MLE under multinomial |
| 6 | **Hinge Loss** | $\max(0, 1 - y\hat{y})$ | $\{-1, +1\}$ or $\mathbb{R}$ | SVM, max-margin classifiers | Zero loss when margin $\ge 1$ |
| 7 | **Squared Hinge** | $(\max(0, 1 - y\hat{y}))^2$ | $\mathbb{R}$ | SVM variants | Smoother gradients than hinge |
| 8 | **Focal Loss** | $-\alpha(1-p_t)^\gamma \log p_t$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced classification | Down-weights easy examples |
| 9 | **KL Divergence** | $\sum_{k} p_k \log\frac{p_k}{q_k}$ | Probability distributions | Distillation, VAE | Asymmetric; not a true metric |
| 10 | **Contrastive Loss** | $(1-Y)\frac{1}{2}d^2 + Y\frac{1}{2}\max(0, m-d)^2$ | Embeddings | Siamese nets | Pulls similar pairs together |
| 11 | **Triplet Loss** | $\max(0, d(a, p) - d(a, n) + \alpha)$ | Embeddings | Face recognition, metric learning | Relative distance learning |
| 12 | **Dice Loss** | $1 - \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Binary masks | Image segmentation | Overlap-based, handles class imbalance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in the table:** $e = \hat{y} - y$ denotes the residual (error). All absolute value bars are typeset as $\vert \cdot \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command to remain compatible with markdown tables.

## 2.4 Why These Specific Forms? — The MLE Connection

> [!NOTE]
> **Statistical Foundation (KTU High-Yield):**
> Every classical loss function can be derived as the **negative log-likelihood (NLL)** of a probabilistic model:
> $$\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y) = -\log P(y \mid \hat{y})$$

| Probabilistic Assumption | Resulting Loss |
|---|---|
| $y \sim \mathcal{N}(\hat{y}, \sigma^2)$ (Gaussian noise) | MSE |
| $y \sim \text{Laplace}(\hat{y}, b)$ (Laplace noise) | MAE |
| $y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\hat{y})$ | Binary Cross-Entropy |
| $y \sim \text{Categorical}(\hat{y})$ | Categorical Cross-Entropy |
| $y \sim \text{Poisson}(\hat{y})$ | Poisson NLL |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Preferred Loss | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous vehicle steering angle | MSE or Huber | Continuous control; rare outliers from sensor noise |
| Medical image segmentation | Dice / Focal | Severe class imbalance (small tumor) |
| Credit-card fraud detection | Focal | Extreme imbalance (1 fraud per 10,000) |
| LLM text generation | Cross-Entropy (token-level) | Next-token classification |
| Face verification | Triplet / Contrastive | Need embedding geometry |
| Knowledge distillation | KL Divergence | Match soft probability distributions |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Squared Error from Maximum Likelihood

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that MSE is the optimal loss under the assumption of Gaussian-distributed residual errors.

**Step 1 — Model Assumption:**
Assume the true target $y$ is generated by a function $f_\theta(x)$ with **additive Gaussian noise** $\epsilon$:

$$
y = f_\theta(x) + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Likelihood of a Single Sample:**
The probability density of observing target $y$ given prediction $\hat{y} = f_\theta(x)$ is:

$$
P(y \mid \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{(y - \hat{y})^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood:**
Take the natural logarithm to convert the product (over samples) into a sum:

$$
\log P(y \mid \hat{y}) = -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(y - \hat{y})^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL):**
Since the optimizer minimizes, we negate. Drop the constant $-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2)$ as it is independent of $\theta$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = -\log P(y \mid \hat{y}) = \frac{(y - \hat{y})^2}{2\sigma^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Simplified Form:**
Since $\sigma^2$ is a constant hyperparameter, it only rescales the gradient step. Drop it to get the standard MSE:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(\hat{y}, y) = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Gradient w.r.t. $\hat{y}$:**
Differentiate the squared error. The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the exponent:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}}{\partial \hat{y}} = (\hat{y} - y)
$$

This is the simplest gradient possible — a linear function of the error — which is one reason MSE is numerically friendly for regression.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Binary Cross-Entropy from Bernoulli MLE

**Step 1 — Bernoulli Assumption:**
For binary classification, the target $y \in \{0, 1\}$ is modeled as a Bernoulli random variable with success probability $\hat{y}$ (the network's output, e.g., from a sigmoid):

$$
P(y \mid \hat{y}) = \hat{y}^{\,y} \, (1 - \hat{y})^{\,(1-y)}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood:**
Applying $\log$ to both sides:

$$
\log P(y \mid \hat{y}) = y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1 - \hat{y})
$$

**Step 3 — Negative Log-Likelihood:**
Negate to get the loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\,y \log \hat{y} - (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y})
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Single Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(\hat{y}, y) = -\left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y) \log(1-\hat{y}) \right]\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\hat{y}$ (assuming $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$):**
Differentiating with respect to the pre-activation $z$ (via the chain rule and $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$) gives the elegant result:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
$$

This is the same elegant linear form as MSE, which is the *fundamental* reason why **sigmoid + BCE** is preferred over **sigmoid + MSE** in classification — the gradient never saturates when paired with the correct loss.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Categorical Cross-Entropy with Softmax

**Step 1 — Softmax Function:**
The network outputs raw logits $z_k$ for $K$ classes, converted to probabilities:

$$
\hat{y}_k = \text{softmax}(z_k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}
$$

**Step 2 — Categorical Distribution:**
The true target is a one-hot vector $y = (y_1, \dots, y_K)$ with $\sum_k y_k = 1$. The likelihood is:

$$
P(y \mid \hat{y}) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \hat{y}_k^{\,y_k}
$$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood:**

$$
\log P(y \mid \hat{y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k
$$

**Step 4 — Negative Log-Likelihood (CCE):**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. logits $z_k$:**
Substitute the softmax form and differentiate:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k
$$

Again, the gradient is just **(prediction − truth)** — a property known as the **canonical link function** property, which makes softmax + CCE numerically ideal.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Huber Loss

The Huber loss smoothly transitions between L2 (quadratic) and L1 (linear) regimes, controlled by threshold $\delta$:

**Step 1 — Define the residual:**

$$
e = \hat{y} - y
$$

**Step 2 — Piecewise Definition:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{Huber}}(e) =
\begin{cases}
\frac{1}{2}\,e^2 & \text{if } \vert e \vert \le \delta \\[4pt]
\delta \left( \vert e \vert - \frac{1}{2}\delta \right) & \text{if } \vert e \vert > \delta
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Gradient Computation:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{Huber}}}{\partial e} =
\begin{cases}
e & \text{if } \vert e \vert \le \delta \\[4pt]
\delta \cdot \text{sign}(e) & \text{if } \vert e \vert > \delta
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Why this matters:** For small errors $\vert e \vert \le \delta$, the gradient is *small* (quadratic regime), so the model makes careful corrections. For large errors, the gradient is *bounded* by $\delta$ (linear regime), preventing outlier-driven explosion — this is the **robustness property**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Focal Loss

**Step 1 — Start from Cross-Entropy:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(p_t) = -\log(p_t)
$$

where $p_t$ is the model's estimated probability for the *true* class:

$$
p_t = \begin{cases} \hat{y} & \text{if } y = 1 \\ 1 - \hat{y} & \text{if } y = 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Add Modulating Factor $(1 - p_t)^\gamma$:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{Focal}} = -\alpha_t (1 - p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Effect of $\gamma$ (focusing parameter):**
- When $p_t \to 1$ (easy example), $(1-p_t)^\gamma \to 0$ → loss is **down-weighted**.
- When $p_t \to 0$ (hard example), $(1-p_t)^\gamma \to 1$ → loss is **preserved**.
- $\gamma = 0$ reduces focal loss to standard cross-entropy.
- Typical KTU-board value: $\gamma = 2$, $\alpha = 0.25$.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Exam Pattern):** A binary classifier outputs $\hat{y} = 0.9$ for a sample with true label $y = 1$. Compare (a) MSE-loss and (b) BCE-loss.

**Solution:**

**(a) MSE:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.9 - 1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.1)^2 = 0.005
$$

**(b) BCE:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -[1 \cdot \log(0.9) + 0 \cdot \log(0.1)] = -\log(0.9) = 0.1054
$$

**Observation:** BCE penalizes the confident-but-wrong case much more heavily. If $\hat{y} = 0.01$ with $y = 1$, MSE gives $0.5 \cdot 0.99^2 \approx 0.49$, but BCE gives $-\log(0.01) = 4.605$. BCE is **aggressively punishing** — exactly the desired property for classification.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Loss Function in the Deep Learning Training Loop

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** showing where the loss function sits in the training pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Batch X] --> B[Neural Network f_theta]
    B --> C[Predictions y_hat]
    D[Ground Truth Labels y] --> E{Loss Function Selection}
    C --> E
    E --> F[Compute Loss L]
    F --> G[Backpropagation]
    G --> H[Compute Gradients dL_dtheta]
    H --> I[Optimizer SGD or Adam]
    I --> J[Update Weights theta]
    J --> K[Convergence Check]
    K -->|Not Converged| A
    K -->|Converged| L[Trained Model]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Loss Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: What is the Task] --> T1{Target Type}
    T1 -->|Continuous Real Values| R[Regression Branch]
    T1 -->|Discrete Classes| C[Classification Branch]
    T1 -->|Probability Distribution| P[Distributional Branch]

    R --> R1{Outliers Present}
    R1 -->|No| R2[Use MSE]
    R1 -->|Yes| R3[Use Huber or MAE]

    C --> C1{Binary or Multi Class}
    C1 -->|Binary| C2[Use Binary Cross Entropy with Sigmoid]
    C1 -->|Multi Class Single Label| C3[Use Categorical Cross Entropy with Softmax]
    C1 -->|Multi Label| C4[Use Binary Cross Entropy per Class]

    C2 --> C5{Class Imbalance}
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 -->|Yes| C6[Use Focal Loss with alpha and gamma]
    C5 -->|No| C7[Use Standard Cross Entropy]

    P --> P1[Use KL Divergence]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Gradient Flow Through Loss Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Linear Layer Output z] --> SM[Softmax Activation]
    SM --> P[Probabilities p_hat]
    Y[One Hot True y] --> L[CCE Loss Computation]
    P --> L
    L --> G[Gradient dL_dz equals p_hat minus y]
    G --> BP[Backprop to Earlier Layers]
```

## 4.4 Loss Function Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LF[Loss Functions] --> REG[Regression Losses]
    LF --> CLS[Classification Losses]
    LF --> EMB[Embedding Losses]
    LF --> PROB[Probabilistic Losses]

    REG --> MSE[MSE]
    REG --> MAE[MAE]
    REG --> HUB[Huber]
    REG --> QNT[Quantile]

    CLS --> BCE[Binary Cross Entropy]
    CLS --> CCE[Categorical Cross Entropy]
    CLS --> HNG[Hinge]
    CLS --> FOC[Focal]

    EMB --> CTR[Contrastive]
    EMB --> TRI[Triplet]
    EMB --> CEN[Center]

    PROB --> KL[KL Divergence]
    PROB --> JS[JS Divergence]
    PROB --> WASS[Wasserstein]
```
