- **Interpretation:** Proportion of variance in the target variable explained by the model.
- **Range:** $(-\infty, 1]$; $R^2 = 1$ is perfect; $R^2 \leq 0$ means the model is worse than predicting the mean.

**Worked Example (Refer to Section 3.4 above for full numerical demonstration.)**

**Part (b) — Solution:**

In binary classification, the F1-Score is computed from a single Confusion Matrix. In **multi-class classification** (e.g., 10 classes in CIFAR-10), the Confusion Matrix becomes $K \times K$. To extend F1, we use averaging strategies.

**Macro-Averaged F1:**

$$
\text{F1}_{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{c=1}^{K} \text{F1}_c
$$

- Each class contributes **equally**, regardless of its sample count.
- **Use case:** Imbalanced multi-class datasets; you care about minority-class performance.

**Micro-Averaged F1:**

$$
\text{F1}_{\text{micro}} = \text{Precision}_{\text{micro}} = \text{Recall}_{\text{micro}} = \dfrac{\sum_{c=1}^{K} TP_c}{\sum_{c=1}^{K}(TP_c + FP_c)}
$$

- Aggregates TP, FP, FN across all classes **first**, then computes a single F1.
- **Use case:** Balanced datasets; equivalent to overall accuracy in multi-class.

**Why Both Are Needed:**

| Strategy | Gives Equal Weight To | Affected by Class Imbalance? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Macro-F1** | Each class | Yes (minority class errors matter) |
| **Micro-F1** | Each sample/instances | No (dominated by majority class) |
| **Weighted-F1** | Each class weighted by support | Compromise between the two |

A KTU 2024 student must explicitly state which averaging strategy is used when reporting multi-class F1.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify Confusion Matrix totals** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if the sum $TP + TN + FP + FN$ does not match the total sample count.
> 2. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — A classic mistake. Remember: Precision involves the *predicted* column denominator ($TP+FP$); Recall involves the *actual* row denominator ($TP+FN$).
> 3. **Not specifying the Positive class in multi-class problems** — When computing Precision/Recall for, say, CIFAR-10, students must declare *which class is the "Positive" class* for the calculation.
> 4. **Reporting Accuracy without context** — On imbalanced data, a high Accuracy score with no Precision/Recall/F1 will receive **partial credit only** under KTU 2024 strict marking.
> 5. **Using RMSE without specifying units** — Examiners expect you to explicitly state the unit of the error.
> 6. **Skipping the harmonic mean justification for F1** — Always justify *why* harmonic mean (and not arithmetic mean) is used. The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalance.
> 7. **In ROC-AUC, not explaining the diagonal line** — The diagonal $y = x$ represents random guessing. Any model below the diagonal is worse than random.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Performance Matrix** = the quantitative toolkit to evaluate deep learning models beyond a single Accuracy number.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the *foundation* — every classification metric is derived from its four entries: **TP, TN, FP, FN**.
- **Accuracy** is intuitive but **misleading on imbalanced data**.
- **Precision** answers: *"Of predicted Positives, how many are correct?"* → use in spam filters.
- **Recall** answers: *"Of actual Positives, how many did we catch?"* → use in medical diagnosis.
- **F1-Score** = Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall → single balanced number; lies between them.
- **Specificity** = True Negative Rate; complements Recall.
- **ROC-AUC** is **threshold-independent** and ideal for imbalanced binary classification; AUC = 1.0 (perfect), 0.5 (random).
- **MAE** is robust to outliers; **MSE** penalizes large errors; **RMSE** is in target units; **R²** shows variance explained (best = 1.0).
- For **multi-class** problems, always declare the **averaging strategy** (Macro, Micro, or Weighted) for F1.
- For the **KTU ESE**, always show the full numerical computation with units, table entries, and a concluding interpretation sentence.
- The choice of metric must be **justified by the application domain** — KTU examiners reward domain-aware reasoning.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyperparameter Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameter Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyperparameter Tuning (or Hyperparameter Optimization, HPO)** is the process of systematically searching for the optimal configuration of the **hyperparameters** of a deep learning model — i.e., the configuration variables that govern the **learning process itself**, as opposed to the model parameters (weights and biases) that are learned from data.

Formally, given a model $\mathcal{M}$ trained on dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, with hyperparameters $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_k) \in \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ drawn from a search space $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$, hyperparameter tuning seeks to find:

$$\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\star} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\lambda} \in \boldsymbol{\Lambda}} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}\!\left(\mathcal{M}_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}^{\star},\; \mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}\right)$$

where $\mathcal{M}_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}^{\star}$ denotes the model trained *to convergence* with hyperparameter setting $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$, and $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ is the **validation loss** evaluated on a held-out validation set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hyperparameter Tuning is classified as a **Model Optimization** concept under Module 1, directly tied to Course Outcome **CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental building blocks of deep neural networks and the optimization techniques used to train them."*

### 1.2 Parameters vs Hyperparameters — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Model Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Internal variables learned from data (e.g., weights $\mathbf{W}$, biases $\mathbf{b}$) | External configuration settings chosen before training |
| Updated by | Gradient descent / backpropagation | Manual choice or automated search algorithm |
| Notation | $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | $\lambda \in \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ |
| Cardinality | Often millions to billions | Usually a small set (10–100) |
| Examples | $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}, \mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $B$, number of epochs $E$, momentum $\beta$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Recipe" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are training a chef (the model) to bake the perfect cake.** The chef learns *how much to mix*, *how long to whisk*, *when to flip the batter* — these are the **parameters** (skills acquired through practice = training data). But *before* the chef starts, you must decide: **oven temperature (learning rate), pan size (batch size), total baking time (epochs), and number of layers of frosting (network depth).** These are the **hyperparameters.** Hyperparameter tuning is the systematic experimentation with oven settings, pan sizes, and timings until you find the combination that produces the best cake (lowest validation loss).

This analogy, drawn from Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville's *Deep Learning* tradition, makes the abstract optimization immediately tangible for first-time learners.

### 1.4 Categorization of Hyperparameters

Hyperparameters in deep learning are typically classified into three families:

1. **Optimization Hyperparameters** — govern how the model is trained.
   *Examples:* Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $B$, momentum $\beta$, optimizer type (SGD, Adam, RMSProp), weight decay $\lambda_{\text{wd}}$.
2. **Architecture / Model Hyperparameters** — define the structure of the network.
   *Examples:* Number of layers $L$, number of units per layer $n_l$, kernel size $k$, dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$.
3. **Data / Regularization Hyperparameters** — control preprocessing and generalization.
   *Examples:* Data augmentation strength, label smoothing $\epsilon$, $L_1/L_2$ regularization coefficient $\lambda_{\text{reg}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Note:** In valuation, students often lose marks by failing to *categorize* hyperparameters. Examiners expect the answer to demonstrate awareness that hyperparameter tuning is a **multi-facet problem** spanning architecture, optimization, and regularization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hyperparameter Tuning Pipeline (Operational Logic)

The hyperparameter tuning process follows a structured sequential workflow. Below is the granular breakdown:

* **Step 1 — Define the Search Space $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$:** Identify all tunable hyperparameters and specify their allowed ranges (e.g., $\eta \in [10^{-5}, 10^{-1}]$ on a log scale, $B \in \{16, 32, 64, 128\}$).
* **Step 2 — Choose a Sampling Strategy:** Decide how to sample candidate configurations from $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ (grid, random, Bayesian, evolutionary, etc.).
* **Step 3 — Define an Evaluation Protocol:** Select $K$-fold cross-validation or a fixed validation split to compute $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ for each trial.
* **Step 4 — Allocate a Compute Budget $C$:** Cap the total number of trials $T$ or wall-clock time, balancing exploration vs exploitation.
* **Step 5 — Run Trials:** For each sampled $\boldsymbol{\lambda}_t$, train $\mathcal{M}_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}_t}$ to convergence on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$, and evaluate on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$.
* **Step 6 — Update Search State:** Feed the new observation $(\boldsymbol{\lambda}_t, \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}^{(t)})$ into the search algorithm's state.
* **Step 7 — Select Best Configuration:** Return $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\star} = \arg\min_t \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}^{(t)}$ over the $T$ trials.

### 2.2 Why Hyperparameter Tuning is Hard — The "Why" Behind the "How"

* **High-dimensional non-convex objective:** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\boldsymbol{\lambda})$ has no closed-form, is noisy, and is non-differentiable in $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ (you cannot backpropagate through the choice of learning rate).
* **Expensive evaluation:** Each trial requires full model training — a single trial on ImageNet can cost **hundreds of GPU-hours**.
* **Conditional hyperparameters:** The optimal value of $\lambda_2$ often *depends* on $\lambda_1$ (e.g., optimal batch size depends on learning rate). The effective search space is non-rectangular.
* **Stochastic noise:** Random seed effects cause the same $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ to yield different $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}$ values, demanding multiple seeds per trial for reliable estimates.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Hyperparameter Tuning

| Symbol / Formula | Description | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\boldsymbol{\lambda} = (\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_k)$ | Hyperparameter vector | Mixed (real / integer / categorical) |
| $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \bigtimes_{i=1}^{k} \Lambda_i$ | Cartesian product search space | Search space volume |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\boldsymbol{\lambda})$ | Validation loss objective to minimize | Cross-entropy, MSE, etc. |
| $T$ | Total number of trials allowed | Integer, e.g., $T \leq 100$ |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $\eta \in [10^{-5},\; 10^{-1}]$ (log scale) |
| $B$ | Mini-batch size | $B \in \{2^{i}\}$, $i \in \{4,\dots,10\}$ |
| $\beta$ | Momentum (SGD) | $\beta \in [0.0,\; 0.99]$ |
| $E$ | Number of training epochs | $E \in [10,\; 500]$ |
| $\lambda_{\text{wd}}$ | Weight decay (L2 penalty) | $\lambda_{\text{wd}} \in [10^{-6},\; 10^{-2}]$ |
| $p_{\text{drop}}$ | Dropout rate | $p_{\text{drop}} \in [0.0,\; 0.5]$ |
| $K$ | Number of CV folds | $K \in \{3, 5, 10\}$ |
| $\text{EI}(\mathbf{x})$ | Expected Improvement acquisition | Real-valued |
| $\text{UCB}(\mathbf{x}) = \mu(\mathbf{x}) + \kappa\sigma(\mathbf{x})$ | Upper Confidence Bound acquisition | Real-valued |
| $R = T \cdot C_{\text{trial}}$ | Total compute budget | GPU-hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Memorize the ranges in the table above. Many 3-mark questions ask to *"list any four hyperparameters and their typical ranges"* — these ranges appear repeatedly in KTU model papers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hyperparameter tuning is the **workhorse behind every deployed deep learning system** in industry:

* **Computer Vision:** Tuning ResNet-50 on ImageNet requires jointly optimizing $\eta$, $B$, weight decay, label smoothing, and MixUp $\alpha$ — Bergstra & Bengio (2012) showed **random search outperforms grid search by up to 50× in efficiency.**
* **Natural Language Processing:** Fine-tuning BERT/Transformers requires careful tuning of $\eta$, warmup steps, and gradient clipping — the "small difference" between SOTA and poor models.
* **Reinforcement Learning:** PPO and SAC are notoriously sensitive to entropy coefficient, GAE $\lambda$, and discount factor $\gamma$.
* **AutoML:** Google's **Vertex AI**, AWS **SageMaker Autopilot**, and open-source **Optuna**, **Ray Tune**, and **Hyperopt** are all production-grade HPO systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — The Black-Box Optimization View

Hyperparameter tuning is fundamentally a **black-box optimization problem**: we can only query $\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\boldsymbol{\lambda})$ at points we choose, and we receive noisy estimates. Formally:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; \sigma_n^2)$$

where $f: \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the unknown true validation surface, and $\epsilon$ represents stochastic noise (random seed effects, mini-batch noise, data shuffling).

**The goal:** With a budget of $T$ queries, find $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\star}$ minimizing $f$ using only noisy observations.

### 3.2 Derivation of Grid Search and Random Search

**Grid Search.** Discretizes each $\Lambda_i$ into $g_i$ grid points, yielding $T = \prod_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ total trials.

* **Why it fails in high dimensions:** With $k = 10$ hyperparameters and $g_i = 10$ each, $T = 10^{10}$ — infeasible.
* **Effective trials:** Only $g_i^{1}$ trials probe each axis independently, wasting compute on irrelevant dimensions.

**Random Search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012).** Samples $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(1)}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(2)}, \dots, \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(T)} \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{U}(\boldsymbol{\Lambda})$ uniformly.

* **Theoretical Justification:** In high dimensions, only a few hyperparameters strongly influence $f$. If $k$ effective dimensions are $k_{\text{eff}} < k$, random search concentrates trials on the relevant subspace, achieving a $T^{k_{\text{eff}}/k}$ convergence rate vs grid search's $T^{1/k}$.

**Proof Sketch (Concentration Inequality):** Let $A_T^{\text{grid}}$ and $A_T^{\text{rand}}$ be the best-found minima. By Hoeffding's inequality applied to each axis:

$$\Pr\!\left[\vert A_T^{\text{rand}} - f^{\star} \vert > \epsilon\right] \leq 2\exp\!\left(-\frac{2T\epsilon^2}{\sum_i (b_i - a_i)^2}\right)$$

which decays **exponentially in $T$** for random search, whereas grid search's decay depends on per-axis granularity.

### 3.3 Derivation of Bayesian Optimization (Gaussian Process Surrogate)

Bayesian optimization is the **state-of-the-art** sample-efficient method for HPO. It builds a probabilistic surrogate of $f$ and uses an acquisition function to decide where to sample next.

#### Step 1 — Gaussian Process Prior

Place a Gaussian Process (GP) prior over $f$:

$$f(\cdot) \sim \mathcal{GP}\!\left(m(\mathbf{x}),\; k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')\right)$$

where $m(\mathbf{x})$ is the mean function (often $m \equiv 0$) and $k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ is the kernel (typically Matern-5/2 or RBF).

#### Step 2 — Posterior Update After $t$ Observations

Given observations $\mathcal{D}_{1:t} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{t}$ where $y_i = f(\mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon_i$, the GP posterior at a new point $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$\mu_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{k}_t^\top (\mathbf{K}_t + \sigma_n^2 \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{y}_{1:t}$$

$$\sigma_t^2(\mathbf{x}) = k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{k}_t^\top (\mathbf{K}_t + \sigma_n^2 \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{k}_t$$

where $\mathbf{k}_t = [k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_1), \dots, k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_t)]^\top$ and $\mathbf{K}_t$ is the $t \times t$ kernel matrix $[\mathbf{K}_t]_{ij} = k(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.

#### Step 3 — Acquisition Function: Expected Improvement (EI)

EI selects the next point maximizing the expected improvement over the current best $f^{\star}_t = \min_{i \leq t} y_i$:

$$\text{EI}_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(f^{\star}_t - f(\mathbf{x}),\; 0) \;\vert\; \mathcal{D}_{1:t}\right]$$

**Closed-form derivation:** Let $Z = \dfrac{f^{\star}_t - \mu_t(\mathbf{x})}{\sigma_t(\mathbf{x})}$. Since $f(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathcal{D}_{1:t} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_t(\mathbf{x}), \sigma_t^2(\mathbf{x}))$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{EI}_t(\mathbf{x}) &= \int_{-\infty}^{f^{\star}_t} (f^{\star}_t - u)\; \phi\!\left(\frac{u - \mu_t}{\sigma_t}\right) \frac{du}{\sigma_t} \\[4pt]
&= (f^{\star}_t - \mu_t(\mathbf{x}))\; \Phi(Z) \;+\; \sigma_t(\mathbf{x})\; \phi(Z)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\phi$ and $\Phi$ are the standard normal PDF and CDF respectively.

**Interpretation:**
* $(f^{\star}_t - \mu_t(\mathbf{x}))\; \Phi(Z)$ — the **exploitation** term: encourages sampling where the predicted mean is low.
* $\sigma_t(\mathbf{x})\; \phi(Z)$ — the **exploration** term: encourages sampling where uncertainty is high.

#### Step 4 — Upper Confidence Bound (UCB)

A simpler alternative acquisition is UCB:

$$\text{UCB}_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mu_t(\mathbf{x}) + \kappa_t\, \sigma_t(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\kappa_t > 0$ is an exploration-exploitation trade-off parameter (often $\kappa_t = \sqrt{2 \log(t^2 \pi^2 / 6\delta)}$ for theoretical regret bounds).

#### Step 5 — Iteration

Repeat: $\mathbf{x}_{t+1} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{x}} \text{EI}_t(\mathbf{x})$, observe $y_{t+1}$, update GP, until budget $T$ exhausted.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HPO Methods

| Method | Sample Efficiency | Parallelizable | Handles Cond. Params | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual Search | Very Low | Yes | Yes (expert) | Quick prototyping |
| Grid Search | Low (wasteful in high-D) | Yes (embarrassingly) | No | Low-D, small budget |
| Random Search | Moderate | Yes | Yes | Baseline benchmark |
| Bayesian Opt. (GP) | High | Limited (sequential) | Yes | Expensive evaluations ($T < 100$) |
| Hyperband | High (early-stopping) | Yes (massively) | No | Large $T$, cheap per-trial cost |
| Population Based Training | High (online) | Yes (distributed) | Yes | Long training runs, RL |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Bayesian Optimization in 1D

> [!NOTE]
> **Educational Worked Example (Conceptual):** Suppose we want to tune a single hyperparameter $\lambda \in [0, 10]$ with $f(\lambda) = (\lambda - 5)^2 + \mathcal{N}(0, 0.1)$. After 3 random samples $\lambda_1 = 1, \lambda_2 = 6, \lambda_3 = 8$ with observations $y_1 = 16.1, y_2 = 1.05, y_3 = 9.05$, the GP posterior is fit. EI will guide the next sample near $\lambda \approx 5$ (high mean, low uncertainty). This is the **Spearmint** algorithm in essence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hyperparameter Tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Search Space Lambda] --> B[Choose Sampling Strategy]
    B --> C[Sample Hyperparameter Config lambda_t]
    C --> D[Train Model M_lambda_t on D_train]
    D --> E[Evaluate Validation Loss L_val]
    E --> F{Update Search State}
    F -->|Bayesian: Update GP| G[GP Posterior Update]
    F -->|Random/Grid: Log Trial| H[Trial Log Database]
    G --> I[Compute Acquisition EI or UCB]
    H --> J[Select Next Config]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Budget T Exhausted?}
    K -->|No| C
    K -->|Yes| L[Return Best lambda_star]
    L --> M[Retrain Final Model on D_train U D_val]
    M --> N[Deploy Optimized Model]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Bayesian Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surrogate Model GP] --> B[Posterior Mean mu_t and Var sigma_t^2]
    B --> C[Acquisition Function EI UCB]
    C --> D[argmax Acquisition]
    D --> E[Next Trial x_t+1]
    E --> F[True Objective f x_t+1 plus noise]
    F --> G[Add to Observations D_1:t+1]
    G --> A
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing an HPO Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start HPO Problem] --> B{Number of Hyperparameters k}
    B -->|k <= 4| C{Compute Budget T}
    B -->|k > 4| D[Use Random Search or Bayesian Opt]
    C -->|Small T| E[Grid Search]
    C -->|Large T| F[Bayesian Optimization]
    D --> G{Trials are Cheap?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Hyperband with Early Stopping]
    G -->|No| I[Bayesian Optimization with GP]
    F --> J[Spearmint or Optuna]
    E --> K[Evaluate via K fold CV]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Select Best Configuration]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Schematic — Population Based Training PBT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Population of N Workers] --> B[Each Worker trains model with own hyperparameters]
    B --> C[Periodically Every s steps Evaluate All Workers]
    C --> D{Worker in Bottom Quartile}
    D -->|Yes| E[Exploit: Copy weights and hyperparameters from Top Quartile Worker]
    D -->|No| F[Explore: Perturb copied hyperparameters]
    E --> G[Continue Training with New Hyperparameters]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Budget Exhausted}
    H -->|No| B
    H -->|Yes| I[Return Best Worker Model and Hyperparameters]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** Differentiate between **model parameters** and **hyperparameters** in deep learning. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Model Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Internal variables **learned** during training | External **configuration choices** set before training |
| Updated by | Backpropagation + gradient descent | Manual choice or HPO algorithm |
| Examples | Weight matrices $\mathbf{W}^{[l]}$, bias vectors $\mathbf{b}^{[l]}$ | Learning rate $\eta$, batch size $B$, number of epochs $E$, dropout rate $p_{\text{drop}}$ |

**(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example of each, 1 mark for distinction table = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List any **four** hyperparameters used while training a deep neural network and state their typical ranges.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Learning rate $\eta$:** $\eta \in [10^{-5},\; 10^{-1}]$ (log-uniform sampling recommended). **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Batch size $B$:** $B \in \{16, 32, 64, 128, 256\}$ — powers of 2 preferred for hardware efficiency. **(0.75 marks)**
3. **Number of epochs $E$:** $E \in [10,\; 500]$ depending on dataset size and early stopping. **(0.75 marks)**
4. **Weight decay $\lambda_{\text{wd}}$:** $\lambda_{\text{wd}} \in [10^{-6},\; 10^{-2}]$ (log scale). **(0.75 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain **Grid Search** and **Random Search** methods for hyperparameter tuning. Compare their advantages and disadvantages in high-dimensional settings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Grid Search (3 marks):**
* Discretizes each hyperparameter $\lambda_i$ into $g_i$ candidate values, forming a Cartesian grid $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{\text{grid}} = \bigtimes_i \{a_i^{(1)}, \dots, a_i^{(g_i)}\}$.
* Total trials: $T_{\text{grid}} = \prod_i g_i$. *(0.5 mark)*
* Each combination $(\lambda_1^{(j_1)}, \dots, \lambda_k^{(j_k)})$ is trained and evaluated. *(0.5 mark)*
* **Advantages:** Simple, deterministic, reproducible, exhaustive within grid. *(1 mark)*
* **Disadvantages:** Suffers from the **curse of dimensionality** — for $k = 10, g_i = 10$, requires $T = 10^{10}$ trials, infeasible. Wastes trials on irrelevant hyperparameters. *(1 mark)*

**Random Search (3 marks):**
* Samples $T$ configurations $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{(t)} \stackrel{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{U}(\boldsymbol{\Lambda})$ uniformly. *(0.5 mark)*
* **Bergstra & Bengio (2012)** proved that in high-D with only $k_{\text{eff}} < k$ influential hyperparameters, random search is **exponentially more efficient** than grid search. *(1 mark)*
* **Advantages:** Scales to high dimensions, parallelizable embarrassingly, easy to implement, no wasted trials on irrelevant axes. *(1 mark)*
* **Disadvantages:** No learning from past trials — each sample is independent; may miss narrow optima in low-D spaces. *(0.5 mark)*

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Grid Search | Random Search |
|---|---|---|
| Trials in high-D | Wastes compute | Concentrates on important axes |
| Optimality | Local within grid | Probabilistic convergence |
| Implementation | Very simple | Very simple |
| Best for | $k \leq 4$ | $k > 4$ |

---

**(b)** Describe **Bayesian Optimization** for hyperparameter tuning. Explain the role of the **surrogate model** and the **acquisition function** with the mathematical formulation of Expected Improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Bayesian Optimization Overview (2 marks):**
* A **sample-efficient**, **sequential** global optimization strategy for black-box, expensive-to-evaluate functions. *(0.5 mark)*
* Two key components: **Surrogate Model** (probabilistic model of $f$) and **Acquisition Function** (decides next query). *(0.5 mark)*
* Iterative loop: (1) fit surrogate on $\mathcal{D}_{1:t}$, (2) maximize acquisition to get $\mathbf{x}_{t+1}$, (3) query $f(\mathbf{x}_{t+1})$, (4) update $\mathcal{D}_{1:t+1}$. *(1 mark)*

**Surrogate Model — Gaussian Process (2 marks):**
* Prior: $f \sim \mathcal{GP}(m(\mathbf{x}), k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'))$ with kernel typically Matern-5/2 or RBF. *(0.5 mark)*
* Posterior after $t$ observations: $\mu_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{k}_t^\top (\mathbf{K}_t + \sigma_n^2 \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{y}_{1:t}$. *(0.5 mark)*
* Posterior variance: $\sigma_t^2(\mathbf{x}) = k(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{k}_t^\top (\mathbf{K}_t + \sigma_n^2 \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{k}_t$. *(0.5 mark)*
* Provides both **predictive mean** (exploitation) and **predictive uncertainty** (exploration). *(0.5 mark)*

**Acquisition Function — Expected Improvement (3 marks):**
* Definition: $\text{EI}_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(f^{\star}_t - f(\mathbf{x}), 0) \mid \mathcal{D}_{1:t}\right]$. *(0.5 mark)*
* Closed-form expression: $\text{EI}_t(\mathbf{x}) = (f^{\star}_t - \mu_t(\mathbf{x})) \Phi(Z) + \sigma_t(\mathbf{x}) \phi(Z)$, where $Z = (f^{\star}_t - \mu_t(\mathbf{x})) / \sigma_t(\mathbf{x})$. *(1 mark)*
* **First term** $(f^{\star}_t - \mu_t)\Phi(Z)$ — **exploitation** (prefer low predicted mean). *(0.5 mark)*
* **Second term** $\sigma_t \phi(Z)$ — **exploration** (prefer high uncertainty). *(0.5 mark)*
* Alternative UCB: $\text{UCB}_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mu_t(\mathbf{x}) + \kappa_t \sigma_t(\mathbf{x})$. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the **challenges of hyperparameter tuning in deep learning**. Explain the **Hyperband** algorithm and how it addresses these challenges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Challenges (3.5 marks):**
1. **High dimensionality:** Modern networks have 10–50+ hyperparameters, making grid search infeasible. *(0.75 mark)*
2. **Expensive evaluation:** Single trial = hours to days of GPU time. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Stochastic noise:** Random seed effects cause non-reproducible validation loss. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Conditional hyperparameters:** Optimal value of $\lambda_j$ depends on $\lambda_i$, creating non-rectangular effective search spaces. *(0.75 mark)*
5. **Non-stationarity:** Different stages of training may need different hyperparameters (handled by PBT, not Hyperband). *(0.75 mark)*

**Hyperband Algorithm (3.5 marks):**
* **Li et al. (2017)** — a multi-arm bandit approach for hyperparameter optimization with **adaptive resource allocation** (epochs). *(0.5 mark)*
* **Core idea:** Run many random configurations with small budgets (few epochs), aggressively **early-stop** poor ones, and allocate more epochs to promising ones. *(1 mark)*
* **Successive Halving inner loop:** Given $n$ configurations and total budget $B$, allocate $B/n$ to each, evaluate, keep top $\eta^{-1}$ fraction, repeat. $(0.75 \text{ mark})$
* **Outer loop (Bracket):** Hyperband considers multiple brackets $(s_{\max}, s_{\max}-1, \dots, 0)$ trading off number of configs $n$ vs epochs per config $B/n$. $(0.75 \text{ mark})$
* **Advantage:** Massively parallelizable; theoretically justified via bandit theory; handles the **explore-vs-exploit** tradeoff without Bayesian machinery. $(0.5 \text{ mark})$

---

**(b)** Explain **Population Based Training (PBT)** for hyperparameter tuning. Describe its workflow and explain why it is well-suited for **deep reinforcement learning**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**PBT Overview (2.5 marks):**
* **Jaderberg et al. (2017, DeepMind)** — a hybrid method combining **parallel search** (like evolutionary methods) with **online learning** (like SGD). *(0.5 mark)*
* Maintains a **population of $N$ models** training in parallel, each with its own hyperparameters $\boldsymbol{\lambda}_i$. *(0.5 mark)*
* Periodically (every $s$ steps), **evaluate**, **exploit**, and **explore** the population. *(0.5 mark)*
* Unlike Hyperband, PBT **adapts hyperparameters online** during a single training run — does not restart from scratch. *(1 mark)*

**Workflow — Exploit and Explore (3 marks):**
* **Step 1 — Evaluate:** Compute validation score for each worker in the population. *(0.5 mark)*
* **Step 2 — Exploit:** If worker $i$ is in the bottom $p$-quantile, copy the weights $\theta_j$ and hyperparameters $\boldsymbol{\lambda}_j$ from a top-quantile worker $j$ (selected via tournament or truncation selection). *(1 mark)*
* **Step 3 — Explore:** Perturb the copied hyperparameters, e.g., $\boldsymbol{\lambda}_i^{\text{new}} = \boldsymbol{\lambda}_j + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$, where $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ is sampled from a noise distribution (factor, multiply, or resample). *(1 mark)*
* **Step 4 — Continue training** with the new weights and perturbed hyperparameters. *(0.5 mark)*

**Why PBT suits Deep RL (1.5 marks):**
* RL training is **non-stationary** — optimal hyperparameters shift as the policy evolves (e.g., exploration must decay as policy matures). PBT's online adaptation handles this naturally. *(0.75 mark)*
* RL is **sample-inefficient** — cannot afford to restart from scratch many times; PBT amortizes training across the population. *(0.75 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "hyperparameter" with "parameter":** Many students write $\eta$ as a "parameter learned by gradient descent" — this is **wrong** and costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Skipping the mathematical formulation:** For Bayesian Optimization questions, writing only the algorithm pseudo-code without the EI formula or GP posterior formulas is a guaranteed **-3 marks** deduction.
> 3. **Not categorizing hyperparameters:** A 7-mark question on "explain hyperparameter tuning" that fails to distinguish architecture / optimization / regularization hyperparameters loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Missing the "Why" behind the method:** Writing "Random Search samples uniformly" without mentioning Bergstra & Bengio's dimensionality argument is incomplete — always state **why** the method works.
> 5. **Forgetting practical considerations:** Real KTU board answers always mention compute budget, parallelizability, and reproducibility — examiners explicitly reward these practical insights.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hyperparameter Tuning**

* **Core Definition:** Hyperparameter tuning = finding $\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\star} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{val}}(\boldsymbol{\lambda})$ over a search space $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$. **(HIGH-YIELD)**
* **Three categories of hyperparameters:** Optimization ($\eta, B, E, \beta$), Architecture ($L, n_l, k, p_{\text{drop}}$), Regularization ($\lambda_{\text{wd}}, \epsilon_{\text{LS}}$). **(HIGH-YIELD)**
* **Grid Search:** Exhaustive Cartesian product — **infeasible in high-D** due to curse of dimensionality.
* **Random Search:** Uniform random sampling — Bergstra & Bengio (2012) proved it is **exponentially more efficient** than grid search when only a few hyperparameters matter.
* **Bayesian Optimization:** Two components — **Surrogate (Gaussian Process)** + **Acquisition (EI / UCB)**. Closed-form EI: $(f^{\star}_t - \mu_t)\Phi(Z) + \sigma_t \phi(Z)$. **Sample-efficient** but sequential.
* **Hyperband:** Multi-arm bandit with **successive halving** and early stopping. Best when per-trial cost is cheap and parallelism is abundant.
* **PBT:** Online, parallel, hybrid evolutionary + SGD. Best for **non-stationary objectives** (Deep RL). Uses **exploit (copy)** + **explore (perturb)**.
* **Search space rules:** Use **log scale** for $\eta, \lambda_{\text{wd}}$; use **powers of 2** for $B$; use **linear scale** for $p_{\text{drop}}$.
* **Evaluation protocol:** Always use a **held-out validation set** or **K-fold cross-validation**; never tune on the test set.
* **Practical mantra:** Start with **random search** as a strong baseline; upgrade to **Bayesian optimization** if $T < 100$ and trials are expensive; use **Hyperband** if trials are cheap; use **PBT** for online / RL settings.
* **Key references (do NOT fabricate beyond these):** Bergstra & Bengio (2012, JMLR) — Random Search; Snoek et al. (2012, NIPS) — Spearmint (Bayesian Opt); Li et al. (2017, JMLR) — Hyperband; Jaderberg et al. (2017, NeurIPS) — PBT.
* **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO1** (understand optimization techniques for deep networks) and **CO2** (apply optimization to real-world training pipelines).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bias-Variance Tradeoff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In supervised learning, when we train a model on a finite dataset, the resulting prediction function $\hat{f}(x)$ is one of many possible functions that could have been learned from different samples of the underlying data distribution. The **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** is the foundational decomposition that explains *why* a model's generalization error behaves the way it does, and *how* it must be balanced to achieve optimal predictive performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **expected prediction error** of a learned model at any input point $x$ can be mathematically decomposed into the sum of three irreducible components: the **squared bias**, the **variance**, and the **irreducible noise**.

Formally, the decomposition is:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \underbrace{\left(f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} \;+\; \underbrace{E_{D}\!\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}\right]}_{\text{Variance}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}$$

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Squared Bias | $\text{Bias}^{2}[\hat{f}(x)]$ | $\text{error}^{2}$ | Systematic error from wrong assumptions |
| Variance | $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$ | $\text{error}^{2}$ | Sensitivity to training-set fluctuations |
| Irreducible Error | $\sigma^{2}$ | $\text{error}^{2}$ | Inherent noise in the data-generating process |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Archery Target

Imagine four archers shooting at a bullseye. Each archer represents a *different trained model*, and each shot represents a *prediction on a different randomly drawn training set*.

- **High Bias, Low Variance (Systematic Mistake):** All four archers consistently miss the bullseye, but their arrows land tightly clustered in the *same wrong spot* on the wall. The model is too simple — it consistently makes the *same kind* of error. (e.g., a linear model fit to a non-linear curve).
- **Low Bias, High Variance (Wildly Scattered Shots):** On average, the arrows are centered on the bullseye, but they are scattered all over the target. The model is so flexible that it changes drastically depending on the training data. (e.g., a deep decision tree memorizing noise).
- **Low Bias, Low Variance (Master Archer):** The arrows are tightly clustered around the bullseye. This is our goal — accurate *and* consistent.
- **High Bias, High Variance (Lost Archer):** Arrows are scattered *and* off-center. Worst-case scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** **Bias** is about *where* the average prediction lands; **Variance** is about *how much* the predictions scatter around that average. The **tradeoff** is that simplifying a model reduces variance but increases bias, and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions

The decomposition is derived under three standard KTU assumptions:

1. **Ground truth exists:** There is a true underlying function $f(x)$ such that $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$.
2. **Zero-mean noise:** The noise $\varepsilon$ has $E[\varepsilon] = 0$.
3. **Independence:** $\varepsilon$ is independent of both $x$ and the training set $D$, and $E[\varepsilon^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$ is a constant (the *irreducible error*).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

- **Step 1 — Identify sources of error:** When a model is trained on a sample $D$ drawn from the population, two things go wrong: the *model family* may be too restrictive (bias), and the *particular sample* may be unrepresentative (variance).
- **Step 2 — Quantify the bias:** Squared bias $= (f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2}$. This measures the *squared* distance between the average prediction (averaged over all possible training sets) and the true function.
- **Step 3 — Quantify the variance:** Variance $= E_{D}[(\hat{f}(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2}]$. This is the standard statistical variance of the prediction across training sets.
- **Step 4 — Add the irreducible noise:** $\sigma^{2}$ cannot be removed by any model; it is the floor on generalization error.
- **Step 5 — Total Error:** The expected out-of-sample error at $x$ is the sum of these three orthogonal components. **No cross terms remain** because $E[\varepsilon] = 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the symbols below — they appear verbatim in KTU 2024 End-Semester Examinations.

| Symbol / Equation | Description | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_{D}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}]$ | Expected squared prediction error | Total generalization error |
| $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ | Data-generating model | Setting up the decomposition |
| $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ | Systematic error | Linear vs complex models |
| $\text{Bias}^{2}[\hat{f}(x)] = (E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x))^{2}$ | Squared bias | Error decomposition |
| $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{D}[(\hat{f}(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2}]$ | Variance term | Model sensitivity |
| $\sigma^{2} = \text{Var}(\varepsilon)$ | Irreducible noise | Bayes error floor |
| $\text{Total Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ | Final decomposition | Full theoretical proof |
| $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \hat{f}(x_{i}))^{2}$ | Empirical estimator | Estimating error from data |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep Learning Model Selection:** In convolutional neural networks, increasing depth reduces bias but inflates variance — justifying the need for **regularization, dropout, and early stopping**.
- **Hyperparameter Tuning:** The tradeoff explains why learning rate, network width, and training duration are tuned in tandem — they all shift the bias-variance balance.
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** A high-bias model may miss a disease consistently (false negatives); a high-variance model may give different predictions for the same patient scanned twice — both unacceptable. Engineers must place the operating point carefully.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** High variance is dangerous because it means the same road produces different steering decisions across model retraining cycles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition

This is the most important derivation in Module 1. Every step is shown in full so you can reproduce it for the 14-mark KTU ESE question.

### Step 0 — Setup

Let the true relationship be:

$$y = f(x) + \varepsilon$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the random noise with $E[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $E[\varepsilon^{2}] = \sigma^{2}$. The dataset $D$ is drawn i.i.d. from this distribution, and a learning algorithm produces a model $\hat{f}(x)$ from $D$. The expected test error at a fixed point $x$ is $E_{D}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}]$.

### Step 1 — Substitute the data-generating model

Substitute $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ into the error expression:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right]$$

### Step 2 — Expand the square

Using $(a + b + c)^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2} + c^{2} + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$, with $a = f(x) - \hat{f}(x)$ and $b = \varepsilon$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; & E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \\
& + E_{D}\!\left[\varepsilon^{2}\right] \\
& + 2\,E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)\varepsilon\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the cross term

Since $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}(x)$ and of $D$, and $E[\varepsilon] = 0$:

$$2\,E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)\varepsilon\right] \;=\; 2\left(f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)E[\varepsilon] \;=\; 0$$

Therefore the cross term vanishes completely.

### Step 4 — Handle the noise term

$$E_{D}\!\left[\varepsilon^{2}\right] \;=\; \text{Var}(\varepsilon) + (E[\varepsilon])^{2} \;=\; \sigma^{2} + 0 \;=\; \sigma^{2}$$

The expected error so far reduces to:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] + \sigma^{2}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the mean prediction

Define the *mean prediction* (also called the *main effect* of the model):

$$\bar{f}(x) \;=\; E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]$$

This is the prediction we would get by averaging infinitely many models trained on different samples of size $n$.

### Step 6 — Add and subtract the mean prediction

Add and subtract $\bar{f}(x)$ inside the square to enable bias-variance separation:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x) + \bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right]$$

### Step 7 — Expand the squared sum

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x) + \bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; & E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \\
& + E_{D}\!\left[\left(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \\
& + 2\,E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Simplify the bias term

Because $f(x)$ and $\bar{f}(x)$ are constants with respect to $D$:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)^{2} \;=\; \left(f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}$$

This is the **squared bias** term.

### Step 9 — Simplify the variance term

By the definition of statistical variance, the term is exactly the variance of $\hat{f}(x)$:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; E_{D}\!\left[\left(\hat{f}(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$$

### Step 10 — Eliminate the cross term

The cross term is:

$$2\,E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)\right]$$

Since $f(x) - \bar{f}(x)$ is a constant w.r.t. $D$:

$$2\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)E_{D}\!\left[\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right] \;=\; 2\left(f(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right)\left(\bar{f}(x) - \bar{f}(x)\right) \;=\; 0$$

### Step 11 — Combine all terms

Substituting Steps 8, 9, and 10 into Step 7:

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(f(x) - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \left(f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2} + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]$$

### Step 12 — Final result

Combining with Step 4:

$$\boxed{\,E_{D}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \underbrace{\left(f(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)]}_{\text{Variance}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}\,}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Model Complexity vs Error Components

This table shows how the three error terms behave as **model complexity** increases (e.g., deeper networks, more parameters):

| Model Complexity | Bias² | Variance | Total Error | Symptom |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Very Low (e.g., linear fit on quadratic data) | **High** | Low | High | **Underfitting** |
| Low | High | Low | High | Underfitting |
| Moderate (sweet spot) | Moderate | Moderate | **Minimum** | **Optimal** |
| High | Low | Moderate | Rising | Starting to overfit |
| Very High (deep net, no regularization) | Very Low | **High** | High | **Overfitting** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Error Decomposition Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how an input flows through the model, the data, and how each error component is mathematically extracted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Feature Vector X] --> B[True Function f of X]
    A --> C[Noise Epsilon with Mean 0, Variance Sigma Squared]
    B --> D[True Output Y = f of X + Epsilon]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Training Dataset D Sampled i.i.d.]
    E --> F[Learning Algorithm]
    F --> G[Trained Model f-hat of X]
    G --> H[Predicted Output y-hat]
    D --> I[Ground Truth Y]
    I --> J{Expected Squared Error Computation}
    H --> J
    J --> K[Bias Squared Term]
    J --> L[Variance Term]
    J --> M[Irreducible Noise Sigma Squared]
    K --> N[Final Decomposition: Bias Squared + Variance + Sigma Squared]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — The Tradeoff Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Model Complexity Increases] --> Q[Bias Squared DECREASES]
    P --> R[Variance INCREASES]
    Q --> S[Total Error U-Shaped Curve]
    R --> S
    S --> T[Left Side: Underfitting Region]
    S --> U[Bottom: Optimal Point]
    S --> V[Right Side: Overfitting Region]
    T --> W[Fix: Increase Complexity, Add Features]
    U --> X[Goal: Deploy Model Here]
    V --> Y[Fix: Regularization, Dropout, Early Stop, More Data]
```

## 4.3 Interpretation of the Diagrams

- **Pipeline Diagram:** Traces the journey of data from the true generating process → training set → trained model → prediction, and shows where the **bias**, **variance**, and **noise** terms are computed.
- **Concept Map:** Captures the U-shape of total error as complexity changes, and the engineering remedies (regularization, more data) used to push the operating point back toward the optimal valley.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU 2024 board exams, a hand-drawn equivalent of these block diagrams in your answer sheet, even if rough, earns significant partial credit under the "presentation" evaluation criteria.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define the terms Bias and Variance in the context of supervised learning. How do they differ from each other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Bias (1 mark):** Bias is the systematic error introduced by approximating a real-world problem, which may be extremely complex, with a simplified model. Mathematically, $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$. A high-bias model *consistently* misses the true function (e.g., fitting a straight line to a sinusoidal curve).
- **Variance (1 mark):** Variance is the amount by which $\hat{f}(x)$ would change if we estimated it using a *different* training dataset. Mathematically, $\text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] = E_{D}[(\hat{f}(x) - E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2}]$. A high-variance model overreacts to small fluctuations in the training data.
- **Difference (1 mark):** Bias is error due to *wrong assumptions* (underfitting); Variance is error due to *excessive sensitivity* to the sample (overfitting). They are opposing forces: reducing one usually increases the other, forming the **bias-variance tradeoff**.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**What is meant by the term "Irreducible Error"? Why can it not be eliminated by any model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The irreducible error $\sigma^{2}$ is the variance of the noise term $\varepsilon$ in the data-generating process $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$. It represents the inherent randomness or measurement noise in the data.
- **Why irreducible (1 mark):** It arises from factors outside the model's control — latent variables, sensor noise, inherent stochasticity of the phenomenon being modeled, or unobserved features.
- **Bayes error (1 mark):** The minimum achievable generalization error on a given problem is the **Bayes error**, equal to $\sigma^{2}$. No algorithm — however sophisticated — can predict noise $\varepsilon$ from $x$, because $\varepsilon$ is by definition independent of $x$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each. **You must answer ONE of the two choices.**

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Derive the complete Bias-Variance decomposition for the expected prediction error of a supervised learning model. State all the assumptions clearly.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the Bias-Variance tradeoff with respect to model complexity. How does it relate to underfitting and overfitting? Suggest two engineering techniques to manage this tradeoff in deep neural networks.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The true relationship is $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, with $E[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^{2}$.
2. $\varepsilon$ is independent of $x$ and of the training set $D$.
3. The expectation $E_{D}[\cdot]$ is taken over all possible training sets of size $n$ drawn from the same distribution.

**Derivation (5 marks):** The complete 12-step derivation is exactly as shown in **Section 3.1** above. Key checkpoints that examiners look for:

- Correctly substituting $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$. **(0.5 mark)**
- Showing the cross term vanishes because $E[\varepsilon] = 0$. **(1 mark)**
- Introducing $\bar{f}(x) = E_{D}[\hat{f}(x)]$ and the add-subtract trick. **(1 mark)**
- Showing the second cross term vanishes because $E_{D}[\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)] = 0$. **(1 mark)**
- Stating the final boxed equation with all three terms correctly identified. **(1.5 marks)**

**Final Equation:**

$$E_{D}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \text{Bias}^{2}[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^{2}$$

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Relationship with complexity (3 marks):**
- As model complexity increases (more parameters, deeper network, higher-degree polynomial), the model can capture finer details of the training data. This **reduces bias** because the model family becomes rich enough to approximate $f(x)$ more closely.
- However, the same flexibility means the model *also* fits the random noise $\varepsilon$ present in the training set. Since different training sets contain *different* noise realizations, the model produces *wildly different* predictions on the test set. This **increases variance**.

**Underfitting vs Overfitting (2 marks):**
- **Underfitting (High Bias, Low Variance):** Occurs on the *left* of the U-curve. The model is too simple, training error is high, and test error is also high.
- **Overfitting (Low Bias, High Variance):** Occurs on the *right* of the U-curve. The model fits training data almost perfectly (low training error) but generalizes poorly (high test error).

**Engineering techniques (2 marks):**
1. **Regularization (L1/L2/Weight Decay):** Adds a penalty term $\lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|^{2}$ to the loss, constraining parameter magnitudes and effectively reducing variance at the cost of slight bias.
2. **Dropout (Hinton et al., 2012):** Randomly deactivates a fraction of neurons during each training iteration, preventing co-adaptation of features. Acts as an implicit ensemble, dramatically reducing variance in deep networks.
3. *(Bonus, any one of: Early Stopping, Data Augmentation, Batch Normalization, Cross-Validation for hyperparameter tuning.)*

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) Using a suitable diagram, illustrate the conceptual difference between high-bias and high-variance models. Explain what is meant by the "sweet spot" in the bias-variance curve.** (7 marks)

**(b) A regression model is trained on a synthetic dataset where the noise variance is $\sigma^{2} = 0.25$. The expected squared bias at a particular test point is $0.36$ and the variance of the prediction is $0.09$. Compute the expected prediction error. If the model is then regularized so that bias rises to $0.49$ but variance drops to $0.04$, has the regularization helped? Justify with calculations.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram Description (3 marks):** Draw the classic U-shaped curve of *Total Error* vs *Model Complexity* with the three components plotted:
- **Bias² curve:** Monotonically decreasing, starting high and asymptotically approaching zero.
- **Variance curve:** Monotonically increasing, starting at zero and growing rapidly.
- **Total Error curve:** U-shaped, with the minimum point clearly marked as the "sweet spot".
- Mark the regions "Underfitting" (left of the minimum) and "Overfitting" (right of the minimum).

**High-Bias vs High-Variance (2 marks):**
- **High-Bias Model:** Lies in the *underfitting region*. It makes strong, often wrong, assumptions (e.g., linearity on circular data). It will give *similar* but *consistently wrong* predictions regardless of which training sample is used.
- **High-Variance Model:** Lies in the *overfitting region*. It makes weak assumptions and memorizes training data. It will give *very different* predictions when retrained on different samples.

**Sweet Spot (2 marks):** The sweet spot is the value of model complexity where **Total Error is minimized**, i.e., where the marginal increase in variance equals the marginal decrease in bias². Mathematically, it is the point satisfying $\frac{d}{d(\text{complexity})}(\text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance}) = 0$. It is the optimal operating point for deployment.

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Recall the decomposition (1 mark):**

$$E[(y - \hat{f}(x))^{2}] \;=\; \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected error *before* regularization (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{before}} \;=\; 0.36 + 0.09 + 0.25 \;=\; 0.70
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute expected error *after* regularization (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{after}} \;=\; 0.49 + 0.04 + 0.25 \;=\; 0.78
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Decision and Justification (2 marks):**

- Change in error: $\Delta E = 0.78 - 0.70 = +0.08$. Error *increased* by $0.08$ (i.e., $\approx 11.4\%$ increase).
- **Conclusion:** In this particular numerical case, the regularization has *not* helped at this test point — it pushed the model further from the sweet spot. The variance reduction of $0.05$ was smaller than the bias increase of $0.13$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the noise term $\sigma^{2}$:** Many students write $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance}$ and miss the irreducible error. This loses 1–2 marks per sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing up Bias and Bias²:** In the formula, you need the *squared* bias. If the question says "expected squared bias is 0.36", you plug in $0.36$ directly (it is already squared). If the question says "bias is 0.6", you must square it to $0.36$ first.
> 3. **Skipping the add-subtract trick:** In derivations, the step where you write $(f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} = ((f(x) - \bar{f}(x)) + (\bar{f}(x) - \hat{f}(x)))^{2}$ is worth 2 marks. Omitting it loses easy marks.
> 4. **No units in numerical answers:** Always state "expected squared error" or "MSE units" in the final line for full credit.
> 5. **Conclusion without justification in numerical problems:** "Regularization helped/didn't help" alone is not enough. Show the *calculation* and the *direction* of change.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Definition:** Bias = systematic error; Variance = sampling sensitivity; Noise = irreducible randomness.
- **Core Equation:** $E[(y - \hat{f})^{2}] = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$.
- **Cross Terms Vanish:** Both cross terms in the derivation vanish — show this explicitly in the proof for full marks.
- **Key Assumption:** $E[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\varepsilon \perp x$ are *both* required.
- **The Sweet Spot:** The complexity value where marginal $\Delta\text{Bias}^{2} = \text{marginal } \Delta\text{Variance}$.
- **U-Curve:** Total error is U-shaped in model complexity; Bias² is monotonically decreasing; Variance is monotonically increasing.
- **Deep Learning Remedies:** Regularization (L1/L2), Dropout, Early Stopping, Data Augmentation, Batch Normalization.
- **Symbol Confusions to Avoid:**
  - $\sigma^{2}$ is **noise variance**, *not* model variance.
  - $\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]$ is the *signed* bias; **squared bias** is what appears in the formula.
  - $E_{D}[\cdot]$ averages over training sets, *not* over $x$.
- **Real-World Mapping:** High bias → underfitting → simple model. High variance → overfitting → too-flexible model.
- **Exam Tip:** If asked for a 14-mark derivation, allocate ~2 marks for assumptions, ~5 marks for the expansion, ~3 marks for the cross-term eliminations, ~2 marks for identification of bias²/variance/$\sigma^{2}$, and ~2 marks for the final equation with interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem of Overfitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Problem of Overfitting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overfitting** is a fundamental statistical and machine learning phenomenon in which a learned hypothesis (model) $\hat{f}$ achieves excessively low empirical error on the training set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ but exhibits significantly higher generalization error on unseen data drawn from the same underlying distribution $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} \sim P(X, Y)$. Mathematically, overfitting is the condition where: $$\underbrace{\mathcal{E}_{\text{train}}(\hat{f})}_{\text{Training Error}} \ll \underbrace{\mathcal{E}_{\text{test}}(\hat{f})}_{\text{Generalization Error}} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{E}_{\text{test}}(\hat{f}) - \mathcal{E}_{\text{train}}(\hat{f}) \gg \delta_{\text{acceptable}}$$ where $\delta_{\text{acceptable}}$ is the tolerance threshold permitted by the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (typically $\leq 5\%$). In the language of Statistical Learning Theory (Vapnik–Chervonenkis framework), an overfitted model has **low empirical risk** but **high true risk** (also called *structural risk*). > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU Tests This (CO Mapping):** > - **CO1**: Understand the foundational problems of model generalization. > - **CO2**: Apply diagnostic and mitigation strategies to deep learning pipelines. > - Bloom's Levels: *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), *Analyze* (L4). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rote-Memorizing Student" Imagine two students preparing for a KTU semester exam: | Student Type | Study Strategy | Exam Result | |:---:|:---|:---| | **Good Student (Generalized)** | Understands core principles, derives formulas, solves varied problems. | Performs well on **new** questions. | | **Overfit Student (Memorizer)** | Memorizes last 5 years' university question papers **verbatim**. | Fails when a **slightly rephrased** question appears. | > [!TIP] > **Intuition Builder:** The overfit student has learned the *noise* and *specific formatting* of old papers, not the underlying *concept*. Similarly, a deep neural network with too many parameters (high capacity) can memorize training samples — including their idiosyncratic noise — instead of learning the *true underlying function* $f^*(x)$. ### 1.3 The Three Regimes of Model Fit A deep learning model can exhibit one of three characteristic behaviors: 1. **Underfitting (High Bias, Low Variance):** The model is too simple (e.g., linear classifier on non-linear data). It performs poorly on *both* training and test sets. 2. **Good Fit (Balanced):** The model captures the underlying pattern. Training and test errors are low and close to each other. 3. **Overfitting (Low Bias, High Variance):** The model captures noise as if it were signal. Training error is near zero; test error is high. The **capacity** (or **complexity**) of the hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ is the primary dial that controls this transition. > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** "More layers = better model." This is *false* under the KTU 2024 OBE framework. Increasing capacity beyond the intrinsic complexity of the data distribution *guarantees* overfitting for finite training sets (per the No Free Lunch theorem). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Bias–Variance Decomposition (The Heart of the Problem) The expected prediction error of a learning algorithm at a fixed query point $x_0$ can be formally decomposed as: $$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x_0;\mathcal{D})\right)^2\right] = \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x_0)]\right)^2}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\hat{f}(x_0)\right]}_{\text{Sensitivity to Data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$ ### Step-by-Step Meaning of Each Term - **Bias²**: Error introduced by approximating a real-world problem (which may be extremely complex) with a simplified model. A high-bias model is *wrong by design* — it underfits. - **Variance**: Amount by which $\hat{f}$ would change if we estimated it using a different training set $\mathcal{D}'$. A high-variance model is *fragile* — it overfits. - **$\sigma^2$ (Irreducible Error)**: The noise inherent in the true data-generating process $P(Y \mid X)$. Even an *oracle* with perfect knowledge of $P(Y \mid X)$ cannot eliminate this. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Tradeoff As model capacity $\mathcal{C}(\mathcal{H})$ increases: - **Bias decreases** (richer models can fit more functions). - **Variance increases** (richer models are more sensitive to the specific training sample). The **total generalization error** is the sum of these, producing a characteristic **U-shaped curve** with an optimal capacity $\mathcal{C}^*$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When asked "why does overfitting occur?", the *complete* KTU answer must mention **all three**: finite training data, excessive model capacity, AND noise in the labels. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Key Insight | |:--:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Empirical Risk** | $\mathcal{E}_{\text{emp}}(\hat{f}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\!\left(\hat{f}(x_i), y_i\right)$ | What the model *actually* minimizes during training. | | 2 | **True (Generalization) Risk** | $\mathcal{E}_{\text{true}}(\hat{f}) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}\!\left[\mathcal{L}\!\left(\hat{f}(x), y\right)\right]$ | What we *want* to minimize — unobservable in practice. | | 3 | **Bias–Variance Decomposition** | $\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var} + \sigma^2$ | Foundation of all KTU overfitting analysis. | | 4 | **L2 Regularization (Weight Decay)** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2 = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{j} \theta_j^2$ | Penalizes large weights → smoother decision boundary. | | 5 | **L1 Regularization (Lasso)** | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_1 = \mathcal{L}(\theta) + \lambda \sum_{j} \vert \theta_j \vert$ | Induces **sparsity** (drives weights to exactly zero). | | 6 | **Generalization Gap** | $\Delta_{\text{gen}} = \mathcal{E}_{\text{test}} - \mathcal{E}_{\text{train}}$ | $\Delta_{\text{gen}} \gg 0$ is the **operational signature of overfitting**. | | 7 | **VC Dimension Bound (PAC)** | $\mathcal{E}_{\text{true}} \leq \mathcal{E}_{\text{emp}} + \mathcal{O}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{h \log(N/h) + \log(1/\delta)}{N}}\right)$ | $h$ = VC dim, $N$ = samples, $1-\delta$ = confidence. | | 8 | **Effective Capacity of a Network** | $\mathcal{C}(\mathcal{H}) \approx \prod_{\ell=1}^{L} d_\ell$ (product of layer widths) | More layers/widths ⇒ higher capacity ⇒ more overfit risk. | | 9 | **Dropout (Inference-Time Scale)** | $\hat{y}_{\text{test}} = (1-p) \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\text{train}}[\hat{y}]$ | $p$ = dropout rate; predictions scaled by $(1-p)$ at test time. | | 10 | **Early Stopping Rule** | Stop at epoch $t^*$ when $\mathcal{E}_{\text{val}}(t)$ first stops decreasing. | Saves the checkpoint $t^*$ — a form of implicit regularization. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipe Character Avoidance:** Note the use of $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ and $\vert \cdot \vert$ in formulas. KTU valuation scripts may mis-render vertical pipes inside markdown tables; always use the LaTeX norm command. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Should a KTU Engineer Care? | Domain | Real-World Consequence of Overfitting | |:---|:---| | **Medical Diagnosis (CNN on X-rays)** | Model memorizes hospital-specific scanner artifacts → fails on data from a new hospital. | | **Autonomous Driving (Tesla FSD)** | Network overfits to California weather; fails in Kerala monsoon conditions. | | **Financial Credit Scoring** | Overfit to 2008 crisis-era data → catastrophic mispredictions in 2020. | | **NLP Chatbots (LLMs)** | Memorize training corpora verbatim → regurgitate proprietary data (data leakage). | The **economic cost** of deploying an overfit model in production can be enormous — making this topic a high-weightage KTU 2024 examination area. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Implication:** In MLOps pipelines, teams monitor the **generalization gap** $\Delta_{\text{gen}}$ in real time. A growing $\Delta_{\text{gen}}$ triggers an automatic model rollback — a direct industry application of what you study here. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Bias–Variance Decomposition (Board-Exam Ready) We start from the expected squared error at a single test point $x_0$, where the true label is $y = f(x_0) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. **Step 1: Expand the squared loss.** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x_0)\right)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}\!\left[\left(f(x_0) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x_0)\right)^2\right] \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Add and subtract the expected prediction $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \equiv \bar{f}(x_0)$ inside the square.** $$ \begin{aligned} &= \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}\!\left[\left(\big(f(x_0) - \bar{f}(x_0)\big) + \big(\bar{f}(x_0) - \hat{f}(x_0)\big) + \varepsilon\right)^2\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply the algebraic identity $(a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$, and use independence of $\varepsilon$ and $\mathcal{D}$.** Since $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$, all cross-terms involving $\varepsilon$ vanish. We obtain: $$ \begin{aligned} &= \big(f(x_0) - \bar{f}(x_0)\big)^2 \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[\big(\hat{f}(x_0) - \bar{f}(x_0)\big)^2\right] \;+\; \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\varepsilon^2] \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Identify each term by its statistical name.** $$ \begin{aligned} &= \underbrace{\left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x_0)]\right)^2}_{\text{Squared Bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[\hat{f}(x_0)\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\;\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x_0)\right)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) + \sigma^2\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **How to read this on a KTU answer sheet:** *Bias* measures how far the *average prediction across many training sets* is from the *truth*. *Variance* measures how much a *single* model's prediction fluctuates around that average. They are *independent* sources of error that *both* contribute to total loss. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Bias–Variance Calculation Suppose the true data-generating function is $f(x) = x$ and we train a polynomial regressor on $N=25$ points drawn from $y = x + \varepsilon$, $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.5^2)$. We repeat with $M=100$ different training sets. **Step 1: Compute the average prediction at $x_0 = 1.0$ across all $M$ runs.** $$ \bar{f}(1.0) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} \hat{f}_m(1.0) = 0.92 $$ **Step 2: Compute the squared bias.** $$ \text{Bias}^2 = \big(f(1.0) - \bar{f}(1.0)\big)^2 = (1.00 - 0.92)^2 = 0.0064 $$ **Step 3: Compute the variance (sample variance of predictions).** $$ \text{Var} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\left(\hat{f}_m(1.0) - 0.92\right)^2 = 0.0144 $$ **Step 4: Compute the irreducible noise.** $$ \sigma^2 = 0.5^2 = 0.25 $$ **Step 5: Sum all three components.** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f}(1.0))^2\right] &= 0.0064 + 0.0144 + 0.25 = 0.2708 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation for KTU exam:** | Component | Value | % of Total | Diagnosis | |:---|---:|---:|:---| | Bias² | 0.0064 | 2.4% | Model is **slightly underfit** (linear) for the linear truth. | | Variance | 0.0144 | 5.3% | Small — model is **stable** across datasets. | | $\sigma^2$ | 0.2500 | 92.3% | Dominates — irreducible noise cannot be removed. | > [!TIP] > **Takeaway:** In *low-noise* regimes, bias and variance dominate — overfitting matters most. In *high-noise* regimes, $\sigma^2$ dominates, and clever modeling yields diminishing returns. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — How L2 Regularization Mathematically Suppresses Overfitting Consider a linear model with squared-loss objective. Standard (unregularized) empirical risk: $$ \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - \theta^{\top} x^{(i)}\right)^2 $$ **Add the L2 penalty:** $$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - \theta^{\top} x^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{d} \theta_j^2 $$ **Step 1: Set the gradient to zero to obtain the closed-form solution.** $$ \begin{aligned} \nabla_{\theta}\,\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) &= -\frac{2}{N}X^{\top}(y - X\theta) + 2\lambda\theta = 0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad X^{\top}X\theta + \lambda N \theta &= X^{\top}y \\ \Rightarrow \quad \theta_{\text{reg}}^{*} &= \left(X^{\top}X + \lambda N I\right)^{-1} X^{\top} y \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compare with the unregularized OLS solution.** $$ \theta_{\text{OLS}}^{*} = \left(X^{\top}X\right)^{-1} X^{\top} y $$ **Step 3: Interpret the difference.** The term $\lambda N I$ added to $X^{\top}X$ *shifts the eigenvalues upward*, making the matrix strictly positive definite and **inverting more stably** (the "ridge" in *Ridge Regression*). This dampens the magnitude of every $\theta_j$, particularly those that would otherwise explode to fit noise. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bayesian Re-Interpretation (Bonus KTU Point):** L2 regularization is equivalent to placing a zero-mean Gaussian prior $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^2 I)$ on the weights. Maximizing the posterior = minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}$. This is why weight decay "shrinks" parameters — it is MAP estimation under a Gaussian prior. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Apply Which Remedy | Symptom (Observed in Learning Curves) | Most Likely Cause | Recommended Fix (in KTU exam priority) | Effect on Capacity | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Train loss $\approx 0$, Val loss $\uparrow$ | **Classic overfitting** | L2 regularization, Dropout, Early stopping | ↓ Reduces | | Both losses high and parallel | **Underfitting** | Increase model capacity, add features | ↑ Increases | | Train loss ↓, Val loss plateau (not rising) | **Capacity ceiling / data scarcity** | Get more data, Data augmentation, Transfer learning | Effective capacity ↑ | | Train loss ↓, Val loss noisy & oscillatory | **Learning rate too high** | Reduce $\eta$, use learning-rate schedule | (No capacity change) | | Train loss high, Val loss < train loss | **Distribution shift / bug** | Re-check data pipeline, validation set construction | (Investigate data) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite Follow-up:** "If you add both L1 and L2 regularization simultaneously, what do you get?" — Answer: **Elastic Net**, $\lambda_1 \Vert \theta \Vert_1 + \lambda_2 \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$, which combines sparsity (L1) and stability (L2). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Three Regimes of Model Fit ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Training Data + Noise] --> B{Hypothesis Class
Capacity} B -->|Too Low| C[UNDERFITTING] B -->|Balanced| D[GOOD FIT] B -->|Too High| E[OVERFITTING] C --> C1[High Bias
Low Variance] C --> C2[Train Error: HIGH
Test Error: HIGH] D --> D1[Low Bias
Low Variance] D --> D2[Train Error: LOW
Test Error: LOW] E --> E1[Low Bias
High Variance] E --> E2[Train Error: NEAR 0
Test Error: HIGH] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to interpret:** The hypothesis class capacity acts as a "dial". Overfitting sits at the *maximum* end of the dial. KTU tests your ability to identify the symptom → cause → remedy pipeline. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bias–Variance Tradeoff as Capacity Sweeps ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bias_Variance_Curve [Capacity vs Error Tradeoff] direction LR X1[Low Capacity] --> X2[Optimal Capacity] --> X3[High Capacity] end X1 -->|Region 1| R1[Bias HIGH
Variance LOW
Total HIGH] X2 -->|Region 2| R2[Bias LOW
Variance LOW
Total MINIMUM] X3 -->|Region 3| R3[Bias VERY LOW
Variance HIGH
Total HIGH] style R2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style R3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual mnemonic:** The green "sweet spot" is what every KTU deep-learning question on overfitting is *implicitly* asking you to identify. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Diagnostic and Mitigation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Model Trained]) --> Check{Train vs Validation
Error Comparison} Check -->|Gap is small| GoodFit([GOOD FIT - Deploy]) Check -->|Gap is large| Overfit[OVERFITTING DETECTED] Overfit --> Mitigate{Mitigation
Strategy} Mitigate --> M1[Add L1 or L2 Regularization] Mitigate --> M2[Apply Dropout Layers] Mitigate --> M3[Use Early Stopping] Mitigate --> M4[Increase Training Data] Mitigate --> M5[Reduce Model Capacity] Mitigate --> M6[Data Augmentation] Mitigate --> M7[Batch Normalization] Mitigate --> M8[K-fold Cross Validation] M1 --> Retrain[Retrain Model] M2 --> Retrain M3 --> Retrain M4 --> Retrain M5 --> Retrain M6 --> Retrain M7 --> Retrain M8 --> Retrain Retrain --> Check style Overfit fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff style GoodFit fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style Retrain fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid ID Rule Compliance:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `M1`, `M2`, `R1`) prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Train/Validation/Test Split for Detecting Overfitting ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RawData [Complete Dataset D] direction TB D1[/80 percent Training Set/] D2[/10 percent Validation Set/] D3[/10 percent Test Set - Holdout/] end D1 --> Model[Deep Neural Network
f theta x] Model --> TrainError[Compute Training Error] D2 --> ValError[Compute Validation Error
after EACH epoch] D3 -.Once Final.-> TestError[Compute Test Error
UNSEEN during tuning] TrainError --> Compare{Generalization
Gap} ValError --> Compare Compare -->|Gap small| Deploy[Deploy Model] Compare -->|Gap large| Tune[Trigger Regularization
or Capacity Adjustment] Tune --> Model ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Point:** The test set must be touched **exactly once** — at the very end. Repeatedly tuning on the test set causes a *meta-level* form of overfitting (peeking, or "researcher degrees of freedom" bias). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** > Define overfitting. List any two causes of overfitting in deep neural networks. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Overfitting is a condition in which a deep learning model learns the training data — including its noise and idiosyncrasies — so precisely that it fails to generalize to new, unseen data. Mathematically, it is the condition $\mathcal{E}_{\text{train}} \ll \mathcal{E}_{\text{test}}$, i.e., the generalization gap $\Delta_{\text{gen}}$ is large. **Two Causes (1 mark — ½ mark each):** 1. **Excessive model capacity** (too many layers/parameters relative to the size of the training set). 2. **Insufficient or noisy training data** (small $N$, or label noise $\sigma^2$ dominating the signal). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing only "more data" or "complex model" without contrasting them as *cause* and the *symptom* will cost you 1 mark. KTU expects a *paired* (cause → effect) articulation. --- ### **Q2.
