[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > Differentiate between L1 and L2 regularization in the context of preventing overfitting. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge) | |:---|:---|:---| | **Penalty term** | $\lambda \sum_j \vert \theta_j \vert$ | $\lambda \sum_j \theta_j^2$ | | **Effect on weights** | Drives many weights to **exactly zero** (sparsity) | Shrinks weights smoothly toward zero (no exact zeros) | | **Resulting model** | Sparse, feature selection | Dense, all features used with dampened magnitudes | | **Bayesian prior** | Laplace prior | Gaussian prior | | **Use case** | High-dimensional data with redundant features | Multicollinear features, smooth shrinkage | **Conclusion (1 mark):** L1 yields *interpretable* sparse models, while L2 yields *stable* dense models. Elastic Net combines both. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *effect* of L1 and L2. Memorize: **L1 → sparse (sharp corners), L2 → shrunk (smooth, differentiable)**. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]* > **(a)** Derive the bias–variance decomposition of the expected squared prediction error of a regression model. Clearly state the meaning of each term in the resulting expression. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the concept of overfitting with a suitable diagram. Discuss **any three** techniques used to mitigate overfitting in deep neural networks. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the setup. Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Let $\hat{f}(x_0; \mathcal{D})$ be the model trained on dataset $\mathcal{D}$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Expand the expected squared error: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D},\varepsilon}\!\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f + \varepsilon - \hat{f})^2\right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[(f - \bar{f})^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\!\left[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2\right] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2] + \text{cross terms} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Show that cross-terms vanish because $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f} - \bar{f}] = 0$ by definition of the expectation. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Present the final boxed identity and name each term: $$ \boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y - \hat{f})^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})}_{\text{Systematic Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f})}_{\text{Sensitivity to Data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}\;} $$ **Meaning of terms (full marks requires the description table):** | Term | Meaning | |:---|:---| | $\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})$ | $(f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x_0)])^2$ — How far the *average* prediction deviates from the truth. | | $\text{Var}(\hat{f})$ | $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[(\hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x_0)])^2]$ — How much the prediction fluctuates across training sets. | | $\sigma^2$ | The noise in the true data-generating process. Unavoidable. | #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Concept (2 marks):** Overfitting occurs when the model captures the *noise* in training data instead of the *signal*. The training error becomes near zero while the test/validation error remains high or starts increasing. **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a learning-curve plot with: - X-axis: Training epochs (1 to T). - Y-axis: Loss. - Two curves: Training loss (monotonically decreasing) and Validation loss (decreasing, then increasing after a point $t^*$). - Mark the optimal stopping epoch $t^*$. ``` Loss | \ Training Loss (monotonically ↓) | \_____________ | \ Validation Loss | \ ___,---.._ | \_/ ``--.._ |___________________________ Epochs ↑ t* (Early Stopping Point) ``` **Three mitigation techniques (3 marks — 1 each):** 1. **L2 Regularization (Weight Decay):** Add $\lambda \Vert \theta \Vert_2^2$ to the loss. Penalizes large weights, producing smoother decision boundaries and reducing model sensitivity to individual training examples. 2. **Dropout:** During each forward pass, randomly zero out neurons with probability $p$. Prevents co-adaptation of features, forcing the network to learn redundant robust representations. At inference, scale outputs by $(1-p)$. 3. **Early Stopping:** Monitor validation loss at every epoch. Stop training when validation loss has not improved for a patience window. Revert to the saved best checkpoint $\theta_{t^*}$. This is a form of *implicit regularization* that limits the effective number of gradient updates. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (7-mark questions):** Examiners award 1 mark for the **diagram**. If you skip the diagram, you cannot score full marks regardless of how well you write the text. Also, do *not* confuse *regularization strength* $\lambda$ with the *learning rate* $\eta$ — they are independent hyperparameters. --- ### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Re-cast for 2024 Scheme]* > **(a)** What is the role of the validation set in detecting overfitting? Explain **$k$-fold cross-validation** as a robust alternative when the dataset is small. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With reference to the bias–variance tradeoff, explain how increasing model complexity affects each of the following in a deep neural network: (i) training error, (ii) test error, (iii) variance, (iv) bias. Sketch the typical tradeoff curve. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Role of Validation Set (3 marks):** The validation set is a *held-out portion* of the training data used to: 1. Estimate the **generalization error** during hyperparameter tuning (without touching the test set). 2. Detect overfitting by computing the **generalization gap** $\Delta_{\text{gen}} = \mathcal{E}_{\text{val}} - \mathcal{E}_{\text{train}}$. 3. Trigger early stopping or model selection (e.g., choosing the best epoch, regularization strength, or architecture). **$k$-Fold Cross-Validation (4 marks):** 1. **Procedure (2 marks):** Partition the training set into $k$ equal-sized *folds*. For each fold $i \in \{1, \dots, k\}$: - Train on the remaining $k-1$ folds. - Validate on fold $i$. - Record the validation score $s_i$. 2. **Aggregation (1 mark):** The final performance estimate is the mean: $$ \hat{\mathcal{E}}_{\text{CV}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i $$ 3. **Why robust for small datasets (1 mark):** Every sample is used *both* for training and validation, reducing variance in the performance estimate. For small $N$ where a single train/validation split would be unreliable, $k$-fold provides a low-variance, high-utilization estimate. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU values:** $k = 5$ or $k = 10$ are most common. For very small datasets, *Leave-One-Out CV* ($k = N$) is used at higher computational cost. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Effect of increasing model complexity (4 marks — 1 each):** | Quantity | Trend as Complexity ↑ | Reason | |:---|:---|:---| | (i) Training error | **Monotonically decreases** | More capacity ⇒ easier to interpolate the training set. | | (ii) Test error | **U-shaped** (decreases, then increases) | Sweet spot at optimal complexity $\mathcal{C}^*$. | | (iii) Variance | **Monotonically increases** | More parameters ⇒ greater sensitivity to sampling fluctuations. | | (iv) Bias | **Monotonically decreases** | Richer hypothesis class can fit the truth more closely. | **Tradeoff curve sketch (3 marks):** ``` Error | \ Test Error | \ ___,--- | \__/ ← U-shape (overfitting past C*) | ↑ | | Optimal C* (min test error) | / | / Training Error | /__________________↓ (monotone) |_________________________ Complexity (C) ↑ C* ``` > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall (KTU 2024):** A common error is to draw the test-error curve as *monotonically increasing* from the start. The correct U-shape *first decreases* (region of underfit-to-fit transition) and *then increases* (overfit region). Drawing it incorrectly costs 1 mark. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Problem of Overfitting** - ✅ **Definition:** Model fits training noise; $\mathcal{E}_{\text{train}} \ll \mathcal{E}_{\text{test}}$. - ✅ **Three causes:** (1) Excessive capacity, (2) Limited/noisy data, (3) Inadequate regularization. - ✅ **Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$ decomposition** — must be derivable from scratch. - ✅ **Bias ↓ and Variance ↑ as capacity ↑** — the fundamental tradeoff. - ✅ **L1 vs L2:** L1 → sparse; L2 → shrunk; Elastic Net → both. - ✅ **Dropout:** Random neuron deactivation at training time; scale by $(1-p)$ at test time. - ✅ **Early stopping:** Save best checkpoint based on validation loss; implicit regularizer. - ✅ **Data augmentation & k-fold CV:** Combat data scarcity. - ✅ **Generalization gap** $\Delta_{\text{gen}} = \mathcal{E}_{\text{val}} - \mathcal{E}_{\text{train}}$ — the diagnostic metric. - ✅ **Test set hygiene:** Touch the test set **once**, at the very end. - ✅ **Bayesian view:** L2 ↔ Gaussian prior, L1 ↔ Laplace prior on weights. - ✅ **High-yield KTU diagram:** Always include the *learning curve* (train vs. validation loss). - ✅ **Mnemonic:** "Overfit = Low Training Loss + High Validation Loss" → Generalization Gap EXPLODES. > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic — Problem of Overfitting | Module 1 | PEEBT746 Deep Learning Techniques | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Deep Learning**, **Regularization** is defined as any modification made to a learning algorithm that is intended to **reduce its generalization error** but **not its training error**. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, regularization is a set of mathematical and structural techniques used to constrain the hypothesis space of a neural network so that the learned function generalizes well to **unseen test data**, thereby mitigating the problem of **overfitting** (high variance).

Mathematically, regularization transforms the canonical empirical risk minimization (ERM) objective:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ERM}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$$

into a **regularized risk** objective:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}}(\theta) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})}_{\text{Data fidelity term}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{\Omega(\theta)}_{\text{Regularizer}}$$

where:
- $\theta$ represents the trainable parameters (weights and biases) of the network.
- $\mathcal{L}$ is the per-sample loss function.
- $\Omega(\theta)$ is the **regularization penalty** imposed on the parameters.
- $\lambda \ge 0$ is the **regularization coefficient** (also called the **regularization strength** or **hyperparameter**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module-1 scope of regularization is restricted to **parameter-norm penalties (L1 and L2)**, the **bias–variance decomposition**, and the **Bayesian / Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) justification** of these penalties. Other techniques (Dropout, BatchNorm, Data Augmentation) belong to later modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cramming vs. Understanding Student"

Imagine two students preparing for an exam:

- **Student A (Overfitted, No Regularization):** Memorizes the entire textbook word-for-word, including the sample questions and their exact answers. Performs brilliantly on the textbook questions but fails badly when the examiner changes a single number in the problem. He has **memorized the noise**.
- **Student B (Regularized):** Studies the concepts, works on a *variety* of practice problems, and focuses on understanding the underlying principles. He performs consistently on both practice and unseen exam questions. He has **learned the signal**.

The **regularization parameter** $\lambda$ acts as the "discipline level" imposed on Student A. A small $\lambda$ allows memorization, while a large $\lambda$ forces the model to keep its weights small and simple — analogous to forcing the student to explain concepts in their own simple words.

## 1.3 The Core Problem Regularization Solves — Overfitting

A model **overfits** when it captures random fluctuations (noise) in the training data as if they were genuine patterns. The hallmark symptoms are:

1. **Training loss $\ll$ Validation loss** (large generalization gap).
2. **Extremely large weight magnitudes** (the network is using high weights to fit small noise spikes).
3. **Poor performance on out-of-distribution samples.**

The **bias–variance decomposition** of the expected prediction error expresses this formally. For a true function $y = f(x) + \epsilon$ with $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^{2}$, the expected squared prediction error at a point $x$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^{2}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{Systematic error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right]}_{\text{Sensitivity to data}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}}_{\text{Irreducible noise}}$$

Regularization works by **trading a small increase in bias for a large reduction in variance**, achieving a lower total generalization error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Regularization is **not** the same as optimization. Optimization minimizes the training objective. Regularization is a separate design choice that controls *what* the optimizer is allowed to fit. A perfectly optimized unregularized objective is *not* the same as a well-regularized model.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Regularization Techniques (Module 1 Scope)

| Category | Specific Technique | Penalty / Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameter Norm Penalty** | L2 Regularization (Ridge / Weight Decay) | $\Omega(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}$ |
| **Parameter Norm Penalty** | L1 Regularization (Lasso) | $\Omega(\theta) = \|\theta\|_{1}$ |
| **Hybrid Norm Penalty** | Elastic Net | $\Omega(\theta) = \alpha\|\theta\|_{1} + (1-\alpha)\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}$ |
| **Implicit Regularization** | Early Stopping | Halts training before variance grows |
| **Bayesian Justification** | MAP Estimation | Penalty = negative log-prior |

The remainder of these notes focuses exhaustively on **L1 and L2 parameter-norm penalties**, their derivations, and their KTU-relevant exam problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 L2 Regularization (Ridge / Weight Decay)

### 2.1.1 Definition

L2 regularization adds the **squared Euclidean (L2) norm** of the parameter vector as a penalty. For a network with weights $W$ (we conventionally penalize only weights, not biases), the regularized loss is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{L2}}(W) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(W) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|W\|_{2}^{2} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(W) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{i,j} W_{i,j}^{2}$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is purely a mathematical convenience — it cancels the exponent "2" when the gradient is taken.

### 2.1.2 Why Does It Work? — The "Why" and the "How"

**The "Why":** A network with very large weights is extremely sensitive to small changes in the input. Multiplying a noisy input feature by a weight of magnitude 1000 amplifies noise by a factor of 1000, causing wild output swings on test data. L2 forces weights to remain small, making the network **smoother and more robust**.

**The "How":** Optimization now has two competing forces:
1. The **data term** pulls the weights toward values that fit the training points.
2. The **penalty term** pulls the weights toward the origin.

The equilibrium produces weights that are *just large enough* to fit the signal but *too small* to fit the noise.

### 2.1.3 Gradient Update Rule (Gradient Descent with Weight Decay)

Taking the gradient of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{L2}}$ with respect to $W$:

$$\nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}_{\text{L2}} = \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda W$$

Performing one step of vanilla gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$:

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}_{\text{L2}}$$

$$\boxed{\,W \leftarrow (1 - \eta\lambda)W - \eta \nabla_{W} \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}\,}$$

The term $(1 - \eta\lambda)$ is the **weight-decay factor**. It multiplies the weight by a number slightly less than 1 *before* the gradient step, hence the name **"weight decay."** This is why L2 regularization is also called **weight decay** in deep-learning literature.

## 2.2 L1 Regularization (Lasso)

### 2.2.1 Definition

L1 regularization adds the **L1 norm** (sum of absolute values) of the weights as a penalty:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{L1}}(W) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(W) + \lambda \|W\|_{1} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(W) + \lambda \sum_{i,j} \vert W_{i,j} \vert$$

### 2.2.2 Why Does It Work? — Sparsity

Unlike L2 which shrinks weights smoothly toward zero, L1 drives many weights to **exactly zero**, producing a **sparse** model. The reason is geometric: the L1 constraint region (a diamond in 2D) has sharp corners that lie on the axes, so the elliptical contours of the data-loss function are most likely to first touch the constraint region at these corners, which correspond to zero weight values.

### 2.2.3 Sub-Gradient Update Rule

The L1 norm is **not differentiable** at $W_{i,j} = 0$. We must use the **sub-gradient**:

$$\frac{\partial \|W\|_{1}}{\partial W_{i,j}} = \text{sign}(W_{i,j})$$

where $\text{sign}(x)$ is the sign function (+1, -1, or undefined at 0; any value in $[-1, 1]$ is a valid sub-gradient at 0). The update rule becomes:

$$W_{i,j} \leftarrow W_{i,j} - \eta\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}}{\partial W_{i,j}} + \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(W_{i,j})\right)$$

Equivalently, this is the **soft-thresholding** operation:

$$W_{i,j} \leftarrow \text{sign}\!\left(W_{i,j} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}}{\partial W_{i,j}}\right) \max\!\left(0, \left\vert W_{i,j} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}}{\partial W_{i,j}} \right\vert - \eta\lambda\right)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ERM Objective | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ERM}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f_{\theta}(x^{(i)}),y^{(i)})$ | Baseline (no regularization) |
| 2 | General Regularized Objective | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$ | All regularizers fit this form |
| 3 | L2 Penalty | $\Omega_{2}(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\|\theta\|_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\theta_{i}^{2}$ | Differentiable everywhere |
| 4 | L2 Gradient | $\nabla_{\theta}\Omega_{2} = \theta$ | Linear in $\theta$ |
| 5 | L1 Penalty | $\Omega_{1}(\theta) = \|\theta\|_{1} = \sum_{i}\vert \theta_{i} \vert$ | Not differentiable at $\theta=0$ |
| 6 | L1 Sub-Gradient | $\nabla_{\theta}\Omega_{1} = \text{sign}(\theta)$ | Produces sparsity |
| 7 | Weight Decay Update | $W \leftarrow (1 - \eta\lambda)W - \eta\nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}$ | Equivalent to L2 for SGD |
| 8 | Bias–Variance Decomposition | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ | Decomposes expected test error |
| 9 | L2 Closed-Form (Linear Reg.) | $W^{*} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}y$ | Solves normal equations |
| 10 | Bayesian Prior Mapping | L2 $\leftrightarrow$ Gaussian prior, L1 $\leftrightarrow$ Laplace prior | Justifies regularization via MAP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive the update rule for L2 regularization," always write the *gradient* first, then the *update*. KTU valuation keys explicitly check for the appearance of the term $\lambda W$ in the gradient and the factor $(1-\eta\lambda)$ in the update.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering practice, regularization is the **single most important knob** for making a deep-learning model deployable. Specific applications include:

- **Computer Vision (CNNs):** Weight decay is the default in virtually every modern architecture (ResNet, EfficientNet, ViT) trained on ImageNet to keep features transferable.
- **Natural Language Processing (LLMs):** L2 regularization is used during pre-training of large transformers to prevent weight explosion and to support stable scaling laws.
- **Medical Imaging & Bioinformatics:** L1 regularization is heavily used because the number of features (e.g., gene expressions) is much larger than the number of samples, and **interpretability demands sparse models** that select only the most relevant biomarkers.
- **Signal Processing & Control:** L2-regularized least squares (Tikhonov regularization) is the standard technique for solving ill-posed inverse problems such as image deblurring and seismic inversion.
- **Autonomous Systems:** Regularization in the policy network of a reinforcement-learning agent is critical to prevent the agent from overfitting to a simulator's quirks and failing in the real world (the **sim-to-real gap**).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — L2 Regularized Gradient for a Single Linear Neuron

**Problem Setup.** Consider a single-output linear model $f(x) = w^{T}x$ with the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss on $N$ training samples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$. The L2-regularized cost is:

$$J(w) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(w^{T}x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^{2}}_{\text{MSE data term}} + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|_{2}^{2}$$

**Step 1.** Expand the squared error inside the sum and define the design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ (rows are samples) and target vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$.

The data term can be written compactly as:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) = \frac{1}{N}\|Xw - y\|_{2}^{2} = \frac{1}{N}(Xw - y)^{T}(Xw - y)$$

**Step 2.** Take the gradient of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}$ with respect to $w$. Using the matrix identity $\nabla_{w}(Aw - b)^{T}(Aw - b) = 2A^{T}(Aw - b)$:

$$\nabla_{w}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} = \frac{2}{N}X^{T}(Xw - y)$$

**Step 3.** Take the gradient of the L2 penalty:

$$\nabla_{w}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|_{2}^{2}\right) = \frac{\lambda}{2}\nabla_{w}(w^{T}w) = \frac{\lambda}{2}(2w) = \lambda w$$

**Step 4.** Combine the two gradients to get the full gradient of $J(w)$:

$$\nabla_{w}J(w) = \frac{2}{N}X^{T}(Xw - y) + \lambda w$$

**Step 5.** Set the gradient to zero to find the **closed-form optimum** $w^{*}$:

$$\frac{2}{N}X^{T}(Xw^{*} - y) + \lambda w^{*} = 0$$

**Step 6.** Distribute and group the $w^{*}$ terms:

$$\frac{2}{N}X^{T}Xw^{*} - \frac{2}{N}X^{T}y + \lambda w^{*} = 0$$

$$\left(\frac{2}{N}X^{T}X + \lambda I\right)w^{*} = \frac{2}{N}X^{T}y$$

**Step 7.** Multiply both sides by $\frac{N}{2}$:

$$\left(X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I\right)w^{*} = X^{T}y$$

**Step 8.** Invert the matrix to obtain the closed-form solution:

$$\boxed{\,w^{*} = \left(X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I\right)^{-1}X^{T}y\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The $\lambda I$ term added to $X^{T}X$ guarantees that the matrix is **invertible** even when $X^{T}X$ is singular (which happens when $N < d$, i.e., fewer samples than features). This is the algebraic heart of why L2 regularization solves ill-posed problems.

## 3.2 Derivation B — L1 Induces Sparsity: A 1D Geometric Argument

**Problem Setup.** Consider the constrained form of L1 regularization in one dimension. We minimize the data term subject to an L1 budget $\|w\|_{1} \le s$:

$$\min_{w} \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \vert w \vert \le s$$

For a quadratic data loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) = (w - w_{0})^{2}$ (which is the local Taylor expansion of any smooth loss around its unconstrained minimum $w_{0}$), the unconstrained minimum is $w_{0}$.

**Step 1.** The constraint region in 1D is the closed interval $[-s, s]$.

**Step 2.** The loss is a parabola with minimum at $w_{0}$.

**Step 3.** **Case 1 —** If $\vert w_{0} \vert \le s$, the unconstrained minimum lies *inside* the feasible region, and the constrained optimum is simply $w^{*} = w_{0}$. No shrinkage occurs.

**Step 4.** **Case 2 —** If $\vert w_{0} \vert > s$, the unconstrained minimum lies *outside* the feasible region. The constrained optimum is the point in $[-s, s]$ closest to $w_{0}$, which is the boundary:

$$w^{*} = \text{sign}(w_{0}) \cdot s$$

**Step 5.** Combining both cases using $\text{soft}(w_{0}, s) = \text{sign}(w_{0})\max(\vert w_{0} \vert - s, 0)$:

$$\boxed{\,w^{*} = \text{soft}(w_{0}, s) = \text{sign}(w_{0})\max\!\left(\vert w_{0} \vert - s,\ 0\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If $\vert w_{0} \vert$ is smaller than the threshold $s$, the weight is set to **exactly zero**. This is the geometric origin of L1's **sparsity-inducing** property. Repeating the same 1D argument along each coordinate of a multi-dimensional weight vector proves that L1 produces a *sparse* solution.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Equivalence of L2 and MAP Estimation with a Gaussian Prior

**Problem Setup.** We seek the parameters $\theta$ that are **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** most probable given the data $\mathcal{D}$:

$$\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{\theta} p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{\theta}\ p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$$

We assume:
- **Likelihood:** $p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} p(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}, \theta)$ with a Gaussian noise model: $y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}, \theta \sim \mathcal{N}(w^{T}x^{(i)}, \sigma^{2})$.
- **Prior:** $\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^{2} I)$ (an isotropic Gaussian prior on the weights).

**Step 1.** Take the negative logarithm of the posterior (since $\log$ is monotonic, maximizing $p$ is equivalent to minimizing $-\log p$):

$$\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_{\theta}\ \Big[-\log p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) - \log p(\theta)\Big]$$

**Step 2.** Expand the negative log-likelihood. With $p(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}, \theta) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)})^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$:

$$-\log p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \text{const}$$

This is the **sum of squared errors** — equivalent to the MSE data term up to a positive constant.

**Step 3.** Expand the negative log-prior:

$$p(\theta) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\tau^{2})^{d/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}\right)$$

$$-\log p(\theta) = \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}\|\theta\|_{2}^{2} + \text{const}$$

This is **exactly the L2 penalty** (up to a positive constant).

**Step 4.** Combine the two terms:

$$\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_{\theta}\ \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}$$

**Step 5.** Multiply the entire objective by $2\sigma^{2}$ (which does not change the argmin) and identify $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\tau^{2}}$$

The objective becomes:

$$\boxed{\,\theta_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_{\theta}\ \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \lambda\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Result:** Minimizing the **L2-regularized** loss is *mathematically identical* to **MAP estimation under a Gaussian prior** on the weights. The regularization strength $\lambda$ is the ratio of the noise variance $\sigma^{2}$ to the prior variance $\tau^{2}$. Larger $\lambda \iff$ smaller prior variance $\iff$ a stronger belief that the weights are near zero.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Bias–Variance Decomposition of the L2-Regularized Estimator

**Problem Setup.** For a fixed true parameter $w^{*}$, consider the L2-regularized least-squares estimator (with $N$ samples) trained on data drawn from $y = w^{*T}x + \epsilon$, $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$. We want to show how $\lambda$ affects the bias and variance of the estimator.

**Step 1.** The closed-form estimator from Derivation A is:

$$\hat{w}_{\lambda} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}y$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $y = Xw^{*} + \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ is the noise vector):

$$\hat{w}_{\lambda} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}(Xw^{*} + \epsilon)$$

**Step 3.** Split into the signal part and the noise part:

$$\hat{w}_{\lambda} = \underbrace{(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}X w^{*}}_{\text{Signal-dependent (biased)}} + \underbrace{(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}\epsilon}_{\text{Noise-dependent (random)}}$$

**Step 4.** The **bias** of the estimator, taking expectation over the data (and treating $X$ as fixed for simplicity), is:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{w}_{\lambda}) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{w}_{\lambda}] - w^{*} = \left[(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}X - I\right]w^{*}$$

**Step 5.** The matrix $A = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}X$ satisfies $A = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}(X^{T}X + \lambda I - \lambda I) = I - \lambda(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}$.

Therefore:

$$\text{Bias}(\hat{w}_{\lambda}) = -\lambda(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}w^{*}$$

The bias is **proportional to $\lambda$**: as $\lambda \to 0$, the bias vanishes; as $\lambda \to \infty$, the estimator shrinks toward zero, maximizing the bias.

**Step 6.** The **variance** of the estimator (treating $X$ as fixed) is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{w}_{\lambda}) = \sigma^{2}(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}X(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}$$

This is a **decreasing function of $\lambda$** — larger $\lambda$ shrinks the variance. The expected test error can be written as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\|\hat{w}_{\lambda} - w^{*}\|_{X^{T}X}^{2}\right] = \underbrace{\lambda^{2} w^{*T}(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-2}X^{T}X\ w^{*}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}\text{ contribution}} + \underbrace{\sigma^{2}\text{tr}\!\left[(X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^{T}X\right]}_{\text{Variance contribution}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The bias contribution is a monotonically *increasing* function of $\lambda$, while the variance contribution is a monotonically *decreasing* function of $\lambda$. The optimal $\lambda$ minimizes the sum — the formal proof of why the bias–variance tradeoff exists and why tuning $\lambda$ is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Where Regularization Sits in the Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData["Raw Dataset D"] --> split["Train / Validation / Test Split"]
    split --> trainSet["Training Set"]
    split --> valSet["Validation Set"]
    trainSet --> modelDef["Model Definition f_theta"]
    modelDef --> dataLoss["Compute Data Loss L_data"]
    dataLoss --> regPenalty["Compute Regularization Penalty lambda * Omega"]
    regPenalty --> totalLoss["Total Loss L_reg = L_data + lambda * Omega"]
    totalLoss --> optimizer["Optimizer Gradient Descent / Adam"]
    optimizer --> update["Update Weights W <-- W - eta * grad L_reg"]
    update --> earlyStop{"Validation Loss Improving?"}
    earlyStop -- Yes --> update
    earlyStop -- No --> stopTrain["Early Stop / Save Best Model"]
    stopTrain --> eval["Evaluate on Test Set"]
    valSet --> earlyStop
    eval --> finalModel["Final Regularized Model"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the parallel branches for *data loss* and *regularization penalty*. Both feed the total loss, and both are differentiated together during backpropagation. The validation set is **not** used for training — it monitors generalization to trigger early stopping.

## 4.2 Bias–Variance Tradeoff Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Underfit["Low Lambda - Underfitting"]
        u1["High Bias"] --> u2["Low Variance"] --> u3["Smooth Decision Boundary"]
    end
    subgraph Sweet["Optimal Lambda"]
        s1["Balanced Bias"] --> s2["Balanced Variance"] --> s3["Generalizes Well"]
    end
    subgraph Overfit["High Lambda - Over-Regularized"]
        o1["Low Bias"] --> o2["Low Variance"] --> o3["Underfit / High Bias"]
    end
    Underfit -->|Increase lambda| Sweet
    Sweet -->|Increase lambda| Overfit
    Sweet -->|Decrease lambda| Underfit
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** As $\lambda$ increases, the model is forced to be simpler. Initially, the variance drops faster than the bias grows, so total error decreases. Past the sweet spot, further increases in $\lambda$ make the model too constrained, the bias dominates, and total error rises again. The optimal $\lambda$ is found by cross-validation.

## 4.3 L1 vs L2 — Geometric Constraint Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph L2Region["L2 Constraint Region"]
        l2["Circular Boundary radius sqrt lambda. Smooth shrinkage toward zero. No exact zeros."]
    end
    subgraph L1Region["L1 Constraint Region"]
        l1["Diamond Boundary with sharp corners on axes. Corners lie on weight axes. Intersection promotes exact zeros."]
    end
    subgraph Loss["Loss Contours"]
        contour["Elliptical data-loss contours centered on unconstrained minimum w0"]
    end
    Loss --> L2Region
    Loss --> L1Region
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The data-loss contours are ellipses (centered on the unconstrained minimum). The constraint region is the set of allowable weights. The constrained optimum is the first point where an expanding ellipse touches the boundary. For L1, this first contact almost always happens at a corner of the diamond — i.e., where one or more weight components are *exactly zero*. For L2, the first contact is a smooth tangent point — weights are shrunk but generally nonzero.

## 4.4 Regularization as a Bayesian Update — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prior["Prior Belief p theta - Gaussian for L2, Laplace for L1"] --> bayes["Bayes Theorem"]
    likelihood["Likelihood p D given theta - from data"] --> bayes
    bayes --> posterior["Posterior p theta given D"]
    posterior --> map["MAP Estimate = Regularized MLE"]
    map --> prediction["Predictive Distribution"]
    l2map["L2 Regularization = MAP with Gaussian prior"] -.-> map
    l1map["L1 Regularization = MAP with Laplace prior"] -.-> map
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every parameter-norm regularizer corresponds to a specific choice of prior. L2 corresponds to a Gaussian prior (preference for small weights). L1 corresponds to a Laplace prior (preference for weights clustered exactly at zero). Selecting a regularizer is equivalent to encoding prior knowledge about the weights.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

**Q1.** Define **regularization** in the context of deep learning. Why is it necessary, and how does it relate to the bias–variance tradeoff?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Regularization is a set of techniques that modify a learning algorithm to **reduce its generalization error without affecting the training error**. It is necessary because deep neural networks, being highly over-parameterized, can easily overfit the training data — achieving near-zero training loss while performing poorly on unseen data.

The **bias–variance decomposition** expresses the expected test error as:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(y - \hat{f}(x)\right)^{2}\right] = \text{Bias}^{2}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] + \text{Variance}\left[\hat{f}(x)\right] + \sigma^{2}$$

Regularization introduces a small amount of **bias** to substantially **reduce the variance**, which lowers the total generalization error. Common forms include L1 and L2 parameter-norm penalties added to the loss with strength $\lambda$. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

**Q2.** Distinguish between **L1** and **L2** regularization in terms of their penalty forms, the type of solution they produce, and their corresponding Bayesian priors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | L1 (Lasso) | L2 (Ridge / Weight Decay) |
|---|---|---|
| Penalty | $\lambda \sum_{i} \vert \theta_{i} \vert$ | $\frac{\lambda}{2}\sum_{i}\theta_{i}^{2}$ |
| Solution Type | **Sparse** (many exact zeros) | **Dense but small** (all weights shrink) |
| Differentiability | Not differentiable at 0 (uses sub-gradient) | Differentiable everywhere |
| Bayesian Prior | Laplace prior $p(\theta) \propto \exp(-\vert\theta\vert/b)$ | Gaussian prior $p(\theta) \propto \exp(-\theta^{2}/2\tau^{2})$ |
| Geometric Shape | Diamond constraint region | Circular constraint region |
| Use Case | Feature selection, high-dim sparse problems | General-purpose weight decay in deep nets |

(3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. The student is given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, both drawn from the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [Module 1, KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q(a) [7 marks].** Derive the closed-form solution for the **weights of linear regression with L2 regularization**. Show explicitly how the regularization term guarantees invertibility of the normal equations.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Given $N$ training samples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the L2-regularized MSE cost is:

$$J(w) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(w^{T}x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|w\|_{2}^{2}$$

Step 1 — Compact form using $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$ and $y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$:

$$J(w) = \frac{1}{N}(Xw - y)^{T}(Xw - y) + \frac{\lambda}{2}w^{T}w$$

Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. $w$:

$$\nabla_{w} J = \frac{2}{N}X^{T}(Xw - y) + \lambda w = 0$$

Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $\frac{N}{2}$:

$$X^{T}Xw - X^{T}y + \frac{\lambda N}{2}w = 0$$

Step 4 — Group the $w$ terms and factor:

$$\left(X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I\right)w = X^{T}y$$

Step 5 — Invert to obtain the closed-form solution:

$$\boxed{\,w^{*} = \left(X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I\right)^{-1}X^{T}y\,}$$

**Invertibility argument (1 mark):** The eigenvalues of $X^{T}X$ are non-negative real numbers $\sigma_{1}^{2}, \sigma_{2}^{2}, \ldots, \sigma_{d}^{2}$. Adding $\frac{\lambda N}{2}I$ shifts every eigenvalue to $\sigma_{i}^{2} + \frac{\lambda N}{2}$. Since $\lambda \ge 0$ and $N > 0$, all eigenvalues are strictly positive. Therefore the matrix $X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I$ is symmetric positive definite, hence **invertible**, even when $X^{T}X$ is singular (e.g., when $N < d$). This is precisely why L2 regularization is the standard remedy for ill-posed regression.

---

**Q(b) [7 marks].** Show that **L2-regularized least squares is mathematically equivalent to Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation under a Gaussian prior on the weights**. Identify the role of the regularization coefficient $\lambda$ in this Bayesian interpretation.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Posterior formulation (1 mark).** By Bayes' theorem, the posterior over the weights given the data is:

$$p(w \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p(\mathcal{D} \mid w)\,p(w)$$

The MAP estimate is $\hat{w}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{w} \log p(w \mid \mathcal{D}) = \arg\min_{w}\left[-\log p(\mathcal{D} \mid w) - \log p(w)\right]$.

**Step 2 — Likelihood (Gaussian noise model) (2 marks).** Assume $y^{(i)} = w^{T}x^{(i)} + \epsilon^{(i)}$ with $\epsilon^{(i)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$. Then:

$$p(\mathcal{D} \mid w) = \prod_{i=1}^{N}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^{2}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)})^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

$$-\log p(\mathcal{D} \mid w) = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \text{const}$$

**Step 3 — Prior (Gaussian on weights) (2 marks).** Assume $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \tau^{2}I)$:

$$p(w) = \frac{1}{(2\pi\tau^{2})^{d/2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\|w\|_{2}^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}\right)$$

$$-\log p(w) = \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}\|w\|_{2}^{2} + \text{const}$$

**Step 4 — Combine and identify $\lambda$ (2 marks).** Adding the two log terms:

$$\hat{w}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_{w}\ \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \frac{1}{2\tau^{2}}\|w\|_{2}^{2}$$

Multiplying the objective by $2\sigma^{2}$ (a positive constant) and defining $\lambda = \sigma^{2}/\tau^{2}$:

$$\hat{w}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\min_{w}\ \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y^{(i)} - w^{T}x^{(i)}\right)^{2} + \lambda\|w\|_{2}^{2}$$

This is **exactly** the L2-regularized sum-of-squared-errors objective. The coefficient $\lambda = \sigma^{2}/\tau^{2}$ controls the *ratio* of assumed noise variance to prior variance — a larger $\lambda$ corresponds to a more confident prior that the weights lie close to zero.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Module 1, KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q(a) [7 marks].** Explain the **L1 regularization** mechanism. Using a 1D geometric argument, show rigorously that L1 produces **sparse** solutions, while L2 produces only **small but nonzero** solutions.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — L1 objective (1 mark).** The L1-regularized loss is:

$$J(w) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) + \lambda \sum_{i} \vert w_{i} \vert$$

Equivalently, in **constrained form**:

$$\min_{w}\ \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) \quad \text{subject to}\quad \|w\|_{1} \le s$$

where $s$ is monotonically decreasing in $\lambda$ (larger $\lambda$ means tighter budget $s$).

**Step 2 — Local quadratic approximation (2 marks).** For a smooth loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}$ with unconstrained minimum $w_{0}$, the Taylor expansion around $w_{0}$ is $\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) \approx (w - w_{0})^{T}H(w - w_{0}) + \text{const}$, where $H$ is the Hessian. To isolate the effect of the penalty, consider the simplified 1D case $\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(w) \approx (w - w_{0})^{2}$ (this corresponds to a coordinate-aligned Hessian).

**Step 3 — L1 analysis (2 marks).** The 1D L1 problem $\min_{w} (w - w_{0})^{2}$ subject to $\vert w \vert \le s$ has the closed-form **soft-thresholding** solution:

$$w^{*} = \text{sign}(w_{0})\max(\vert w_{0} \vert - s,\ 0)$$

If $\vert w_{0} \vert \le s$, then $w^{*} = 0$ — **exact sparsity**. Geometrically: the L1 feasible region is the interval $[-s, s]$; the parabola has minimum at $w_{0}$; if $w_{0}$ is inside the region, the constraint is inactive and the solution is $w_{0}$ (which may be nonzero). If $w_{0}$ is outside, the solution is the boundary point closest to $w_{0}$ — at $\pm s$ — but the soft-thresholding form shows that whenever the data-derived signal $\vert w_{0} \vert$ is too small to overcome the budget, the weight collapses to **exactly zero**.

**Step 4 — L2 analysis (2 marks).** The corresponding L2 problem is $\min_{w} (w - w_{0})^{2}$ subject to $w^{2} \le s^{2}$, i.e., $\vert w \vert \le s$. The constrained solution is:

$$w^{*} = w_{0} \cdot \min\!\left(1,\ \frac{s}{\vert w_{0} \vert}\right)$$

Notice this is **proportional shrinkage** — the weight is scaled down by the factor $\min(1, s/\vert w_{0} \vert)$ but is **never set to exactly zero** (except in the trivial case $w_{0} = 0$). In multi-dimensions, the L2 constraint region is a sphere with a smooth boundary; the loss contour touches this boundary at a smooth tangent point where *every* weight is generally nonzero.

**Conclusion:** L1 = sparse (exact zeros), L2 = dense shrinkage (small but nonzero). The sharp corners of the L1 diamond are the geometric origin of L1's sparsity.

---

**Q(b) [7 marks].** A deep neural network is trained without regularization and exhibits: (i) near-perfect training accuracy, (ii) poor validation accuracy, (iii) very large weight magnitudes. Diagnose the issue, propose two regularization remedies, and derive the **gradient descent update rule** for L2 regularization.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Diagnosis (2 marks).** The symptoms — (i), (ii), (iii) together — are the classic signature of **overfitting (high variance)**. The model has memorized the training data (including noise) but fails to generalize. The large weight magnitudes amplify small input perturbations into large output swings, confirming that the model is using its capacity to fit noise.

**Step 2 — Remedies (2 marks).**
- **Remedy 1: L2 (Weight Decay) Regularization.** Add $\frac{\lambda}{2}\|W\|_{2}^{2}$ to the loss. This penalizes large weights and forces the network to learn smoother functions.
- **Remedy 2: Early Stopping.** Monitor validation loss during training and stop when it begins to rise. This halts optimization before the network overfits.
- (Optional additional remedies: Dropout, Data Augmentation, Reduce Model Capacity — but per Module 1 scope, focus on parameter-norm penalties and early stopping.)

**Step 3 — Derivation of L2 gradient descent update (3 marks).** The L2-regularized cost is:

$$J(W) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}(W) + \frac{\lambda}{2}\|W\|_{2}^{2}$$

Differentiating term by term:

$$\nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} = \text{(data-gradient, computed via backpropagation)}$$

$$\nabla_{W}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{2}\|W\|_{2}^{2}\right) = \lambda W$$

Total gradient:

$$\nabla_{W}J = \nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda W$$

Gradient descent update with learning rate $\eta$:

$$W \leftarrow W - \eta\nabla_{W}J = W - \eta\!\left(\nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda W\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,W \leftarrow (1 - \eta\lambda)\,W - \eta\nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}\,}$$

The factor $(1 - \eta\lambda)$ **decays** the weight multiplicatively at every step, which is why L2 regularization is also called **weight decay**. Typical values: $\eta = 10^{-3}$, $\lambda = 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 1 Regularization)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "regularization" with "optimization" (–1 to –2 marks).** Students often write that regularization *minimizes* the loss. It does not. Regularization *modifies* the loss being minimized. The training error may even *increase* after regularization, but the *test* error decreases — that is the whole point.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in the L2 penalty (–0.5 marks).** Always write $\frac{\lambda}{2}\|W\|_{2}^{2}$, not $\lambda\|W\|_{2}^{2}$. The $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the exponent and yields a clean gradient $\lambda W$ instead of $2\lambda W$.
> 3. **Differentiating the L1 norm naively (–1 mark).** Writing $\nabla \|W\|_{1} = 1$ instead of $\text{sign}(W)$ is a frequent error. L1 is **not** differentiable at 0, and the sub-gradient $\text{sign}$ is the only correct form.
> 4. **Missing the invertibility argument in L2 closed-form derivations (–1 mark).** When asked why L2 works in $N < d$ settings, students must explicitly mention that adding $\lambda I$ makes the matrix positive definite.
> 5. **Calling L2 = L1 or vice versa (–1 mark).** The penalty forms, the sparsity behavior, and the Bayesian priors are different. Confusing them is treated as a fundamental conceptual error in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Writing $\lambda = \eta \lambda$ or similar notational confusions (–0.5 marks).** $\eta$ is the learning rate (optimizer hyperparameter), $\lambda$ is the regularization strength (regularization hyperparameter). Keep them separate.
> 7. **Skipping the geometric argument in L1 sparsity questions (–2 marks).** When a 7-mark question asks "show that L1 produces sparse solutions," a verbal statement is not enough. The KTU key requires the explicit soft-thresholding derivation $w^{*} = \text{sign}(w_0)\max(\vert w_0\vert - s, 0)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Regularization (Module 1, PEEBT746)**

- **Definition:** Regularization = any technique that reduces generalization error *without* reducing training error.
- **General Form:** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{data}} + \lambda \Omega(\theta)$, where $\lambda \ge 0$ is the **regularization strength**.
- **L2 (Ridge / Weight Decay):** Penalty $\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}$; differentiable; gradient is $\lambda\theta$; induces smooth shrinkage; weight-decay update is $W \leftarrow (1 - \eta\lambda)W - \eta\nabla_{W}\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}$.
- **L1 (Lasso):** Penalty $\lambda\|\theta\|_{1}$; sub-gradient is $\text{sign}(\theta)$; produces **sparse** solutions; soft-thresholding rule $w^{*} = \text{sign}(w_0)\max(\vert w_0 \vert - s, 0)$.
- **Closed-Form L2 Solution (Linear Regression):** $w^{*} = \left(X^{T}X + \frac{\lambda N}{2}I\right)^{-1}X^{T}y$ — guaranteed invertible due to the $\lambda I$ term.
- **Bayesian Justification:**
  - L2 ↔ MAP with **Gaussian prior** on weights $p(\theta) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{\theta^{2}}{2\tau^{2}}\right)$.
  - L1 ↔ MAP with **Laplace prior** on weights $p(\theta) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert\theta\vert}{b}\right)$.
  - Regularization coefficient: $\lambda = \sigma^{2}/\tau^{2}$ (L2) — the ratio of noise variance to prior variance.
- **Bias–Variance Decomposition:** $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$. Regularization trades a small bias increase for a large variance decrease.
- **Sparsity Comparison:** L1 → exact zeros (sparse); L2 → small but generally nonzero (dense). Geometric reason: L1 constraint region has sharp corners on the axes.
- **Effect of $\lambda$:** $\lambda = 0$ recovers ordinary least squares; very large $\lambda$ → underfitting (weights $\to 0$).
- **Implementation Note:** Modern frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) implement L2 regularization as **weight decay**, decoupled from the gradient. The two are equivalent only for vanilla SGD.
- **KTU Module 1 Boundaries:** Stick to L1, L2, Elastic Net, Early Stopping, and Bayesian priors. Dropout, BatchNorm, and Data Augmentation are not in scope for Module 1.
- **Exam Readiness:** Be able to (1) state the closed-form L2 solution, (2) derive the gradient update, (3) explain the L1 sparsity result geometrically, (4) map any L-penalty to a Bayesian prior, and (5) discuss the bias–variance tradeoff in words and equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batch Normalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Batch Normalization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Batch Normalization (BN)** is a regularization and acceleration technique introduced by **Sergey Ioffe and Christian Szegedy (2015)** that normalizes the inputs to a layer within a Deep Neural Network for every mini-batch during training, in order to stabilize learning, reduce **Internal Covariate Shift (ICS)**, and permit the use of significantly higher learning rates.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Batch Normalization is classified as a **layer-level normalization scheme** that introduces two *learnable parameters* — a scale parameter $\gamma$ and a shift parameter $\beta$ — which allow the network to recover the representational power of the un-normalized activations if optimal learning requires it.

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Batch Normalization — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Classroom Grading Curve" Analogy**

Imagine a university where every professor uses a wildly different grading scale. Professor A gives 90% average, Professor B gives 50% average, Professor C uses strict bell curves. Students transferring between courses (i.e., between layers of a neural network) are constantly confused — what was an "A" in one class is a "C" in another.

**Batch Normalization is the registrar's standardized grading curve.** At the start of every semester (mini-batch), the registrar looks at all current grades, computes the **average (mean)** and **spread (variance)**, and rescales everything to a common standard. Now, regardless of which professor taught the previous course, every student enters the next course on equal footing.

In deep learning terms:
- **Mean ($\mu_B$)** = the "average activation" in a mini-batch
- **Variance ($\sigma^2_B$)** = the "spread" of activations
- **The normalization step** = forcing activations to a common scale
- **$\gamma$ and $\beta$** = the "professor's right to override the curve" if they know better

## 1.3 The Problem It Solves: Internal Covariate Shift

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Covariate Shift (ICS)** is defined as the change in the distribution of layer inputs caused by updates to the parameters of *previous layers* during training. As earlier layers update their weights, the input distribution seen by later layers constantly shifts, forcing them to continuously re-adapt — drastically slowing convergence.

**Key metrics highlighted in the KTU syllabus:**
- **Mini-batch size $m$** — typically $32, 64, 128$ or $256$
- **Numerical stability constant $\varepsilon$** — usually $10^{-5}$
- **Momentum coefficient** for moving averages — typically $0.9$ or $0.99$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Operational Pipeline

Batch Normalization is not a single operation — it is a **pipeline of four distinct transformations** applied element-wise to activations.

### Stage 1 — Mini-Batch Mean Computation
For a mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}$ of scalar activations (one feature, one batch):

$$\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$$

**Why?** We need a *central tendency* representative of the current batch's distribution.

### Stage 2 — Mini-Batch Variance Computation
$$\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$$

**Why?** The variance quantifies *how spread out* the activations are; without it, normalization is meaningless.

### Stage 3 — Normalization (Zero Mean, Unit Variance)
$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$$

**Why?** Subtracting the mean centers the distribution; dividing by the standard deviation scales it. The $\varepsilon$ term prevents division by zero and improves numerical stability.

> [!NOTE]
> After Stage 3, the activations are guaranteed to have **zero mean** and **unit variance** within the mini-batch — a clean, standardized input for the next layer.

### Stage 4 — Scale and Shift (Learnable Affine Transformation)
$$y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$$

**Why?** Forcing the network to *only* use zero-mean, unit-variance inputs could limit representational power. If the optimal activation distribution is *not* standard, the network should be free to learn it. The parameters $\gamma$ and $\beta$ are learned via backpropagation, exactly like weights and biases.

## 2.2 The Critical Distinction: Training vs. Inference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **During training:** Use the *current mini-batch's* mean $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ and variance $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$.
>
> **During inference:** Use *population statistics* — exponentially weighted moving averages (EWMAs) accumulated during training.

This asymmetry exists because at inference time:
- We may process a **single sample** (batch size = 1), making batch statistics meaningless.
- We require **deterministic, reproducible** outputs.

### Moving Average Updates (computed during training):
$$\mu_{\text{running}} \leftarrow \alpha \cdot \mu_{\text{running}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$$

$$\sigma^2_{\text{running}} \leftarrow \alpha \cdot \sigma^2_{\text{running}} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **momentum** hyperparameter (commonly $0.9$ or $0.1$ depending on framework convention).

## 2.3 Placement in the Network — A Subtle but High-Yield Topic

There are two valid placement strategies in modern architectures:

| Placement Strategy | Formula | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-activation (BN-ReLU)** | $z = Wx + b \;\rightarrow\; \text{BN}(z) \;\rightarrow\; \text{ReLU}(\cdot)$ | ResNet, modern CNNs |
| **Post-activation (ReLU-BN)** | $z = Wx + b \;\rightarrow\; \text{ReLU}(z) \;\rightarrow\; \text{BN}(\cdot)$ | Older architectures |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know that the **bias term $b$ becomes redundant** in the pre-activation placement, since the $\beta$ parameter of BN absorbs and subsumes the role of the bias. Hence $b$ is typically set to zero or omitted entirely.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mini-batch mean | $\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$ | Computed per feature, per batch |
| 2 | Mini-batch variance | $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$ | Unbiased estimator uses $m-1$, but BN uses $m$ |
| 3 | Standard normalized activation | $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$ | $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$ |
| 4 | BN output (learnable affine) | $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$ | $\gamma, \beta$ are trainable |
| 5 | Running mean (inference) | $\mu_{\text{run}} \leftarrow \alpha \mu_{\text{run}} + (1-\alpha)\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ | $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$ |
| 6 | Running variance (inference) | $\sigma^2_{\text{run}} \leftarrow \alpha \sigma^2_{\text{run}} + (1-\alpha)\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$ | Used at test time |
| 7 | Inference normalization | $y = \gamma \cdot \frac{x - \mu_{\text{run}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}} + \beta$ | No batch dependency |
| 8 | Number of extra parameters per BN layer | $2 \cdot d$ | $\gamma_d, \beta_d$ for $d$ features |
| 9 | Equivalent BN fold (for deployment) | $W' = \gamma \cdot W / \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}$ | $b' = \beta - \gamma \cdot \mu_{\text{run}} / \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Batch Normalization is the **backbone of modern computer vision**:
- **ResNet, Inception, MobileNet, EfficientNet** — all use BN extensively.
- Enables training of **very deep networks** (50, 101, 152+ layers) that would otherwise suffer from vanishing/exploding gradients.
- Permits **aggressive learning rates** (e.g., $0.1$ in ResNet vs $0.001$ without BN), reducing training time by an order of magnitude.
- In production, BN layers are typically **folded into the preceding Conv/Linear layer** for inference speed, as shown in formulas 8 and 9 above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Backpropagation Gradients Through BN

This is the most commonly asked **14-mark derivation** in KTU University Examinations. We derive $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i}$, $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \gamma}$, and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta}$ given the upstream gradient $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i}$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Chain

We have the four BN operations acting on each $x_i$ in the mini-batch $\mathcal{B} = \{x_1, \dots, x_m\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\mathcal{B}} &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \\[4pt]
\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} &= \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2 \\[4pt]
\hat{x}_i &= \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \\[4pt]
y_i &= \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta
\end{aligned}
$$

The loss $\mathcal{L}$ is a function of $y_1, \ldots, y_m$.

### Step 2 — Gradients w.r.t. $\gamma$ and $\beta$ (Simplest)

Since $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$, the partial derivatives are immediate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \gamma} &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial \gamma} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \cdot \hat{x}_i \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta} &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \cdot \frac{\partial y_i}{\partial \beta} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Differentiating $y_i$ w.r.t. $\beta$ gives 1, so the gradient is just the sum of upstream gradients. Differentiating w.r.t. $\gamma$ gives $\hat{x}_i$, so each upstream gradient is weighted by the corresponding normalized activation.

### Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. $\hat{x}_i$

From $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \cdot \gamma
$$

**Logic:** A simple scalar multiplication.

### Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$

From $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$, let $s = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$. Then $\frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}} = -\frac{1}{2}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) \cdot s^{-3}$.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \left( -\frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{2(\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon)^{3/2}} \right)
$$

**Logic:** The variance $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$ affects *every* $\hat{x}_i$, so we must sum the chain rule over all $i$.

### Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$

$\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ enters $\hat{x}_i$ in two ways: directly (numerator) and indirectly (through $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$).

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}} \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}
$$

We compute each piece:

- $\dfrac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$
- $\dfrac{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} -2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) = -\dfrac{2}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) = 0$ (since the deviations sum to zero by definition of the mean).

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \right) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i}
$$

### Step 6 — Gradient w.r.t. $x_i$ (The Final Answer)

$x_i$ also enters through $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ and $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}} \cdot \frac{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i}
$$

Computing each piece:

- $\dfrac{\partial \hat{x}_i}{\partial x_i} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$
- $\dfrac{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i} = \dfrac{1}{m}$
- $\dfrac{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}}{\partial x_i} = \dfrac{2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m}$

Substituting everything:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} = \;& \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{x}_i} \\[4pt]
& + \frac{1}{m} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mu_{\mathcal{B}}} \\[4pt]
& + \frac{2(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 3, 4, and 5 back in:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} = \;& \frac{\gamma}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \\[4pt]
& - \frac{\gamma}{m \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j} \\[4pt]
& - \frac{\gamma (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m (\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon)^{3/2}} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j} (x_j - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Logic of the three terms:**
1. **Direct path** — how $x_i$ influences $\hat{x}_i$ directly.
2. **Mean path** — how $x_i$ shifts $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$, affecting *all* $\hat{x}_j$.
3. **Variance path** — how $x_i$ shifts $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$, affecting *all* $\hat{x}_j$ via the denominator.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Conceptual Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **Toy example to build intuition** — let $m = 4$, batch activations $x = [1, 3, 5, 7]$.

**Step 1 — Mean:**
$$
\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1 + 3 + 5 + 7}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Variance:**
$$
\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{(1-4)^2 + (3-4)^2 + (5-4)^2 + (7-4)^2}{4} = \frac{9 + 1 + 1 + 9}{4} = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize (assume $\varepsilon \approx 0$):**
$$
\hat{x} = \frac{[1, 3, 5, 7] - 4}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{[-3, -1, 1, 3]}{\sqrt{5}} \approx [-1.342, -0.447, 0.447, 1.342]
$$

**Step 4 — Scale and shift** (assume $\gamma = 1, \beta = 0$ for simplicity):
$$
y = \hat{x} = [-1.342, -0.447, 0.447, 1.342]
$$

**Verification:** Mean of $y$ is $0$, variance is $1$. This confirms the zero-mean, unit-variance property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If we set $\gamma = \sqrt{5}$ and $\beta = 4$, then $y_i = \sqrt{5} \cdot \hat{x}_i + 4 = x_i$ — the network has **perfectly recovered the original distribution**, demonstrating that BN can represent the identity transformation when needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of Batch Normalization (Training Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Mini-Batch Input x1 to xm"] --> B["Stage 1: Compute Mean muB"]
    A --> C["Stage 2: Compute Variance sigma2B"]
    B --> D["Stage 3: Normalize xHat"]
    C --> D
    A --> D
    D --> E["Stage 4: Scale and Shift yi"]
    F["Learnable Gamma"] --> E
    G["Learnable Beta"] --> E
    E --> H["Output Activations y1 to ym"]
    B --> I["Update Running Mean"]
    C --> J["Update Running Variance"]
    I --> K["Stored Population Statistics for Inference"]
    J --> K
    H --> L["Next Layer or Activation ReLU"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: BN Placement in a CNN Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Feature Map"] --> B["Conv2D Layer"]
    B --> C["Batch Normalization"]
    C --> D["ReLU Activation"]
    D --> E["Output to Next Block"]
    F["Bias b is redundant here"] -.-> C
    G["Gamma and Beta learned per channel"] -.-> C
```

## 4.3 BN Inference Pathway (Using Running Statistics)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Single Test Sample x"] --> B["Retrieve Stored Running Mean muRun"]
    A --> C["Retrieve Stored Running Variance sigma2Run"]
    B --> D["Normalize using muRun and sigma2Run"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Apply Learned Gamma and Beta"]
    E --> F["Deterministic Output y"]
    G["No batch dependency, no stochasticity"] -.-> F
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Training vs. Inference Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAIN["Training Phase"]
        T1["Mini-Batch Activations"] --> T2["Per-Batch muB and sigma2B"]
        T2 --> T3["Normalize and Affine Transform"]
        T3 --> T4["Loss Computation and Backprop"]
        T4 --> T5["Update Gamma, Beta, Weights"]
        T2 --> T6["Update Running muRun and sigma2Run via EWMA"]
    end
    subgraph INFER["Inference Phase"]
        I1["Single Sample or Test Batch"] --> I2["Use Stored muRun and sigma2Run"]
        I2 --> I3["Normalize and Affine Transform"]
        I3 --> I4["Output Prediction"]
    end
    T6 -.-> I2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define Internal Covariate Shift. How does Batch Normalization address it? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Internal Covariate Shift (ICS)** is the phenomenon in deep neural networks where the statistical distribution of inputs to a given layer changes during training as the parameters of preceding layers are updated. This constant distributional shift forces each layer to continuously re-adapt to new input statistics, slowing down convergence and complicating hyperparameter tuning.

Batch Normalization addresses ICS by **explicitly normalizing the inputs** to each layer to have zero mean and unit variance (computed over a mini-batch), and then applying a learnable affine transformation $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$ to preserve representational capacity. This stabilizes the input distribution, allowing higher learning rates and faster convergence.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. Why are learnable parameters $\gamma$ and $\beta$ necessary in Batch Normalization? What happens if both are set to fixed values $\gamma = 1$ and $\beta = 0$? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The parameters $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift) are necessary because **forcing activations to strictly have zero mean and unit variance may limit the network's representational power** — certain layers or activation functions may perform optimally with non-standard distributions. By making $\gamma$ and $\beta$ trainable, the network can learn to **undo the normalization** if it benefits learning.

If $\gamma = 1$ and $\beta = 0$ are fixed, BN is reduced to a pure zero-mean unit-variance normalization. The network **loses the flexibility to recover identity** or shift to a better-suited distribution. In such a case, the network may underfit or require more careful initialization to compensate for the imposed distribution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Derive the complete backpropagation gradients of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to the input $x_i$, the scale parameter $\gamma$, and the shift parameter $\beta$ for a Batch Normalization layer operating on a mini-batch of size $m$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above. The complete derivation yields:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \gamma} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} \hat{x}_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\gamma}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_i} - \frac{\gamma}{m\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j} - \frac{\gamma(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})}{m(\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon)^{3/2}} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j}(x_j - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})
$$

**Mark distribution (KTU standard):**
- Forward pass setup with all 4 equations: **2 marks**
- Gradient w.r.t. $\gamma$ and $\beta$: **1 mark**
- Gradient w.r.t. $\hat{x}_i$ and $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$: **1.5 marks**
- Gradient w.r.t. $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$: **1 mark**
- Final gradient w.r.t. $x_i$ with all 3 terms: **1.5 marks**

**Q. (b)** Explain the training vs. inference behavior of a Batch Normalization layer. Why are running averages used at inference, and how are they updated? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**During Training:**
The BN layer computes the mean $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ and variance $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$ from the *current mini-batch*. The normalized output is $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$ where $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})/\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$. In parallel, exponentially weighted moving averages (EWMAs) of the mean and variance are maintained:

$$
\mu_{\text{run}} \leftarrow \alpha \mu_{\text{run}} + (1 - \alpha) \mu_{\mathcal{B}}
$$

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{run}} \leftarrow \alpha \sigma^2_{\text{run}} + (1 - \alpha) \sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}
$$

**During Inference:**
- A test sample may be processed **individually** (batch size 1), making per-batch statistics unreliable or undefined.
- Outputs must be **deterministic** — the stochasticity of batch statistics must be removed.
- Hence, the *population statistics* ($\mu_{\text{run}}, \sigma^2_{\text{run}}$) accumulated during training are used:

$$
y = \gamma \cdot \frac{x - \mu_{\text{run}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}} + \beta
$$

The momentum $\alpha$ (typically $0.9$ or $0.99$) controls how quickly the running statistics adapt to new batches. A higher $\alpha$ means slower adaptation but smoother estimates.

**Mark distribution:**
- Training pipeline explanation: **2 marks**
- EWMA update equations and role of $\alpha$: **2 marks**
- Inference behavior and rationale: **2 marks**
- Final inference formula: **1 mark**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Discuss the four operational stages of Batch Normalization in detail. Show mathematically that if $\gamma = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$ and $\beta = \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$, then the BN layer recovers the identity mapping. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Four Stages** (refer Section 2.1 for full detail):

1. **Mean computation:** $\mu_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i$
2. **Variance computation:** $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})^2$
3. **Normalization:** $\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}}$
4. **Affine transformation:** $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$

**Identity Recovery Proof:**

Substituting Stage 3 into Stage 4:

$$
y_i = \gamma \cdot \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} + \beta
$$

Now set $\gamma = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$ and $\beta = \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$:

$$
y_i = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon} \cdot \frac{x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}}{\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}} + \mu_{\mathcal{B}}
$$

$$
y_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}}) + \mu_{\mathcal{B}} = x_i
$$

**Therefore, $y_i = x_i$ for all $i$, confirming that BN can represent the identity function.** This is a critical property: if normalization is unhelpful for a particular layer, the network can *learn* to disable it through gradient descent on $\gamma$ and $\beta$.

**Mark distribution:**
- All four stages with formulas: **3 marks**
- Substitution and simplification: **3 marks**
- Final conclusion ($y_i = x_i$): **1 mark**

**Q. (b)** Explain the placement of Batch Normalization in a deep network. Compare pre-activation vs. post-activation placement and discuss the redundancy of the bias term. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pre-activation (BN → Activation):**
Used in modern architectures like ResNet. The pipeline is:
$$
z = Wx + b \;\rightarrow\; \text{BN}(z) \;\rightarrow\; \text{ReLU}(\text{BN}(z))
$$
Since BN's $\beta$ parameter acts as a learnable shift, the bias $b$ becomes **redundant** and is typically set to zero or omitted. Removing $b$ reduces parameters and avoids the bias being "washed away" by subsequent normalization.

**Post-activation (Activation → BN):**
Used in older architectures. The pipeline is:
$$
z = Wx + b \;\rightarrow\; \text{ReLU}(z) \;\rightarrow\; \text{BN}(\text{ReLU}(z))
$$
Here the bias $b$ is meaningful because the activation output is non-negative (for ReLU), and BN normalizes the activated values. This is mathematically more interpretable but empirically less effective in very deep networks.

**Why BN Enables Higher Learning Rates:**
Without BN, large learning rates cause activations to grow unboundedly (especially with saturating activations like sigmoid/tanh), leading to gradient explosion. BN clamps the activation scale, allowing learning rates like $0.1$ (ResNet) versus $0.001$ in un-normalized networks.

**Why BN Acts as a Regularizer:**
The mini-batch statistics introduce slight noise (since each batch is a *sample* of the full dataset), which acts as an implicit regularizer similar to dropout. This often eliminates the need for dropout in BN-equipped networks.

**Mark distribution:**
- Pre-activation placement + redundancy of bias: **3 marks**
- Post-activation placement and trade-offs: **2 marks**
- Higher learning rate and regularization effects: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$ term** in the denominator during normalization — this is a recurring deduction.
> 2. **Confusing training and inference formulas** — at inference, you *must* use $\mu_{\text{run}}$ and $\sigma^2_{\text{running}}$, **never** per-batch statistics.
> 3. **Omitting the three-term structure** in $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x_i$ — students often give only the "direct path" term, missing the mean path and variance path. Each missing term costs ~1 mark.
> 4. **Stating "BN removes internal covariate shift"** without defining what ICS is. Examiners expect the *definition* before the *solution*.
> 5. **Mixing up momentum conventions** — some frameworks (PyTorch) use $\alpha$ such that $\text{new} = (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{batch} + \alpha \cdot \text{old}$, while others (TensorFlow) invert it. Mention the convention explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing "BN reduces the need for dropout"** with "BN replaces dropout" — they serve overlapping but distinct roles.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Batch Normalization**

- **Full form & origin:** Batch Normalization, Ioffe & Szegedy (2015), paper titled *"Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift."*
- **Core purpose:** Stabilize layer input distributions → enable higher learning rates → faster convergence.
- **Four stages:** (1) Compute $\mu_{\mathcal{B}}$, (2) Compute $\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}}$, (3) Normalize via $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu_{\mathcal{B}})/\sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$, (4) Affine transform $y_i = \gamma \hat{x}_i + \beta$.
- **Two trainable parameters:** $\gamma$ (scale) and $\beta$ (shift) — learned via backprop.
- **Identity recovery:** Setting $\gamma = \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\mathcal{B}} + \varepsilon}$ and $\beta = \mu_{\mathcal{B}}$ yields $y_i = x_i$.
- **Bias redundancy:** In pre-activation placement, the bias $b$ before BN is redundant and typically removed.
- **Training vs. inference asymmetry:** Training uses mini-batch statistics; inference uses exponentially weighted moving averages of those statistics.
- **Hyperparameters to memorize:** $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-5}$, momentum $\alpha \in \{0.9, 0.99\}$, mini-batch size $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$.
- **Regularization side-effect:** Mini-batch noise acts as implicit regularization; reduces (but does not eliminate) the need for dropout.
- **Architectures that use BN heavily:** ResNet, Inception, MobileNet, EfficientNet, VGG (with modifications).
- **Inference optimization:** BN can be *folded* into the preceding Conv/Linear layer using $W' = \gamma W / \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}$ and $b' = \beta - \gamma \mu_{\text{run}} / \sqrt{\sigma^2_{\text{run}} + \varepsilon}$, eliminating runtime cost.
- **Differentiator from Layer Norm / Group Norm:** BN normalizes across the batch dimension (per feature), Layer Norm normalizes across the feature dimension (per sample), and Group Norm is a hybrid — relevant for transformers and small-batch scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dataset Augmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
