```text
import theano.tensor as T

X = T.dmatrix('X')
y = T.dvector('y')
W = theano.shared(value=np.zeros((d, 1)), name='W')
b = theano.shared(value=0.0, name='b')

y_hat = T.dot(X, W) + b
cost  = T.mean((y - y_hat.flatten()) ** 2)

gW = T.grad(cost, W)   # symbolic gradient wrt W
gb = T.grad(cost, b)   # symbolic gradient wrt b
```

The `T.grad(cost, W)` call internally builds a **reverse-mode autodiff graph** that evaluates to exactly the expression derived in Step 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification with KTU marking scheme:** Full marks require (a) setting up the cost, (b) writing the gradient by hand, and (c) showing the corresponding Theano symbolic statements.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Logistic Regression Cost & Gradient in Theano

**Problem Statement:**
For a binary classification problem, derive the cross-entropy cost and its gradient for logistic regression, and show how Theano would compute it.

**Setup:**

$$
P(y=1 \mid x; W, b) = \sigma(z), \quad z = W^{\top} x + b
$$

$$
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Likelihood for one sample**

$$
P(y_i \mid x_i; W, b) = \sigma(z_i)^{y_i} \cdot (1 - \sigma(z_i))^{1 - y_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood (sum over N samples)**

$$
\ell(W, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \sigma(z_i) + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \sigma(z_i)) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-Entropy Cost (negative mean log-likelihood)**

$$
L(W, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \ell(W, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log (1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

where $\hat{y}_i = \sigma(z_i)$.

**Step 4 — Gradient derivation**
Using the elegant property of the sigmoid:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = \hat{y}_i - y_i
$$

Therefore, by chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \frac{1}{N} X^{\top} (\hat{y} - y)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)
$$

**Step 5 — Theano Implementation**

```text
X = T.dmatrix('X')
y = T.dvector('y')
W = theano.shared(value=np.zeros(d), name='W')
b = theano.shared(value=0.0, name='b')

z      = T.dot(X, W) + b
y_hat  = T.nnet.sigmoid(z)

cost   = T.mean(T.nnet.binary_crossentropy(y_hat, y))
gW     = T.grad(cost, W)
gb     = T.grad(cost, b)
```

The internal gradient computed by `T.grad` matches our hand-derived expression in Step 4 exactly.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Theano Training Loop (Full SGD Pipeline)

**Problem Statement:**
Construct the complete Theano function for one step of mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent on a linear regression problem.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Update rule from gradient descent**

$$
W \leftarrow W - \eta \cdot g_W
$$

$$
b \leftarrow b - \eta \cdot g_b
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate and $g_W$, $g_b$ are the gradients computed via `T.grad`.

**Step 2 — Symbolic update expression**

```text
eta  = T.scalar('eta')
upd_W = W - eta * gW
upd_b = b - eta * gb
```

**Step 3 — Compile training function with updates parameter**

```text
train = theano.function(
    inputs  = [X, y, eta],
    outputs = cost,
    updates = [(W, upd_W), (b, upd_b)]
)
```

The `updates` parameter is one of Theano's most elegant features — it specifies that every time `train` is called, the shared variables `W` and `b` are *in-place replaced* by the new values.

**Step 4 — Iterative training loop**

```text
for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for batch_X, batch_y in minibatches:
        epoch_cost = train(batch_X, batch_y, learning_rate)
```

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Theano Functions

| Theano Symbol | Meaning | Use Case | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `T.dmatrix`, `T.fmatrix` | Double/Float 2D symbolic tensor | Input features | Very High |
| `T.dvector`, `T.fvector` | 1D symbolic tensor | Labels | Very High |
| `T.scalar` | 0D symbolic tensor | Hyperparameters | High |
| `theano.shared(value)` | Stateful symbolic variable | Weights/biases | Very High |
| `T.dot(A, B)` | Matrix multiplication | Forward pass | Very High |
| `T.nnet.sigmoid(x)` | Sigmoid activation | Output layer | High |
| `T.nnet.softmax(x)` | Softmax activation | Multi-class output | High |
| `T.nnet.binary_crossentropy(o, t)` | Binary cross-entropy loss | Classification | High |
| `T.mean`, `T.sum` | Reduction operations | Aggregation in loss | High |
| `T.grad(cost, wrt)` | Reverse-mode autodiff | Backpropagation | Very High |
| `theano.function([in], [out])` | Compiles symbolic graph | Execution | Very High |
| `updates=[(var, expr)]` | In-place update rule | SGD step | Very High |
| `givens={}` | Substitution at runtime | Variable sharing | Medium |
| `mode=Mode(linker='cvm')` | Low-level linker choice | Optimization | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Theano's Compilation Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Python Source Code with Symbolic Tensors] --> B[Symbolic Graph Construction]
    B --> C[Graph Optimization Passes]
    C --> D1[Constant Folding]
    C --> D2[Dead Code Elimination]
    C --> D3[Kernel Fusion]
    C --> D4[Subexpression Elimination]
    D1 --> E[Optimized Internal Graph IR]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{Target Device}
    F -->|CPU| G1[C C++ Compilation via g++]
    F -->|GPU| G2[CUDA Compilation via NVCC]
    G1 --> H1[Native CPU Binary]
    G2 --> H2[CUDA Kernels]
    H1 --> I[Runtime Execution on Data]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Numerical Output]
```

> **Caption:** The complete Theano pipeline — from symbolic Python code down to compiled native machine code.

---

## 4.2 Theano's Computational Graph (Reverse-Mode Autodiff)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input X symbolic] --> M1[Multiply X * W]
    W[Weight W shared] --> M1
    M1 --> A1[Add bias b]
    B[Bias b shared] --> A1
    A1 --> P1[Prediction y_hat]
    YT[Target y symbolic] --> SUB[Subtract y - y_hat]
    P1 --> SUB
    SUB --> SQ[Square]
    SQ --> MEAN[Mean Reduction]
    MEAN --> COST[Cost L]
    COST --> GRAD[Reverse Autodiff]
    GRAD --> GW[grad W symbolic]
    GRAD --> GB[grad b symbolic]
    GW --> UPD[Update Rule]
    GB --> UPD
    UPD --> W
    UPD --> B
```

> **Caption:** Computational graph of a single linear-regression training step in Theano, showing forward pass (top-down) and backward autodiff (bottom-up arrows).

---

## 4.3 Theano's Memory & Variable Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYM[Symbolic Layer No Data Yet]
        X[Symbolic Tensor T.dmatrix]
        Y[Symbolic Tensor T.dvector]
    end
    subgraph SHA[Shared Layer Stateful In Memory]
        W[Shared Variable theano.shared Weights]
        B[Shared Variable theano.shared Bias]
    end
    subgraph GRP[Computational Graph]
        OP1[Operations T.dot, T.add, T.mean]
        OP2[Gradients T.grad]
        OP3[Updates dict]
    end
    subgraph CMP[Compiled Function]
        F[theano.function]
    end
    SYM --> GRP
    SHA --> GRP
    GRP --> CMP
    CMP --> RUN[Runtime Call with Data]
    SHA -.->|persistent across calls| CMP
```

> **Caption:** The three-tier memory model: symbolic (stateless) → shared (stateful parameters) → compiled function (executable artifact).

---

## 4.4 Theano vs. Modern Frameworks — Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EPOCH1[Era 1 2010-2014]
        T1[Theano Define-and-Run]
    end
    subgraph EPOCH2[Era 2 2015-2017]
        T2[TensorFlow Define-and-Run v1]
        K1[Keras Theano TF Backend]
    end
    subgraph EPOCH3[Era 3 2016-Present]
        T3[PyTorch Define-by-Run]
        T4[TensorFlow 2.x Eager]
    end
    subgraph EPOCH4[Era 4 2018-Present]
        T5[JAX Functional Autodiff]
        T6[PyTensor Theano Fork]
    end
    EPOCH1 --> EPOCH2
    EPOCH2 --> EPOCH3
    EPOCH3 --> EPOCH4
```

> **Caption:** Theano's lineage — every modern framework traces its roots back to the symbolic graph concept Theano pioneered.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Define Theano. List any four of its key features."**

**Model Answer:**

Theano is an open-source Python library and optimizing compiler for defining, optimizing, and evaluating mathematical expressions involving multi-dimensional arrays, particularly used in deep learning research. It was developed at the University of Montreal (MILA) by Yoshua Bengio's research group.

**Four key features:**
1. **Tight integration with NumPy** — `numpy.ndarray` ↔ Theano `tensor` interop.
2. **Transparent GPU acceleration** — the same code runs on CPU or CUDA-enabled GPU.
3. **Automatic symbolic differentiation** — `T.grad(cost, wrt)` computes gradients via reverse-mode autodiff.
4. **Graph optimization** — performs constant folding, kernel fusion, dead code elimination, and subexpression elimination before execution.
5. **Speed and stability optimizations** — uses C/CUDA compilation for production-level performance.
6. **Dynamic C compilation** — entire expressions evaluated with minimal Python overhead.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, name **at least four features** *and* mention it is a *Python library and optimizing compiler*.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Explain the concept of a 'shared variable' in Theano with an example."**

**Model Answer:**

A **shared variable** in Theano is a hybrid symbolic-numeric variable that:
- Behaves like a **symbolic tensor** in graph construction.
- Has a **persistent numerical value** stored in memory (similar to a NumPy array).
- Can be **read** during function execution and **updated in-place** using the `updates` parameter of `theano.function`.

Shared variables are typically used for **model parameters** (weights, biases) that need to retain state across many function calls (e.g., during training).

**Example:**

```text
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
import numpy as np

W = theano.shared(value=np.zeros((3, 1)), name='W')
b = theano.shared(value=0.0, name='b')

X = T.dmatrix('X')
y = T.dvector('y')

y_hat = T.dot(X, W).flatten() + b
cost  = T.mean((y - y_hat) ** 2)
gW, gb = T.grad(cost, [W, b])

train = theano.function(
    inputs=[X, y],
    outputs=cost,
    updates=[(W, W - 0.01 * gW), (b, b - 0.01 * gb)]
)
```

Here, `W` and `b` are shared variables whose values are updated automatically every time `train(...)` is called.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts — typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The internal choice is *between* two full questions (A or B), not between sub-parts.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of Theano with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the symbolic computational graph and automatic differentiation in Theano.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete Theano program to implement a single-layer neural network for binary classification using the sigmoid activation and cross-entropy loss. Show the gradient computation and the training loop.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — [7 marks]

**Theano's architecture** can be understood as a layered compilation stack:

1. **Symbolic Layer (Frontend):**
   - User defines tensors using `theano.tensor` (e.g., `T.dmatrix`, `T.fvector`).
   - These are *symbolic* — they have a name, type, and dimensionality, but no data.
   - Operations (`T.dot`, `T.mean`, `T.nnet.sigmoid`, etc.) build the computational graph.

2. **Optimization Layer (Middle-end):**
   - Multiple optimization passes are applied: constant folding, algebraic simplification, subexpression elimination, kernel fusion, and dead code elimination.
   - This layer is what makes Theano *faster* than naive Python loops.

3. **Code Generation Layer (Backend):**
   - The optimized graph is converted to **C code** (for CPU) using `g++` or **CUDA C** (for GPU) using `nvcc`.
   - Generated code is compiled and cached in `$THEANO_FLAGS['compiledir']`.

4. **Execution Layer:**
   - The compiled `function` is invoked with real numerical data.
   - Shared variables retain state between calls (used for parameters and updates).

**Role of the Symbolic Computational Graph:**

- The graph is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where nodes are operations and edges are data dependencies.
- It enables **global optimization** — unlike imperative code, Theano sees the *entire* computation and can rewrite it before any data arrives.
- It enables **automatic differentiation** because the graph encodes the full chain of dependencies.

**Role of Automatic Differentiation:**

- Theano uses **reverse-mode autodiff** (backpropagation) implemented symbolically.
- For any scalar cost $L$ and any parameter $W$, `T.grad(L, W)` returns a *symbolic expression* for $\partial L / \partial W$.
- The derivative graph is built by traversing the original graph in reverse, applying the chain rule at each node.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual).**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — [7 marks]

**Complete Theano Program:**

```text
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
import numpy as np

# ---------- Step 1: Hyperparameters ----------
lr     = 0.1
n_in   = 4
n_out  = 1
n_epochs = 1000

# ---------- Step 2: Shared Parameters ----------
W = theano.shared(value=np.zeros(n_in), name='W')
b = theano.shared(value=0.0, name='b')

# ---------- Step 3: Symbolic Inputs ----------
X = T.dmatrix('X')
y = T.dvector('y')

# ---------- Step 4: Forward Pass ----------
z     = T.dot(X, W) + b
y_hat = T.nnet.sigmoid(z)

# ---------- Step 5: Cost Function ----------
cost = T.mean(T.nnet.binary_crossentropy(y_hat, y))

# ---------- Step 6: Gradients (Automatic Differentiation) ----------
gW = T.grad(cost, W)
gb = T.grad(cost, b)

# ---------- Step 7: Compile Training Step ----------
train = theano.function(
    inputs   = [X, y],
    outputs  = cost,
    updates  = [(W, W - lr * gW), (b, b - lr * gb)]
)

predict = theano.function(inputs=[X], outputs=y_hat)

# ---------- Step 8: Training Loop ----------
X_train = np.random.randn(100, n_in).astype(theano.config.floatX)
y_train = (np.random.randn(100) > 0).astype(theano.config.floatX)

for epoch in range(n_epochs):
    avg_cost = train(X_train, y_train)
    if epoch % 100 == 0:
        print(f"Epoch {epoch:4d}  Cost = {avg_cost:.4f}")

# ---------- Step 9: Predictions ----------
predictions = predict(X_train)
```

**Explanation of Key Parts (for marks):**
- (2 marks) Symbolic input declaration: `X = T.dmatrix('X')`, `y = T.dvector('y')`.
- (2 marks) Forward pass with sigmoid activation: `y_hat = T.nnet.sigmoid(T.dot(X, W) + b)`.
- (1 mark) Cross-entropy loss.
- (1 mark) Automatic differentiation: `T.grad(cost, W)`, `T.grad(cost, b)`.
- (1 mark) Compile and update rule using `theano.function` with `updates=[(W, W - lr*gW), ...]`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Missing `updates` parameter** — students often forget to actually update `W` and `b` in the training step. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing `T.mean` with `T.sum`** — average cost vs. total cost affects gradient scale. Always verify.
> 3. **Forgetting to use `theano.config.floatX`** — leads to GPU/CPU precision mismatch. Loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing `W = W - lr*gW` outside `updates`** — this re-binds the Python name, not the shared variable. Theano's `updates` parameter is the *only* correct way.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **Theano (Define-and-Run)** with **PyTorch (Define-by-Run)**. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each paradigm with examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how Theano performs **graph optimization**. List and briefly describe any **four optimization techniques** with examples.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — [7 marks]

| Aspect | Theano (Define-and-Run) | PyTorch (Define-by-Run) |
|---|---|---|
| **Graph Construction** | Built *before* execution; static | Built *during* execution; dynamic |
| **Execution Style** | Symbolic | Eager (imperative) |
| **Debugging** | Difficult — graph is opaque | Easy — standard Python debugger works |
| **Optimization Scope** | Global — entire graph visible | Local — only current execution path |
| **Performance** | High for production | High for research iteration |
| **Deployment** | Easy (graph is finalized) | Requires TorchScript tracing |
| **Flexibility** | Static structures hard to vary | Variable-length inputs trivial |
| **Code Example** | `f = theano.function([x], y)` then `f(data)` | `y = model(x)` runs immediately |

**Theano's Define-and-Run paradigm** is ideal for production scenarios where the computation graph is fixed and maximum optimization is desired.

**PyTorch's Define-by-Run paradigm** is ideal for research where models are exploratory and inputs may have variable shapes (e.g., RNNs with variable sequence lengths).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — [7 marks]

Theano applies the following optimization passes to the symbolic graph before compilation:

1. **Constant Folding (1 mark):**
   - Sub-expressions with only constant inputs are evaluated at compile time.
   - Example: `2 * 3 + x` is replaced by `6 + x`, freeing the runtime from a multiplication.

2. **Dead Code Elimination (1 mark):**
   - Subgraphs whose outputs are never used are removed.
   - Example: If `temp = T.exp(x)` is computed but never used, the `exp` node is pruned.

3. **Kernel Fusion (Element-wise Fusion) (2 marks):**
   - Multiple element-wise operations are merged into a single kernel to reduce memory bandwidth.
   - Example: `a + b * c` would normally require two passes (one for `b*c`, one for `+a`). Theano fuses this into a single CUDA kernel.
   - This is one of the biggest sources of Theano's GPU speedup.

4. **Subexpression Elimination (1 mark):**
   - Common sub-expressions are computed once and reused.
   - Example: In `y = x*x + T.exp(x*x)`, the `x*x` sub-expression is computed once and reused.

5. **Algebraic Simplification (1 mark):**
   - Simple identities are applied.
   - Example: `x * 1 = x`, `x + 0 = x`, `x / x = 1` (when safe).

6. **Stabilizing Optimizations (1 mark):**
   - Theano replaces numerically unstable expressions with stable ones.
   - Example: `T.log(T.nnet.softmax(x))` is replaced with the numerically stable `T.logsoftmax(x)`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Theano Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam Hall:**
> 1. **Confusing `T.grad` with numerical gradients** — Theano uses *symbolic* reverse-mode autodiff; do not write the chain rule manually in code.
> 2. **Forgetting to call `theano.function(...)` to compile** — writing expressions alone does not execute them. Theano is a *two-step* system: define, then compile.
> 3. **Misusing `givens`** — `givens` substitutes one variable for another at runtime; it is not a Python-level `=` assignment. Many students confuse the two.
> 4. **Skipping the `updates` parameter during SGD** — without `updates`, parameters will *not* change, and the model will not learn. This is the single most common error in Theano exam answers.
> 5. **Writing `T.grad` for non-scalar outputs** — `T.grad` only works on *scalar* costs. Squeezing or summing the loss is mandatory before calling it.
> 6. **Confusing `T.scalar` and `T.dscalar`** — `T.scalar` defaults to the platform's default float, which can cause GPU/CPU mismatches. Always use `T.fscalar`/`T.dscalar` explicitly.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- 🔑 **Theano = Python library + optimizing compiler for tensor mathematics.**
- 🔑 Developed at **MILA, University of Montreal** (Yoshua Bengio's group).
- 🔑 Uses **Define-and-Run (symbolic) paradigm** — graph is built *first*, executed *later*.
- 🔑 **Three foundational features:** symbolic graphs, automatic differentiation, transparent GPU compilation.
- 🔑 **Symbolic tensor variables** (`T.dmatrix`, `T.fvector`, etc.) are placeholders, not data.
- 🔑 **Shared variables** (`theano.shared(...)`) carry persistent state — used for weights/biases.
- 🔑 **`theano.function([inputs], [outputs])` compiles the graph** — this is the execution trigger.
- 🔑 **`updates=[(var, expr)]` parameter** is how Theano performs in-place parameter updates (SGD step).
- 🔑 **`T.grad(cost, wrt)` performs reverse-mode automatic differentiation** symbolically.
- 🔑 Theano applies **graph optimization passes** (constant folding, dead code elimination, kernel fusion, subexpression elimination) before code generation.
- 🔑 Backend compilation: **C/C++ for CPU, CUDA for GPU**.
- 🔑 Development **ceased in 2017**; succeeded by **TensorFlow, PyTorch, and JAX**.
- 🔑 Theano's **historical significance**: pioneered symbolic-graph DL, inspired TensorFlow, was original Keras backend.
- 🔑 **Key successor**: **PyTensor** (community-maintained fork with Theano 2.x features).
- 🔑 Direct Theano line of influence: **Theano → TensorFlow → Keras/TF2 ; Theano → concept of autograd → PyTorch ; Theano + XLA → JAX**.

> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU-Standard):**
> *"Theano is not just a tool — it is a paradigm. Understand the symbolic graph, the reverse-mode autodiff, the shared-variable state, and the `updates` rule. Master these four concepts, and you master the topic."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 TensorFlow — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TensorFlow** is an open-source, end-to-end machine learning platform developed originally by the **Google Brain Team** (released in **November 2015**) for high-performance numerical computation. It expresses algorithms as computational graphs (dataflow graphs) where nodes represent mathematical operations and edges represent multi-dimensional data arrays (tensors) communicated between them.

TensorFlow follows a **declarative** paradigm — the programmer first defines the entire computation as a static graph, then executes it inside a `Session`. This makes TensorFlow highly optimisable, deployable across CPUs, GPUs, TPUs, mobile (TensorFlow Lite), and browsers (TensorFlow.js).

Key identifiable constants of TensorFlow (board favourite):
- **Version 1.x** — Static Graph (placeholder + session-based)
- **Version 2.x** — Eager Execution by default (since **September 2019**)
- Backbone of **Keras** as the official high-level API
- Uses **XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra)** compiler for optimisation

## 1.2 PyTorch — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PyTorch** is an open-source deep learning framework developed by **Facebook's AI Research Lab (FAIR)** (released in **October 2016**), built on top of the **Torch** library and the **Python** language with strong GPU acceleration using **CUDA** and **cuDNN** libraries.

PyTorch uses **define-by-run** (dynamic computational graph) semantics — the graph is constructed on-the-fly as operations are executed, mirroring standard Python control flow (loops, conditionals). It is heavily favoured in academic research because debugging is identical to standard Python debugging.

Key constants:
- **Based on Torch (Lua) → rewritten in Python/Cython**
- Uses **Autograd** — automatic differentiation engine
- Integrated with **Torchvision, TorchText, TorchAudio**
- Used heavily in research papers at **NeurIPS, ICML, CVPR**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Framework | Real-World Analogy | Mental Picture |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **TensorFlow v1 (Static)** | A fully planned **architecture blueprint** before construction | You draw every wall, pipe and wire first; only then is the building materialised |
| **PyTorch (Dynamic)** | An **improvisational chef** cooking live in front of you | Each step is performed, tasted, and the next step decided based on what just happened |
| **TensorFlow v2 (Eager)** | A modern chef who can both pre-plan menus AND improvise | Combines static optimisation with dynamic execution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tensors** are simply **generalisations of matrices to N-dimensions**:
> - **Scalar** → 0-D tensor (rank 0)
> - **Vector** → 1-D tensor (rank 1)
> - **Matrix** → 2-D tensor (rank 2)
> - **RGB image** → 3-D tensor `(H, W, C)`
> - **Batch of images** → 4-D tensor `(N, H, W, C)`
> - **Video clip** → 5-D tensor `(N, Frames, H, W, C)`

## 1.4 Etymology and Pedagogical Hook

The name **"TensorFlow"** literally means the **flow of tensors** through a computational graph. PyTorch is named because it is the **Python version of Torch** with tensor operations at its core. Both frameworks treat tensors as the **first-class citizen**, exactly like NumPy `ndarray`, but with two superpowers:

1. **GPU acceleration** via CUDA kernels.
2. **Automatic gradient tracking** for backpropagation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Computational Graph

A computational graph $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$ is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where:

- Each **node** $v_i \in V$ represents a mathematical operation (e.g., $\text{add}$, $\text{matmul}$, $\text{ReLU}$).
- Each **edge** $e_{ij} \in E$ represents the flow of a tensor from node $i$ to node $j$.
- **Leaf nodes** are input tensors (placeholders in TF v1, `tf.Variable` / inputs in TF v2 & PyTorch).
- **Root nodes** represent loss or output values.

### 2.1.1 Static Graph (TensorFlow v1 style)

- **Construction phase**: Build the entire graph symbolically.
- **Execution phase**: Evaluate nodes inside a session by feeding data.
- Optimisation: The graph can be **frozen, serialised, deployed** to any device.
- Drawback: Hard to debug, no native Python control flow (requires `tf.cond`, `tf.while_loop`).

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Graph (PyTorch / TF v2 Eager)

- The graph is **rebuilt every iteration**.
- Supports standard Python `for`, `if`, `print()`.
- Debugger behaves exactly like native Python.
- Trade-off: Slightly less optimisation opportunity, but vastly improved developer ergonomics.

## 2.2 Tensor Algebra — Foundation Mathematics

A tensor $\mathbf{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times I_2 \times \cdots \times I_n}$ has **rank** $n$ and **shape** $(I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n)$.

### 2.2.1 Element-wise Operations

Given two tensors $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}$ of identical shape:

$$
\mathbf{C}_{i,j} = \mathbf{A}_{i,j} + \mathbf{B}_{i,j}
$$

### 2.2.2 Matrix Multiplication (2-D tensors)

$$
\mathbf{C}_{i,k} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_{i,j} \, \mathbf{B}_{j,k} \qquad \text{where } \mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, \mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}
$$

### 2.2.3 Broadcasting (NumPy/PyTorch/TF semantics)

When shapes differ, the smaller tensor is virtually expanded to match the larger one following strict **NumPy broadcasting rules**:

- Rule 1: If tensors have different ranks, prepend 1s to the shape of the smaller one.
- Rule 2: A dimension of size 1 is stretched (broadcast) to match the other tensor.
- Rule 3: If neither dimension is 1 and they differ, broadcasting fails.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Unit / Type | Use Case |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Tensor element indexing | $\mathbf{T}[i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_n]$ | Scalar | Slicing tensors |
| 2 | Matrix multiply | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{B}$ | Tensor | Dense layers |
| 3 | Element-wise multiply | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \odot \mathbf{B}$ | Tensor (Hadamard product) | Masking, gating |
| 4 | Reduction sum | $s = \sum_{i,j} \mathbf{A}_{i,j}$ | Scalar | Loss aggregation |
| 5 | Reduction mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_i x_i$ | Scalar | Normalisation layer |
| 6 | Gradient of scalar loss | $\nabla_{\theta} L = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$ | Same as $\theta$ | Backprop |
| 7 | Chain rule (Autograd) | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial y}\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x}$ | Tensor | Multi-layer backprop |
| 8 | Softmax (classification) | $\sigma(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Probability | Output layer |
| 9 | Cross-entropy loss | $L = -\sum_i y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Scalar (nats) | Classification |
| 10 | MSE loss | $L = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Scalar (squared units) | Regression |
| 11 | SGD update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_{\theta} L$ | Same as $\theta$ | Optimisation |
| 12 | Tensor reshape | $\mathbf{T}^{\prime} \in \mathbb{R}^{(I_1 \cdot I_2) \times I_3}$ | Tensor | Flatten before FC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** TensorFlow dominates **production deployment** (TF Serving, TF Lite, TF.js) at scale; PyTorch dominates **research publications** (≈ 70% of papers at top venues in 2023–24 use PyTorch). Mastering both is now a mandatory hiring skill at DeepMind, FAIR, Google Research, and OpenAI.

## 2.4 The Autograd Mechanism — How Gradients Are Computed

Both frameworks implement **reverse-mode automatic differentiation**. For a node $y = f(x)$, they store the **Jacobian** $J_f$ and during backward pass compute:

$$
\bar{x} = \bar{y} \, \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial x} \quad \text{where } \bar{y} = \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

This is implemented as **operator overloading**:
- PyTorch: every tensor has a `.grad_fn` attribute pointing to the operation that created it.
- TF: uses `tf.GradientTape` to record operations on watched variables.

The gradient flows backward from scalar $L$ to inputs via the chain rule (see SECTION 3 for derivation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Backpropagation Through a 2-Layer Network in Framework Notation

Consider a simple fully-connected network used for regression:

$$
\mathbf{h} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1)
$$

$$
\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}_2 \mathbf{h} + \mathbf{b}_2
$$

$$
L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2
$$

### Step 1 — Compute gradient of loss w.r.t. output prediction $\hat{y}$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = \frac{2}{N}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)
$$

In matrix form, writing $\mathbf{e} = \hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}$:

$$
\nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} L = \frac{2}{N}\mathbf{e}
$$

### Step 2 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_2$ and $\mathbf{b}_2$

Using chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_2} = \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} L \cdot \mathbf{h}^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}_2} = \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} L
$$

Explanation: The gradient of a linear layer's weight equals the **outer product of upstream gradient and the input to that layer**.

### Step 3 — Gradient w.r.t. hidden activation $\mathbf{h}$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}} = \mathbf{W}_2^{\top} \cdot \nabla_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}} L
$$

### Step 4 — Gradient w.r.t. pre-activation $\mathbf{z}_1 = \mathbf{W}_1 \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}_1$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}} \odot \sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{z}_1)
$$

where $\odot$ denotes **element-wise (Hadamard) multiplication**. For sigmoid, $\sigma^{\prime}(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$; for ReLU, $\sigma^{\prime}(z) = \mathbb{1}[z > 0]$.

### Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. $\mathbf{W}_1$ and $\mathbf{b}_1$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_1} \cdot \mathbf{x}^{\top}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{z}_1}
$$

### Step 6 — Parameter Update (SGD)

$$
\mathbf{W}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{W}_k - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{W}_k}, \quad k = 1, 2
$$

$$
\mathbf{b}_k \leftarrow \mathbf{b}_k - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{b}_k}, \quad k = 1, 2
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$).

### 3.1.1 How the framework implements this automatically

When you write in PyTorch:

```python
loss = criterion(model(x), y)
loss.backward()   # autograd walks the dynamic graph in reverse
optimizer.step()  # applies theta <- theta - eta * grad
```

The framework builds a graph of `Function` objects. Each `Function` knows its forward operation and its **backward formula**. Calling `.backward()` traverses this list in reverse topological order and accumulates gradients into the `.grad` attribute of each `requires_grad=True` tensor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Softmax + Cross-Entropy Combined Gradient (Classification)

For classification with logits $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$ and one-hot label $\mathbf{y}$:

$$
\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \qquad \text{(softmax)}
$$

$$
L = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k) \qquad \text{(cross-entropy)}
$$

### Step 1 — Combine into single expression

$$
L = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \left( z_k - \log \sum_{j} e^{z_j} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. a single logit $z_i$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = -\left( y_i - \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} \right) = \hat{y}_i - y_i
$$

This elegant result is exactly why frameworks fuse `softmax + cross-entropy` into a single numerically stable op — the gradient is just the **difference between prediction and truth**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TensorFlow vs PyTorch

| Evaluation Criterion | TensorFlow (v2 / Keras) | PyTorch |
|-----------------------|-------------------------|---------|
| **Defining feature** | Static + Eager hybrid, XLA compile | Dynamic graph, Pythonic |
| **Graph type** | Static (TF 1) / Dynamic (TF 2 eager) | Dynamic (define-by-run) |
| **Computation definition** | `tf.function` decorator for graph mode | Native Python execution |
| **Automatic differentiation** | `tf.GradientTape` context | `tensor.backward()` / autograd |
| **Debugging** | Eager mode easy, graph mode needs `tf.print` | Native Python pdb works directly |
| **Deployment** | TF Serving, TF Lite, TF.js, TFX | TorchServe, ONNX export |
| **Hardware support** | CPU, GPU, **TPU** | CPU, GPU |
| **High-level API** | **Keras** (`tf.keras`) | `torch.nn`, Ignite, Lightning |
| **Research adoption (2024)** | ~25% of NeurIPS papers | ~70% of NeurIPS papers |
| **Industry adoption** | Google, Uber, Airbnb, Twitter | Meta, Tesla, OpenAI |
| **Distributed training** | `tf.distribute.Strategy` | `torch.nn.parallel.DistributedDataParallel` |
| **Ecosystem libraries** | TF Hub, TF Datasets, TFX | Torchvision, TorchText, TorchAudio |
| **Primary data structure** | `tf.Tensor` | `torch.Tensor` |
| **GPU transfer** | `.to('GPU')` / `.gpu()` | `.to('cuda')` |
| **Gradient tracking** | `tf.Variable` inside `GradientTape` | `requires_grad=True` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line mnemonic for exam:** *TensorFlow is a factory-built car (planned, optimised, production-ready); PyTorch is a custom-built car (improvised, flexible, research-friendly).*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Graph — A Simple Linear Regression Example

```mermaid
graph TD
    X["Input Tensor X shape m by n"] --> MUL["Matmul Op W transpose X"]
    W["Weight Tensor W shape n by 1"] --> MUL
    MUL --> ADD["Add Op plus bias"]
    B["Bias Tensor b shape 1 by 1"] --> ADD
    ADD --> LOSS["Loss MSE Op"]
    Y["True Y shape m by 1"] --> LOSS
    LOSS --> GRAD["Backward Pass Autograd"]
    GRAD --> UPDATE["Optimizer SGD Step"]
    UPDATE --> W
    UPDATE --> B
```

## 4.2 Static vs Dynamic Graph — Execution Flow Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph STATIC["TensorFlow v1 Static Graph"]
        S1["Define placeholders x and y"] --> S2["Build symbolic graph"]
        S2 --> S3["Open session"]
        S3 --> S4["Run session with feed dict"]
        S4 --> S5["Fetch outputs"]
    end
    subgraph DYNAMIC["PyTorch Dynamic Graph"]
        D1["Create tensors x and y"] --> D2["Define forward pass inline"]
        D2 --> D3["Compute loss immediately"]
        D3 --> D4["Call loss backward"]
        D4 --> D5["Optimizer step"]
    end
```

## 4.3 TensorFlow High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    USER["User Code Python or C plus plus"] --> KERAS["tf.keras High Level API"]
    USER --> CORE["Core TensorFlow tf.add tf.matmul"]
    KERAS --> CORE
    CORE --> XLA["XLA Compiler"]
    CORE --> GRAPH["Graph Runtime"]
    XLA --> EXEC["Execution Layer"]
    GRAPH --> EXEC
    EXEC --> CPU["CPU Backend"]
    EXEC --> GPU["GPU CUDA cuDNN"]
    EXEC --> TPU["TPU Backend"]
```

## 4.4 PyTorch High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PY["Python Frontend torch nn functional"] --> ATEN["ATen C plus plus Tensor Library"]
    PY --> AUTOGRAD["Autograd Engine"]
    ATEN --> CUDA["CUDA Kernels"]
    ATEN --> CPUBACK["CPU Backend MKL OpenMP"]
    AUTOGRAD --> ATEN
    CUDA --> GPU["NVIDIA GPU"]
    CPUBACK --> CPURAW["CPU Hardware"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Workflow of a Typical Training Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Initialize model parameters W and b"] --> B["Forward pass y pred equals model times X"]
    B --> C["Compute loss L using loss function"]
    C --> D{"Loss converges?"}
    D -- No --> E["Backward pass compute gradients via autograd"]
    E --> F["Update weights using optimizer SGD Adam RMSProp"]
    F --> B
    D -- Yes --> G["Save model checkpoint .pt or .h5"]
    G --> H["Deploy for inference"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams use strictly alphanumeric node identifiers (`A`, `B`, `C`...) and uppercase labels without markdown formatting to ensure Mermaid renders without compilation errors across all browsers.
