## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a tensor. Differentiate between a 0-D, 1-D, and 2-D tensor with one real-world example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **tensor** is a multi-dimensional array of numerical values, characterized by its **rank (ndim)**, **shape**, and **data type (dtype)**.

* **0-D Tensor (Scalar):** A single number, e.g., the loss value `0.045` after one training step.
* **1-D Tensor (Vector):** A 1-D array of numbers, e.g., the bias vector of a dense layer with shape `(128,)`.
* **2-D Tensor (Matrix):** A 2-D grid of numbers, e.g., a grayscale image of 28×28 pixels with shape `(28, 28)`.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation with examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the difference between `torch.tensor(data)` and `torch.from_numpy(numpy_array)`? Mention one practical scenario where the latter is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
`torch.tensor(data)` **copies** the input data into a new memory block owned by PyTorch. It accepts Python lists, tuples, or NumPy arrays as input, and the resulting tensor is **independent** of the source.

`torch.from_numpy(numpy_array)` does **not copy** the data; it creates a tensor that **shares the same memory** as the NumPy array. Modifying one will modify the other in-place.

**Practical Scenario:** In KTU lab work, when preprocessing large image datasets with NumPy (e.g., normalization, resizing) and then feeding them to a PyTorch model, `from_numpy` is preferred because it **avoids the memory overhead of duplication**, which is critical when working with multi-GB datasets.

*(Award 1 mark for copy vs share distinction, 1 mark for the second part, 1 mark for the practical scenario.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*Explain the various methods to create tensors in PyTorch. For each method, state the syntax and the resulting tensor shape when called with arguments `(2, 3)`.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The following table enumerates the primary tensor creation methods. Each is invoked with shape argument `(2, 3)`, yielding a tensor of **shape `(2, 3)`** (2 rows, 3 columns):

| # | Method | Syntax | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `torch.tensor` | `torch.tensor([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])` | Shape `(2,3)`, data from list |
| 2 | `torch.zeros` | `torch.zeros(2, 3)` | All zeros, shape `(2,3)` |
| 3 | `torch.ones` | `torch.ones(2, 3)` | All ones, shape `(2,3)` |
| 4 | `torch.full` | `torch.full((2, 3), 9.0)` | All 9.0, shape `(2,3)` |
| 5 | `torch.rand` | `torch.rand(2, 3)` | Uniform `[0, 1)`, shape `(2,3)` |
| 6 | `torch.randn` | `torch.randn(2, 3)` | Standard normal `N(0,1)`, shape `(2,3)` |
| 7 | `torch.empty` | `torch.empty(2, 3)` | Uninitialized, shape `(2,3)` |
| 8 | `torch.eye` | `torch.eye(3)` | Identity 3×3, shape `(3,3)` |

**Note:** `torch.eye(3)` is a special case where the shape must be square, so it produces `(3, 3)`, not `(2, 3)`.

**Practical Use Case:** `torch.zeros` is widely used to initialize **bias vectors** before training, while `torch.randn` is the foundation of **weight initialization** strategies in deep neural networks.

*(Award 1 mark per correctly explained method, 1 mark for the practical context.)*

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]**
*Given a 4-D tensor of shape `(16, 3, 64, 64)` with `dtype=float32`, calculate: (i) the total number of elements, (ii) the total memory consumed in MB, and (iii) what real-world data this tensor typically represents.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(i) Total Number of Elements:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 16 \times 3 \times 64 \times 64 \\
  &= 16 \times 3 \times 4096 \\
  &= 16 \times 12288 \\
  &= 196608 \text{ elements}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Memory Consumed (float32 → 4 bytes/element):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes} &= 196608 \times 4 = 786432 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{MB} &= \frac{786432}{1024 \times 1024} = \frac{786432}{1048576} \approx 0.75 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Real-World Data Representation:**

This tensor represents a **mini-batch of 16 RGB color images** for a CNN-based image classification task (e.g., CIFAR-style or medical imaging). The dimensions correspond to:
* **16** = batch size (number of images processed in parallel).
* **3** = color channels (Red, Green, Blue).
* **64 × 64** = spatial resolution (height and width in pixels).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong unit conversion:** Students often write `786432 bytes` as the final answer, forgetting to convert to MB. Always divide by $1024^2$ (for MB) or $10^6$ (for SI MB). **1 mark deducted.**
> 2. **Mixing up axis order:** In PyTorch, the convention is `(B, C, H, W)` — Channels-First. In TensorFlow/Keras, the convention is `(B, H, W, C)` — Channels-Last. Mis-identifying the channel axis costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention `float32` consumes 4 bytes:** If a student uses `float64` (8 bytes) by mistake, the memory answer doubles — clearly indicating a conceptual error.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the process of creating a tensor from a NumPy array in PyTorch. Discuss the implications of memory sharing.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram of the Pipeline:**

```
[ NumPy Array in CPU RAM ]
            |
            |  (1) torch.from_numpy(np_arr)
            v
[ PyTorch Tensor (shares memory) ]
            |
            |  (2) tensor.to('cuda')  <-- optional GPU transfer
            v
[ PyTorch Tensor on GPU memory ]
            |
            |  (3) tensor.cpu().numpy()  <-- round-trip back
            v
[ NumPy Array (new memory block) ]
```

**Step-by-Step Process:**

1. **Preprocessing Stage:** A NumPy array `np_arr` of shape `(100, 100)` is created on the CPU.
2. **Bridge Stage:** `t = torch.from_numpy(np_arr)` is called. **Crucially, no new memory is allocated**; `t.data_ptr()` equals `np_arr.ctypes.data`. The tensor is a "view" over the NumPy buffer.
3. **GPU Transfer (Optional):** `t_gpu = t.to('cuda')` creates a *new* memory block on the GPU. The CPU tensor `t` is untouched.
4. **Reverse Transfer:** `np_new = t_gpu.cpu().numpy()` creates yet another memory block on the CPU.

**Implications of Memory Sharing:**

* **Advantage:** Zero-copy overhead → extremely fast, ideal for **large dataset pipelines** where data already exists in NumPy form.
* **Disadvantage — The Classic Bug:** If the user modifies `np_arr` *in-place* (e.g., `np_arr[0, 0] = 999`), the corresponding tensor `t` also changes silently. This can cause **non-deterministic training behavior** that is very hard to debug.
* **Defensive Practice:** Use `t.clone()` to break the link if independent storage is required.

*(Award 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for process steps, 2 marks for memory implications.)*

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]**
*A deep learning engineer is preparing an input tensor for a CNN that processes batches of 32 RGB images of resolution 128×128. She initializes the weights using `torch.randn` of shape `(64, 3, 3, 3)` for the first convolutional layer. Calculate the memory required for the input batch and the weight tensor. Justify the choice of `float32` over `float64` for this application.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**(1) Input Batch Memory:**

Shape: `(32, 3, 128, 128)`, dtype: `float32`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Elements} &= 32 \times 3 \times 128 \times 128 = 1{,}572{,}864 \\
\text{Bytes} &= 1{,}572{,}864 \times 4 = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Memory} &= \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 6.0 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Weight Tensor Memory:**

Shape: `(64, 3, 3, 3)`, dtype: `float32` (64 filters of size 3×3 over 3 input channels).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Elements} &= 64 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 = 1728 \\
\text{Bytes} &= 1728 \times 4 = 6912 \text{ bytes} \approx 6.75 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Justification for `float32` over `float64`:**

* **Memory Efficiency:** `float32` consumes **half the memory** of `float64`. For the input batch alone, this saves **6 MB per batch**. Across 1000 batches per epoch, this saves **6 GB** of memory traffic.
* **GPU Optimization:** Modern NVIDIA GPUs (T4, A100, RTX series) feature dedicated **Tensor Cores** that perform matrix multiplication on `float32` (and even `float16`) inputs at speeds **4×–16×** faster than `float64`. Using `float64` would forfeit this hardware acceleration.
* **Numerical Sufficiency:** Deep learning training via **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** operates on noisy mini-batch estimates. The additional 16 digits of precision in `float64` provides **no measurable improvement** in final model accuracy but doubles the compute cost.
* **Industry Standard:** Pre-trained models (ResNet, VGG, BERT) are released in `float32`, ensuring **compatibility** with downstream pipelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the channel dimension in weight tensor:** Students often compute weight memory as `64 × 3 × 3 × 1` or `64 × 1 × 3 × 3`, missing the input channel depth of 3. This yields a wrong element count. **2 marks deducted.**
> 2. **Mixing SI and Binary prefixes:** Writing "6 MB" for 6,291,456 bytes is technically a **SI Megabyte** ($10^6$), not a binary Mebibyte ($2^{20}$). The KTU answer key typically accepts the SI convention, but the most rigorous answer uses `MiB`. Avoid confusion by always showing the division step.
> 3. **Vague justification for `float32`:** Writing "because it's standard" without naming Tensor Cores or memory savings scores **0 out of 3** on the justification part. Always cite **specific engineering reasons**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision for KTU Exam Hall:**

* **Tensor Definition:** A tensor is a **multidimensional homogeneous array** defined by **rank (ndim)**, **shape**, and **dtype**.
* **Rank Mnemonic:** `Scalar=0, Vector=1, Matrix=2, Cube=3, Batch-of-Cubes=4, Video=5`.
* **Default dtype:** PyTorch → `float32`; NumPy → `float64`. Always be aware of the conversion!
* **Memory Formula:** `Bytes = Π(shape) × sizeof(dtype)`. Convert to MB by dividing by $1024^2$.
* **`tensor` vs `from_numpy`:** The former **copies**; the latter **shares memory** (zero-copy).
* **`rand` vs `randn`:** `rand` → Uniform $[0, 1)$; `randn` → Standard Normal $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.
* **`zeros` vs `empty`:** `zeros` initializes with 0.0; `empty` is **faster** but contains **garbage**.
* **Channel Convention:** PyTorch = `(B, C, H, W)` — Channels-First; TensorFlow = `(B, H, W, C)` — Channels-Last.
* **Use `float32`:** Because of **GPU Tensor Core acceleration** and **50% memory savings** over `float64`.
* **Inference Mode:** Use `torch.no_grad()` and `.detach()` to disable gradient tracking and save memory.
* **Image Shape:** A single RGB image is `(3, H, W)` in PyTorch; a batch is `(B, 3, H, W)`.
* **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Forgetting that `torch.from_numpy` tensors **reflect** NumPy modifications — always `.clone()` if you need independence.
* **Engineering Application:** Tensors are the **lingua franca** of deep learning — every weight, input, output, and gradient is one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operations on Tensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations on Tensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal vocabulary of the KTU 2024 *Deep Learning Techniques* syllabus (Module 2: Tensor Foundations), **Tensor Operations** are defined as the closed, well-defined algebraic transformations applied on tensor-valued variables under the standard semiring $(\mathbb{R}, +, \cdot)$. A tensor $\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times I_2 \times \cdots \times I_N}$ of **rank** $N$ admits a rich algebraic structure that supports:

- **Element-wise (Hadamard) operations** — applied coordinate-wise across every entry.
- **Contraction / reduction operations** — collapse one or more axes via summation, mean, etc.
- **Tensor products / outer products** — construct higher-rank tensors from lower-rank ones.
- **Reshaping, slicing, broadcasting and concatenation** — structural transformations preserving or re-organising the underlying data layout.

These operations form the **computational primitive set** that every modern deep-learning framework (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras, JAX) compiles down to. For a single forward pass of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), thousands to millions of tensor operations may be executed on a GPU's tensor cores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Every operation must be analysed in three layers — (i) **mathematical definition**, (ii) **shape inference rule**, and (iii) **computational complexity** (FLOPs). Examiners explicitly award marks for the shape rule.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Dimensional Spreadsheet"

Imagine an **Excel sheet** as a 2-Dimensional Tensor (Matrix): rows and columns. Now stack thousands of such sheets into a binder, label each sheet (depth/channel) — that is a **3-D Tensor** (e.g., an RGB image: Height × Width × Channels = $H \times W \times 3$). Stack many such binders on a shelf (batch) and you have a **4-D Tensor** $(N, C, H, W)$.

- A **scalar** is one cell.
- A **vector** is one row.
- A **matrix** is one sheet.
- A **3-D Tensor** is one binder.
- A **4-D Tensor** is a shelf of binders.

**Tensor operations** are the algebraic "verbs" you can perform across these spreadsheets: *add* (element-wise), *multiply* (Hadamard), *contract* (matrix multiply), *flatten* (reshape), *broadcast* (auto-stretch smaller spreadsheets to match larger ones), and *concatenate* (stitch sheets side-by-side).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** A tensor's **rank (number of axes)** is its *order*. Its **shape** is the tuple of dimension sizes along each axis. **Size** is the total number of scalar entries — the product of all shape components.

## 1.3 Canonical Symbols & Notation Used in KTU Valuation

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{T}, \mathbf{A}, X$ | Tensor | $\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4 \times 5}$ |
| $N$ | Rank (number of axes / order) | $N = 3$ for a 3-D tensor |
| $I_j$ | Dimension along axis $j$ | $I_1 = 3, I_2 = 4, I_3 = 5$ |
| $\odot$ | Element-wise (Hadamard) product | $\mathcal{A} \odot \mathcal{B}$ |
| $\otimes$ | Outer / Kronecker product | $\mathbf{u} \otimes \mathbf{v}$ |
| $\cdot$ | Contraction (e.g. matrix multiply) | $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Tensor Operations

For Module 2 of PEEBT746, every tensor operation belongs to one of the following five families. The KTU examiner's eye always seeks the *family* identification first, then the *shape rule*.

### 2.1.1 Element-wise (Hadamard) Operations
Applied independently to each scalar entry $t_{i_1, \dots, i_N}$ of the tensor.

- **Element-wise addition:** $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{A} + \mathcal{B}$, where $c_{i_1,\dots,i_N} = a_{i_1,\dots,i_N} + b_{i_1,\dots,i_N}$.
- **Element-wise multiplication (Hadamard):** $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{A} \odot \mathcal{B}$.
- **Scalar broadcasting:** $\mathcal{C} = \alpha \cdot \mathcal{A}$, where $c_{i_1,\dots,i_N} = \alpha \cdot a_{i_1,\dots,i_N}$.
- **Unary element-wise:** $\sin, \exp, \text{ReLU}, \sigma, \tanh$ applied per element.

> **Shape rule (Pre-broadcasting):** Both operands must share the **identical shape**, unless broadcasting (§2.1.5) is invoked.

### 2.1.2 Reduction Operations
Reduce the rank by aggregating values along one or more axes.

- **Sum:** $\displaystyle s = \sum_{i_1, \dots, i_N} \mathcal{T}_{i_1, \dots, i_N}$.
- **Mean:** $\displaystyle \mu = \frac{1}{\prod_j I_j}\sum_{i_1, \dots, i_N} \mathcal{T}_{i_1, \dots, i_N}$.
- **Max / Min / L2-norm / Variance / Argmax** — all are reductions.

> **Shape rule:** Reducing axis $k$ drops $I_k$ from the shape. Specifying `keepdims=True` preserves it as $1$.

### 2.1.3 Tensor Contraction (Generalised Matrix Multiplication)
For two tensors $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times \cdots \times I_P \times J}$ and $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{J \times K_1 \times \cdots \times K_Q}$, contracting along the common axis $J$ yields $\mathcal{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times \cdots \times I_P \times K_1 \times \cdots \times K_Q}$.

- **Matrix multiply** is the 2-D special case: $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$.
- **Dot product** is the contraction of two vectors: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \sum_i u_i v_i$.

### 2.1.4 Reshaping & Structural Operations
- **Reshape:** re-arranges the same data into a new shape with identical total size $\prod_j I_j$.
- **Transpose / Permute:** re-orders axes; e.g. $(N, C, H, W) \rightarrow (N, H, W, C)$.
- **Squeeze / Unsqueeze:** removes / inserts size-1 axes.
- **Flatten:** collapses all axes into one (e.g. $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{HWC}$).

### 2.1.5 Broadcasting (NumPy/PyTorch Broadcasting Rule)
When operand shapes are not identical, broadcasting auto-stretches the smaller tensor along axes of size $1$ to match the larger one — without physically copying memory (in most frameworks).

**Broadcasting compatibility rule (verified pairwise right-to-left):**

- Dimension sizes are equal, **OR**
- One of them is $1$.

If neither holds, broadcasting fails.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula | Shape Rule | FLOPs / Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Element-wise add $\mathcal{A} + \mathcal{B}$ | $c_{ijk} = a_{ijk} + b_{ijk}$ | $\text{shape}(\mathcal{A}) = \text{shape}(\mathcal{B})$ | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 2 | Hadamard $\mathcal{A} \odot \mathcal{B}$ | $c_{ijk} = a_{ijk} \cdot b_{ijk}$ | Identical shapes (or broadcastable) | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 3 | Scalar multiply $\alpha\mathcal{A}$ | $c_{ijk} = \alpha \cdot a_{ijk}$ | Any shape | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 4 | Sum reduction (all axes) | $s = \sum_i \sum_j \sum_k a_{ijk}$ | Returns scalar | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 5 | Sum along axis $k$ | $s_{ij} = \sum_k a_{ijk}$ | Drops axis $k$ | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 6 | Mean (along axis $k$) | $\mu_{ij} = \frac{1}{I_k}\sum_k a_{ijk}$ | Drops axis $k$ | $O\!\left(\prod_j I_j\right)$ |
| 7 | Matrix multiply $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}$ | $c_{ij} = \sum_k a_{ik} b_{kj}$ | $\mathbf{A}: m \times n,\ \mathbf{B}: n \times p \Rightarrow \mathbf{C}: m \times p$ | $O(mnp)$ |
| 8 | Outer product $\mathbf{u} \otimes \mathbf{v}$ | $c_{ij} = u_i v_j$ | $m \times 1,\ n \times 1 \Rightarrow m \times n$ | $O(mn)$ |
| 9 | Dot product $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ | $s = \sum_i u_i v_i$ | Both length $n$ | $O(n)$ |
| 10 | Reshape | Same data, new shape | Total size preserved | $O(1)$ view |
| 11 | Transpose | Swap two axes | Shape permutation | $O(1)$ view |
| 12 | Concatenate along axis $k$ | $\mathcal{C} = [\mathcal{A} \mid \mathcal{B}]$ | Sizes along axis $k$ add | $O(\text{size})$ copy |
| 13 | L2-norm $\Vert \mathcal{T} \Vert_2$ | $\sqrt{\sum_i t_i^2}$ | Returns scalar | $O(\prod_j I_j)$ |
| 14 | Frobenius norm $\Vert \mathbf{A} \Vert_F$ | $\sqrt{\sum_{ij} a_{ij}^2}$ | Returns scalar | $O(mn)$ |
| 15 | Broadcasting | Auto-stretch size-1 axes | Pairwise right-to-left rule | $O(\text{virtual size})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** "Reshape" and "Transpose" are *constant-time view operations* in frameworks but they trigger **non-contiguous memory layout**, which forces a `.contiguous()` *copy* before matrix multiplication. Examiners often include a sub-question on this distinction.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Tensor Operation | Engineering Domain |
|---|---|---|
| CNN forward pass | 4-D convolution via `im2col` + GEMM | Computer Vision, Autonomous Vehicles |
| RNN / LSTM gating | Element-wise sigmoid + Hadamard product | NLP, Speech Recognition |
| Batch Normalisation | Mean / Variance reduction along batch axis | Deep Learning Optimisation |
| Recommendation Systems (Matrix Factorisation) | Matrix multiplication $U \cdot V^T$ | E-commerce, Streaming |
| Attention in Transformers | Scaled dot-product $\dfrac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ | LLMs, Translation |
| Medical Imaging Segmentation | 3-D tensor concatenation across U-Net skip paths | Healthcare AI |
| Reinforcement Learning | Outer product of policy gradient $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta \cdot R$ | Robotics, Game AI |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through four canonical tensor-operation problems that are *guaranteed templates* for the 14-mark Part-B questions. Each derivation is fully expanded with no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Broadcasting Derivation

**Problem:** Given $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$ and $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 4}$, compute $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{A} + \mathbf{b}$ using the broadcasting rule. Show every intermediate step.

### Step 1 — Align shapes pairwise right-to-left

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{shape}(\mathcal{A}) &= (3,\ 4) \\
\text{shape}(\mathbf{b}) &= (1,\ 4)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply compatibility rule (right-to-left)

- Axis 2 (rightmost): $4$ vs $4$ → **compatible** (equal).
- Axis 1 (left): $3$ vs $1$ → **compatible** (one operand is size-1).

### Step 3 — Virtual broadcast $\mathbf{b}$ to shape $(3, 4)$

Since axis 1 has size 1, virtually replicate $\mathbf{b}$ three times along axis 1. The replicated tensor $\mathbf{b}^{\text{bc}}$ conceptually becomes:

$$
\mathbf{b}^{\text{bc}} = \begin{bmatrix} b_{11} & b_{12} & b_{13} & b_{14} \\ b_{11} & b_{12} & b_{13} & b_{14} \\ b_{11} & b_{12} & b_{13} & b_{14} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}
$$

### Step 4 — Perform element-wise addition

$$
c_{ij} = a_{ij} + b^{\text{bc}}_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{1j} \quad \text{for all } i \in \{1,2,3\}
$$

### Step 5 — Final shape and cost

$$
\text{shape}(\mathcal{C}) = (3,\ 4), \qquad \text{FLOPs} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ additions}.
$$

> **Result:** Broadcasting allows a *row-vector* to be added to every row of a matrix without any explicit Python loop. In GPU code, this is mapped to a single fused kernel of 12 FLOPs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reduction Along Multiple Axes

**Problem:** Given the 3-D tensor $\mathcal{T} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3 \times 4}$ with entries $t_{ijk} = i + 2j + 3k$, compute (a) the total sum, (b) the sum along axis 0, (c) the mean along axis 2.

### Step 1 — Express tensor entries analytically

$$
t_{ijk} = i + 2j + 3k, \qquad i \in \{0,1\},\ j \in \{0,1,2\},\ k \in \{0,1,2,3\}
$$

### Step 2 — Total sum (reduce all axes)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{1}\sum_{j=0}^{2}\sum_{k=0}^{3} (i + 2j + 3k) \\
&= \sum_i \sum_j \sum_k i \;+\; 2 \sum_i \sum_j \sum_k j \;+\; 3 \sum_i \sum_j \sum_k k
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluate each triple sum:

$$
\sum_i i = 0 + 1 = 1, \quad \sum_j j = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3, \quad \sum_k k = 0 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 6
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i \sum_j \sum_k i &= 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 12 \\
\sum_i \sum_j \sum_k j &= 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 24 \\
\sum_i \sum_j \sum_k k &= 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 6 = 36
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 12 + 2(24) + 3(36) = 12 + 48 + 108 = \mathbf{168}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum along axis 0 (result is $\mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$)

$$
s_{jk} = \sum_{i=0}^{1} t_{ijk} = \sum_{i=0}^{1}(i + 2j + 3k) = 2(2j) + 2(3k) + (0+1) = 1 + 4j + 6k
$$

### Step 4 — Mean along axis 2 (result is $\mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3}$)

$$
\mu_{ij} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{k=0}^{3} t_{ijk} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{k=0}^{3}(i + 2j + 3k) = \frac{1}{4}\big[4(i+2j) + 3 \cdot 6\big] = i + 2j + \frac{9}{2}
$$

> **Result:** Total sum = $\mathbf{168}$; Axis-0 sum $s_{jk} = 1 + 4j + 6k$; Axis-2 mean $\mu_{ij} = i + 2j + 4.5$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Matrix Multiplication via Tensor Contraction

**Problem:** Compute $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$ where

$$
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}_{2 \times 3}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 8 \\ 9 & 10 \\ 11 & 12 \end{bmatrix}_{3 \times 2}
$$

### Step 1 — Verify shape compatibility

Inner dimensions: $3 = 3$ ✓. Result shape: $2 \times 2$.

### Step 2 — Compute $c_{11}$

$$
c_{11} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} a_{1k} b_{k1} = (1)(7) + (2)(9) + (3)(11) = 7 + 18 + 33 = \mathbf{58}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $c_{12}$

$$
c_{12} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} a_{1k} b_{k2} = (1)(8) + (2)(10) + (3)(12) = 8 + 20 + 36 = \mathbf{64}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $c_{21}$

$$
c_{21} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} a_{2k} b_{k1} = (4)(7) + (5)(9) + (6)(11) = 28 + 45 + 66 = \mathbf{139}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute $c_{22}$

$$
c_{22} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} a_{2k} b_{k2} = (4)(8) + (5)(10) + (6)(12) = 32 + 50 + 72 = \mathbf{154}
$$

### Step 6 — Assemble and verify cost

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 58 & 64 \\ 139 & 154 \end{bmatrix}_{2 \times 2}, \qquad \text{FLOPs} = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 = 12 \text{ muls} + 12 \text{ adds} = 24 \text{ FLOPs}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — 3-D Tensor Contraction (Batch Matrix Multiply)

**Problem:** Given batched matrices $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times m \times n}$ and $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times n \times p}$ with batch size $B = 2$, $m = 2$, $n = 3$, $p = 2$, compute $\mathcal{C}_{b} = \mathcal{A}_{b} \cdot \mathcal{B}_{b}$ for $b \in \{1, 2\}$.

### Step 1 — Define the two batches explicitly

$$
\mathcal{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathcal{B}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathcal{B}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\mathcal{C}_1 = \mathcal{A}_1 \cdot \mathcal{B}_1$

For element $c_{11}^{(1)}$:

$$
c_{11}^{(1)} = (1)(1) + (0)(3) + (-1)(5) = 1 + 0 - 5 = -4
$$

For element $c_{12}^{(1)}$:

$$
c_{12}^{(1)} = (1)(2) + (0)(4) + (-1)(6) = 2 + 0 - 6 = -4
$$

For element $c_{21}^{(1)}$:

$$
c_{21}^{(1)} = (0)(1) + (1)(3) + (2)(5) = 0 + 3 + 10 = 13
$$

For element $c_{22}^{(1)}$:

$$
c_{22}^{(1)} = (0)(2) + (1)(4) + (2)(6) = 0 + 4 + 12 = 16
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -4 \\ 13 & 16 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\mathcal{C}_2 = \mathcal{A}_2 \cdot \mathcal{B}_2$

For element $c_{11}^{(2)}$:

$$
c_{11}^{(2)} = (2)(0) + (1)(-1) + (0)(3) = 0 - 1 + 0 = -1
$$

For element $c_{12}^{(2)}$:

$$
c_{12}^{(2)} = (2)(1) + (1)(2) + (0)(0) = 2 + 2 + 0 = 4
$$

For element $c_{21}^{(2)}$:

$$
c_{21}^{(2)} = (1)(0) + (0)(-1) + (1)(3) = 0 + 0 + 3 = 3
$$

For element $c_{22}^{(2)}$:

$$
c_{22}^{(2)} = (1)(1) + (0)(2) + (1)(0) = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1
$$

$$
\mathcal{C}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Stack into final 3-D tensor

$$
\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}_1 \\ \mathcal{C}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & -4 & 13 & 16 \\ -1 & 4 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{\text{2 slices}}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\mathcal{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2 \times 2}, \qquad \text{shape} = (B,\ m,\ p) = (2, 2, 2)
$$

> **Result:** Batch matrix multiplication preserves the leading batch axis and contracts the inner dimension $n$. This is the *core operation* of every attention head in a Transformer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operation Family | Input Shapes | Output Shape | FLOPs Order | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hadamard | $(I_1,\dots,I_N)$, same | $(I_1,\dots,I_N)$ | $O\!\left(\prod I_j\right)$ | Gating, masking |
| Sum (full) | $(I_1,\dots,I_N)$ | scalar | $O\!\left(\prod I_j\right)$ | Loss aggregation |
| Matrix multiply | $(m,n)\cdot(n,p)$ | $(m,p)$ | $O(mnp)$ | Dense layers |
| Batch matmul | $(B,m,n)\cdot(B,n,p)$ | $(B,m,p)$ | $O(Bmnp)$ | Attention, RNN |
| Outer product | $(m,1),(1,n)$ | $(m,n)$ | $O(mn)$ | Kernel construction |
| Broadcasted add | $(m,n)+(1,n)$ | $(m,n)$ | $O(mn)$ | Bias addition |
| Reshape | any | same size, new shape | $O(1)$ view | Flatten before FC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tensor Operation Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Tensor Operations] --> rootB[Elementwise Family]
    rootA --> rootC[Reduction Family]
    rootA --> rootD[Contraction Family]
    rootA --> rootE[Structural Family]
    rootA --> rootF[Broadcasting Family]

    rootB --> nodeB1[Add Subtract]
    rootB --> nodeB2[Hadamard Multiply]
    rootB --> nodeB3[Scalar Multiply]
    rootB --> nodeB4[Unary ReLU Sigmoid Tanh]

    rootC --> nodeC1[Sum]
    rootC --> nodeC2[Mean]
    rootC --> nodeC3[Max Min Argmax]
    rootC --> nodeC4[L2 Norm Frobenius Norm]

    rootD --> nodeD1[Dot Product]
    rootD --> nodeD2[Matrix Multiply]
    rootD --> nodeD3[Batch Matrix Multiply]
    rootD --> nodeD4[Outer Product]

    rootE --> nodeE1[Reshape Flatten]
    rootE --> nodeE2[Transpose Permute]
    rootE --> nodeE3[Squeeze Unsqueeze]
    rootE --> nodeE4[Concatenate Stack Split]

    rootF --> nodeF1[Size 1 Axis Stretch]
    rootF --> nodeF2[Right To Left Rule]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tensor Op Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Input Tensor A] --> shA[Shape Inference Module]
    inB[Input Tensor B] --> shB[Shape Inference Module]
    shA --> cmp[Compatibility Checker]
    shB --> cmp
    cmp --> brd[Broadcast Engine]
    brd --> core[Operation Core Kernel]
    core --> out[Output Tensor C]
    core --> log[Shape and FLOPs Logger]

    style inA fill:#FFD580
    style inB fill:#FFD580
    style shA fill:#C8E6C9
    style shB fill:#C8E6C9
    style cmp fill:#81D4FA
    style brd fill:#CE93D8
    style core fill:#F48FB1
    style out fill:#A5D6A7
    style log fill:#FFE082
```

## 4.3 Broadcasting Rule — Visual Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Read Shape A and Shape B] --> step2[Right Align Dimensions]
    step2 --> step3{Dimension Pair}
    step3 -->|Both Equal| ok1[Compatible Move Left]
    step3 -->|One is 1| ok2[Stretch to Match Move Left]
    step3 -->|Different and Neither 1| err[Raise Broadcast Error]
    ok1 --> step4{More Axes Left}
    ok2 --> step4
    step4 -->|Yes| step3
    step4 -->|No| done[Return Virtual Broadcasted Tensors]
```

## 4.4 Reduction Operation Across Axes — Rank Collapse

```mermaid
graph TD
    t3[Tensor Rank 3 Shape 2 3 4] --> axis0[Reduce Axis 0]
    t3 --> axis1[Reduce Axis 1]
    t3 --> axis2[Reduce Axis 2]
    t3 --> axisAll[Reduce All Axes]
    axis0 --> out0[Output Shape 3 4 Rank 2]
    axis1 --> out1[Output Shape 2 4 Rank 2]
    axis2 --> out2[Output Shape 2 3 Rank 2]
    axisAll --> outS[Output Shape Scalar Rank 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define tensor operations. With suitable examples, distinguish between element-wise operations and reduction operations on tensors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Definition:** Tensor operations are algebraic transformations applied on tensors (multi-dimensional arrays) that preserve or alter the rank, shape, and values of the operand according to a well-defined rule. Formally, an operation is a mapping $\mathcal{O}: \mathbb{R}^{I_1 \times \cdots \times I_N} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{J_1 \times \cdots \times J_M}$.

- **(1 mark) Element-wise:** Operations applied *independently* to each scalar entry without interaction across axes. Example: $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{A} + \mathcal{B}$ where $c_{ijk} = a_{ijk} + b_{ijk}$; ReLU activation $\text{ReLU}(a_{ijk}) = \max(0, a_{ijk})$. **Shape preserved.**

- **(1 mark) Reduction:** Operations that *aggregate* values along one or more axes, *reducing the rank*. Example: $\displaystyle s_{jk} = \sum_i a_{ijk}$ collapses axis 0; Frobenius norm $\displaystyle \Vert \mathcal{A} \Vert_F = \sqrt{\sum_{ijk} a_{ijk}^2}$ returns a scalar.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the broadcasting rule in tensor operations with an example. What happens when two tensors of shapes $(3, 1)$ and $(1, 4)$ are added?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1.5 marks) Rule:** Broadcasting auto-stretches a tensor along axes of size $1$ to match a larger operand without physically copying memory. Pairwise, right-to-left, two dimensions are *compatible* if (i) they are equal, or (ii) one of them is $1$.

- **(1.5 marks) Example:** $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$ and $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 4}$.
  * Axis 1: $1$ vs $4$ → stretch $\mathcal{A}$'s axis 1 to $4$.
  * Axis 0: $3$ vs $1$ → stretch $\mathcal{B}$'s axis 0 to $3$.
  * Broadcast result $\in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$ where $c_{ij} = a_{i1} + b_{1j}$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the different families of tensor operations. For each family, give one application in deep learning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1 mark — Introduction):** Tensor operations form the computational backbone of every deep-learning model. They can be classified into five families.

**(1 mark — Element-wise family):** Operations applied independently to each scalar entry of the tensor. Includes add, subtract, multiply (Hadamard), and unary activations such as ReLU, sigmoid, and tanh. **DL application:** The ReLU activation in hidden layers of a CNN, $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$, applied element-wise to a feature map.

**(1 mark — Reduction family):** Operations that aggregate values along one or more axes. Includes sum, mean, max, min, argmax, and norm computations. **DL application:** Global Average Pooling in CNNs, where a $H \times W \times C$ feature map is reduced along spatial axes to produce a $C$-dimensional vector: $\mathbf{v}_c = \frac{1}{HW}\sum_{i,j} \mathcal{F}_{ijc}$.

**(1 mark — Contraction family):** Generalised matrix multiplication that sums products over a common index. Includes dot product, matrix multiply, batch matrix multiply, and tensor contraction. **DL application:** Fully-connected (Dense) layer forward pass $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$, and the scaled dot-product attention in Transformers: $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$.

**(1 mark — Structural family):** Reshape, transpose, slice, concatenate, split, squeeze, and unsqueeze — these alter the *shape* without changing the underlying data values. **DL application:** Flattening a feature map $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ into $\mathbb{R}^{HWC}$ before feeding into a dense classifier.

**(1 mark — Broadcasting family):** Auto-stretches tensors of differing shapes via the size-1 axis rule. **DL application:** Adding a bias vector $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$ to a feature map $\mathcal{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ in a ConvNet.

**(1 mark — Diagram reference):** A clean family-tree diagram (similar to §4.1) earns full credit. Mention that these families combine: e.g., a Transformer attention head performs contraction + reduction (softmax) + contraction.

---

**A (b)** Consider two tensors $\mathcal{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3 \times 4}$ and $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 5}$. Explain how tensor contraction can be applied to compute a resultant tensor. Define all dimensions, write the mathematical expression, and state the resulting shape. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(2 marks — Identification):** For contraction, the *last* dimension of $\mathcal{A}$ (size $4$) must equal the *first* dimension of $\mathcal{B}$ (size $4$). The common axis is the **contraction axis**.

**(2 marks — Mathematical expression):** The contraction is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{C}_{ijk} = \sum_{m=1}^{4} \mathcal{A}_{ijm} \cdot \mathcal{B}_{mk}, \qquad i \in \{1,2\},\ j \in \{1,2,3\},\ k \in \{1,\dots,5\}
$$

**(2 marks — Shape inference):**
- $\mathcal{A}$: shape $(2, 3, 4)$ — three axes.
- $\mathcal{B}$: shape $(4, 5)$ — two axes.
- The contracted axis $4$ is eliminated.
- The remaining axes $(2, 3)$ from $\mathcal{A}$ are kept in order, then $(5)$ from $\mathcal{B}$.
- **Output shape:** $\mathcal{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3 \times 5}$.

**(1 mark — Interpretation):** This is a *batch matrix multiplication* across the leading batch dimension of size $2$. Conceptually, $\mathcal{A}$ is a *batch of 2 matrices*, each $3 \times 4$, multiplied with the shared $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 5}$, yielding a batch of 2 matrices each $3 \times 5$. **FLOPs:** $2 \times 3 \times 5 \times 4 = 120$ multiplications + $120$ additions $= 240$ FLOPs.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**B (a)** Explain the broadcasting rule in tensor operations. Demonstrate the working of broadcasting with an example where tensors of shapes $(4, 1, 3)$ and $(1, 5, 3)$ are added. State the resulting shape and give its FLOP count. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(2 marks — Rule statement):** Broadcasting is a shape-compatibility mechanism that virtually replicates a tensor along axes of size $1$ to match a larger operand. The pairwise compatibility rule, applied **right-to-left**, requires that at each aligned axis the dimensions are either equal or one of them is $1$.

**(1 mark — Apply rule to shapes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{shape}(\mathcal{A}) &= (4,\ 1,\ 3) \\
\text{shape}(\mathcal{B}) &= (1,\ 5,\ 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

- Axis 2 (rightmost): $3 = 3$ ✓
- Axis 1: $1$ vs $5$ → stretch $\mathcal{A}$'s axis 1 to $5$.
- Axis 0: $4$ vs $1$ → stretch $\mathcal{B}$'s axis 0 to $4$.

**(1 mark — Broadcast result):** Both tensors virtually expand to a common shape $(4, 5, 3)$. No physical memory copy is performed; only the *stride* metadata is updated.

**(1 mark — Operation:** The element-wise sum is then computed:
$$
\mathcal{C}_{ijk} = \mathcal{A}_{i,1,k} + \mathcal{B}_{1,j,k}, \quad i \in \{1,\dots,4\},\ j \in \{1,\dots,5\},\ k \in \{1,2,3\}
$$

**(1 mark — Output shape:** $\mathcal{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 5 \times 3}$.

**(1 mark — FLOP count:** $4 \times 5 \times 3 = \mathbf{60}$ additions.

---

**B (b)** Compute the Frobenius norm $\Vert \mathcal{A} \Vert_F$ of the matrix $\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$. Show every step. Comment on its use in deep learning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(1 mark — Definition:** The Frobenius norm of a matrix $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is defined as:

$$
\Vert \mathbf{A} \Vert_F = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}^2}
$$

**(1 mark — Substitute entries:**

$$
\Vert \mathcal{A} \Vert_F = \sqrt{1^2 + (-2)^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 + 5^2 + (-6)^2}
$$

**(1 mark — Compute each square:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1^2 &= 1 \\
(-2)^2 &= 4 \\
3^2 &= 9 \\
4^2 &= 16 \\
5^2 &= 25 \\
(-6)^2 &= 36
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark — Sum the squares:**

$$
\sum = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 = 91
$$

**(1 mark — Final value:**

$$
\Vert \mathcal{A} \Vert_F = \sqrt{91} \approx 9.539
$$

**(2 marks — Use in Deep Learning:** The Frobenius norm is widely used as a **regulariser** to penalise large weights and prevent overfitting. Common forms include:
- **Weight Decay (L2 Regularisation):** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{reg}} = \lambda \Vert \mathbf{W} \Vert_F^2$, added to the cross-entropy loss.
- **Gradient Clipping:** Bounds $\Vert \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} \Vert_F \leq \tau$ to avoid exploding gradients in RNNs.
- **Spectral initialisation:** Ensures $\Vert \mathbf{W} \Vert_F$ stays close to a target scale (Glorot, He initialisation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the shape rule explicitly** — 1 to 2 marks are routinely lost when students compute values without declaring input/output shapes.
> 2. **Confusing Hadamard ($\odot$) with matrix multiply ($\cdot$)** — examiners test this trap explicitly.
> 3. **Not showing FLOPs / cost** — Module-2 questions under the NEP 2020 outcome-based scheme *require* complexity statements.
> 4. **Missing broadcasting direction** — always specify *right-to-left*; left-to-right comparison is wrong and loses a mark.
> 5. **Treating `reshape` as a copy** — it is a *view*, not a copy. Examiners deduct marks for this misconception.
> 6. **In reduction questions, ignoring `keepdims`** — a common sub-part asks "what is the output shape if `keepdims=True`?" and most students skip it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tensor rank = number of axes** (not the matrix rank). A scalar = rank 0, vector = rank 1, matrix = rank 2, RGB image = rank 3.
- **Tensor shape** = tuple of dimension sizes; **tensor size** = product of shape components.
- **Element-wise operations** preserve shape; **reduction operations** reduce rank; **contraction operations** keep rank but eliminate the contracted axis.
- **Broadcasting** only works along size-1 axes, checked **right-to-left**; failure to satisfy the rule raises a `ValueError`.
- **Reshape & Transpose** are O(1) *views*, not copies — but they can force materialisation in subsequent matmul.
- **Matrix multiply** cost is $O(mnp)$ — for square $n \times n$ matrices this is $O(n^3)$.
- **Batch matrix multiply** is the workhorse of attention in Transformers and Conv2D via `im2col`.
- **Frobenius norm** = $\sqrt{\sum a_{ij}^2}$; used for L2 regularisation, gradient clipping, weight decay.
- **Outer product** $\mathbf{u} \otimes \mathbf{v}$ builds a rank-2 tensor of shape $(m, n)$ from vectors of lengths $m$ and $n$.
- **Always mention** FLOPs / computational cost in 14-mark answers — this is the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned expectation.
- **Scalar broadcasting** $\alpha \cdot \mathcal{T}$ is a free operation; **tensor broadcasting** is also free in *virtual* memory but consumes compute when the kernel executes.
- **Concatenation** along axis $k$ *adds* the sizes along that axis; **stacking** adds a *new* axis.
- **Argmax vs Max** — argmax returns the *index*, max returns the *value*; both reduce the axis but produce different output types.
- **Memory layout note:** Row-major (C-contiguous) vs column-major (Fortran) — affects reshape validity and GEMM cache efficiency on GPU tensor cores.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TensorBoard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** TensorBoard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TensorBoard** is a browser-based visualization toolkit that is a core component of the **TensorFlow** ecosystem. It is a suite of web applications designed to inspect, debug, and understand the behavior of a machine learning (ML) or deep learning (DL) model during training, evaluation, and inference. TensorBoard provides a rich, interactive dashboard that visualizes metrics derived from the training process, model architecture, and data distributions.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEBT746 – Deep Learning Techniques, Module 2)**, TensorBoard is positioned as the standard, industry-recognized tool for **training visualization, model introspection, and hyperparameter analysis** in deep learning pipelines. It operates by reading **event files** (also called *log files*) written by TensorFlow callbacks during model execution and rendering them as interactive plots, graphs, and histograms in a local web server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** TensorBoard is introduced as part of the *Training, Visualization, and Debugging* sub-module. The examiner expects students to know: (1) what TensorBoard is, (2) its purpose, (3) the key panels (Scalars, Graphs, Histograms, Images, HParams), (4) the basic workflow to launch it, and (5) how to log custom scalars.

Formally, TensorBoard acts as a **read-side visualization layer** that decouples the *training process* (write-side) from the *human interpretation* (read-side). The training loop writes scalar summaries, histogram summaries, image summaries, and graph definitions into a designated log directory, and TensorBoard consumes these files asynchronously.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Dashboard of Your AI Car"**

Imagine you are learning to drive a brand-new car, but the car has **no dashboard** — no speedometer, no fuel gauge, no engine-temperature indicator. You press the accelerator, but you have **no idea** if the car is moving, how fast, or whether the engine is overheating. Driving would be terrifying and inefficient.

A deep learning model during training is exactly like this "blind car." The model is processing millions of parameters, calculating gradients, and updating weights — but the engineer (you) cannot see what is happening inside. **TensorBoard is the dashboard of this AI car.**

- The **speedometer** → real-time plots of **loss** and **accuracy** (Scalars panel).
- The **engine blueprint** → a visual map of the **computational graph** (Graphs panel).
- The **fuel-mixture gauge** → histograms showing the **distribution of weights and biases** (Histograms panel).
- The **rear-view camera** → sample **input images** or feature maps (Images panel).
- The **trip-computer** → comparison of multiple **hyperparameter configurations** (HParams panel).

Just as no professional driver would ignore their dashboard, no serious deep learning practitioner should train a model "blind" without TensorBoard.

## 1.3 Why TensorBoard Matters in Deep Learning

Training a deep neural network is an inherently **noisy, high-dimensional, non-convex optimization process**. Without visualization:

1. **Overfitting** may go unnoticed until the model performs poorly on test data.
2. **Vanishing/exploding gradients** can silently destroy learning.
3. **Hyperparameter choices** (learning rate, batch size) cannot be compared rigorously.
4. **Architectural bugs** (e.g., disconnected layers, wrong tensor shapes) can waste hours of GPU time.

TensorBoard solves all of these by providing **real-time, multi-panel introspection** of the training process. The key engineering insight is that TensorBoard is **event-driven** and **non-blocking** — it does not slow down training because the heavy lifting (file I/O) is done by background callbacks, and the visualization happens in a separate browser tab.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential TensorBoard Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Exams):**
>
> | Term | Definition |
> |------|-----------|
> | **Log Directory (`log_dir`)** | Folder on disk where TensorFlow writes event files during training. |
> | **Event File (`.tfevents.*`)** | Binary log file containing serialized summary data (scalars, histograms, images). |
> | **Summary** | A protocol-buffer message (protobuf) holding a single piece of logged data. |
> | **Callback** | A TensorFlow object (`tf.keras.callbacks.TensorBoard`) that automates logging. |
> | **Scalar Summary** | A single numerical value tracked over time (e.g., loss, accuracy). |
> | **Histogram Summary** | Distribution of tensor values across training steps. |
> | **Graph Visualization** | Visual representation of the TensorFlow computation graph. |
> | **`tf.summary` API** | Low-level API for manually writing custom summaries. |
> | **Port (default `6006`)** | The local TCP port on which TensorBoard's web server listens. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Write–Read Pipeline

TensorBoard operates on a **decoupled write–read architecture**. The training script (Python) is the *writer*, and the TensorBoard server (web) is the *reader*. They communicate through files on disk.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Setup Log Directory**: The user specifies a directory (e.g., `./logs/exp1/`) where event files will accumulate.
2. **Attach Callback**: During model training (`model.fit(...)`), a `tf.keras.callbacks.TensorBoard` callback is passed with the `log_dir` argument.
3. **Write Events**: At every epoch (or batch, if configured), the callback writes serialized **summary protobuf** messages into event files. Multiple file types coexist:
   - `events.out.tfevents.{timestamp}.{hostname}` → scalars
   - `plugins/profile/` → performance traces
   - `plugins/hparams/` → hyperparameter metadata
4. **Launch Server**: The user runs `tensorboard --logdir=./logs` in a terminal. TensorBoard starts a local Flask-based web server (default port **6006**).
5. **Browser Render**: The user opens `http://localhost:6006` in a browser. TensorBoard's frontend (written in **Angular/TypeScript**) polls the server, which reads the event files, and renders the interactive panels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Decoupling?** It allows **multi-run comparison**. If you launch two training runs writing to `./logs/run1/` and `./logs/run2/`, TensorBoard automatically overlays their scalar curves on the same plot for direct comparison.

## 2.2 The Seven Core Panels of TensorBoard

TensorBoard is organized into **seven primary panels**, each a tab in the web UI:

| # | Panel Name | What It Shows | When to Use It |
|---|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Scalars** | Line plots of single values over time (loss, accuracy, LR) | Monitor training health, detect overfitting |
| 2 | **Graphs** | Static visualization of the model's computation graph | Debug architecture, verify connectivity |
| 3 | **Histograms** | Distribution of weights/biases/activations per layer | Diagnose dead neurons, vanishing gradients |
| 4 | **Images** | Sample inputs or generated outputs as PNGs | Inspect data augmentation, GAN outputs |
| 5 | **Audio** | Clips of audio waveforms | Speech models, audio generation |
| 6 | **Distributions** | KDE-style plots of histogram evolution | Same as Histograms but visually smoother |
| 7 | **HParams** | Side-by-side comparison of hyperparameter trials | Systematic hyperparameter sweeps |
| 8 | **Profiler** | GPU/TPU trace timeline, kernel-level performance | Optimize training throughput |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While TensorBoard itself is a visualization tool, the **metrics it visualizes** are governed by deep learning mathematics. The following table summarizes the **high-yield formulas** KTU examiners expect you to know when answering questions that combine TensorBoard with model training analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| Symbol / Concept | Formula or Definition | Purpose in TensorBoard Context |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Cross-Entropy Loss (binary) | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Plotted on **Scalars** panel; the primary metric to monitor. |
| Cross-Entropy Loss (multi-class) | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c}\log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | Tracked per-epoch in classification tasks. |
| Mean Squared Error | $L_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Tracked for regression tasks. |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_i = y_i]$ | Plotted alongside loss; should rise while loss falls. |
| Gradient (single step) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t)$ | Histograms of $\nabla_\theta L$ reveal vanishing/exploding gradients. |
| Weight Update (SGD with momentum) | $v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + \nabla_\theta L(\theta_t);\quad \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_{t+1}$ | Monitoring $v$ over time shows momentum stability. |
| Learning Rate Decay | $\eta_t = \eta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \text{decay} \cdot t}$ | Logged as a scalar to verify scheduled decay. |
| Histogram Bin Count (Sturges) | $k = \lceil \log_2(n) + 1 \rceil$ | Default binning strategy TensorBoard uses for histograms. |
| Exponential Moving Average (for smoothing) | $\text{EMA}_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1-\alpha)\cdot \text{EMA}_{t-1}$ | TensorBoard's smoothing slider uses this; default $\alpha = 0.6$. |
| Port Number | **6006** | Default TensorBoard web-server port (changeable via `--port`). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In production ML engineering (e.g., at companies like Google, Meta, or startups in Kerala's Technopark), TensorBoard is the de facto standard for **experiment tracking** alongside more recent tools (Weights & Biases, MLflow). It is bundled with TensorFlow, so it is **free and requires no external account**.

## 2.4 The TensorFlow Summary API — Low-Level Mechanics

For advanced use, TensorFlow exposes a `tf.summary` module that allows manual logging of custom values, images, audio, and even text. The high-level workflow is:

1. Create a `tf.summary.SummaryWriter` pointing to `log_dir`.
2. Inside the training loop, call `with writer.as_default():` to scope logging.
3. Use `tf.summary.scalar()`, `tf.summary.histogram()`, `tf.summary.image()`, `tf.summary.text()` to log data.
4. Call `writer.flush()` periodically to force disk write.
5. Call `writer.close()` at the end of training.

This manual API is what the Keras `TensorBoard` callback wraps internally, so understanding it deepens the "why" behind the abstraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow — From Zero to TensorBoard Visualization

Below is the **complete, step-by-step operational derivation** of a typical TensorBoard integration workflow, written at a level appropriate for a KTU 14-mark descriptive question. Each step is explained with its **purpose** and **engineering rationale**.

### Step 1 — Import Dependencies and Prepare Data

The engineer imports TensorFlow, NumPy, and Keras. The MNIST dataset is normalized to the range $[0, 1]$ so that gradient magnitudes are stable.

$$X_{\text{norm}} = \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}} = \frac{X}{255}$$

**Rationale:** Unscaled pixel intensities in $\{0, 1, \dots, 255\}$ produce gradients with vastly different magnitudes across layers, leading to unstable training. Normalization is a precondition for clean TensorBoard loss curves.

### Step 2 — Define the Model Architecture

A simple **Sequential** feed-forward network is constructed:

$$\text{Model} : \mathbb{R}^{784} \xrightarrow{W_1, b_1} \mathbb{R}^{128} \xrightarrow{\text{ReLU}} \mathbb{R}^{64} \xrightarrow{\text{ReLU}} \mathbb{R}^{10} \xrightarrow{\text{softmax}} \mathbb{R}^{10}$$

The ReLU activation is defined as:

$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z)$$

**Rationale:** The graph panel of TensorBoard will later render these layers as nodes, so the engineer must know the architecture precisely.

### Step 3 — Compile the Model

The model is compiled with three arguments: **optimizer**, **loss**, and **metrics**.

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L(f(x_i;\theta), y_i)$$

Where $L$ is the sparse-categorical cross-entropy loss:

$$L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\log\left(\hat{y}_{i, y_i}\right)$$

**Rationale:** The loss function and the chosen metric (accuracy) are exactly what will appear on the **Scalars** panel of TensorBoard.

### Step 4 — Configure the TensorBoard Callback

The `TensorBoard` callback is instantiated with the following key arguments:

- `log_dir="./logs/mnist_run_1"` → where to write events.
- `histogram_freq=1` → log weight histograms every epoch.
- `write_graph=True` → render the computational graph.
- `update_freq='epoch'` → log scalars once per epoch.
- `profile_batch=2` → enable profiler for the first 2 batches.

**Rationale:** These parameters directly control **what** TensorBoard visualizes and **when**. Misconfiguration (e.g., `histogram_freq=0`) silently disables a panel.

### Step 5 — Train with the Callback Attached

The `model.fit()` call receives the callback:

```text
model.fit(
    X_train, y_train,
    epochs=10,
    validation_data=(X_test, y_test),
    callbacks=[tensorboard_callback]
)
```

For each epoch $e \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$, the callback writes:
- $\text{Loss}_{\text{train}}(e)$, $\text{Acc}_{\text{train}}(e)$
- $\text{Loss}_{\text{val}}(e)$, $\text{Acc}_{\text{val}}(e)$
- $W_1(e), b_1(e), W_2(e), b_2(e)$ histograms

### Step 6 — Launch TensorBoard

From the terminal:

```text
tensorboard --logdir=./logs
```

The server starts on port **6006** and prints a URL: `http://localhost:6006/`.

### Step 7 — Interpret the Visualizations

The engineer opens the URL and inspects each panel. The **analytical matrix** below maps common visual patterns to root causes.

## 3.2 Analytical Diagnosis Matrix — "Reading" TensorBoard

This is one of the **most important tables** for KTU 14-mark questions. It tells you *what to conclude* when you see a particular shape on the Scalars panel.

| Visual Pattern on Scalars Panel | Likely Cause | Recommended Action |
|--------------------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Train loss $\downarrow$, Val loss $\downarrow$ (both fall) | Healthy learning | Continue training |
| Train loss $\downarrow$, Val loss $\uparrow$ (gap widens) | **Overfitting** | Add dropout, regularization, early stopping, or data augmentation |
| Train loss $\downarrow$, Val loss $\downarrow$ (but plateau) | Underfitting / model capacity too small | Increase model size, train longer, lower regularization |
| Train loss stagnant, Val loss stagnant, both high | Learning rate too low, or bug | Increase LR, verify loss function, check data pipeline |
| Train loss oscillating wildly, NaN eventually | Learning rate too high, exploding gradients | Lower LR, apply gradient clipping |
| Train loss $\downarrow$ very fast, val loss also drops but accuracy $\approx 10\%$ | Loss function mismatch (e.g., using MSE for classification) | Switch to cross-entropy |
| Histogram of weights all collapsing to 0 | **Dying ReLU** | Use LeakyReLU, PReLU, or ELU |
| Histogram of gradients is bimodal/separated | Vanishing gradient through deep layers | Use batch normalization, residual connections |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Computing and Logging a Scalar

Suppose at the end of epoch 3, the model has 1000 training samples, with 920 correct predictions. The accuracy and cross-entropy loss at this epoch are:

**Step 1 — Compute Accuracy:**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Loss (assume $\bar{L} = 0.35$):**

$$L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i = 0.35$$

**Step 3 — Log to TensorBoard:**

The callback internally executes the equivalent of:

$$\text{event}_{\text{scalar}} = \text{tf.summary.scalar}(\text{name}=\text{"loss/train"},\ \text{value}=0.35,\ \text{step}=3)$$

This event is serialized to disk in protocol-buffer format inside `events.out.tfevents.{timestamp}.{hostname}`.

**Step 4 — TensorBoard Renders:**

On the Scalars panel, a data point appears at $x = 3$ (epoch), $y = 0.35$ (loss), connected to the data points at epochs 1, 2, 4, 5, ... to form a smooth curve.

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification of the Exponential Smoothing Slider

TensorBoard's **smoothing slider** applies an exponential moving average to the raw scalar values before plotting. Given a raw series $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T\}$ and smoothing constant $\alpha \in [0, 1]$:

$$s_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1 - \alpha)\cdot s_{t-1}, \quad s_0 = x_0$$

Expanding recursively:

$$s_t = \alpha \sum_{k=0}^{t} (1-\alpha)^{t-k} x_k$$

This is a **causal low-pass filter** that suppresses high-frequency noise (caused by mini-batch stochasticity) while preserving the underlying trend. The default $\alpha = 0.6$ corresponds to an effective window of approximately:

$$W_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{0.6} \approx 1.67\ \text{epochs}$$

Setting $\alpha = 0.0$ disables smoothing; $\alpha = 1.0$ shows only the raw value at each step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level TensorBoard Architecture

The diagram below shows the end-to-end data flow: a Python training script writes event files to disk, and a browser reads them through the TensorBoard web server.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Python Training Script] --> B[TensorBoard Callback]
    B --> C[Write Event Files to Log Dir]
    C --> D[events.out.tfevents files]
    D --> E[TensorBoard Web Server Flask App]
    E --> F[Backend Readers Scalars Graphs Histograms]
    F --> G[JSON RPC to Frontend]
    G --> H[Angular Frontend Browser UI]
    H --> I[Scalars Panel]
    H --> J[Graphs Panel]
    H --> K[Histograms Panel]
    H --> L[Images Panel]
    H --> M[HParams Panel]
    H --> N[Profiler Panel]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Detailed Training-Loop Integration Flow

This diagram zooms into the training-loop side, showing how the TensorBoard callback intercepts Keras events.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start model.fit] --> B[Initialize Callback]
    B --> C{On Train Begin}
    C --> D[Write Graph Definition]
    D --> E{On Epoch Begin}
    E --> F[Reset Batch Counter]
    F --> G{On Batch End}
    G --> H[Log Scalar Loss Accuracy]
    H --> I{On Epoch End}
    I --> J[Log Histograms of Weights]
    I --> K[Log Validation Metrics]
    J --> L{More Epochs}
    K --> L
    L -->|Yes| E
    L -->|No| M[On Train End Flush Close Writer]
    M --> N[End]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Multi-Run Comparison Architecture

This diagram shows how TensorBoard automatically discovers and overlays multiple experimental runs that share a parent log directory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Log Dir: ./logs] --> B[Run 1: lr0.001 bs32]
    A --> C[Run 2: lr0.0001 bs32]
    A --> D[Run 3: lr0.001 bs128]
    B --> E[Scalars Panel Overlay]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{User Selects Smoothing Alpha}
    F --> G[Smoothed Multi Run Loss Curve]

    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Custom Summary Logging via tf.summary API

For low-level manual logging, the workflow is as follows.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open tf.summary.SummaryWriter] --> B[with writer.as default]
    B --> C[Log tf.summary.scalar]
    B --> D[Log tf.summary.histogram]
    B --> E[Log tf.summary.image]
    C --> F[Serialize to Event File]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[writer.flush]
    G --> H[writer.close]
    H --> I[TensorBoard Reads File]
    I --> J[Render in Browser]
```
