where $H(\cdot, \cdot)$ denotes the **cross-entropy** between the true and predicted distributions.

### 3.3 Derivation: Perplexity as an Evaluation Metric

**Step 1 — Definition of Perplexity.**
Perplexity is defined as the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy:

$$\boxed{PP = \exp(L) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \hat{y}_{t,\, w_t}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric-Mean Interpretation.**
Exponentiating the average log-probability yields the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities:

$$PP = \left(\prod_{t=1}^{T} \frac{1}{\hat{y}_{t,\, w_t}}\right)^{1/T}$$

**Step 3 — Intuition.**
Perplexity can be interpreted as the **effective branching factor** — the number of equally likely choices the model is "confused" between at each step. A perplexity of **100** means the model is as surprised as if it had to choose uniformly among 100 words.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Walk-Through

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example**
> Consider a vocabulary $\mathcal{V} = \{a, b, c\}$ with $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 3$. Suppose the ground-truth sequence is *"a b c"* and the model emits the following per-step distributions:

| Step $t$ | $w_t$ | $\hat{y}_{t,a}$ | $\hat{y}_{t,b}$ | $\hat{y}_{t,c}$ | $\log \hat{y}_{t,w_t}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | a | **0.70** | 0.20 | 0.10 | $\log 0.70 = -0.357$ |
| 2 | b | 0.30 | **0.55** | 0.15 | $\log 0.55 = -0.598$ |
| 3 | c | 0.15 | 0.25 | **0.60** | $\log 0.60 = -0.511$ |

**Step-by-step computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of log-probs} &= -0.357 + (-0.598) + (-0.511) = -1.466 \\
L &= -\frac{1}{3}(-1.466) = 0.4887 \\
PP &= \exp(0.4887) \approx 1.630
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A perplexity of $\approx 1.63$ (much smaller than $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert = 3$) indicates the model assigns high confidence to the correct sequence.

### 3.5 Back-Propagation Through Time (BPTT) — Conceptual Derivation

The recurrent reuse of $W_{hh}$ means the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to $W_{hh}$ must sum over all time steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}} \\
\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hh}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial W_{hh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $\partial h_t / \partial h_k$ involves a product of Jacobian matrices along the chain $h_k \to h_{k+1} \to \cdots \to h_t$. This is the source of the **vanishing / exploding gradient problem**, motivating the development of **LSTM** and **GRU** architectures.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Synchronous Many-to-Many RNN (e.g., POS Tagging)

The following Mermaid diagram shows a synchronous Many-to-Many RNN unfolded in time. Each time step produces both a hidden state and an output label (e.g., a POS tag).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1["x1 Embedding"] --> nodeH1["h1 = tanh Wx x1 + Wh h0"]
    nodeX2["x2 Embedding"] --> nodeH2["h2 = tanh Wx x2 + Wh h1"]
    nodeX3["x3 Embedding"] --> nodeH3["h3 = tanh Wx x3 + Wh h2"]
    nodeX4["x4 Embedding"] --> nodeH4["h4 = tanh Wx x4 + Wh h3"]

    nodeH0["h0 Zero State"] -.-> nodeH1
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3
    nodeH3 --> nodeH4

    nodeH1 --> nodeO1["o1 = Why h1"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeO2["o2 = Why h2"]
    nodeH3 --> nodeO3["o3 = Why h3"]
    nodeH4 --> nodeO4["o4 = Why h4"]

    nodeO1 --> nodeS1["yhat1 softmax over vocab"]
    nodeO2 --> nodeS2["yhat2 softmax over vocab"]
    nodeO3 --> nodeS3["yhat3 softmax over vocab"]
    nodeO4 --> nodeS4["yhat4 softmax over vocab"]

    nodeS1 --> nodeA1["Loss L1 cross entropy"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeA2["Loss L2 cross entropy"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeA3["Loss L3 cross entropy"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeA4["Loss L4 cross entropy"]

    nodeA1 --> nodeTot["Total Loss L = sum Lk"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeTot
    nodeA3 --> nodeTot
    nodeA4 --> nodeTot
```

**Interpretation:** At every step $t$, the same weight matrices $W_{hx}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused (parameter sharing across time). The outputs $\hat{y}_t$ represent probability distributions over the tag vocabulary.

### 4.2 Asynchronous Many-to-Many RNN (Encoder–Decoder / Seq2Seq)

The following block diagram illustrates the asynchronous variant used for Machine Translation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc1["x1 source token 1"] --> nodeEnc1["h1 enc"]
    nodeSrc2["x2 source token 2"] --> nodeEnc2["h2 enc"]
    nodeSrc3["x3 source token 3"] --> nodeEnc3["h3 enc"]
    nodeSrc4["x4 source token 4"] --> nodeEnc4["h4 enc = context c"]

    nodeEnc0["h0 zero"] -.-> nodeEnc1
    nodeEnc1 --> nodeEnc2
    nodeEnc2 --> nodeEnc3
    nodeEnc3 --> nodeEnc4

    nodeEnc4 --> nodeCtx["Context Vector c"]
    nodeCtx --> nodeDec0["h0 dec = c"]

    nodeDec0 --> nodeDec1["h1 dec"]
    nodeDec1 --> nodeDec2["h2 dec"]
    nodeDec2 --> nodeDec3["h3 dec"]

    nodeDec1 --> nodeOut1["yhat1 target token"]
    nodeDec2 --> nodeOut2["yhat2 target token"]
    nodeDec3 --> nodeOut3["yhat3 EOS or token"]

    nodeOut1 --> nodeInp1["input start token"] --> nodeDec1
    nodeOut2 --> nodeInp2["input previous yhat"] --> nodeDec2
```

**Interpretation:** The encoder compresses the entire source sentence into the context vector $c = h_{T_x}^{(enc)}$. The decoder, initialized from $c$, generates a variable-length target sequence token by token, using its own previous output as the next input (autoregressive decoding).

### 4.3 Functional Block-Level Architecture — Training vs. Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCorpus["Training Corpus"] --> nodeVocab["Build Vocabulary V"]
    nodeVocab --> nodeEmbed["Embedding Matrix E shape V x de"]
    nodeEmbed --> nodeUnroll["Unroll RNN for T time steps"]
    nodeUnroll --> nodeForward["Forward Pass h_t and softmax"]
    nodeForward --> nodeLoss["Cross Entropy Loss L"]
    nodeLoss --> nodeBPTT["BPTT compute gradients"]
    nodeBPTT --> nodeUpdate["Update W_hx W_hh W_hy via SGD or Adam"]
    nodeUpdate --> nodeConverge["Check Convergence by Perplexity"]

    nodeConverge --> nodeInfer["Inference Mode Autoregressive Sampling"]
    nodeInfer --> nodeBeam["Beam Search or Greedy Decoding"]
    nodeBeam --> nodeOutput["Generated Sequence"]
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart separates the **training pipeline** (top: corpus → BPTT) from the **inference pipeline** (bottom: converged model → beam search → output).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define a Many-to-Many RNN architecture. Differentiate between its synchronous and asynchronous variants.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A Many-to-Many RNN is a recurrent architecture that maps an input sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ to an output sequence $(y_1, \ldots, y_{T_y})$. In the **synchronous** variant, an output is produced at every time step ($T_x = T_y$); examples include Part-of-Speech tagging and video frame classification. In the **asynchronous** variant, an Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) structure is used where $T_x \neq T_y$; the encoder reads the full source and the decoder generates a target sequence of different length (e.g., Machine Translation).

---

**Q2. What is perplexity in the context of a language model? How is it computed?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Perplexity (PP) is the standard evaluation metric for language models; it measures the effective branching factor — the average number of choices the model is uncertain about at each step. It is computed as the exponential of the per-token cross-entropy loss:

$$PP = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \hat{y}_{t,\, w_t}\right)$$

A **lower perplexity** indicates a better language model. State-of-the-art large language models typically achieve perplexities in the range of **10–30** on benchmark corpora such as WikiText-103 or Penn Treebank.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / ESE Module Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the Many-to-Many RNN architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss its variants in detail.**
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **Many-to-Many RNN** processes a sequence of inputs and emits a sequence of outputs. The general recurrent update at each time step is:

$$h_t = \tanh(W_{hx}\, x_t + W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + b_h), \quad \hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y)$$

**Variants:**

1. **Synchronous Many-to-Many** — used when input and output sequences have the **same length**. At every step the hidden state produces both the prediction and feeds into the next step. *Applications:* POS tagging, named entity recognition, frame-level video labeling.

2. **Asynchronous Many-to-Many (Encoder–Decoder / Seq2Seq)** — used when input and output lengths differ. The encoder RNN consumes the source sequence and produces a final context vector $c = h_{T_x}^{(enc)}$. The decoder RNN, initialized with $h_0^{(dec)} = c$, autoregressively generates target tokens until an `<EOS>` token is emitted. *Applications:* Machine Translation, Abstractive Summarization, Speech-to-Text.

3. **Many-to-One** — produces a single output after reading the entire input (e.g., sentiment classification). Mentioned here for completeness of the taxonomy.

4. **One-to-Many** — generates a sequence from a single input (e.g., image captioning).

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagrams in Section 4 of these notes for a complete architectural rendering.)

**(b) Explain how an RNN can be used as a language model. Derive the joint probability and the cross-entropy loss formulation.**
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **language model** assigns a probability to any sequence of tokens $w_1, \ldots, w_T$. By the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(w_1, \ldots, w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{<t})$$

The RNN language model approximates each conditional factor using the softmax output conditioned on the recurrent hidden state:

$$P(w_t \mid w_{<t}) \approx \hat{y}_{t,\, w_t} = \frac{\exp(o_{t,\, w_t})}{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(o_{t,j})}$$

**Loss Derivation** — taking the negative log-likelihood and averaging over $T$:

$$L = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log \hat{y}_{t,\, w_t}$$

This is mathematically equivalent to the average **cross-entropy** between the predicted distribution and the one-hot true token.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Discuss the Encoder–Decoder architecture for sequence-to-sequence learning with a neat block diagram. Explain how the context vector is used.**
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Encoder–Decoder** architecture handles asynchronous Many-to-Many mappings ($T_x \neq T_y$). It comprises two RNNs:

- **Encoder**: Consumes the source sequence $(x_1, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ and compresses it into a single fixed-dimensional context vector:

  $$c = h_{T_x}^{(enc)} = f\!\left(W_{hx}^{(e)} x_{T_x} + W_{hh}^{(e)} h_{T_x-1}^{(enc)} + b_h^{(e)}\right)$$

- **Decoder**: Initialized with $h_0^{(dec)} = c$, it autoregressively generates target tokens:

  $$h_t^{(dec)} = f\!\left(W_{hx}^{(d)} y_{t-1} + W_{hh}^{(d)} h_{t-1}^{(dec)} + b_h^{(d)}\right), \quad \hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy}^{(d)} h_t^{(dec)})$$

The context vector $c$ acts as the **initial hidden state** of the decoder, serving as the only information pathway from source to target. During training, teacher forcing is often employed: the previous *ground-truth* token $y_{t-1}$ (not the model's prediction) is fed as input to step $t$.

**Limitation:** All information must pass through a single fixed-size vector $c$, which becomes a bottleneck for long sequences — this motivated the development of **attention mechanisms** and **Transformers**.

**(b) Derive the cross-entropy loss for an RNN language model and explain perplexity as the evaluation metric. Compute perplexity for a sample numerical case.**
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (recap):**

$$L = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert} y_{t,i} \log \hat{y}_{t,i}$$

**Perplexity** is defined as:

$$PP = \exp(L)$$

**Numerical Computation:** Suppose a 4-token sentence is evaluated and the log-probabilities assigned by the model to the true tokens are: $-0.5, -0.8, -0.3, -0.4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= -0.5 - 0.8 - 0.3 - 0.4 = -2.0 \\
L &= -\frac{1}{4}(-2.0) = 0.5 \\
PP &= \exp(0.5) \approx 1.649
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model behaves as if it is choosing among roughly **1.65 equally likely words** at each step — a very confident, well-trained model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Insights)**
> 1. **Confusing Many-to-Many with Many-to-One**: Many students mistakenly describe a Many-to-One (sentiment classifier) as a language model. A true language model emits **one prediction per token** — it is Many-to-Many.
> 2. **Forgetting the chain rule**: When asked to "derive the joint probability," you **must** show $P(w_1,\ldots,w_T) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{<t})$ explicitly, then substitute the RNN softmax output.
> 3. **Mixing up perplexity formula signs**: Perplexity is $\exp(L)$, **not** $\exp(-L)$. The loss $L$ is already a negative log-likelihood, so a *positive* $L$ yields a perplexity greater than 1.
> 4. **Skipping the softmax denominator**: Always show $\sum_j \exp(o_{t,j})$ in the denominator of the softmax; omitting it loses method marks.
> 5. **Drawing the encoder and decoder as one RNN**: In Seq2Seq, the encoder and decoder have **separate** weight sets $W^{(e)}$ and $W^{(d)}$ — they are two different networks.
> 6. **Ignoring teacher forcing**: In training the decoder, mentioning teacher forcing earns a bonus mark in KTU valuations.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Many-to-Many RNN / Language Model**
> - **Architecture taxonomy**: One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-One, **Many-to-Many (sync & async)**.
> - **Synchronous Many-to-Many** → Same length in/out → POS tagging, NER.
> - **Asynchronous Many-to-Many** → Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) → Machine Translation, Summarization.
> - **Hidden state recurrence**: $h_t = \tanh(W_{hx} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$.
> - **Output**: $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$ over the vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$.
> - **Joint probability via chain rule**: $P(w_1,\ldots,w_T) = \prod_t \hat{y}_{t, w_t}$.
> - **Loss**: $L = -\frac{1}{T} \sum_t \log \hat{y}_{t, w_t}$ (cross-entropy / negative log-likelihood).
> - **Perplexity**: $PP = \exp(L)$ — lower is better; interpret as effective branching factor.
> - **Encoder context**: $c = h_{T_x}^{(enc)}$; decoder initialized with $h_0^{(dec)} = c$.
> - **Training**: BPTT unrolls the network across all time steps.
> - **Limitation**: Vanishing/exploding gradients → motivate LSTM and GRU.
> - **Modern extension**: Attention mechanisms overcome the bottleneck of the fixed-size context vector.
> - **Always remember**: Parameter sharing across time means $W_{hx}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused at every step.
> - **Vocabulary size** $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ determines the dimensionality of the output softmax layer and is critical for computational cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vanishing Gradients in Recurrent Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vanishing Gradients in Recurrent Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vanishing Gradient Problem** in Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) is a fundamental numerical instability that occurs during **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**, wherein the gradients of the loss function with respect to the network parameters shrink exponentially as they are propagated backward across many time steps. Consequently, the network fails to learn long-range temporal dependencies because weights associated with distant past inputs receive negligibly small updates during gradient descent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The vanishing gradient problem arises in RNNs when the multiplicative Jacobian products of the chain rule drive the gradient magnitude toward zero, making it computationally infeasible for the optimizer to learn dependencies spanning many time steps. The complementary phenomenon, where gradients grow without bound, is the **Exploding Gradient Problem**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **game of telephone** played in a long corridor of 100 students:

- **Student 1** whispers a complex message to **Student 2**.
- **Student 2** must whisper to **Student 3**, and so on.
- By the time the message reaches **Student 100**, it has either become an **inaudible whisper** (vanishing) or a **screaming distortion** (exploding).

In an RNN, the "message" is the **gradient signal** carrying learning information from the loss function back to early time steps. Just like a whisper that gets quieter with each repetition, the gradient shrinks at every time step when the recurrent weight matrix has small eigenvalues combined with squashing activation functions like **sigmoid** or **tanh**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Examinations**
> This topic is a *high-weightage* concept in Module 3 because it directly motivates advanced architectures like **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)** and **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**, which are heavily tested in Part B questions.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gradient norm | $\|\nabla L\|$ | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{3}$ | Indicates vanishing or exploding |
| Largest singular value of $W_{hh}$ | $\sigma_{\max}$ | $0$ to $\infty$ | Controls gradient flow |
| Sigmoid derivative maximum | $\sigma'(z)_{\max}$ | **0.25** | Causes rapid decay |
| Tanh derivative maximum | $\tanh'(z)_{\max}$ | **1.0** | Boundary case for stability |
| Effective time horizon | $\tau$ | O(1/log($\rho$)) steps | How far back RNN can learn |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematics of Gradient Flow in RNNs

Consider a standard Elman-style RNN unrolled across $T$ time steps with hidden state recurrence:

$$h_t = \tanh\!\left(W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\, x_t + b_h\right)$$

$$y_t = W_{hy}\, h_t + b_y$$

The total loss for a sequence of length $T$ is the sum of per-time-step losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(y_t, \hat{y}_t)$$

### 2.1.1 The Chain Rule Across Time

Using the chain rule of calculus, the gradient of the loss with respect to a hidden state at time $k$ depends on **all future hidden states**:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is the **source of all numerical instabilities** in RNN training.

### 2.1.2 The Recurrent Jacobian

For each time step, the Jacobian of $h_j$ with respect to $h_{j-1}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top} \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2\!\left(W_{hh}\, h_{j-1} + W_{xh}\, x_j + b_h\right)\right)$$

The norm of this Jacobian is bounded by the product of the recurrent weight norm and the activation derivative norm:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot \max_z \left(1 - \tanh^2(z)\right) = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot 1 = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$$

## 2.2 Why Gradients Vanish — The Three Converging Causes

The gradient magnitude over $T-k$ time steps behaves as:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert \propto \Vert W_{hh} \Vert^{\,T-k}$$

Three multiplicative factors conspire to drive this to zero:

- **Cause 1 — Small spectral radius of $W_{hh}$:** If the largest singular value $\sigma_{\max}(W_{hh}) < 1$, the gradient shrinks exponentially.
- **Cause 2 — Saturating activations:** The tanh and sigmoid functions have derivatives strictly less than or equal to 1, with sigmoid bounded at **0.25**, providing an additional contraction factor per step.
- **Cause 3 — Long sequences:** The exponent $T-k$ amplifies the per-step decay, making the problem severe for long sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Think of the gradient as a candle flame. Each time step is a "blow" that can shrink (vanish) or amplify (explode) the flame. With many blows (long sequences), even a small per-step decay accumulates into total darkness.

## 2.3 The Exploding Gradient Companion

When $\sigma_{\max}(W_{hh}) > 1$, the same product explodes exponentially, causing:

- Numerical overflow in floating point arithmetic
- Unstable weight updates
- Training divergence (NaN losses)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY FORMULAS — VANISHING GRADIENTS IN RNNs**

| \# | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Critical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hidden state update | $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b)$ | — |
| 2 | BPTT gradient product | $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} W_{hh}^{\top} \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot))$ | — |
| 3 | Gradient norm bound | $\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert \leq \gamma^{\,t-k}$ | $\gamma = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$ |
| 4 | Sigmoid derivative max | $\sigma'(z)_{\max} = 0.25$ | Vanishing threshold |
| 5 | Tanh derivative max | $\tanh'(0) = 1.0$ | Critical stability point |
| 6 | Exploding condition | $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert > 1$ | Numerical overflow risk |
| 7 | Vanishing condition | $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert < 1$ | Long-range failure |
| 8 | Critical initialization | $\sigma_{\max}(W_{hh}) = 1$ | Gradient-preserving edge |
| 9 | Effective horizon | $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{\log(1/\gamma)}$ time steps | For $\gamma < 1$ |
| 10 | Gradient clipping rule | $g \leftarrow \dfrac{\tau}{\Vert g \Vert} g$ if $\Vert g \Vert > \tau$ | Pascanu et al. (2013) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The vanishing gradient problem is not merely academic — it has driven entire industries of research:

- **Speech Recognition:** Early RNNs could not model phonemes separated by more than ~5 frames; LSTMs extended this to thousands of frames, enabling Siri, Alexa, and Google Assistant.
- **Machine Translation:** Google's Neural Machine Translation system uses LSTMs/GRUs to handle long sentences, which would be impossible with vanilla RNNs.
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Financial models, weather prediction, and biomedical signal processing all rely on gated architectures to capture long-range temporal correlations.
- **Music Generation:** Composing coherent melodies requires remembering motifs introduced many bars earlier.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Vanishing Gradient Magnitude in Vanilla RNNs

We will derive the exact expression for the gradient norm when backpropagating across $T$ time steps. This is a *core KTU derivation* and is highly likely to appear in Part B (14 marks).

### Step 1 — Define the Recurrent Cell

Let the RNN be defined by:

$$h_t = f\!\left(W_{hh}\, h_{t-1} + W_{xh}\, x_t + b_h\right)$$

where $f(\cdot) = \tanh(\cdot)$ is the element-wise activation function.

### Step 2 — Express the Hidden State as a Composition of Functions

By repeated substitution, $h_t$ depends on $h_{t-1}$, which depends on $h_{t-2}$, and so on:

$$h_t = f\!\left(W_{hh}\, f\!\left(W_{hh}\, h_{t-2} + W_{xh}\, x_{t-1} + b_h\right) + W_{xh}\, x_t + b_h\right)$$

This nested composition is the source of the deep multiplicative product in the chain rule.

### Step 3 — Apply the Multivariable Chain Rule

Differentiate the loss $L$ at the final time step $T$ with respect to $h_k$ for some $k < T$:

$$\frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k} = \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \cdot \frac{\partial h_T}{\partial h_{T-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{T-1}}{\partial h_{T-2}} \cdots \frac{\partial h_{k+1}}{\partial h_k}$$

Written compactly:

$$\frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k} = \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Per-Step Jacobian

For each factor in the product:

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top} \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(f'\!\left(W_{hh}\, h_{j-1} + W_{xh}\, x_j + b_h\right)\right)$$

### Step 5 — Bound the Norm of Each Jacobian

Using the submultiplicative property of matrix norms and the fact that $\vert f'(z) \vert \leq 1$ for $\tanh$:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot 1 = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$$

### Step 6 — Bound the Full Product

Applying the submultiplicative property $T-k$ times:

$$\left\Vert \prod_{j=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \prod_{j=k+1}^{T} \left\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert^{\,T-k}$$

### Step 7 — The Final Vanishing/Exploding Condition

Defining $\gamma = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$, we obtain the celebrated result:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert \leq \gamma^{\,T-k} \cdot \left\Vert \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \right\Vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:**
> - If $\gamma < 1$: Gradient **vanishes** exponentially as $T - k$ grows.
> - If $\gamma > 1$: Gradient **explodes** exponentially as $T - k$ grows.
> - If $\gamma = 1$: Gradient magnitude is preserved — but this is a knife-edge condition rarely maintained in practice.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Exam Style):**
> Consider a vanilla RNN with $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert = 0.8$ and tanh activation. Compute the gradient magnitude at time step $k=1$ relative to time step $T=20$, and interpret the result.

**Solution:**

Apply the bound:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial L_{20}}{\partial h_1} \right\Vert \leq (0.8)^{20-1} \cdot \left\Vert \frac{\partial L_{20}}{\partial h_{20}} \right\Vert$$

$$(0.8)^{19} = 0.8^{19} \approx 0.0144$$

The gradient at $h_1$ is roughly **1.44%** of the gradient at $h_{20}$. With a learning rate of $0.001$, the weight update for $h_1$ is approximately $1.44 \times 10^{-5}$ times the original loss gradient — essentially negligible. The RNN will fail to learn any meaningful relationship between input at time 1 and output at time 20.

**Effect of activation function:** If we replace tanh with sigmoid, the per-step bound becomes $0.8 \times 0.25 = 0.2$, and the gradient decays as $(0.2)^{19} \approx 5.2 \times 10^{-14}$ — effectively zero in floating-point precision.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Sigmoid Activation | Tanh Activation | ReLU Activation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max derivative | **0.25** | **1.0** | **1.0** |
| Vanishing severity | Severe | Moderate | Mild (dying ReLU risk) |
| Sign of output | Always positive | Zero-centered | Always positive |
| Recommended in RNN? | No | Yes (classic) | Use with care |
| KTU Exam Recommendation | Avoid | Default choice | Discuss dying ReLU issue |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Unrolled RNN Architecture Showing Gradient Path

This diagram shows how a recurrent network is unrolled across time steps, with the gradient signal flowing backward through the chain of identical weight matrices $W_{hh}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["INPUT X1"] --> n1["HIDDEN H1"]
    n1 --> n2["HIDDEN H2"]
    n2 --> n3["HIDDEN H3"]
    n3 --> n4["HIDDEN HT"]
    n4 --> yT["OUTPUT YT"]
    Whh1["WEIGHT WHH"] -.-> n1
    Whh2["WEIGHT WHH"] -.-> n2
    Whh3["WEIGHT WHH"] -.-> n3
    Whh4["WEIGHT WHH"] -.-> n4
    yT -.->|"GRADIENT FLOW BACK"| n4
    n4 -.->|"CHAIN RULE"| n3
    n3 -.->|"CHAIN RULE"| n2
    n2 -.->|"CHAIN RULE"| n1
    n1 -.->|"VANISHES NEAR ZERO"| loss1["EFFECTIVE GRADIENT AT T1"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Causes, Effects, and Solutions

This functional architecture flow maps the relationship between the root causes of the vanishing gradient problem, its observable effects, and the engineering solutions used in practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["RECURRENT NEURAL NETWORK"] --> B["TRAINING VIA BPTT"]
    B --> C["GRADIENT COMPUTATION"]
    C --> D{"EIGENVALUE CHECK ON WHH"}
    D -->|"EIGENVALUE LESS THAN 1"| E["CAUSE 1 SMALL SPECTRAL RADIUS"]
    D -->|"ACTIVATION SIGMOID"| F["CAUSE 2 SATURATING NONLINEARITY"]
    D -->|"LONG SEQUENCE LENGTH"| G["CAUSE 3 LARGE EXPONENT T MINUS K"]
    E --> H["GRADIENT VANISHING"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["OBSERVABLE EFFECT 1 NO LEARNING FOR LONG RANGE"]
    H --> J["OBSERVABLE EFFECT 2 STAGNANT LOSS CURVE"]
    H --> K["OBSERVABLE EFFECT 3 UNIFORM WEIGHT UPDATES"]
    I --> L["SOLUTION A LSTM GATED CELLS"]
    J --> M["SOLUTION B GRU GATED RESET UPDATE"]
    K --> N["SOLUTION C GRADIENT CLIPPING"]
    I --> O["SOLUTION D ORTHOGONAL INITIALIZATION"]
    J --> P["SOLUTION E SKIP CONNECTIONS"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Gradient Magnitude vs. Time Step

The following block-flow diagram illustrates the exponential decay of gradient magnitude as we backpropagate through time. The vertical axis represents gradient magnitude and the horizontal axis represents the time step index.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0["TIME STEP 20 GRADIENT 1.0"] --> s1["TIME STEP 19 GRADIENT 0.8"]
    s1 --> s2["TIME STEP 18 GRADIENT 0.64"]
    s2 --> s3["TIME STEP 17 GRADIENT 0.51"]
    s3 --> s4["TIME STEP 16 GRADIENT 0.41"]
    s4 --> s5["TIME STEP 15 GRADIENT 0.33"]
    s5 --> s6["TIME STEP 10 GRADIENT 0.10"]
    s6 --> s7["TIME STEP 5 GRADIENT 0.03"]
    s7 --> s8["TIME STEP 1 GRADIENT 0.014"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:**
> The flow above uses $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert = 0.8$ and shows the gradient shrinking by a factor of 0.8 at every time step. By time step 1, the gradient is approximately **1.4%** of its original value at time step 20, demonstrating the vanishing phenomenon.

## 4.4 Functional Comparison: Vanilla RNN vs. LSTM Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RNN["VANILLA RNN SINGLE Tanh LAYER"] --> RNN1["GRADIENT DECAYS EXPONENTIALLY"]
    RNN1 --> RNN2["NO LONG RANGE MEMORY"]
    LSTM["LSTM CELL WITH FORGET GATE"] --> LSTM1["GRADIENT FLOWS THROUGH CELL STATE UNDISTURBED"]
    LSTM1 --> LSTM2["LONG RANGE MEMORY PRESERVED"]
    RNN2 --> P["KTU ANSWER PREFER LSTM FOR LONG SEQUENCES"]
    LSTM2 --> P
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Vanishing Gradient Definition**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the vanishing gradient problem in Recurrent Neural Networks. Why is it particularly severe in RNNs compared to feedforward networks?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The vanishing gradient problem in Recurrent Neural Networks refers to the phenomenon where, during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), the gradient of the loss function with respect to early time-step weights shrinks exponentially toward zero, making those weights effectively un-trainable.

It is particularly severe in RNNs because of two compounding factors:
1. **Repeated multiplication by the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$** across many time steps creates a long chain-rule product $\prod \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$.
2. **Saturating nonlinearities** like tanh and sigmoid have derivatives bounded at or below 1, multiplying the decay at every step.

In contrast, feedforward networks have a fixed number of layers, so the chain-rule product has bounded length, allowing alternative activations like ReLU to mitigate the problem. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: Role of Activation Function**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Why does the choice of activation function significantly affect the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? Support your answer with the maximum derivative values of sigmoid and tanh.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The activation function directly multiplies the gradient at every time step during backpropagation. If the activation derivative is small, the gradient shrinks at each step, causing vanishing.

- **Sigmoid:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$, with maximum value **0.25** at $z = 0$. This is the most common cause of severe gradient decay in RNNs.
- **Tanh:** $\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^2(z)$, with maximum value **1.0** at $z = 0$. Tanh is preferred over sigmoid for RNNs because its derivative can equal 1, allowing the gradient to be preserved (combined with appropriate weight initialization).

If the activation derivative is consistently less than 1, even a unitary recurrent matrix will see the gradient decay at the rate of the activation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A: Detailed Analysis of Vanishing Gradients in RNNs**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Mathematical Derivation (7 marks)

**Q:** Consider a vanilla RNN defined by the recurrence $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b)$ with loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$. Derive the expression for the gradient $\frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k}$ and show under what conditions this gradient vanishes.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the BPTT gradient formula:**

$$\frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k} = \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \cdot \prod_{j=k+1}^{T} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the per-step Jacobian:**

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = W_{hh}^{\top} \cdot \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(W_{hh} h_{j-1} + W_{xh} x_j + b)\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the operator norm:**

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot \max_z \vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$$

**Step 4 — Apply submultiplicativity across $T-k$ steps:**

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_k} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert^{\,T-k} \cdot \left\Vert \frac{\partial L_T}{\partial h_T} \right\Vert$$

**Step 5 — State vanishing condition:** If $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert < 1$, the bound $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert^{\,T-k}$ decays exponentially as $T - k$ grows, forcing the gradient to zero. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Solutions and Analysis (7 marks)

**Q:** List and briefly explain three architectural or algorithmic solutions to the vanishing gradient problem. Justify why LSTM is the most effective among them.

**Model Solution:**

**Solution 1 — Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Networks (3 marks):**
LSTM introduces a **cell state** $C_t$ that propagates through time with a **forget gate** controlling how much past information to retain:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

The gradient flows through the cell state via element-wise multiplication by $f_t$ (the forget gate, output in $[0, 1]$), which can be learned to be close to 1, preserving gradient information over long time horizons.

**Solution 2 — Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) (2 marks):**
GRU simplifies the LSTM by combining the forget and input gates into a single **update gate** $z_t$, and uses a **reset gate** $r_t$ to control short-term memory. It achieves similar performance to LSTM with fewer parameters.

**Solution 3 — Gradient Clipping (2 marks):**
Proposed by Pascanu et al., this technique rescales gradients whose norm exceeds a threshold $\tau$:

$$g \leftarrow \frac{\tau}{\Vert g \Vert} g \quad \text{if} \quad \Vert g \Vert > \tau$$

This addresses the **exploding** gradient problem but does not solve vanishing gradients directly. It is a complementary technique.

**Justification for LSTM superiority:** LSTMs solve the vanishing problem architecturally by providing an **additive gradient path** through the cell state, in contrast to the purely multiplicative path in vanilla RNNs. This is a fundamentally more powerful design for long sequences. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B: Numerical Analysis and Architectural Remedies**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Q:** A vanilla RNN uses the sigmoid activation and has a recurrent weight matrix with spectral norm $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert = 0.9$. Estimate the ratio of the gradient magnitude at $h_1$ to that at $h_{20}$ when the sequence length is $T = 20$. Repeat the calculation if tanh activation is used (treating the maximum derivative as 1). Comment on the practical implication.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Establish the gradient ratio formula:**

$$\frac{\left\Vert \frac{\partial L_{20}}{\partial h_1} \right\Vert}{\left\Vert \frac{\partial L_{20}}{\partial h_{20}} \right\Vert} = \left(\Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot \max \vert f'(z) \vert\right)^{T-1} = \left(\gamma_{\text{eff}}\right)^{19}$$

**Step 2 — Sigmoid calculation:**

$$\gamma_{\text{eff}} = 0.9 \times 0.25 = 0.225$$

$$\text{Ratio}_{\text{sigmoid}} = (0.225)^{19}$$

Computing: $0.225^2 = 0.0506$, $0.225^4 \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-3}$, ..., $0.225^{19} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-13}$.

The gradient is effectively **zero** in 32-bit floating point. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Tanh calculation:**

$$\gamma_{\text{eff}} = 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.9$$

$$\text{Ratio}_{\text{tanh}} = (0.9)^{19}$$

Computing: $0.9^{19} \approx 0.135$ or about **13.5%** of the original gradient. The signal is preserved, though diminished. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Practical implication:** With sigmoid, the network cannot learn any dependency between time step 1 and time step 20. With tanh, learning is degraded but still possible, which is why **tanh is the default activation for vanilla RNNs** in classical architectures. **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Activation Function and Initialization Trade-offs (7 marks)

**Q:** Discuss how orthogonal initialization of $W_{hh}$ addresses the vanishing gradient problem. Why is it insufficient as a standalone solution for very long sequences?

**Model Solution:**

**Orthogonal Initialization (4 marks):**
A matrix $W$ is orthogonal if $W^{\top} W = I$. Its singular values are all equal to 1, so the spectral norm is exactly 1:

$$\Vert W_{hh} \Vert = 1 \implies \gamma = 1$$

With orthogonal $W_{hh}$ and tanh activation, the per-step gradient norm is bounded by 1, preventing the **exponential decay** responsible for vanishing gradients. The gradient signal is therefore preserved (neither vanishing nor exploding) at each step, allowing the network to learn longer-range dependencies than with random Gaussian initialization (which typically has singular values $\ll 1$).

However, this is an **upper bound**. The actual per-step norm can be strictly less than 1 due to the tanh derivative at non-zero activations, which is always strictly less than 1 when $z \neq 0$:

$$\max_z \vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert = 1 \text{ achieved only at } z = 0$$

In practice, the activation inputs $W_{hh} h_{j-1} + W_{xh} x_j + b$ are rarely exactly zero, so the gradient is bounded by $\gamma_{\text{eff}} < 1$, leading to slow but non-vanishing decay.

**Why Insufficient for Very Long Sequences (3 marks):**
Even with orthogonal initialization, the gradient decays as $\gamma_{\text{eff}}^{T-k}$ for some $\gamma_{\text{eff}} < 1$. For very long sequences (e.g., $T > 200$), the cumulative decay can still render early gradients negligible. Furthermore, orthogonal initialization only addresses the **first** of the three vanishing causes (spectral radius); it does not address the activation-induced decay. A more robust solution is to use a **gated architecture** like LSTM, which provides an **additive gradient path** through the cell state, allowing gradients to flow over thousands of steps with minimal decay. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the activation derivative in the gradient bound:** Many students write only $\Vert W_{hh} \Vert^{T-k}$ and ignore the factor of $\max \vert f'(z) \vert$. For sigmoid, this omission yields an answer that is **four times too large**, missing the actual vanishing severity. **Always include both factors.**
>
> 2. **Confusing vanishing with exploding:** The bound is the same form, but the condition reverses: vanishing when $\gamma < 1$, exploding when $\gamma > 1$. Examiners will deduct marks if you state the wrong direction.
>
> 3. **Stating that LSTM *eliminates* the problem entirely:** LSTM *mitigates* the problem by providing an additive gradient path. The problem is reduced, not eliminated.
>
> 4. **Confusing BPTT with standard backpropagation:** BPTT is backpropagation applied to an *unrolled* computational graph. Examiners expect you to mention "unrolling" explicitly.
>
> 5. **Hand-waving on the orthogonality condition:** When asked about orthogonal initialization, you must explicitly state that all singular values equal 1, giving spectral norm = 1.
>
> 6. **Missing the chain rule product:** A common error is to write a sum rather than a product across time steps. The product form is what creates the exponential behavior.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — VANISHING GRADIENTS IN RNNS**

- **Core definition:** Gradients shrink exponentially during BPTT in RNNs, preventing learning of long-range dependencies. **(Module 3 core concept)**
- **Mathematical signature:** Gradient norm bounded by $\gamma^{\,T-k}$ where $\gamma = \Vert W_{hh} \Vert \cdot \max \vert f'(z) \vert$.
- **Vanishing condition:** $\gamma < 1$ (most common in practice).
- **Exploding condition:** $\gamma > 1$ (less common, easier to fix with clipping).
- **Sigmoid maximum derivative:** **0.25** — primary cause of severe vanishing.
- **Tanh maximum derivative:** **1.0** — preferred activation for vanilla RNNs.
- **Three causes of vanishing:** (1) small spectral radius of $W_{hh}$, (2) saturating activations, (3) long sequences.
- **Orthogonal initialization:** Sets all singular values to 1, preserving gradient norm — but does not fully solve the problem for very long sequences.
- **Gradient clipping:** Addresses exploding gradients only; not a solution for vanishing.
- **LSTM:** Provides additive gradient path via cell state; the most effective architectural solution.
- **GRU:** Simplified LSTM with update and reset gates; comparable performance with fewer parameters.
- **Effective horizon formula:** $\tau \approx 1 / \log(1/\gamma)$ time steps — quantifies how far back the network can learn.
- **Exam tip:** Always write the chain rule product explicitly and bound each factor separately.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** is a specialized variant of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architecture introduced by **Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber (1997)**, engineered to overcome the **Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem** inherent in conventional RNNs during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT).

Unlike vanilla RNNs that maintain a single hidden state $h_t$, LSTMs introduce a **memory cell** (also called a **cell state** $C_t$) regulated by three multiplicative **gating mechanisms**:

1. **Forget Gate** ($f_t$) — controls what information to discard from the cell state.
2. **Input Gate** ($i_t$) — controls what new information to store in the cell state.
3. **Output Gate** ($o_t$) — controls what parts of the cell state to output as the hidden state.

A fourth candidate mechanism, the **Cell Candidate** ($\tilde{C}_t$), produces new values that could be added to the state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to derive the forward pass equations of all four gates, explain why LSTMs mitigate vanishing gradients, and contrast them with **Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt with Smart Valves"

Imagine a **factory conveyor belt** running along a long production line:

- The **belt itself** = the **Cell State** $C_t$. It carries raw material (information) from the beginning of the sequence ($t=1$) to the end ($t=T$) with minimal alteration.
- **Three smart valves** are installed along the belt, each controlled by a tiny **regulator** (a sigmoid neural network layer):
  - **Valve 1 (Forget Gate)** — decides whether old material should be **dumped** or **kept**.
  - **Valve 2 (Input Gate)** — decides whether **new material** should be **loaded** onto the belt.
  - **Valve 3 (Output Gate)** — decides how much of the belt's current contents should be **delivered** to the next workstation (the next hidden state).

This way, **important signals can travel hundreds of timesteps without dilution**, solving the vanishing gradient problem.

---

## 1.3 Standard Hyperparameters & Initialization Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are standard defaults used across KTU reference implementations and most production-grade LSTM deployments:

| Hyperparameter | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Activation (gates)** | **sigmoid ($\sigma$)** — output range $\mathbf{[0, 1]}$ | Acts as a soft "valve" (0 = block, 1 = pass) |
| **Activation (candidate/output)** | **tanh** — output range $\mathbf{[-1, 1]}$ | Keeps cell values bounded |
| **Weight Initialization** | **Xavier/Glorot** for $\tanh$ layers | Prevents early saturation |
| **Gate bias initialization** | Often **$b_f = 1$** (forget bias) | Encourages initial remembering |
| **Hidden size $n_h$** | 128 – 512 | Capacity of memory |
| **Sequence length $T$** | 50 – 500 | Number of timesteps |

---

## 1.4 Why LSTMs Matter — Engineering Use Cases

- **Time-Series Forecasting** (stock prices, energy load, weather).
- **Natural Language Processing** (machine translation, sentiment analysis, speech recognition).
- **Anomaly Detection** in IoT sensor streams.
- **Music Generation**, **Video Captioning**, **Medical Signal Analysis** (ECG, EEG).
- Foundation for **Encoder-Decoder** and **Seq2Seq** architectures (Module 4 preview).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture Walk-Through — The LSTM Cell at a Single Timestep $t$

At every timestep $t$, the LSTM cell receives **two inputs**:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ — the current input vector (dimension $d$).
- $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — the previous hidden state (dimension $h$).

It maintains **two state vectors**:
- $C_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — the previous cell (memory) state.
- $h_{t-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ — the previous hidden state.

It produces **two outputs**:
- $C_t$ — the updated cell state.
- $h_t$ — the updated hidden state (which is also the output $y_t$ when no extra output layer exists).

---

## 2.2 The Four Sub-Layers (Step-by-Step Logic)

### 🔹 Step 1 — Forget Gate $f_t$
**Why?** Determines what proportion of the *previous* memory to retain.
**How?** A sigmoid layer looks at $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ and outputs a value in $[0, 1]$ for each dimension of $C_{t-1}$. A value of **1** means "keep completely"; **0** means "forget completely".

### 🔹 Step 2 — Input Gate $i_t$ and Candidate Cell $\tilde{C}_t$
**Why?** Two operations decide *what new information* to store:
- $i_t$ (sigmoid) acts as the **selector**.
- $\tilde{C}_t$ ($\tanh$) creates the **candidate vector** of new values.
**How?** Both layers read the concatenated $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$; their product decides what new memory gets written.

### 🔹 Step 3 — Cell State Update $C_t$
**Why?** Combines old memory (after forgetting) with new candidate memory (after input gating).
**How?** Element-wise multiplication and addition:
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

### 🔹 Step 4 — Output Gate $o_t$ and Hidden State $h_t$
**Why?** Controls *what part of the cell state* is exposed as the hidden output for the current timestep.
**How?** $o_t$ filters the $\tanh$-squashed $C_t$ to produce $h_t$:
$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use the exact symbol conventions below for your KTU 2024 ESE answers — they match board valuation key patterns.**

| # | Equation | Purpose | Activation | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Forget gate | sigmoid | $[0, 1]^h$ |
| 2 | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Input gate | sigmoid | $[0, 1]^h$ |
| 3 | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | Candidate memory | tanh | $[-1, 1]^h$ |
| 4 | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Cell state update | linear | $\mathbb{R}^h$ |
| 5 | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Output gate | sigmoid | $[0, 1]^h$ |
| 6 | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Hidden state output | tanh-filtered | $[-1, 1]^h$ |

### 🔸 Dimensions Reference

| Symbol | Shape | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $x_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d \times 1}$ | Input at time $t$ |
| $h_{t-1}, h_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times 1}$ | Hidden states |
| $C_{t-1}, C_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times 1}$ | Cell (memory) states |
| $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times (h+d)}$ | Gate weight matrices |
| $b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$ | $\mathbb{R}^{h \times 1}$ | Gate bias vectors |
| $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ | $\mathbb{R}^{(h+d) \times 1}$ | Concatenated input vector |
| $\odot$ | — | **Element-wise (Hadamard) product** |

### 🔸 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **predictive maintenance of rotating machinery**, an LSTM is fed vibration signals of length $T = 1024$. The cell state $C_t$ retains long-term wear signatures across thousands of cycles, while the gates dynamically suppress transient noise — a classic application where **conventional RNNs fail** due to gradient decay over $T \geq 100$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Concatenation Trick and Gate Unification

For computational efficiency, the four weight matrices $W_f, W_i, W_C, W_o$ are often stacked into a single matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{4h \times (h+d)}$. Here is the **unified derivation**.

### Step 1 — Concatenate input
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } z_t &= [h_{t-1};\ x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{(h+d) \times 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> We stack the previous hidden state $h_{t-1}$ (size $h$) above the current input $x_t$ (size $d$) to form a single column vector of length $h+d$.

### Step 2 — Compute the gate pre-activations in a single matrix product
$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} a_f \\ a_i \\ a_C \\ a_o \end{bmatrix}_t
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
W_f \\ W_i \\ W_C \\ W_o
\end{bmatrix}
\cdot z_t
+
\begin{bmatrix}
b_f \\ b_i \\ b_C \\ b_o
\end{bmatrix}
\\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad
\mathbf{a}_t &= W \cdot z_t + b \quad \in \mathbb{R}^{4h \times 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This reduces 4 separate matrix multiplications to 1, giving roughly a **4× GPU-speedup** in practice.

### Step 3 — Apply element-wise activations
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma(a_f^{(t)}), \quad i_t = \sigma(a_i^{(t)}), \quad o_t = \sigma(a_o^{(t)}) \\[4pt]
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(a_C^{(t)})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Update cell state and hidden state
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\[4pt]
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **complete forward pass** at timestep $t$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Gradient Flow Through the Cell State (Why LSTMs Beat RNNs)

The cell state update rule $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ is essentially a **learned residual connection**.

Let us compute $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}
&= \frac{\partial}{\partial C_{t-1}} \Big( f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

Because $f_t$ depends on $C_{t-1}$ only **indirectly** through $h_{t-1}$ (which itself is a function of $C_{t-1}$), the **direct** partial derivative — the one that matters for the gradient highway — is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}\bigg|_{\text{direct}} &= f_t \quad \text{(an element-wise product)}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the gradient flowing back through $k$ timesteps along the cell-state highway is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}}
&= \prod_{j=1}^{k} f_{t-j+1} \quad \text{(element-wise)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare this to vanilla RNN** where the analogous term is $\prod_{j=1}^{k} \text{diag}(\tanh'(...) W_h)$. Since $\tanh'(\cdot) \leq 1$ and the $W_h$ spectral radius is often $\lt 1$, this product **decays exponentially** — the **vanishing gradient**.

In an LSTM, the **forget gate $f_{t-j+1}$ is learned** and can stay close to **1**, so the gradient product remains **near 1**, enabling learning across **100+ timesteps**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in your Module 3 syllabus. KTU examiners frequently award a full 7-mark sub-question for this analysis.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Single-Timestep Forward Pass

**Given** (hypothetical KTU numerical):
- $x_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$, $h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix}$, $C_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$
- Forget bias $b_f = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (standard forget-bias initialization).

Assume all weights $W_f = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.2 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.4 & 0.1 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$ and the others are pre-supplied.

### Step 1 — Concatenate
$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] = [0.2,\ -0.3,\ 0.5,\ 1.0]^T
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute forget-gate pre-activation
$$
\begin{aligned}
a_f^{(t)} = W_f \cdot z_t + b_f
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.3(0.2) + 0.1(-0.3) + 0.2(0.5) + (-0.1)(1.0) \\ -0.2(0.2) + 0.4(-0.3) + 0.1(0.5) + 0.3(1.0) \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.06 - 0.03 + 0.10 - 0.10 \\ -0.04 - 0.12 + 0.05 + 0.30 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.03 \\ 0.19 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.03 \\ 1.19 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply sigmoid
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t = \sigma\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} 1.03 \\ 1.19 \end{bmatrix}\right) &\approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.737 \\ 0.767 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Students should use $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$.)

> Repeat analogous steps for $i_t$, $\tilde{C}_t$, $o_t$, then combine using $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ and $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Single LSTM Cell Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef gate fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef state fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000

    xt[/x_t input/]:::state
    hprev[/h_t-1 hidden prev/]:::state
    cprev[/C_t-1 cell prev/]:::state

    concat1((concat z_t)):::op
    concat2((concat z_t)):::op
    concat3((concat z_t)):::op
    concat4((concat z_t)):::op

    Wf[Wf matmul + bf]:::gate
    Wi[Wi matmul + bi]:::gate
    Wc[Wc matmul + bc]:::gate
    Wo[Wo matmul + bo]:::gate

    sigf[sigmoid sigma]:::act
    sigi[sigmoid sigma]:::act
    sigo[sigmoid sigma]:::act
    tanhc[tanh]:::act

    fgate[/f_t forget gate/]:::gate
    igate[/i_t input gate/]:::gate
    ctil[/Ctilde_t candidate/]:::state

    mult2((x input: i_t * Ctilde_t)):::op

    tanh2[tanh]:::act
    mult3((x output: o_t * tanh C_t)):::op

    hprev --> concat1
    xt --> concat1

    hprev --> concat2

    hprev --> concat3
    xt --> concat3
    concat3 --> Wc --> tanhc --> ctil

    xt --> concat4

    fgate --> mult1
    cprev --> mult1
    igate --> mult2
    ctil --> mult2
    mult1 --> add1
    mult2 --> add1
    add1 --> cnew
    cnew --> tanh2 --> mult3
    ogate --> mult3
    mult3 --> hnew

    hnew -.feedback to t+1.-> hprev
    cnew -.feedback to t+1.-> cprev
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Forget Gate and the Input Gate of an LSTM cell. Why are sigmoid activations preferred for gates while tanh is used for the cell candidate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forget Gate $f_t$ | Input Gate $i_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Decides what to **discard** from $C_{t-1}$ | Decides what **new info** to **store** in $C_t$ |
| **Multiplies with** | $C_{t-1}$ (old memory) | $\tilde{C}_t$ (new candidate) |
| **Output range** | $[0, 1]$ via sigmoid | $[0, 1]$ via sigmoid |

- Sigmoid output range $[0, 1]$ acts as a soft **multiplicative mask** (0 = block, 1 = allow), ideal for gating.
- $\tanh$ output range $[-1, 1]$ allows the candidate cell $\tilde{C}_t$ to represent both **increases** and **decreases** of memory, providing signed values that can be either added or subtracted via the input gate. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the vanishing gradient problem in vanilla RNNs. Explain in one paragraph how the LSTM cell-state update equation helps to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In vanilla RNNs, the hidden state update $h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t)$ repeated across $T$ timesteps causes gradients to be multiplied by $\text{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot)) \cdot W_h$ at every step. Because $\vert \tanh'(z) \vert \le 1$ and the spectral radius of $W_h$ is typically $< 1$, the gradient product shrinks **exponentially** with $T$ — leading to vanishing gradients and inability to learn long-range dependencies.

In an LSTM, the cell state updates as $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$. The forget gate $f_t$ is **learned** and can remain close to $1$, so the gradient product $\prod f_{t-j+1}$ does not vanish. This additive + multiplicative gating structure creates a **gradient highway** that preserves long-range signals. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of an LSTM cell. Label all gates, the cell state, the hidden state, and write down the forward-pass equations for **all four** mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

The LSTM cell at timestep $t$ receives two inputs — $x_t$ and $h_{t-1}$ — and produces two outputs — $h_t$ and $C_t$. It contains **four neural sub-layers**: three sigmoid gates ($f_t, i_t, o_t$) and one tanh candidate layer ($\tilde{C}_t$).

**Forward Pass Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_f [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_f\right) \quad &\text{(Forget Gate)} \\[4pt]
i_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_i [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_i\right) \quad &\text{(Input Gate)} \\[4pt]
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh\!\left(W_C [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_C\right) \quad &\text{(Candidate Cell)} \\[4pt]
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \quad &\text{(Cell State Update)} \\[4pt]
o_t &= \sigma\!\left(W_o [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_o\right) \quad &\text{(Output Gate)} \\[4pt]
h_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t) \quad &\text{(Hidden State Output)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split:** Diagram — 3 marks; Equation listing — 2 marks; Explanation of each gate — 2 marks. **(Total 7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the gradient $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}$ for the LSTM cell-state update equation and show how this enables learning across long sequences. Compare with vanilla RNN. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}
&= \frac{\partial (f_t \odot C_{t-1})}{\partial C_{t-1}} + \frac{\partial (i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t)}{\partial C_{t-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second term is negligible relative to the dominant first term. Considering the **direct** path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}}\bigg|_{\text{direct}} &= f_t
\end{aligned}
$$

Unrolling across $k$ steps:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} &= \prod_{j=1}^{k} f_{t-j+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since each $f_{t-j+1} \in [0, 1]$ is **learned** and the network can keep it close to $1$, this product **does not decay exponentially** — the gradient flows unimpeded across hundreds of timesteps.

In contrast, vanilla RNN has:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-k}} &= \prod_{j=1}^{k} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - h_{t-j+1}^2\right) W_h
\end{aligned}
$$

whose magnitude decays as $(\lambda_{\max})^{k}$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the spectral radius of $W_h$. For $\lambda_{\max} < 1$, gradients vanish; for $\lambda_{\max} > 1$, they explode. **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **unrolled** LSTM network across $T$ timesteps. Show how backpropagation through time (BPTT) flows backward through the cell-state and hidden-state connections. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Unrolled Diagram Description:**
- $T$ copies of the LSTM cell are drawn sequentially, one per timestep $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$.
- Each cell receives input $x_t$ and feeds output $h_t = y_t$.
- **Horizontal arrows** carry $h_{t-1} \to h_t$ and $C_{t-1} \to C_t$ forward.
- **Loss** $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell_t(y_t, y_t^{\text{true}})$ is computed at the top.

**BPTT Flow:**
At final timestep $T$, compute $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_T}$. Then propagate backward:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} &= \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{t+1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t} + \frac{\partial \ell_t}{\partial h_t}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly for cell states. The total gradient w.r.t. weight $W_f$ accumulates across all timesteps:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_f} &= \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial f_t} \cdot \frac{\partial f_t}{\partial W_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split:** Unrolled diagram — 3 marks; BPTT flow explanation — 2 marks; Gradient accumulation equations — 2 marks. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A sequence classification task uses an LSTM with input size $d = 3$, hidden size $h = 2$, and forget bias initialized to $b_f = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_t &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.0 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
W_f = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & 0.1 & 0.2 & -0.1 & 0.0 \\ -0.2 & 0.4 & 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the forget gate vector $f_t$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concatenate $h_{t-1}$ and $x_t$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
z_t = [h_{t-1}; x_t] = [0.2,\ -0.3,\ 0.5,\ 1.0,\ -0.5]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $W_f \cdot z_t$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(W_f z_t)_1 &= (0.3)(0.2) + (0.1)(-0.3) + (0.2)(0.5) + (-0.1)(1.0) + (0.0)(-0.5) \\
&= 0.06 - 0.03 + 0.10 - 0.10 + 0.00 = 0.03 \\[4pt]
(W_f z_t)_2 &= (-0.2)(0.2) + (0.4)(-0.3) + (0.1)(0.5) + (0.3)(1.0) + (0.2)(-0.5) \\
&= -0.04 - 0.12 + 0.05 + 0.30 - 0.10 = 0.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add forget bias:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
a_f^{(t)} = W_f z_t + b_f = \begin{bmatrix} 0.03 \\ 0.09 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.03 \\ 1.09 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply sigmoid:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_t = \sigma\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} 1.03 \\ 1.09 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.03}} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-1.09}} \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.737 \\ 0.748 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Both dimensions of the forget gate are close to **0.74**, meaning the cell will retain ~74% of its previous memory. **(7 marks)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the element-wise product symbol** $\odot$ in $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — students often write plain multiplication, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing tanh and sigmoid** — gates use **sigmoid** ($\sigma$), candidate uses **tanh**. Swapping them in derivations is the most common single-error deduction.
> 3. **Omitting the bias term** in gate equations — KTU key always includes $+ b_f, b_i, b_C, b_o$.
> 4. **Not unrolling the diagram** for BPTT questions — examiners explicitly require the unrolled form with arrows for $h_t$ and $C_t$ propagation.
> 5. **Skipping the concatenation step** $[h_{t-1}; x_t]$ in numerical problems — dimensions will not match and full marks will not be awarded.
> 6. **In gradient-flow derivations**, students often write $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = 1$ — this is **only approximately true** when $f_t \approx 1$. Mention the learned-gate subtlety to earn full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LSTMs (Module 3)**

- ✅ LSTM was introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)** to solve the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem** in RNNs.
- ✅ An LSTM cell has **two state vectors**: cell state $C_t$ (long-term memory) and hidden state $h_t$ (short-term / output).
- ✅ **Three gates** (sigmoid $\sigma$): forget $f_t$, input $i_t$, output $o_t$ — all output in $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **One candidate** (tanh): $\tilde{C}_t$ — outputs in $[-1, 1]$ to allow signed updates.
- ✅ **Core update equations** (memorize verbatim):
  - $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_f)$
  - $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_i)$
  - $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_C)$
  - $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$
  - $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}; x_t] + b_o)$
  - $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$
- ✅ The **cell-state update is additive** (residual-like), giving a **gradient highway** $\dfrac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-k}} = \prod f_{t-j+1}$.
- ✅ **Forget bias initialization $b_f = 1$** is standard practice.
- ✅ **BPTT** = backpropagation through time — unroll the network across $T$ steps and sum gradients.
- ✅ **Gradient clipping** (threshold $\approx 5$) is commonly used to mitigate exploding gradients.
- ✅ LSTMs have **4× parameters** of vanilla RNN; **GRUs** reduce this with only 2 gates ($z_t$ update, $r_t$ reset).
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, **always show concatenation** $[h_{t-1}; x_t]$ and **carry 4 decimal places** in sigmoid/tanh evaluations.
- ✅ Applications: time-series forecasting, NLP, speech recognition, anomaly detection, Seq2Seq encoders.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bidirectional RNNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (Bi-RNN)** is an advanced sequence-modeling architecture that processes a given input sequence in **two independent directions** — one from the start to the end (**forward pass**) and another from the end to the start (**backward pass**) — and then combines the hidden state representations from both directions at every time step to produce the output. Unlike a vanilla (unidirectional) RNN which conditions the output at time $t$ only on past context $x_{1:t}$, the Bi-RNN conditions the output on both **past and future** context $x_{1:T}$.

Formally, given an input sequence $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$, a Bi-RNN maintains two hidden states:

$$\vec{h}_t = f\left(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} \vec{h}_{t-1} + b_{\vec{h}}\right)$$

$$\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t = f\left(W'_{xh} x_t + W'_{hh} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{t+1} + b_{\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}}\right)$$

The final output is computed as:

$$y_t = g\left(W_{hy} \vec{h}_t + W'_{hy} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t + b_y\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Bidirectional RNNs are listed as a core advanced RNN architecture. Students must understand the architectural split, the dual hidden state mechanism, the joint output computation, and the applicability of the same principle to LSTMs and GRUs (resulting in BiLSTM and BiGRU).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Notation Convention Used in KTU Materials:**
> - $\vec{h}_t$ = Forward (left-to-right) hidden state
> - $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$ = Backward (right-to-left) hidden state
> - Subscripts $x$, $h$, $y$ denote input-to-hidden, hidden-to-hidden, and hidden-to-output weight matrices respectively. Prime ($'$) marks backward direction weights.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are reading a sentence in a mystery novel: *"The detective revealed that the __________ was the murderer."* Before you can fill in the blank, you need to read the **entire sentence**, not just the words before it. The clue "revealed that" tells you that the missing word is a person, but the actual identity is hinted at by future words (or earlier in the story).

- A **unidirectional RNN** is like reading this sentence **left-to-right only** — it has no idea what comes after the blank, so it makes a weaker guess.
- A **Bidirectional RNN** is like reading the sentence **twice**: once forward and once backward, then combining the two understandings to predict the missing word with much higher confidence.

This dual-scan is precisely what makes Bi-RNNs extremely powerful for tasks like **Named Entity Recognition (NER)**, **Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging**, **Speech Recognition**, and **Machine Translation**, where the meaning of a word depends on both its left and right context.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Courtroom Stenographer:** A good stenographer does not just transcribe words as they come in. They keep a mental buffer of the *next* few words too, so that ambiguous phrases (e.g., "their / there / they're") can be correctly resolved. A Bi-RNN does exactly this mathematically.

## 1.3 Core Architectural Metric

| Term | Symbol / Metric | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Number of directional layers | 2 (Forward + Backward) | **Fixed at 2** |
| Total parameter count multiplier | $\approx 2 \times$ unidirectional RNN | $2$ |
| Time steps supported | $T$ | $T \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ |
| Activation (hidden) | $\tanh$ or ReLU | $\tanh$ default |
| Activation (output) | Softmax / Sigmoid | Task-dependent |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Motivation — Why Unidirectional RNNs Fall Short

A vanilla RNN computes the hidden state at time $t$ using only the past:

$$h_t = f(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$$

This creates an **information bottleneck**:
- For sequence labeling tasks, the prediction at position $t$ should ideally depend on the **entire** sequence.
- In tasks like **handwriting recognition** or **protein sequence classification**, the suffix (future) of the sequence contains information essential to classifying the prefix.
- Unidirectional RNNs therefore suffer from **asymmetric context exploitation** and lower accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> The Bi-RNN, introduced by **Schuster & Paliwal (1997)** and popularized by **Graves & Schmidhuber (2005)** for phoneme classification, was the canonical architectural solution to this limitation.

## 2.2 Structural Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Input ingestion:** The input sequence $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_T)$ is fed into **two separate recurrent sub-networks** simultaneously.
2. **Forward sub-network:** Processes $x_1 \rightarrow x_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow x_T$, producing forward hidden states $\vec{h}_1, \vec{h}_2, \dots, \vec{h}_T$.
3. **Backward sub-network:** Processes $x_T \rightarrow x_{T-1} \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow x_1$, producing backward hidden states $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_T, \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{T-1}, \dots, \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_1$.
4. **State concatenation/merging:** At every time $t$, the two hidden states are merged (most commonly by **concatenation**, sometimes by summation or averaging) to form the joint context vector.
5. **Output projection:** A dense output layer maps the merged vector to the final prediction $y_t$.
6. **Loss computation:** Loss $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \ell(y_t, \hat{y}_t)$ is computed and gradients flow through **both** directions during backpropagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Concept — No Information Leakage:** The two directions are completely **disjoint** during the forward pass. They share only the input $x_t$, not each other's hidden states. The merging happens *only at the output*.

## 2.3 Training — Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in Bi-RNN

Training a Bi-RNN requires a modified version of BPTT called **Bidirectional BPTT**:

- Compute forward pass $\rightarrow$ obtain $\vec{h}_t$ for all $t$.
- Compute backward pass $\rightarrow$ obtain $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$ for all $t$.
- Compute loss $\mathcal{L}$.
- Backward pass:
  - Gradients w.r.t. forward weights flow **left-to-right** through $\vec{h}_t$.
  - Gradients w.r.t. backward weights flow **right-to-left** through $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$.
  - Gradients w.r.t. input weights $W_{xh}$ and $W'_{xh}$ receive contributions from **both** directions (sum of forward and backward gradients).

The gradient of the loss w.r.t. an input weight $W_{xh}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_k}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

with an analogous expression for $W'_{xh}$ propagating from $t$ to $T$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM-RELEVANT formula set for Bi-RNN questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward hidden state update | $\vec{h}_t = f(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} \vec{h}_{t-1} + b_{\vec{h}})$ | $f = \tanh$ typically; $\vec{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ |
| 2 | Backward hidden state update | $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t = f(W'_{xh} x_t + W'_{hh} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{t+1} + b_{\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}})$ | $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$ |
| 3 | Output (concatenation merge) | $y_t = g(W_{hy} \vec{h}_t + W'_{hy} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t + b_y)$ | $g = $ softmax / sigmoid |
| 4 | Output (sum merge) | $y_t = g(W_{hy} (\vec{h}_t + \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t) + b_y)$ | Fewer parameters |
| 5 | Total loss (sequence labeling) | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_t^{(c)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(c)}$ | Cross-entropy; $C$ = number of classes |
| 6 | Parameter count (1-layer Bi-RNN) | $\mathcal{P} = 2 \cdot [d(d+d_h) + d_h(d_h+1)] + (2d_h) \cdot d_y + d_y$ | $d$ = input dim, $d_h$ = hidden dim, $d_y$ = output dim |
| 7 | Gradient flow (input weight) | $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_{xh} = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W_{xh} \Big\vert_{\text{fwd}} + \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial W_{xh} \Big\vert_{\text{bwd}}$ | Sum of two BPTT contributions |
| 8 | Context window | Full sequence $[x_1, x_T]$ | **Bidirectional** — both past & future |
| 9 | Computational cost | $\mathcal{O}(T \cdot d_h^2)$ per direction | Linear in $T$ |
| 10 | Memory cost | $2 \times$ unidirectional RNN | Stores both $\vec{h}$ and $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}$ |

> **LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar character has been deliberately replaced with `\Big\vert` and `\sum` aggregations to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

Bi-RNNs (and their BiLSTM / BiGRU variants) are the **backbone of industrial-grade NLP pipelines**:

- **Healthcare:** Clinical Named Entity Recognition from Electronic Health Records (EHRs) — both preceding symptoms and later diagnoses are contextually relevant.
- **Finance:** Sentiment analysis of earnings call transcripts; both forward tone and the concluding stance help classify sentences.
- **Speech Systems:** Google's early voice search used BiLSTMs; the full utterance context is required to disambiguate phonemes.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein secondary structure prediction (e.g., **CRF-BiLSTM** hybrids).
- **Robotics:** Sensor-fusion networks where past and predicted future sensor states both inform the current action.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industrial Context:** For PEEBT746 project evaluations, BiLSTM/BiGRU models trained on IMDB, CoNLL-2003, or UCI sentiment datasets are commonly accepted mini-project choices.
