# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (Bi-RNN). Mention two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (Bi-RNN)** is a sequence-processing neural architecture that maintains **two independent hidden state sequences** — one scanning the input in the forward direction (from $t = 1$ to $T$) and the other scanning in the backward direction (from $t = T$ to $1$). The hidden states from both directions are merged at each time step to produce the output.

**Mathematical form:**

$$\vec{h}_t = f(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} \vec{h}_{t-1} + b_{\vec{h}})$$

$$\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t = f(W'_{xh} x_t + W'_{hh} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{t+1} + b_{\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}})$$

$$y_t = g(W_{hy} \vec{h}_t + W'_{hy} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t + b_y)$$

**Advantages:**
1. **Full context exploitation** — the output at time $t$ depends on both past and future context, improving accuracy in sequence labeling tasks.
2. **Better gradient signal** for input embeddings — receives gradient contributions from both forward and backward BPTT, leading to richer learned representations.

**Limitation:**
- **Cannot be used for real-time or online / streaming applications**, because the backward sub-network requires the entire input sequence to be available before any output can be computed at $t$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students frequently write only *one* advantage. The KTU mark split for 3-mark Part A questions is **1 mark for definition + 1 mark for advantages + 1 mark for limitation/application**. Always list at least **two** advantages.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain how the hidden states are merged in a Bi-RNN. List the three common merging strategies.**

**Model Answer:**

The forward hidden state $\vec{h}_t$ and the backward hidden state $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$ are merged at each time step $t$ to form a joint representation that feeds the output layer. The three standard merging strategies are:

1. **Concatenation (most common):**
$$h_t = \left[\vec{h}_t \,\, ; \,\, \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t\right]$$
The resulting vector has dimension $2 d_h$, where $d_h$ is the per-direction hidden size.

2. **Element-wise Summation:**
$$h_t = \vec{h}_t + \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$$
The resulting vector retains dimension $d_h$, reducing the output layer's parameter count.

3. **Element-wise Averaging:**
$$h_t = \frac{1}{2}\left(\vec{h}_t + \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t\right)$$
Similar to summation but normalizes the magnitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **summation** with **averaging**. Remember: summation *doubles* the expected magnitude of the merged vector; averaging *preserves* it. This affects weight initialization choices for the output layer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Part B 14-mark questions typically have **internal choice** — students answer either **Question A** or **Question B**. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a Bidirectional Recurrent Neural Network (Bi-RNN) with a neat diagram. Derive the forward and backward hidden state update equations and the output computation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Bi-RNN with input dimension $d = 4$, per-direction hidden dimension $d_h = 3$, and output dimension $d_y = 2$ is trained on a 5-time-step sequence. Compute the total number of trainable parameters assuming concatenation-based output merging.

**Model Answer — A(a):**

The Bi-RNN architecture consists of **two parallel recurrent sub-networks** operating on the same input sequence in opposite directions. The architecture was introduced by **Schuster and Paliwal (1997)**.

**Forward pass equations:**

$$\vec{h}_t = \tanh\left(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} \vec{h}_{t-1} + b_{\vec{h}}\right), \quad \vec{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$$

**Backward pass equations:**

$$\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t = \tanh\left(W'_{xh} x_t + W'_{hh} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{t+1} + b_{\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}}\right), \quad \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Output computation (concatenation merge):**

$$z_t = W_{hy} \vec{h}_t + W'_{hy} \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t + b_y$$

$$y_t = \text{softmax}(z_t)$$

**Weight matrices and their dimensions** (assuming $d$ = input dim, $d_h$ = per-direction hidden dim):

| Weight | Dimensions | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{xh}$ | $d \times d_h$ | Input $\rightarrow$ forward hidden |
| $W_{hh}$ | $d_h \times d_h$ | Forward recurrent |
| $W_{hy}$ | $d_h \times d_y$ | Forward hidden $\rightarrow$ output |
| $W'_{xh}$ | $d \times d_h$ | Input $\rightarrow$ backward hidden |
| $W'_{hh}$ | $d_h \times d_h$ | Backward recurrent |
| $W'_{hy}$ | $d_h \times d_y$ | Backward hidden $\rightarrow$ output |

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the unrolled Bi-RNN schematic.)

**Mark distribution for A(a):**
- Architecture explanation: **2 marks**
- Forward and backward update equations: **3 marks**
- Output computation with diagram reference: **2 marks**

---

**Model Answer — A(b):**

**Step 1 — Per-direction parameters (forward and backward are identical):**
- $W_{xh}$: $d \times d_h = 4 \times 3 = 12$
- $W_{hh}$: $d_h \times d_h = 3 \times 3 = 9$
- $b_{\vec{h}}$: $d_h = 3$
- Per-direction total: $12 + 9 + 3 = 24$

**Step 2 — Both directions:**
$$2 \times 24 = 48 \text{ parameters}$$

**Step 3 — Output layer (concatenation merge):**
The merged hidden state has dimension $2 d_h = 2 \times 3 = 6$.
- $W_{hy}$: $2 d_h \times d_y = 6 \times 2 = 12$
- $b_y$: $d_y = 2$
- Output layer total: $12 + 2 = 14$

**Step 4 — Grand total:**
$$\mathcal{P} = 48 + 14 = \boxed{62 \text{ trainable parameters}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B parameter count):** A *very* common error is forgetting to double the $W_{hy}$ input dimension (students write $3 \times 2 = 6$ instead of $6 \times 2 = 12$). The **concatenation** strategy means the output layer's input is $2 d_h$, **not** $d_h$. KTU examiners specifically test this in 14-mark problems. **Lose 1-2 marks if missed.**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Bidirectional RNNs with Unidirectional RNNs. Discuss two applications where Bi-RNNs outperform unidirectional RNNs and explain why.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm for a Bi-RNN. Show how the gradient w.r.t. the input weight matrix $W_{xh}$ receives contributions from both the forward and backward passes.

**Model Answer — B(a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Unidirectional RNN | **Bidirectional RNN** |
|---|---|---|
| Directional scan | Single (left-to-right) | **Two** (left-to-right and right-to-left) |
| Context at time $t$ | Only past $x_1, \dots, x_t$ | **Full** $x_1, \dots, x_T$ |
| Hidden state at $t$ | One vector $h_t$ | **Two** vectors $\vec{h}_t$ and $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$ |
| Parameter count | $1 \times$ baseline | $\approx 2 \times$ baseline |
| Online inference | **Supported** | **Not supported** |
| Memory requirement | $1 \times d_h$ per time step | $2 \times d_h$ per time step |
| Typical use case | Time-series forecasting, streaming ASR | NER, POS tagging, offline ASR |

**Two applications where Bi-RNNs outperform unidirectional RNNs:**

1. **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** Consider the sentence *"Washington defeated New York in 1789."* The word *Washington* could be a person, location, or sports team. Only by reading the verb *"defeated"* and the object *"New York"* (future context) can the model correctly classify *Washington* as a sports team. A unidirectional RNN would have classified it as a person or location with high probability.

2. **Speech Recognition:** Phonemes are highly context-dependent. For example, the phoneme /p/ in *"pat"* vs. *"bat"* requires the upcoming vowel for disambiguation. Bi-RNNs (and BiLSTMs) utilize the post-phoneme acoustic context, which significantly reduces Word Error Rate (WER) — historically by 5-10% absolute on TIMIT and Wall Street Journal benchmarks.

**Mark distribution for B(a):**
- Comparison table: **3 marks**
- Application 1 (NER) with reasoning: **2 marks**
- Application 2 (Speech) with reasoning: **2 marks**

---

**Model Answer — B(b):**

**Step 1 — Loss function:** The Bi-RNN minimizes the total sequence loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathcal{L}_t, \quad \text{where } \mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_t^{(c)} \log \hat{y}_t^{(c)}$$

**Step 2 — Forward BPTT contribution to $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_{xh}$:**

The forward hidden state at time $t$ depends on $W_{xh}$ through all previous time steps $k \leq t$:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial W_{xh}} = \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_k}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

Therefore, the forward contribution is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}}\right)_{\text{fwd}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_t} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial \vec{h}_t}{\partial \vec{h}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{h}_k}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

**Step 3 — Backward BPTT contribution to $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_{xh}$:**

The backward hidden state at time $t$ depends on $W_{xh}$ (also denoted $W'_{xh}$ in some texts) through all later time steps $k \geq t$:

$$\frac{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t}{\partial W_{xh}} = \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t}{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_k}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

Therefore, the backward contribution is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}}\right)_{\text{bwd}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t} \cdot \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t}{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_k} \cdot \frac{\partial \reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_k}{\partial W_{xh}}$$

**Step 4 — Total gradient (sum of contributions):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}} = \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}}\right)_{\text{fwd}} + \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{xh}}\right)_{\text{bwd}}}$$

**Interpretation:** The input embedding weight matrix $W_{xh}$ receives **two independent gradient signals** — one propagating from the future (backward BPTT) and one from the past (forward BPTT). This doubled gradient signal is one of the empirical reasons Bi-RNNs converge faster and achieve better representations than unidirectional RNNs for sequence labeling tasks.

**Mark distribution for B(b):**
- Loss function setup: **1 mark**
- Forward BPTT derivation: **2 marks**
- Backward BPTT derivation: **2 marks**
- Combined gradient expression and interpretation: **2 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (BPTT derivation):** Students often confuse the **limits of summation** ($k \leq t$ for forward vs. $k \geq t$ for backward). The forward pass propagates from $1$ to $t$ (past-to-present), while the backward pass propagates from $T$ to $t$ (future-to-present). Reversing this is a **sure 2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation. Always draw the unrolled diagram and label the time axes explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Bi-RNNs (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** Bi-RNN processes a sequence in **both** forward and backward directions using two independent recurrent sub-networks.
- **Two hidden states per time step:** $\vec{h}_t$ (forward) and $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_t$ (backward).
- **Initial conditions:** $\vec{h}_0 = \mathbf{0}$ and $\reflectbox{$\vec{\reflectbox{$h$}}$}_{T+1} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Three merging strategies:** **Concatenation** (most common, doubles the output layer input), **Summation** (preserves $d_h$, fewer parameters), **Averaging** (preserves $d_h$ and magnitude).
- **Activation function:** $\tanh$ for hidden states; **softmax** for classification output, **linear** for regression output.
- **Parameter count formula:** $\mathcal{P} = 2(d \cdot d_h + d_h^2 + d_h) + 2 d_h \cdot d_y + d_y$ (concatenation merge).
- **Training algorithm:** Bidirectional BPTT — gradients flow in both directions independently.
- **Gradient w.r.t. $W_{xh}$:** Sum of forward and backward BPTT contributions.
- **Cannot be used online:** Requires the full input sequence; unsuitable for real-time or streaming applications.
- **Family members:** BiRNN, BiLSTM (best for long sequences), BiGRU (cheaper alternative to BiLSTM).
- **Best applications:** Named Entity Recognition (NER), Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging, Speech Recognition, Sentiment Analysis, Protein Structure Prediction.
- **Origin:** Schuster & Paliwal (1997); popularized by Graves & Schmidhuber (2005) for phoneme classification.
- **Disadvantage:** Doubled parameter count and memory requirement compared to unidirectional RNN.
- **Key KTU calculation pitfall:** In concatenation merge, the output layer's input dimension is **$2 d_h$**, not $d_h$.
- **Key KTU formula pitfall:** Forward BPTT summation runs from $k = 1$ to $t$; backward BPTT summation runs from $k = T$ to $t$.
- **Examiner's favorite trick question:** *"Can a Bi-RNN be used for next-word prediction in a live chatbot?"* — **Answer: No**, because the backward pass requires the entire future sentence, which is not available during real-time generation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gated RNNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gated RNNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gated Recurrent Neural Networks (Gated RNNs)** are an advanced class of Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) architectures specifically engineered to overcome the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** inherent in standard (vanilla) RNNs. They achieve this by introducing learnable **gating mechanisms** — sigmoid neural network layers — that explicitly regulate the flow of information through the recurrent hidden state, thereby enabling the network to selectively retain, discard, or update information across long temporal sequences.

The two canonical architectures classified under Gated RNNs are:

1. **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** — proposed by *Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)*, refined by *Gers et al. (2000)* with the addition of the forget gate.
2. **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)** — proposed by *Cho et al. (2014)*, a simplified and computationally efficient variant of the LSTM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Notebook"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Student, The Notebook, and The Eraser**
>
> Imagine you are a student attending a year-long lecture series. Your brain is the **RNN hidden state**. A vanilla RNN behaves like a student who, at every lecture, completely *overwrites* the previous lecture's notes on the same notebook page — by the end of the year, only the last few lectures' content survives (the **short-term memory problem**).
>
> A **Gated RNN**, in contrast, gives you a *smart notebook* with three magical tools:
> - 🧹 **A Forget Gate** (an eraser) — decides which old notes to keep and which to wipe clean.
> - ✍️ **An Input Gate** (a selective pen) — decides which new information from the current lecture to write down.
> - 📤 **An Output Gate** (a highlighter) — decides which stored notes to read out for the current exam question.
>
> This "smart notebook" — the **cell state** — is preserved across the entire sequence, allowing the network to remember information from hundreds of time steps ago. **GRUs** are like a slightly simpler, two-tool version of the same notebook (combine eraser+pen into one, drop the highlighter).

### 1.3 Why Standard RNNs Fail — The Motivation

| Problem | Mathematical Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Vanishing Gradient** | Repeated multiplication of Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ yields values $\to 0$ | Network cannot learn long-range dependencies |
| **Exploding Gradient** | Repeated multiplication yields values $\to \infty$ | Training becomes numerically unstable |
| **Short-Term Memory** | Hidden state $h_t$ compresses entire history | Earlier context is overwritten |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The core insight of Gated RNNs** is to replace the *single multiplicative hidden state update* of the vanilla RNN with an *additive cell state update* controlled by learned gates. This additive structure is what preserves gradient flow over long sequences.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Network

The LSTM maintains **two parallel state vectors** at every time step $t$:
- $h_t$ — the **hidden state** (short-term, exposed to the next layer/output)
- $C_t$ — the **cell state** (long-term, internal memory highway)

These states are regulated by **three sigmoid gates** $f_t$, $i_t$, $o_t$ and one **candidate value** $\tilde{C}_t$.

#### 2.1.1 The Forget Gate $f_t$

> **Purpose:** "What information should we *throw away* from the cell state?"

$$f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)$$

- $\sigma$ denotes the **logistic sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.
- Output range: $f_t \in [0, 1]^{d_c}$, where $0$ = "completely forget" and $1$ = "completely retain".
- $W_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_c \times (d_h + d_x)}$, $b_f \in \mathbb{R}^{d_c}$ are the trainable parameters.

#### 2.1.2 The Input Gate $i_t$ and Candidate Cell State $\tilde{C}_t$

> **Purpose:** "What new information should we *store* in the cell state?"

$$i_t = \sigma\!\left(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right)$$

- $i_t$ acts as a **filter** (sigmoid, $[0,1]$) determining *how much* of the new candidate to admit.
- $\tilde{C}_t$ is the **proposed new content** (tanh, $[-1, +1]$).
- The product $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ represents the *gated* new content.

#### 2.1.3 The Cell State Update $C_t$

> **Purpose:** Combine the "kept old memory" with the "selected new memory."

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

This is the **critical equation** of the LSTM. The use of the *Hadamard (element-wise) product* $\odot$ and **additive update** (not multiplicative as in vanilla RNN) is what enables gradient preservation over long sequences.

#### 2.1.4 The Output Gate $o_t$ and Hidden State $h_t$

> **Purpose:** "What part of the cell state should we *output* as the hidden state?"

$$o_t = \sigma\!\left(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

- The $\tanh$ squashes $C_t$ to $[-1, +1]$ for stable output.
- $o_t$ further filters *which* dimensions of the squashed cell state are exposed.

#### 2.1.5 Peephole Connections (Optional Enhancement)

> [!NOTE]
> Some LSTM variants (Gers & Schmidhuber, 2000) include **peephole connections**, allowing gates to inspect the cell state directly:
> $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [C_{t-1}, h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$, and similarly for $i_t$, $o_t$. This is **not always used** in modern implementations.

### 2.2 The Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)

The GRU **simplifies** the LSTM by merging the cell state and hidden state into a single vector $h_t$, and uses only **two gates**.

#### 2.2.1 The Update Gate $z_t$

> **Purpose:** Analogous to LSTM's forget + input gates combined. Decides "how much past information to keep vs. how much new information to take."

$$z_t = \sigma\!\left(W_z \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z\right)$$

#### 2.2.2 The Reset Gate $r_t$

> **Purpose:** Decides "how much of the past to *forget* when computing the new candidate."

$$r_t = \sigma\!\left(W_r \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r\right)$$

#### 2.2.3 The Candidate Hidden State $\tilde{h}_t$

$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_h \cdot [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h\right)$$

- The reset gate $r_t$ selectively *zeroes out* parts of $h_{t-1}$ before proposing the new candidate.

#### 2.2.4 The Final Hidden State Update $h_t$

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Equivalence:** The GRU's interpolation $(1-z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ is mathematically analogous to the LSTM's $f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, but with a **linear interpolation** between old and new rather than independent gating — yielding fewer parameters and faster training.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Equation | Range / Output | Parameters |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | $[0,1]$ | $W_f, b_f$ |
| 2 | LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | $[0,1]$ | $W_i, b_i$ |
| 3 | LSTM Candidate | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | $[-1,1]$ | $W_C, b_C$ |
| 4 | LSTM Cell Update | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_c}$ | — |
| 5 | LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | $[0,1]$ | $W_o, b_o$ |
| 6 | LSTM Hidden | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | — |
| 7 | GRU Update Gate | $z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$ | $[0,1]$ | $W_z, b_z$ |
| 8 | GRU Reset Gate | $r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$ | $[0,1]$ | $W_r, b_r$ |
| 9 | GRU Candidate | $\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$ | $[-1,1]$ | $W_h, b_h$ |
| 10 | GRU Hidden Update | $h_t = (1-z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ | — |
| 11 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0,1)$ | — |
| 12 | Hyperbolic Tangent | $\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1,1)$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used throughout:** $d_h$ = hidden dimension, $d_x$ = input dimension, $d_c$ = cell-state dimension (LSTM only, often $d_c = d_h$). The brackets $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ denote vector concatenation. The operator $\odot$ denotes element-wise (Hadamard) product.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **LSTM** dominates in: machine translation (sequence-to-sequence), speech recognition (Google's Listen Attend Spell), sentiment analysis on long documents, music generation, medical time-series (ECG, EEG).
- **GRU** is preferred in: real-time applications, low-resource deployment, smaller datasets, reinforcement learning policies, lightweight edge devices — trains **~30% faster** with comparable accuracy on many tasks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Additive Cell Update Solves Vanishing Gradients

We derive the gradient of the loss $L$ (summed over a sequence of length $T$) with respect to a past cell state $C_k$, for $k < t$. The objective is to show that the LSTM cell state gradient **does not collapse to zero** over long ranges.

**Step 1 — Define the total loss over a sequence.**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

We wish to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k}$, which by the chain rule telescopes over all future time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} \cdot \frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the recursive Jacobian $\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k}$.**

From $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, we expand recursively:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each individual Jacobian term.**

For a single step transition $C_{j} = f_j \odot C_{j-1} + i_j \odot \tilde{C}_t$, the derivative with respect to $C_{j-1}$ (treating gates as a function of *inputs*, not $C_{j-1}$ in the first-order approximation used in the LSTM gradient path) is:

$$\frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(f_j\right) + \text{other additive terms}$$

In the **direct multiplicative path** (the path that causes vanishing gradients in vanilla RNNs), the relevant term is:

$$\frac{\partial C_j}{\partial C_{j-1}} \supseteq \text{diag}\!\left(f_j\right)$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the full gradient product.**

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(f_j\right) + \text{additive correction terms}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_k} = \sum_{t=k}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial C_t} \cdot \left[\prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_j) + \text{additive terms}\right]$$

**Step 5 — The critical insight.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **vanilla RNN**, the analogous product is $\prod \text{diag}(\tanh'(\cdot) W_{hh})$, whose entries lie in $(0, 1)$ — causing exponential decay. In the **LSTM**, the product $\prod \text{diag}(f_j)$ involves $f_j \in [0,1]$ which is *learned*. When the network learns $f_j \approx 1$, the product stays close to $1$, and gradients flow unimpeded across hundreds of steps. The **additive** structure (the $i_j \odot \tilde{C}_j$ term) provides an additional gradient pathway that bypasses the multiplicative chain entirely.

### 3.2 Derivation: The GRU Interpolation Identity

We show that the GRU's update rule is a *convex combination* of old and candidate hidden state, parameterized by the update gate.

**Step 1 — Rewrite the update rule.**

$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

**Step 2 — Factor the right-hand side.**

$$h_t = h_{t-1} - z_t \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$

$$h_t = h_{t-1} + z_t \odot (\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

This shows that $h_t$ is obtained by taking a **step of size $z_t$** in the direction $(\tilde{h}_t - h_{t-1})$ from the previous hidden state. The update gate $z_t$ acts analogously to a **learning rate vector**:
- When $z_t \to 0$: $h_t \to h_{t-1}$ (network preserves memory, ignores input).
- When $z_t \to 1$: $h_t \to \tilde{h}_t$ (network overwrites with new candidate).

This elegant linear-interpolation form is a key reason GRUs are computationally efficient — they require only **two sigmoid gates** and **one candidate layer** (3 weight matrices + 3 bias vectors) versus the LSTM's **3 gates + 1 candidate** (4 weight matrices + 4 bias vectors), a parameter reduction of approximately **25%**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LSTM vs GRU

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Number of state vectors | 2 ($h_t$, $C_t$) | 1 ($h_t$) |
| State update mechanism | Additive with independent $f, i$ | Linear interpolation $(1-z)h + z\tilde{h}$ |
| Parameter count (per layer) | $4 \times (d_h + d_x + 1) \cdot d_h$ | $3 \times (d_h + d_x + 1) \cdot d_h$ |
| Training speed | Slower | ~25-30% faster |
| Long-sequence modeling | Excellent | Very good |
| Risk of overfitting (small data) | Higher (more parameters) | Lower |
| Peephole connections | Optional | Not applicable |
| Invented | 1997 (Hochreiter–Schmidhuber) | 2014 (Cho et al.) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Single Time Step of LSTM

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (illustrative magnitudes only):** Suppose $d_h = 2$, $d_x = 1$, and at time $t$ the previous states are:
> $$h_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}, \quad x_t = [0.7]$$
> Let the concatenated input be $z = [h_{t-1}; x_t] = [0.5, -0.3, 0.7]^T$.
> Suppose the trained weight matrices and biases yield the following pre-activation sums (for clarity, simplified values):
> - Forget: $W_f z + b_f = [1.2, 0.1]^T \;\Rightarrow\; f_t = [\sigma(1.2), \sigma(0.1)] \approx [0.768, 0.525]^T$
> - Input: $W_i z + b_i = [0.5, -0.4]^T \;\Rightarrow\; i_t = [\sigma(0.5), \sigma(-0.4)] \approx [0.622, 0.401]^T$
> - Candidate: $W_C z + b_C = [0.3, 0.6]^T \;\Rightarrow\; \tilde{C}_t = [\tanh(0.3), \tanh(0.6)] \approx [0.291, 0.537]^T$
> - Output: $W_o z + b_o = [-0.2, 0.8]^T \;\Rightarrow\; o_t = [\sigma(-0.2), \sigma(0.8)] \approx [0.450, 0.690]^T$

**Step 5a — Compute new cell state.**

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

Element-wise:

$$C_t[1] = (0.768)(1.0) + (0.622)(0.291) = 0.768 + 0.181 = 0.949$$

$$C_t[2] = (0.525)(0.8) + (0.401)(0.537) = 0.420 + 0.215 = 0.635$$

$$C_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.949 \\ 0.635 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5b — Compute new hidden state.**

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

$$\tanh(C_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \tanh(0.949) \\ \tanh(0.635) \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.739 \\ 0.563 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h_t = \begin{bmatrix} (0.450)(0.739) \\ (0.690)(0.563) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.333 \\ 0.388 \end{bmatrix}$$

This completes one forward time step of the LSTM.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LSTM Cell — Recurrent Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt["Input x_t"] --> concat1["Concatenate"]
    hprev["h_t-1"] --> concat1
    concat1 --> fgate["Forget Gate f_t = sigma"]
    concat1 --> igate["Input Gate i_t = sigma"]
    concat1 --> cgate["Candidate C_tilde_t = tanh"]
    concat1 --> ogate["Output Gate o_t = sigma"]
    Cprev["C_t-1"] --> mul1["Element-wise Multiply"]
    fgate --> mul1
    mul1 --> add1["Element-wise Add"]
    cgate --> mul2["Element-wise Multiply"]
    igate --> mul2
    mul2 --> add1
    add1 --> Cnew["C_t Cell State"]
    Cnew --> tanh1["tanh"]
    tanh1 --> mul3["Element-wise Multiply"]
    ogate --> mul3
    mul3 --> hnew["h_t Hidden State"]
    hnew --> hprev
    Cnew --> Cprev
```

### 4.2 GRU Cell — Simplified Two-Gate Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xt2["Input x_t"] --> concat2["Concatenate"]
    hprev2["h_t-1"] --> concat2
    concat2 --> zgate["Update Gate z_t = sigma"]
    concat2 --> rgate["Reset Gate r_t = sigma"]
    hprev2 --> mul4["Element-wise Multiply"]
    rgate --> mul4
    mul4 --> concat3["Concatenate with x_t"]
    xt2 --> concat3
    concat3 --> hcandidate["Candidate h_tilde_t = tanh"]
    zgate --> oneminusz["1 minus z_t"]
    hprev2 --> interp["Linear Interpolation"]
    oneminusz --> interp
    hcandidate --> multz["z_t * h_tilde_t"]
    zgate --> multz
    multz --> add2["Add"]
    interp --> add2
    add2 --> hnew2["h_t New Hidden State"]
    hnew2 --> hprev2
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LSTM Unrolled Through Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["x_1"] --> LSTMA["LSTM Cell t=1"]
    A0["h_0, C_0"] --> LSTMA
    LSTMA --> h1["h_1"]
    LSTMA --> C1["C_1"]
    h1 --> LSTMB["LSTM Cell t=2"]
    C1 --> LSTMB
    x2["x_2"] --> LSTMB
    LSTMB --> h2["h_2"]
    LSTMB --> C2["C_2"]
    h2 --> LSTMC["LSTM Cell t=3"]
    C2 --> LSTMC
    x3["x_3"] --> LSTMC
    LSTMC --> h3["h_3"]
    LSTMC --> C3["C_3"]
    h3 --> yhat["Output y_hat"]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Comparison: Vanilla RNN vs Gated RNN Gradient Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vanilla RNN: gradient = product of tanh derivatives times W_hh"] --> B["Repeated multiplication by values in 0,1"]
    B --> C["Exponential decay: gradient approaches 0"]
    C --> D["Long-term dependencies NOT learned"]
    E["Gated RNN: cell state gradient contains additive term"] --> F["Forget gate learns to be near 1 for important info"]
    F --> G["Gradient product stays near 1, additive bypass exists"]
    G --> H["Long-term dependencies PRESERVED"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Pattern]**
*What is a Gated Recurrent Neural Network? Why are gating mechanisms necessary in RNNs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Gated Recurrent Neural Network (Gated RNN)** is a recurrent architecture that uses learnable **gating units** (sigmoid neural layers producing values in $[0,1]$) to control the flow of information through the hidden state, enabling selective memory retention and forgetting across time steps.

Gating mechanisms are necessary because standard RNNs suffer from the **vanishing and exploding gradient problem** during backpropagation through time (BPTT), caused by repeated multiplication of Jacobian matrices. The hidden state of a vanilla RNN is overwritten at every time step, making it difficult to learn **long-range dependencies**. Gated architectures such as the **LSTM** and **GRU** replace the single multiplicative update with an **additive cell-state update** regulated by gates, allowing gradients to flow across hundreds of time steps without collapse. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark problem statement, 1 mark solution insight)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Pattern]**
*List and briefly explain the three gates of an LSTM network.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The LSTM has three gates, all implemented as **sigmoid layers** producing outputs in $[0,1]$ that act as element-wise filters:

1. **Forget Gate** $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ — controls how much of the *previous cell state* $C_{t-1}$ to retain. Value $0$ = completely discard, $1$ = completely keep.

2. **Input Gate** $i_t = \sigma(W_i [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ — controls how much of the *new candidate cell state* $\tilde{C}_t$ to write into memory.

3. **Output Gate** $o_t = \sigma(W_o [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ — controls how much of the *cell state* to expose as the hidden output $h_t$.

Together, these gates allow the LSTM to selectively remember, write, and read information across long sequences. *(3 marks — 1 mark per gate for equation and interpretation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **DO NOT confuse the LSTM cell state $C_t$ with the hidden state $h_t$** — they are two distinct vectors updated by different equations.
> - **DO NOT use `tanh` for the gates** — gates use **sigmoid** ($\sigma$). Only the *candidate* $\tilde{C}_t$ uses $\tanh$.
> - **DO NOT omit the bias terms** in gate equations — they are trainable and must appear in the formula.
> - **Always mention the additive structure** of $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ when explaining how vanishing gradients are mitigated.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an **LSTM network**. Derive the forward-pass equations for all three gates and the cell-state update. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Perform the **backpropagation-through-time (BPTT) gradient analysis** for the LSTM cell state and show mathematically why the additive cell update mitigates the vanishing gradient problem. Compare the gradient flow with that of a vanilla RNN. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

The **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell, as introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, maintains two state vectors at each time step: the **hidden state** $h_t$ (exposed to the network output and next layer) and the **cell state** $C_t$ (internal long-term memory). Three sigmoid gates regulate information flow.

**Step 1 — Forget gate (1 mark):**
$$f_t = \sigma\!\left(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f\right)$$
This gate decides what fraction of the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$ to retain. Output range: $[0,1]$.

**Step 2 — Input gate and candidate (2 marks):**
$$i_t = \sigma\!\left(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i\right)$$
$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh\!\left(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C\right)$$
The input gate filters the candidate before it is written to memory.

**Step 3 — Cell state update (2 marks):**
$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$
This **additive** update is the defining characteristic — it is the source of LSTM's gradient stability.

**Step 4 — Output gate and hidden state (1 mark):**
$$o_t = \sigma\!\left(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o\right), \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

**Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the LSTM cell schematic showing $h_{t-1}, x_t$ entering three gates and $\tilde{C}_t$, with $C_{t-1} \to C_t$ as the long-term memory highway (refer Section 4.1).

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Vanilla RNN gradient (2 marks):**
For a vanilla RNN with $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t)$, the gradient of the loss with respect to $h_k$ for $k < t$ involves:
$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}\!\left(\tanh'(W_{hh} h_{j-1})\right) W_{hh}$$
Each factor's spectral norm is at most $\lambda_{\max}(W_{hh}) < 1$ in practice, so the product **decays exponentially** as $t - k$ grows.

**Step 2 — LSTM cell state gradient (3 marks):**
From $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$, with gates treated as functions of inputs (not of $C_{t-1}$ in the dominant path):
$$\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_k} = \prod_{j=k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(f_j) + \text{additive correction terms from } i_j \odot \tilde{C}_j$$

**Step 3 — Why vanishing gradient is mitigated (2 marks):**
- The product $\prod \text{diag}(f_j)$ involves **learned** values $f_j \in [0,1]$. The network can learn $f_j \approx 1$ on dimensions carrying long-range information, keeping the product near $1$.
- The **additive** $i_j \odot \tilde{C}_j$ term creates an **alternative gradient pathway** that bypasses the multiplicative chain — a new short-circuit for gradient flow that does not exist in vanilla RNNs.

Hence LSTM can learn dependencies spanning 100+ time steps, whereas vanilla RNNs typically fail beyond 10-20 steps.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]

**(a)** With relevant equations and a block diagram, describe the architecture of a **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**. Explain the role of the update gate and reset gate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **LSTM and GRU** along the dimensions of (i) number of parameters, (ii) number of state vectors, (iii) gating mechanism, (iv) training speed, and (v) typical use cases. State which is preferred in low-resource real-time applications and justify. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

The **GRU** (Cho et al., 2014) simplifies the LSTM by merging the cell state and hidden state into a single vector $h_t$ and using only **two gates**.

**Step 1 — Update gate (1.5 marks):**
$$z_t = \sigma(W_z [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_z)$$
Controls interpolation between old hidden state and new candidate. Analogous to combining LSTM's forget and input gates.

**Step 2 — Reset gate (1.5 marks):**
$$r_t = \sigma(W_r [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_r)$$
Selectively zeros out parts of $h_{t-1}$ when computing the new candidate — controls *how much past to forget* in the candidate computation.

**Step 3 — Candidate (1.5 marks):**
$$\tilde{h}_t = \tanh(W_h [r_t \odot h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_h)$$

**Step 4 — Hidden state update (1.5 marks):**
$$h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$$
This is a **linear interpolation** — when $z_t = 0$ we keep the old state, when $z_t = 1$ we fully adopt the new candidate.

**Step 5 — Diagram (1 mark):** Refer Section 4.2 for the GRU flow.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

| Dimension | LSTM | GRU | Marks |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| (i) Parameters per layer | $4(d_h + d_x + 1)d_h$ | $3(d_h + d_x + 1)d_h$ | 1.5 |
| (ii) State vectors | Two: $h_t, C_t$ | One: $h_t$ | 1 |
| (iii) Gating mechanism | 3 independent sigmoid gates + $\tanh$ candidate, additive cell update | 2 sigmoid gates, linear interpolation $(1-z)h + z\tilde{h}$ | 1.5 |
| (iv) Training speed | Slower (more parameters) | ~25-30% faster | 1 |
| (v) Typical use | Long sequences, large datasets, machine translation, speech | Smaller datasets, real-time, RL, edge deployment | 1 |
| **Verdict** | Best for very long sequences with abundant data | **Preferred for low-resource real-time applications** | 1 |

**Justification (1 mark):** The GRU is preferred in low-resource real-time applications because it has **25% fewer parameters**, requires **fewer matrix multiplications per time step**, trains faster, and has been shown to achieve comparable accuracy to LSTM on many tasks — particularly when the dataset is small (lower overfitting risk due to lower model capacity) and inference latency matters.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gated RNNs**

- ⭐ **Vanilla RNN fails on long sequences** due to vanishing/exploding gradients from repeated multiplication of Jacobians.
- ⭐ **Gating = learned sigmoid control** on information flow, outputs in $[0,1]$.
- ⭐ **LSTM has 3 gates**: Forget ($f_t$), Input ($i_t$), Output ($o_t$) — all sigmoid. Candidate $\tilde{C}_t$ uses $\tanh$.
- ⭐ **LSTM has 2 states**: Cell state $C_t$ (long-term) and hidden state $h_t$ (short-term).
- ⭐ **Master Equation (LSTM)**: $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ — this *additive* structure is the key innovation.
- ⭐ **LSTM Hidden**: $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$.
- ⭐ **GRU has 2 gates**: Update ($z_t$) and Reset ($r_t$) — both sigmoid. Candidate $\tilde{h}_t$ uses $\tanh$.
- ⭐ **GRU has 1 state**: Hidden state $h_t$ only (no separate cell state).
- ⭐ **GRU Update**: $h_t = (1 - z_t) \odot h_{t-1} + z_t \odot \tilde{h}_t$ — a linear interpolation.
- ⭐ **GRU has ~25% fewer parameters** than LSTM and trains ~30% faster.
- ⭐ **Choose LSTM** for: long sequences, large datasets, machine translation, speech recognition.
- ⭐ **Choose GRU** for: small datasets, real-time inference, low-resource/edge deployment, reinforcement learning.
- ⭐ **BPTT gradient flow** in LSTM is preserved because (a) the forget gate product can be learned to stay near 1, and (b) the additive $i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ term provides an alternative gradient pathway.
- ⭐ **Peephole connections** are an optional LSTM enhancement allowing gates to inspect $C_{t-1}$ directly.
- ⭐ **Sigmoid for gates** $\Rightarrow$ $[0,1]$ filtering; **tanh for candidates** $\Rightarrow$ $[-1,1]$ content — never swap these.
- ⭐ **Notation**: $\odot$ = element-wise (Hadamard) product; $[h_{t-1}, x_t]$ = vector concatenation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoder-Decoder Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Encoder-Decoder Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Encoder-Decoder Model** is a class of neural network architectures designed to map an input sequence of variable length to an output sequence of variable length, where the input and output sequences may lie in different domains, have different lengths, and follow different structural rules. It consists of two coupled sub-networks:

- **Encoder**: A neural network (typically an RNN/LSTM/GRU, and later a Transformer) that compresses the input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$ into a fixed-dimensional latent representation called the **context vector** (or **thought vector**) $\mathbf{c}$.
- **Decoder**: A neural network that generates the output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y})$ one token at a time, conditioned on the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens.

Mathematically, the joint conditional distribution is factorized as:

$$p(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y} \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t \mid \mathbf{c}, y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{t-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Encoder-Decoder framework is the foundational paradigm behind **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** learning, popularized by **Sutskever et al. (2014)** and **Cho et al. (2014)**. It is the direct precursor to **Attention Mechanisms** and the **Transformer architecture**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multilingual Tourist"

Imagine a **Japanese tourist in Kerala** who cannot speak Malayalam or English. They need to describe a complex scene in Japanese, and you must tell the shopkeeper in Malayalam. The workflow is:

1. **Encoding Phase (Listening)**: The tourist listens to your full description in Japanese. They build a complete internal mental picture (the **context vector $\mathbf{c}$**) of everything you said. The mental picture is a compressed, abstract representation of your whole sentence.
2. **Decoding Phase (Speaking)**: The tourist then translates this mental picture into Malayalam, **one word at a time**, looking back at their mental picture plus the words they have already spoken to choose the next word.
3. **Information Bottleneck**: The "mental picture" is a single, fixed-size snapshot. If you give a 50-word description, the tourist must squeeze the *entire* meaning into that one snapshot — losing details. (This is exactly why attention mechanisms were invented!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Encoder-Decoder" instead of just "Translator"?** The architecture is *domain-agnostic*. The same blueprint handles machine translation, image captioning, speech recognition, text summarization, and even conversational chatbots — only the encoder/decoder type changes (CNN for images, RNN for text, etc.).

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks at a Glance

| Sub-Network | Input | Output | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder RNN** | $x_1, \dots, x_{T_x}$ | Final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x} = \mathbf{c}$ | Read source sequence, summarize into context |
| **Context Vector $\mathbf{c}$** | $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ | Latent representation | Carries compressed meaning |
| **Decoder RNN** | $\mathbf{c}, y_1, \dots, y_{t-1}$ | $y_t$ at each step | Generate target sequence token-by-token |
| **Output Layer (Softmax)** | Decoder hidden state $s_t$ | Probability distribution over vocabulary | Pick next token |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

### Stage 1: Tokenization & Embedding
The raw input sentence (e.g., *"How are you?"*) is split into discrete **tokens**. Each token $x_t$ is mapped to a dense, learnable vector $\mathbf{e}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$ via an **embedding matrix** $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert \times d_e}$, where $\vert V \vert$ is the vocabulary size.

### Stage 2: Encoder Recurrence
The encoder (using LSTM as the canonical example) processes the embedded sequence, updating its hidden state $h_t$ and cell state $C_t$ at every time step $t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
i_t &= \sigma(W_i [\mathbf{e}_t ; \mathbf{h}_{t-1}] + b_i) \\
f_t &= \sigma(W_f [\mathbf{e}_t ; \mathbf{h}_{t-1}] + b_f) \\
o_t &= \sigma(W_o [\mathbf{e}_t ; \mathbf{h}_{t-1}] + b_o) \\
\tilde{C}_t &= \tanh(W_C [\mathbf{e}_t ; \mathbf{h}_{t-1}] + b_C) \\
C_t &= f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t \\
\mathbf{h}_t &= o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)
\end{aligned}$$

The **final hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ is designated as the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ (sometimes passed through a learnable transformation: $\mathbf{c} = \tanh(W_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x})$).

### Stage 3: Decoder Initialization
The decoder's initial hidden state is set to the context vector: $s_0 = \mathbf{c}$. A special start-of-sequence token $\langle \text{SOS} \rangle$ is fed as the first input.

### Stage 4: Decoder Recurrence (Autoregressive Generation)
At decoding step $t$, the decoder computes:

$$s_t = f_{\text{dec}}(\mathbf{e}_{y_{t-1}}, s_{t-1})$$

where $f_{\text{dec}}$ is the decoder RNN cell. The hidden state $s_t$ is then projected to a vocabulary-sized vector, followed by a **softmax** to obtain a probability distribution:

$$p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$$

### Stage 5: Token Selection
The next token is chosen by either:
- **Greedy decoding**: $y_t = \arg\max_y p(y \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$
- **Beam search**: Maintain top-$k$ candidate sequences to mitigate exposure bias.
- **Sampling**: For generative tasks (chatbots), use nucleus/top-$k$ sampling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" and "How" Matter:** The encoder must capture *all* relevant information in one vector — a severe bottleneck. The decoder must learn to *attend* to this single vector repeatedly across all output steps. This duality is what made attention (introduced in Bahdanau et al., 2015) such a revolutionary idea.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$ | Context vector = final encoder hidden state | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 2 | $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$ | Autoregressive factorization of target sequence | Scalar probability |
| 3 | $p(y_t \mid \cdot) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$ | Output token distribution | $\mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$ |
| 4 | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}^{\ast}, \mathbf{c})$ | Negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy) loss per sequence | Scalar (nats) |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}$ | Per-batch mean loss | Scalar (nats) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{h}_t = f_{\text{RNN}}(\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, x_t)$ | Generic RNN recurrence | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 7 | $i_t, f_t, o_t \in [0,1]^{d_h}$ | LSTM gates (input/forget/output) | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 8 | $\mathbf{c}_{\text{att}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_x} \alpha_t \mathbf{h}_t^{\text{enc}}$ | Attention-based context (dynamic $\mathbf{c}$) | $\mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ |
| 9 | $\alpha_t = \dfrac{\exp(e_t)}{\sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_k)}$ | Softmax attention weights | $\sum \alpha_t = 1$ |
| 10 | $e_t = \text{score}(s_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_t^{\text{enc}})$ | Alignment score (additive/Bahdanau or dot-product/Luong) | Scalar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** When listing dimensions in the answer sheet, always state them explicitly. KTU examiners award 1 mark for correct dimensioning.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Encoder Type | Decoder Type | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Bi-LSTM on source language | LSTM on target language | Translated sentence |
| **Image Captioning** | CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) | LSTM | Natural-language caption |
| **Speech Recognition** | Pyramidal LSTM on spectrograms | LSTM | Phoneme/word sequence |
| **Text Summarization** | Bi-LSTM | LSTM with pointer-generator | Summary tokens |
| **Video Description** | 3D-CNN + LSTM | LSTM | Sentences describing motion |
| **Chatbots / Dialogue** | Bi-LSTM/Transformer | LSTM/Transformer | Conversational reply |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Autoregressive Factorization

We begin with the joint probability of the target sequence conditioned on the source:

$$p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = p(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{T_y} \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{T_x})$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **chain rule of probability** to decompose the joint distribution over target tokens:

$$p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = p(y_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \cdot p(y_2 \mid y_1, \mathbf{x}) \cdot p(y_3 \mid y_1, y_2, \mathbf{x}) \cdots p(y_{T_y} \mid y_{<T_y}, \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the dependence on $\mathbf{x}$ can be replaced by the encoder's context vector $\mathbf{c}$ because $\mathbf{c}$ is a deterministic summary of $\mathbf{x}$. Thus:

$$p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{x}) = p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 3:** Combine into a product form:

$$\boxed{p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})}$$

> This factorization is the **mathematical heart** of every encoder-decoder model. It says: *"The probability of the whole target sentence equals the product of probabilities of each target word, given the context and the words generated so far."*

## 3.2 Derivation: The Negative Log-Likelihood Training Objective

**Step 1:** The model is trained by **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — choose parameters $\theta$ that maximize the log-likelihood of the training corpus $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(n)}, \mathbf{y}^{(n)})\}_{n=1}^{N}$:

$$\theta^{\ast} = \arg\max_{\theta} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y}^{(n)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(n)})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the factorization from Section 3.1:

$$\log p_{\theta}(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \log \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 3:** Convert maximization to minimization by negating, yielding the per-sequence loss:

$$\mathcal{L}^{(n)}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p_{\theta}(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{c}^{(n)})$$

**Step 4:** Average over the mini-batch of size $B$ to obtain the training loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{n=1}^{B} \mathcal{L}^{(n)}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{B} \sum_{n=1}^{B} \sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(n)}} \log p_{\theta}(y_t^{(n)} \mid y_{<t}^{(n)}, \mathbf{c}^{(n)})$$

**Step 5:** Note that $\log p_{\theta}(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \log [\text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)]_{y_t}$, which is equivalent to the **categorical cross-entropy** between the one-hot target $y_t^{\ast}$ and the predicted distribution:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} y_t^{\ast}(j) \cdot \log \hat{p}(j \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

Since $y_t^{\ast}$ is one-hot, this reduces to the single term:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\log \hat{p}(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the exam:** KTU often asks *"Derive the loss function for the Seq2Seq model."* Writing the full chain-rule factorization and the cross-entropy equivalence fetches full marks (typically 5–7 marks).

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient Flow Through the Context Vector

The decoder parameter updates depend on the gradient of the loss w.r.t. decoder weights $W_{\text{dec}}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{\text{dec}}} = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \frac{1}{p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})} \cdot \frac{\partial p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})}{\partial W_{\text{dec}}}$$

Critically, the gradient also flows **back through the context vector** to update encoder weights:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_{\text{enc}}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{c}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{c}}{\partial W_{\text{enc}}}$$

where:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{c}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_t}{\partial s_t} \cdot \frac{\partial s_t}{\partial \mathbf{c}}$$

This shows that the encoder receives **learning signal from every decoding step** — a key reason RNN encoders trained jointly with decoders learn meaningful representations despite the bottleneck.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Basic Encoder-Decoder | Encoder-Decoder + Attention | Transformer (No Recurrence) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottleneck** | Single fixed $\mathbf{c}$ | Dynamic $\mathbf{c}_t$ per step | No bottleneck (full attention) |
| **Long sequences** | Suffers (vanishing gradients) | Handles well | Handles very well (parallel) |
| **Parallelism** | Sequential | Sequential (decoder) | Fully parallel |
| **Time complexity** | $O(T_x + T_y)$ | $O(T_x \cdot T_y)$ | $O((T_x + T_y)^2)$ |
| **Memory** | $O(d_h)$ for $\mathbf{c}$ | $O(T_x \cdot d_h)$ for keys/values | $O((T_x + T_y)^2)$ for attention matrix |
| **Training stability** | Moderate (teacher forcing helps) | Better | Best (with proper init) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of Seq2Seq Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Sentence x1 x2 ... xTx] --> B[Tokenization and Embedding]
    B --> C[Encoder LSTM Layer 1]
    C --> D[Encoder LSTM Layer 2]
    D --> E[Final Hidden State hTx]
    E --> F[Context Vector c]
    F --> G[Decoder LSTM Init s0 equals c]
    G --> H[SOS Token Input]
    H --> I[Decoder Step t equals 1]
    I --> J[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    J --> K[Output y1]
    K --> L[Next Decoder Step t equals 2]
    L --> M[Softmax over Vocabulary]
    M --> N[Output y2]
    N --> O[Continue until EOS Token]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Time-Unrolled Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENC [ENCODER NETWORK]
        e1[Embedding x1] --> h1[LSTM Cell]
        e2[Embedding x2] --> h2[LSTM Cell]
        e3[Embedding x3] --> h3[LSTM Cell]
        eN[Embedding xTx] --> hN[LSTM Cell]
        h1 --> h2
        h2 --> h3
        h3 --> hN
    end
    hN --> ctx[Context Vector c]
    ctx --> s0[Decoder Hidden s0]

    subgraph DEC [DECODER NETWORK]
        s0 --> s1[Decoder LSTM Cell t=1]
        s1 --> p1[Softmax]
        p1 --> y1[Output y1]
        y1 --> s2[Decoder LSTM Cell t=2]
        s2 --> p2[Softmax]
        p2 --> y2[Output y2]
        y2 --> s3[Decoder LSTM Cell t=3]
        s3 --> p3[Softmax]
        p3 --> y3[Output y3]
    end
```

## 4.3 Training vs. Inference Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAIN [TRAINING PHASE - TEACHER FORCING]
        t1[Ground Truth y1 star] --> td1[Decoder Input]
        t2[Ground Truth y2 star] --> td2[Decoder Input]
        t3[Ground Truth y3 star] --> td3[Decoder Input]
        ctxT[Context c] --> td1
        td1 --> td2
        td2 --> td3
        td3 --> loss[Cross Entropy Loss]
    end

    subgraph INF [INFERENCE PHASE - AUTOREGRESSIVE]
        ctxI[Context c] --> id1[Decoder Step]
        iSOS[SOS Token] --> id1
        id1 --> ip1[Sample Argmax]
        ip1 --> oy1[Output y1 hat]
        oy1 --> id2[Decoder Step]
        id2 --> ip2[Sample Argmax]
        ip2 --> oy2[Output y2 hat]
        oy2 --> id3[Decoder Step]
        id3 --> ip3[Sample Argmax]
        ip3 --> EOS[EOS Token Stop]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Teacher Forcing** is the technique of feeding the *ground-truth* token (instead of the model's own prediction) at every decoder step during training. This stabilizes training but introduces **exposure bias** at inference. KTU often asks this as a 3-mark conceptual question.

## 4.4 Information Flow in Encoder-Decoder with Attention (Pre-Transformer Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Encoder Hidden States h1 h2 ... hTx] --> K[Key Projections]
    sPrev[Previous Decoder State st minus 1] --> Q[Query Projection]
    Q --> S[Alignment Scores e1 e2 ... eTx]
    K --> S
    S --> SM[Softmax over Source Steps]
    SM --> AW[Attention Weights alpha 1 ... alpha Tx]
    AW --> CtxAtt[Dynamic Context c t equals sum alpha h]
    A --> CtxAtt
    CtxAtt --> Dec[Decoder Step t]
    sPrev --> Dec
    Dec --> St[New State st]
    St --> Out[Output yt]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram is the **conceptual bridge** from basic encoder-decoder to the Transformer. The attention module replaces the static $\mathbf{c}$ with a **dynamic, time-varying** $\mathbf{c}_t$ that selectively focuses on relevant source tokens.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define an Encoder-Decoder model. List its two main components and their roles.

**Model Answer:**

An **Encoder-Decoder model** is a neural architecture that maps a variable-length input sequence $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_{T_x})$ to a variable-length output sequence $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_{T_y})$, where input and output may differ in domain and length.

**Two main components:**

1. **Encoder**: A neural network (typically RNN/LSTM/GRU) that processes the input sequence sequentially, compressing all information into a fixed-dimensional **context vector** $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$ (the final hidden state).
2. **Decoder**: A neural network that uses the context vector $\mathbf{c}$ and previously generated tokens to autoregressively produce output tokens one at a time, modeled as $p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$.

*(Diagram with arrows showing encoder → context vector → decoder earns bonus presentation marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is **Teacher Forcing**? Why is it used during training of Seq2Seq models?

**Model Answer:**

**Teacher Forcing** is a training strategy for sequence decoders in which the **ground-truth token** $y_{t-1}^{\ast}$ from the training data is fed as input to the decoder at step $t$, *instead of* the model's own previous prediction $\hat{y}_{t-1}$.

**Why it is used:**

1. **Faster convergence**: The model always sees correct history, so errors do not compound during training.
2. **Stable gradients**: Avoids the snowball effect where a wrong early prediction corrupts all subsequent steps.
3. **Parallel-friendly loss computation**: Each decoder step's loss can be computed independently since input history is known.

**Drawback**: At inference, the model must generate autoregressively without ground truth, leading to **exposure bias** — a mismatch between training and inference distributions. Mitigations include **scheduled sampling** and **Professor Forcing**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

#### **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq) Encoder-Decoder model in detail. Clearly describe the encoder, the context vector, and the decoder with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Seq2Seq Encoder-Decoder model** (Sutskever et al., 2014) is designed to transform an input sequence of length $T_x$ into an output sequence of length $T_y$, where $T_x \neq T_y$ in general. It has three logical components:

**1. The Encoder** (4-layer stacked LSTM in the original paper):

- Each input token $x_t$ is converted into a dense embedding $\mathbf{e}_t = E[x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$.
- The LSTM updates its hidden state at every step:
$$h_t = \text{LSTM}(h_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t), \quad t = 1, 2, \dots, T_x$$
- The final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{T_x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ encodes a **summary of the entire source sequence**.

**2. The Context Vector** $\mathbf{c}$:

- In the simplest form, $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}$.
- A learnable transformation is often applied: $\mathbf{c} = \tanh(W_c \mathbf{h}_{T_x})$, allowing $\mathbf{c}$ and the decoder's initial state to have different dimensions.

**3. The Decoder** (4-layer stacked LSTM):

- Initialized with $s_0 = \mathbf{c}$.
- At step $t$, it takes the embedding of the previously generated token $\mathbf{e}_{y_{t-1}}$ and the previous hidden state $s_{t-1}$:
$$s_t = \text{LSTM}(s_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_{y_{t-1}})$$
- The hidden state is projected to vocabulary space and softmaxed:
$$p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$$

**Block Diagram:**

```
Input: x1 → x2 → ... → xTx
       ↓      ↓           ↓
   [ENC LSTM h1] [h2] ... [hT] → c (Context)
                                     ↓
                                  s0 = c
                                     ↓
   y0 = <SOS> → [DEC LSTM s1] → softmax → ŷ1
        ŷ1     → [DEC LSTM s2] → softmax → ŷ2
        ŷ2     → [DEC LSTM s3] → softmax → ŷ3
        ...        (until <EOS>)
```

---

#### **(b)** Derive the loss function used to train a Seq2Seq model. Discuss why cross-entropy is preferred over MSE for sequence generation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of the Loss Function:**

Using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**, we maximize the log-probability of the ground-truth target sequence $\mathbf{y}^{\ast}$ given the source $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1**: Factorize the joint probability of the target sequence using the chain rule:

$$p(\mathbf{y}^{\ast} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}^{\ast}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 2**: Take the log:

$$\log p(\mathbf{y}^{\ast} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}^{\ast}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 3**: Negate to obtain a minimization objective (the **negative log-likelihood**):

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_t^{\ast} \mid y_{<t}^{\ast}, \mathbf{c})$$

**Step 4**: Substitute the softmax form, $p(y_t^{\ast} \mid \cdot) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)_{y_t^{\ast}}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \log \left[ \frac{\exp((W_o s_t + b_o)_{y_t^{\ast}})}{\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp((W_o s_t + b_o)_j)} \right]$$

**Step 5**: This is exactly the **categorical cross-entropy** between the one-hot encoded ground truth and the predicted distribution:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\sum_{j=1}^{\vert V \vert} y_t^{\ast}(j) \cdot \log \hat{p}_t(j)$$

**Why Cross-Entropy over MSE for Sequence Generation:**

| Aspect | Cross-Entropy | Mean Squared Error |
|---|---|---|
| **Probabilistic interpretation** | Directly tied to MLE | Indirect |
| **Gradient behavior** | Strong gradients even when prediction is wrong (logarithmic) | Vanishing for nearly correct outputs |
| **Output type** | Designed for probability distributions (softmax outputs) | Designed for real-valued regression |
| **Class imbalance** | Works well with sparse one-hot targets | Penalizes uniformly |
| **Saturation** | Saturates only when softmax is extremely confident | Saturates quickly, hindering learning |

Hence, **cross-entropy is the canonical loss** for classification-style token prediction in language generation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

#### **(a)** Discuss the limitations of the basic Encoder-Decoder model. How does the **attention mechanism** overcome these limitations? Provide the mathematical formulation of the attention-based context vector. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of the Basic Encoder-Decoder:**

1. **Information Bottleneck**: The entire source sentence must be compressed into a **single fixed-dimensional vector** $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$. For long sequences (e.g., 100+ words), this is a severe compression bottleneck — fine-grained details are lost.
2. **Vanishing Gradients**: In RNN/LSTM encoders, gradients from later decoder steps must travel back through *all* encoder time steps, leading to **long-term dependency issues** for very long sequences.
3. **Unequal Importance of Tokens**: In translation, only specific source words matter for a given target word. The fixed $\mathbf{c}$ treats all source words equally, forcing the decoder to "re-discover" relevant information at every step.
4. **Lack of Alignment Modeling**: The model has no explicit mechanism to learn word-to-word or phrase-to-phrase correspondences between source and target.

**How Attention Overcomes These:**

The **attention mechanism** (Bahdanau et al., 2015) replaces the static context vector with a **dynamic, time-varying context** $\mathbf{c}_t$ that is recomputed at every decoding step, allowing the decoder to "look back" at *all* encoder states and weight them by relevance.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

Given encoder hidden states $\mathbf{H} = \{\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2, \dots, \mathbf{h}_{T_x}\}$ and the previous decoder state $s_{t-1}$:

**Step 1 — Alignment Scores** (Bahdanau additive style):

$$e_{t,i} = \mathbf{v}_a^{\top} \tanh(W_a s_{t-1} + U_a \mathbf{h}_i)$$

where $W_a, U_a, \mathbf{v}_a$ are learnable parameters.

**Step 2 — Attention Weights** (softmax over source positions):

$$\alpha_{t,i} = \frac{\exp(e_{t,i})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T_x} \exp(e_{t,k})}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic Context Vector** (weighted sum of encoder states):

$$\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} \, \mathbf{h}_i$$

**Step 4 — Decoder Update** (concatenate context with input):

$$s_t = \text{LSTM}\big(s_{t-1}, \, [\mathbf{e}_{y_{t-1}} ; \mathbf{c}_t]\big)$$

> This eliminates the bottleneck, allows soft alignment learning, and significantly improves BLEU scores in machine translation.

---

#### **(b)** Compare **Greedy Decoding** and **Beam Search** for inference in Encoder-Decoder models. Under what conditions does each become preferable? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Greedy Decoding:**

- At each decoding step $t$, select the token with the **highest probability**:
$$y_t = \arg\max_{y} \, p(y \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$$
- **Pros**: Very fast ($O(T_y \cdot \vert V \vert)$), simple to implement, low memory.
- **Cons**: **Myopic** — a locally optimal choice may lead to globally suboptimal sequences. Cannot backtrack.

**Beam Search (width $k$):**

- Maintain the top-$k$ partial sequences (hypotheses) at every step.
- For each hypothesis, expand by all vocabulary tokens, score the resulting $k \cdot \vert V \vert$ candidates, and keep the top $k$.
- The score is the **log-probability** of the partial sequence:
$$\text{score}(\mathbf{y}_{1:t}) = \sum_{j=1}^{t} \log p(y_j \mid y_{<j}, \mathbf{c})$$
- A **length normalization** is typically applied to avoid penalizing longer sequences:
$$\text{score}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{1}{T_y^{\alpha}} \sum_{j=1}^{T_y} \log p(y_j \mid y_{<j}, \mathbf{c})$$
- **Pros**: Explores multiple hypotheses, yields significantly better translations/summaries.
- **Cons**: Slower ($O(k \cdot T_y \cdot \vert V \vert)$), more memory, may still favor generic high-frequency tokens.

**When to Prefer Each:**

| Scenario | Preferred Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time chatbot response | **Greedy** | Latency constraints |
| High-stakes machine translation | **Beam Search** ($k$=5–10) | Translation quality critical |
| Creative text generation | **Sampling** (top-$k$, nucleus) | Diversity matters |
| Latency-sensitive ASR | **Greedy** or **Beam $k$=2$** | Sub-second response required |
| Offline document translation | **Beam $k$=10$** | Quality > speed |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Encoder-Decoder Exam Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the context vector dimension** — Always state $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$ explicitly. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing dimensions.
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{c}$ with $h_t$** — The context vector is *fixed* across all decoder steps in the basic model; it is *not* updated as $s_t$ evolves. Many students incorrectly write $\mathbf{c}_t = s_t$.
> 3. **Mixing up teacher forcing and scheduled sampling** — These are *different* techniques. Teacher forcing uses ground truth always; scheduled sampling gradually transitions to model predictions.
> 4. **Skipping the chain-rule derivation** — A common 3-mark loss question. Writing the loss without showing the chain-rule factorization loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing "encoder" and "decoder" parameters in backprop** — Encoder parameters receive gradients *via* the context vector from the decoder loss, not directly.
> 6. **Writing "attention" without softmax** — Attention weights $\alpha_t$ must be a *probability distribution*; always include the softmax.
> 7. **Dimensions in Mermaid diagrams** — The diagrams above intentionally omit dimension labels for clarity; in the answer sheet, label shapes like $d_h$, $d_e$, $\vert V \vert$ to earn method marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Encoder-Decoder Models**

- [x] An **Encoder-Decoder** maps variable-length input to variable-length output via a fixed-size **context vector $\mathbf{c}$**.
- [x] **Encoder**: $h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$; final state = $\mathbf{c}$.
- [x] **Decoder**: $p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) = \text{softmax}(W_o s_t + b_o)$.
- [x] **Autoregressive factorization**: $p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{x}) = \prod_{t=1}^{T_y} p(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$.
- [x] **Loss** = Negative log-likelihood = Categorical cross-entropy summed over time steps.
- [x] **Teacher Forcing** feeds ground-truth tokens during training; causes **exposure bias** at inference.
- [x] **Greedy decoding** picks $\arg\max$ per step; **Beam search** keeps top-$k$ hypotheses.
- [x] **Attention mechanism** replaces static $\mathbf{c}$ with **dynamic $\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_i \alpha_{t,i} \mathbf{h}_i$**; attention weights $\alpha$ are softmaxed alignment scores.
- [x] **Limitation of basic model**: information bottleneck; **solved** by attention.
- [x] **Applications**: Machine Translation, Image Captioning, Speech Recognition, Summarization, Chatbots.
- [x] **Original Seq2Seq papers**: Sutskever et al. (NIPS 2014), Cho et al. (EMNLP 2014).
- [x] **Attention paper**: Bahdanau et al. (ICLR 2015).
- [x] **Always state dimensions** in exam answers: $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{d_h}$, $\mathbf{e}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d_e}$, output distribution $\in \mathbb{R}^{\vert V \vert}$.
- [x] **Total parameters** in basic encoder-decoder: encoder $W_{\text{enc}}$, decoder $W_{\text{dec}}$, embedding $E$, output projection $W_o$ — all updated via backpropagation-through-time (BPTT).
