topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attention Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Attention Mechanism:** An attention mechanism is a computational module in a neural network that dynamically assigns differential importance weights (attention scores) to different elements of an input sequence, enabling the model to focus selectively on the most relevant parts of the input when producing each element of the output. In modern deep learning, attention is formalized as a differentiable **key-value lookup** that maps a *Query* to a weighted sum of *Values* indexed by *Keys*.

Mathematically, an attention layer is a function that maps a **Query** $Q$ and a set of **Key-Value** pairs $(K, V)$ to an output, where the query, keys, values, and output are all **vectors**. The output is computed as a weighted sum of the values, where the weight assigned to each value is derived from a compatibility function between the query and the corresponding key.

The canonical form, **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**, is given as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

where $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the key vectors, and the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the dot products from growing excessively large in magnitude (which would push the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients).

## 1.2 Intuition Through a Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Library Search:** Imagine you are a student researching for a term paper in a vast library. Instead of reading every book cover-to-cover, you formulate a **query** (your research question). The librarian looks at the **index cards (keys)** of every book and tells you which books are most relevant, assigning a relevance **score** to each. You then pull the most useful **content (values)** from the top-ranked books. The library's catalog never alters the books themselves — it merely tells you *how much attention* to pay to each. Attention networks do the same: they leave the input vectors untouched and only re-weight their influence on the output.

- The **Query ($Q$)** is your current information need.
- The **Keys ($K$)** are descriptive labels (titles, topics) of available memories.
- The **Values ($V$)** are the actual content of those memories.
- The **attention weights** (softmax output) are the relevance scores.
- The **final output** is a context vector that mixes the values according to their relevance.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Attention Replaced Pure Recurrence:** Before attention, sequence models (RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs) had to compress an entire input sequence into a single fixed-size hidden state vector, creating an **information bottleneck**. Attention eliminates this bottleneck by allowing the decoder to "look back" at *all* encoder states at every decoding step, weighted by learned relevance — a paradigm shift formalized in *Bahdanau et al. (2014)* and generalized by *Vaswani et al. (2017)* in the Transformer.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Variants (KTU High-Priority Terms)

| Variant | Description | Introduced By |
|---|---|---|
| **Bahdanau (Additive) Attention** | Uses a feed-forward network to compute alignment scores $e_{ij} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_{i-1} + U_a h_j)$ | Bahdanau et al., 2014 |
| **Luong (Multiplicative) Attention** | Computes scores as a simple dot product $e_{ij} = s_i^{\top} h_j$ | Luong et al., 2015 |
| **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** | Adds a $1/\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling to the dot product for numerical stability | Vaswani et al., 2017 |
| **Self-Attention** | $Q$, $K$, $V$ all derived from the *same* input sequence | Vaswani et al., 2017 |
| **Multi-Head Attention** | Runs $h$ parallel attention "heads" with different learned projections, then concatenates | Vaswani et al., 2017 |
| **Cross-Attention** | $Q$ comes from one source (e.g., decoder) while $K, V$ come from another (e.g., encoder) | Vaswani et al., 2017 |

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants & Hyperparameters

- **Scaling factor** $\sqrt{d_k}$: standard in Scaled Dot-Product Attention; **prevents softmax saturation**.
- **Number of heads $h$** in Multi-Head Attention: commonly $h = 8$ (Transformer base) or $h = 16$.
- **Model dimension $d_{\text{model}}$**: typically **512** (base Transformer).
- **Per-head dimension $d_v = d_k = d_{\text{model}} / h$**: typically **64** when $d_{\text{model}} = 512$ and $h = 8$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through — How Attention Works (Step by Step)

> [!NOTE]
> The following logic flow applies to **Scaled Dot-Product Attention**, the core operator of every modern Transformer-based architecture.

1. **Project the inputs** — From the input embeddings $X$, derive three matrices through learned linear projections:
   - Queries: $Q = X W^Q$
   - Keys: $\;\;K = X W^K$
   - Values: $\;\;V = X W^V$

   Here $W^Q, W^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$ and $W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$ are trainable parameter matrices.

2. **Compute raw compatibility scores** — Measure how well each query matches every key using a dot product:
   $$S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$
   Each entry $S_{ij}$ represents the unnormalized relevance of position $j$ to position $i$.

3. **Scale the scores** — Divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to keep gradients well-behaved:
   $$S' = \frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}$$

4. **(Optional) Apply masking** — For causal/autoregressive settings, set future positions to $-\infty$ before softmax so they receive zero attention.

5. **Normalize via softmax** — Convert the scaled scores into a valid probability distribution (rows sum to 1):
   $$A = \text{softmax}(S') = \frac{\exp(S'_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \exp(S'_{ik})}$$

6. **Compute the weighted sum of values** — The final output at each position is a context-aware mixture of all value vectors:
   $$\text{Output} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 (Projections):** Different learned subspaces allow the model to *separately* encode "what to look for" ($Q$), "what is available" ($K$), and "what to extract" ($V$).
- **Step 2 (Dot product):** Efficient inner-product similarity — equivalent to cosine similarity up to a magnitude term, and *very* parallelizable on GPUs.
- **Step 3 (Scaling):** As $d_k$ grows, dot-product magnitudes grow proportionally, pushing softmax into saturated regions with near-zero gradient. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of scores close to **1**.
- **Step 5 (Softmax):** Provides a differentiable, normalized selector. Soft weights allow gradient-based training.
- **Step 6 (Aggregation):** A smooth interpolation of values — soft retrieval rather than hard selection.

## 2.3 Multi-Head Attention — The "How" of Parallelism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multi-Head Attention (MHA)** allows the model to jointly attend to information from *different representation subspaces* at *different positions*. A single attention head can only capture one type of relationship; multiple heads capture many in parallel.

The mathematical formulation:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$$

where each head is computed independently as:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, \, K W_i^K, \, V W_i^V)$$

- $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$.
- $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$ is the output projection that mixes the heads.
- Typical choice: $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}} / h$, keeping total compute comparable to single-head attention with full dimensionality.

## 2.4 Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention

| Property | Self-Attention | Cross-Attention |
|---|---|---|
| Source of $Q$ | Same sequence as $K, V$ | Different sequence (e.g., decoder) |
| Source of $K, V$ | Same sequence as $Q$ | Different sequence (e.g., encoder) |
| Typical Location | Encoder & decoder of Transformer | Decoder of Transformer |
| Purpose | Capture intra-sequence dependencies | Bridge two different modalities/sequences |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Shape / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Query matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ |
| $K$ | Key matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$ |
| $V$ | Value matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$ |
| $d_k$ | Key/query dimension | positive integer |
| $d_v$ | Value dimension | positive integer |
| $n$ | Sequence length | positive integer |
| $A$ | Attention weight matrix | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| $W^Q, W^K, W^V, W^O$ | Learnable projection matrices | matrices of appropriate shape |
| $h$ | Number of attention heads | positive integer |
| $d_{\text{model}}$ | Model hidden dimension | positive integer |

| # | Formula | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{softmax}(z_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Softmax normalization | Convert logits to probabilities |
| 2 | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | Core attention operation |
| 3 | $e_{ij} = v_a^{\top}\tanh(W_a s_{i-1} + U_a h_j)$ | Additive (Bahdanau) Score | Compute alignment |
| 4 | $e_{ij} = s_i^{\top} h_j$ | Multiplicative (Luong) Score | Simpler alignment |
| 5 | $\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V)$ | Single head | One parallel subspace |
| 6 | $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h) W^O$ | Multi-Head Attention | Combine subspaces |
| 7 | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Layer Normalization | Stabilize training |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Attention networks power **machine translation** (Google Translate), **document understanding** (BERT), **code generation** (GitHub Copilot), **medical image captioning**, **autonomous driving perception**, **speech recognition** (Whisper), and **protein structure prediction** (AlphaFold). Understanding attention is therefore a non-negotiable skill for any modern AI/ML engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the Scaling Factor is $\sqrt{d_k}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. The derivation shows that without scaling, the variance of dot-product scores grows linearly with $d_k$, saturating the softmax.

### Setup

Assume the components of $Q$ and $K$ are independent random variables with **mean 0** and **variance 1**. Let:

$$q_i, k_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{(independent, zero mean, unit variance)}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the mean of a single dot-product entry

The raw dot product is $s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$.

$$\mathbb{E}[s] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i k_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0$$

(using independence and zero mean).

### Step 2 — Compute the variance of a single dot-product entry

Because $q_i$ and $k_i$ are independent:

$$\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2 k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i k_i])^2 = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] - 0$$

With unit variance, $\mathbb{E}[q_i^2] = \mathbb{E}[k_i^2] = 1$, so $\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = 1$.

Since the $d_k$ terms are independent:

$$\text{Var}(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = d_k$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the result

The dot-product score has variance $d_k$. As $d_k$ grows (e.g., $d_k = 64$ in standard Transformers), the score magnitudes grow proportionally. Feeding such large values into softmax pushes it into regions where:

$$\text{softmax}(z_i) \approx \delta_{i,\arg\max z_i}$$

producing **one-hot-like outputs** whose gradients are vanishingly small.

### Step 4 — Apply the fix

To restore unit variance, divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$:

$$s' = \frac{s}{\sqrt{d_k}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{Var}(s') = \frac{\text{Var}(s)}{d_k} = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1$$

This is exactly the **scaled dot-product** used in the Transformer. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computational Complexity of Self-Attention

A common exam question asks students to compare the complexity of self-attention with recurrent and convolutional layers.

### Step 1 — Per-layer complexity

| Layer Type | Complexity per Layer | Sequential Ops | Max Path Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-Attention** | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Recurrent (RNN)** | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Convolution (kernel $k$)** | $O(k \cdot n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_k n)$ |

### Step 2 — Justify the self-attention complexity

- The $Q K^{\top}$ multiplication costs $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ (matrix multiply of $n \times d$ and $d \times n$).
- The softmax over each of $n$ rows costs $O(n^2)$.
- Multiplying $A V$ costs $O(n^2 \cdot d)$.
- **Total: $O(n^2 \cdot d)$** in time.

For **space**, the attention matrix $A$ is $n \times n$, giving $O(n^2)$ memory.

### Step 3 — Interpret the trade-off

Self-attention is **quadratic in sequence length** — expensive for very long sequences ($n > 10^4$). This motivated efficient variants such as **Linformer**, **Performer**, **Longformer**, and **FlashAttention** (KTU: mention these in modern applications).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Mini Attention Computation

> [!TIP]
> A classic 7-mark KTU question: "Compute the attention output for the given query, key, value vectors."

Let:

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$$

Here $n = 2$ queries, $n = 3$ keys, $d_k = 2$, $d_v = 3$.

### Step 1 — Raw scores $S = Q K^{\top}$

$$S = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k} = \sqrt{2}$

$$S' = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \\ 0 & 0.7071 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3 — Row-wise softmax

**Row 1:** $z = (0.7071,\, 0,\, 0.7071)$

$$e^z = (2.0281,\, 1.0000,\, 2.0281), \quad \sum e^z = 5.0562$$

$$A_{1,:} = (0.4013,\, 0.1978,\, 0.4013)$$

**Row 2:** $z = (0,\, 0.7071,\, 0.7071)$

$$e^z = (1.0000,\, 2.0281,\, 2.0281), \quad \sum e^z = 5.0562$$

$$A_{2,:} = (0.1978,\, 0.4013,\, 0.4013)$$

So the attention matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4013 & 0.1978 & 0.4013 \\ 0.1978 & 0.4013 & 0.4013 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Final output $\text{Output} = A V$

Recall $V \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ (3 keys, $d_v = 3$):

$$V = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{constructed from the given } V^{\top})$$

Computing each output row:

**Output row 1:**

$$o_1 = 0.4013 \begin{bmatrix} 1\\2\\3 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1978 \begin{bmatrix} 4\\5\\6 \end{bmatrix} + 0.4013 \begin{bmatrix} 7\\8\\9 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$o_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4013 \\ 0.8026 \\ 1.2039 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.7912 \\ 0.9890 \\ 1.1868 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2.8091 \\ 3.2104 \\ 3.6117 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.0016 \\ 5.0020 \\ 6.0024 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Output row 2:** (by symmetry of this example)

$$o_2 \approx \begin{bmatrix} 4.9984 \\ 5.9980 \\ 6.9976 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Each output row is a convex combination of value rows (weights sum to 1), so the resulting vectors lie inside the convex hull of $V$ — exactly what attention is supposed to do. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Relevant)

| Property | Bahdanau Attention | Luong Attention | Scaled Dot-Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Score function** | Additive (tanh + linear) | Multiplicative (dot) | Scaled dot-product |
| **Computational cost** | Higher (tanh + linear layers) | Lower | Lowest (single matmul) |
| **Parallelizability** | Limited | Limited | Fully parallel |
| **Used in** | Early NMT (2014–2016) | NMT (2015) | Transformer family |
| **Key advantage** | Strong expressivity | Simplicity | Speed + scaling stability |
| **Key disadvantage** | Slow, sequential | Can saturate for large $d_k$ | Quadratic in $n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputSeq[Input Embeddings X] --> projQ[Linear Projection WQ]
    inputSeq --> projK[Linear Projection WK]
    inputSeq --> projV[Linear Projection WV]
    projQ --> Qmat[Query Matrix Q]
    projK --> Kmat[Key Matrix K]
    projV --> Vmat[Value Matrix V]
    Qmat --> matmul[Matrix Multiply Q times K transpose]
    Kmat --> matmul
    matmul --> scale[Scale by 1 over sqrt dk]
    scale --> mask[Optional Mask future positions]
    mask --> softmax[Softmax row wise]
    softmax --> weights[Attention Weight Matrix A]
    weights --> weightedSum[Weighted Sum A times V]
    Vmat --> weightedSum
    weightedSum --> output[Context Vector Output]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Head Attention Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputX[Input X] --> split[Split into h heads]
    split --> head1[Head 1 attention]
    split --> head2[Head 2 attention]
    split --> headN[Head h attention]
    head1 --> concat[Concatenate heads]
    head2 --> concat
    headN --> concat
    concat --> outputProj[Linear Projection WO]
    outputProj --> outNode[MHA Output]
```

## 4.3 Self-Attention vs Cross-Attention — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SelfAttention
        SAin[Input Sequence] --> SAQ[Q from input]
        SAin --> SAK[K from input]
        SAin --> SAV[V from input]
        SAQ --> SAatt[Attention]
        SAK --> SAatt
        SAV --> SAatt
        SAatt --> SAout[Output same shape as input]
    end
    subgraph CrossAttention
        CAq[Decoder State] --> CAQ[Q from decoder]
        CAenc[Encoder Output] --> CAK[K from encoder]
        CAenc --> CAV[V from encoder]
        CAQ --> CAatt[Attention]
        CAK --> CAatt
        CAV --> CAatt
        CAatt --> CAout[Decoder Context]
    end
```

## 4.4 Full Transformer Encoder Block — Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    in[Input Embeddings] --> posAdd[Add Positional Encoding]
    posAdd --> mha[Multi-Head Self-Attention]
    mha --> addNorm1[Add and LayerNorm]
    addNorm1 --> ffn[Feed-Forward Network]
    ffn --> addNorm2[Add and LayerNorm]
    addNorm2 --> encOut[Encoder Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each encoder block performs *self-attention over its own previous output*, followed by a *position-wise feed-forward network (FFN)*. Residual connections (the "Add" arrows) and LayerNorm stabilize training of deep stacks. This same block is stacked $N$ times (typically $N = 6$ in the base Transformer).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q:** Define the **attention mechanism** in deep learning. What problem in encoder-decoder RNNs does it solve?

**Model Answer:**

The attention mechanism is a neural network component that computes a weighted combination of input representations, where the weights (attention scores) reflect the **relevance** of each input element to a current decoding step. It is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

It solves the **information bottleneck problem** of traditional encoder-decoder RNNs, in which the entire input sequence must be compressed into a single fixed-size context vector, causing performance degradation on long sequences. Attention allows the decoder to "look back" at *all* encoder states at every step, selectively focusing on the most relevant ones. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q:** What is the difference between **self-attention** and **cross-attention**?

**Model Answer:**

- **Self-Attention:** All of $Q$, $K$, and $V$ are derived from the *same* input sequence. It captures **intra-sequence dependencies** (e.g., relationships between words in the same sentence). Used in both encoder and decoder of the Transformer.
- **Cross-Attention:** $Q$ comes from one source (e.g., the decoder's previous layer) while $K$ and $V$ come from a *different* source (e.g., the encoder's output). It bridges two different sequences, allowing the decoder to align with the encoder. Used only in the decoder's "encoder-decoder attention" sub-layer. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer any **ONE** full question from the choice.**

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With suitable diagrams and mathematical formulations, explain the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** mechanism. Show step-by-step how the scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ is derived and justify its necessity. **(14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Scaled Dot-Product Attention — Concept and Formulation (7 marks)**

Scaled Dot-Product Attention computes the output as a weighted sum of value vectors, where weights are derived from scaled query-key compatibility scores:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

*Step 1:* Compute raw similarity scores $S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$. Each entry $S_{ij}$ is the dot product of query $i$ and key $j$, indicating how much the model should attend from position $i$ to position $j$.

*Step 2:* Scale the scores: $S' = S / \sqrt{d_k}$. The scaling prevents dot-product magnitudes from growing with dimension.

*Step 3:* (Optional) Apply a mask — for causal language modeling, set masked positions to $-\infty$ so they get zero weight after softmax.

*Step 4:* Apply row-wise softmax to obtain attention weights $A_{ij} = \exp(S'_{ij}) / \sum_k \exp(S'_{ik})$. Each row is a probability distribution over input positions.

*Step 5:* Compute output as weighted sum: $\text{Output} = A V \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$. The diagram is as shown in **Section 4.1**.

**(b) Derivation of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ Scaling Factor (7 marks)**

Assume $q_i, k_i$ are independent with mean **0** and variance **1**. The raw score $s = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$.

- **Mean:** $\mathbb{E}[s] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \mathbb{E}[q_i]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i] = 0$.
- **Variance:** $\text{Var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\,\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] = 1$ (using independence and unit variance). So $\text{Var}(s) = d_k$.

Without scaling, the dot product magnitude grows like $O(\sqrt{d_k})$, pushing softmax into saturated regions where the gradient $\approx 0$. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ gives unit variance:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{s}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) = \frac{d_k}{d_k} = 1$$

This keeps the softmax in a regime where gradients remain healthy, ensuring stable training — especially when $d_k$ is large (e.g., 64 or 128 in practice). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the **Multi-Head Attention** mechanism in detail. Compare it with a single attention head, and show how the outputs of multiple heads are combined. Mention the role of projection matrices $W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V, W^O$. **(14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Multi-Head Attention (7 marks)**

Multi-Head Attention (MHA) runs $h$ attention operations in **parallel**, each on a different learned linear projection of $Q$, $K$, and $V$. The motivation is that a single attention head can capture only **one type of relationship** at a time; multiple heads allow the model to jointly attend to information from **different representation subspaces** at **different positions**.

Mathematically:

$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \text{head}_2, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O$$

where each head is:

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\; K W_i^K,\; V W_i^V)$$

- $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$, $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}$.
- Common configuration: $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$ (e.g., $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$, so $d_k = d_v = 64$).
- The output projection $W^O \in \mathbb{R}^{h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}}$ mixes the concatenated heads back into the model dimension.

The key insight: the **total compute cost** of MHA is approximately the same as single-head attention with full $d_{\text{model}}$ dimensionality, because the per-head dimension is reduced by $1/h$. See **Section 4.2** for the block diagram.

**(b) Comparison with Single-Head and Role of Projections (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Single-Head | Multi-Head |
|---|---|---|
| Subspaces attended | 1 (averaged) | $h$ (parallel, distinct) |
| Expressivity | Limited | Rich, multi-perspective |
| Same total params? | — | Approximately same |
| Captures diverse patterns? | No | Yes (syntactic, semantic, positional) |

**Role of each projection matrix:**

- $W_i^Q$: projects input into the **query subspace** for head $i$ — encodes "what information am I looking for?"
- $W_i^K$: projects input into the **key subspace** for head $i$ — encodes "what content do I contain?"
- $W_i^V$: projects input into the **value subspace** for head $i$ — encodes "what should I contribute to the output if attended to?"
- $W^O$: **mixes** the $h$ parallel outputs into a coherent final vector, allowing cross-head interaction.

Empirically, different heads learn specialized functions — e.g., one head may focus on the previous token (positional), another on syntactic subject-verb links, another on long-range coreference. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Attention-Network Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling factor** — Writing $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top})V$ instead of $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks** lost.
> 2. **Confusing $W_i^Q$ shapes** — Writing $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k \times d_{\text{model}}}$ (transposed). The correct projection maps $d_{\text{model}} \to d_k$, so $W_i^Q \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}$.
> 3. **Mixing up self-attention and cross-attention** — In cross-attention, $Q$ comes from the **decoder**, $K$ and $V$ from the **encoder**, not all from the same source.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of $\sqrt{d_k}$** — Examiners explicitly look for the variance argument. Merely stating "it prevents softmax saturation" without deriving it **costs at least 3 marks**.
> 5. **Writing softmax over the entire matrix instead of row-wise** — Attention weights are normalized *per query*, i.e., row-wise softmax, not a single global softmax.
> 6. **Omitting masking in causal tasks** — For autoregressive models, forgetting to mask future positions is a frequent oversight.
> 7. **Stating "$W^O$ is a scalar"** — $W^O$ is a matrix of shape $h d_v \times d_{\text{model}}$, never a scalar.
> 8. **Confusing "head" and "layer"** — A Transformer has multiple **layers** (each containing MHA + FFN) and each layer has multiple **heads**. They are not the same thing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Attention Networks (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Attention computes a soft, weighted sum of *Values* using the similarity between *Query* and *Key* as weights.
- **Three matrices:** $Q$ (what to look for), $K$ (what is available), $V$ (what to extract).
- **Core formula:** $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$:** Derived from the variance of dot products being $d_k$ when components are zero-mean unit-variance — **must derive, not just state**.
- **Softmax is row-wise** — each query gets a probability distribution over keys.
- **Self-Attention:** $Q, K, V$ all from the *same* sequence; captures intra-sequence relations.
- **Cross-Attention:** $Q$ from decoder, $K, V$ from encoder; bridges two sequences.
- **Multi-Head Attention:** $h$ parallel heads with distinct learned projections, then **Concat + $W^O$** projection.
- **Standard hyperparameters:** $d_{\text{model}} = 512$, $h = 8$, $d_k = d_v = 64$.
- **Complexity:** Self-attention is $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ time and $O(n^2)$ memory — quadratic in sequence length.
- **Masking:** In causal language models, future positions are masked to $-\infty$ before softmax.
- **Residual + LayerNorm:** Every attention sub-layer in a Transformer is wrapped with a residual connection and layer normalization.
- **Why attention replaced recurrence:** Solves the information bottleneck; enables **parallelization**; allows $O(1)$ path length between any two positions.
- **Real-world impact:** Powers BERT, GPT, T5, Vision Transformers (ViT), Whisper, AlphaFold, Stable Diffusion.
- **Variants to know by name:** Linformer, Performer, Longformer, FlashAttention — all address the $O(n^2)$ bottleneck.
- **Examiner's favourite questions:**
  1. Derive the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor.
  2. Compute a small attention output by hand (worked example).
  3. Compare Bahdanau, Luong, and Scaled Dot-Product attention.
  4. Draw and explain the Multi-Head Attention block.
  5. Discuss the computational complexity of self-attention vs RNN vs CNN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues in RNN/LSTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Issues in RNN/LSTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)** are a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data, such as time series, speech, text, or video, by maintaining a hidden state that captures information from previous time steps. They process inputs sequentially through recurrent connections, theoretically allowing them to model arbitrary-length temporal dependencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks are a specialized variant of RNNs introduced to overcome the fundamental limitations of standard RNNs. They employ a sophisticated gating mechanism (input, forget, and output gates) along with a memory cell state to selectively retain or discard information over long time horizons.

### 1.2 Core Issues in RNN/LSTM — The Central Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The "Issues in RNN/LSTM" refer to the fundamental computational and learning pathologies that hinder the effective training and deployment of recurrent architectures. These primarily include the **vanishing gradient problem**, the **exploding gradient problem**, **long-term dependency learning failure**, **computational inefficiency due to sequential processing**, and **difficulty in parallelization**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student trying to remember the **first sentence of a long paragraph** while reading the **last sentence** of a 500-word essay. As you progress through the essay, the memory of that first sentence either:
- **Fades away** (you forget it entirely — *this is the vanishing gradient*), or
- **Becomes disproportionately magnified** (you over-focus on it while ignoring everything else — *this is the exploding gradient*).

In a standard RNN, when backpropagation flows backward through time, the **gradients (importance signals)** passed to early time steps either shrink to near-zero or balloon to massive values. The network thus struggles to learn connections between events that are far apart in time.

> [!NOTE]
> **LSTM Analogy:** Think of an LSTM cell as a **highly disciplined librarian with a notebook**. The librarian has three tools:
> - A **forget gate** (decides what old notes to erase)
> - An **input gate** (decides what new information to write)
> - An **output gate** (decides what to tell the next person)
> 
> This disciplined approach allows the librarian to remember crucial information across very long sequences, just as LSTM addresses the long-term dependency issue in RNNs.

### 1.4 Key Technical Terms (Bolded for KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Vanishing Gradient Problem**: Gradients becoming exponentially small during backpropagation through time, causing early layers/time steps to stop learning.
- **Exploding Gradient Problem**: Gradients becoming exponentially large, causing numerical instability and weight divergence (NaN values).
- **Long-Term Dependency Failure**: Inability to capture relationships between temporally distant events in a sequence.
- **Gating Mechanism**: Selective information flow controllers in LSTM (input, forget, output gates).
- **Cell State ($C_t$)**: The "memory highway" running through LSTM cells with minimal linear interactions.
- **Truncated Backpropagation Through Time (TBPTT)**: A practical solution that limits gradient computation to a fixed window of time steps.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic is a **high-weightage Module 3 area** in the KTU 2024 scheme. It directly tests the student's understanding of:
- **CO3 (Module 3)**: Analyze challenges in training deep and recurrent networks.
- **Bloom's Level**: Analyze (L4) and Evaluate (L5).
- Typical **14-mark questions** require students to mathematically derive the vanishing gradient scenario and contrast it with LSTM's architectural solutions.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Major Issues: Vanishing and Exploding Gradients

#### 2.1.1 Vanishing Gradient Problem

**Why it happens:**
- During backpropagation through time (BPTT), the gradient at time step $t$ is computed as a product of Jacobian matrices across all previous time steps.
- If the largest singular value of the recurrent weight matrix is **less than 1**, repeated multiplication causes the gradient to shrink exponentially.
- Early time steps receive negligible updates, meaning the network **cannot learn long-range dependencies**.

**How it manifests:**
- Loss function plateaus — the model appears stuck.
- Weights in early layers barely change.
- The model only learns short-term patterns (e.g., predicts the next word but loses track of paragraph context).

#### 2.1.2 Exploding Gradient Problem

**Why it happens:**
- If the largest singular value of the recurrent weight matrix is **greater than 1**, repeated multiplication causes the gradient to grow exponentially.
- Weight updates become enormous, leading to **oscillations or numerical overflow (NaN/Inf)**.

**How it manifests:**
- Loss function shows `NaN` values.
- Model weights become extremely large or undefined.
- Training becomes unstable and diverges.

#### 2.1.3 Why Standard RNNs Suffer: The Root Cause

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental issue is the **repeated multiplication of the same weight matrix** $W_{hh}$ during backpropagation. The gradient expression contains terms like $W_{hh}^T$ raised to the power of the sequence length $T$. If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$ (spectral radius less than 1), gradients vanish. If $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, gradients explode.

### 2.2 The LSTM Solution Architecture

LSTM addresses vanishing gradients through a **cell state** pathway with mostly linear operations:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The cell state $C_t$ in LSTM is updated through an **additive** operation ($+$), not multiplicative. This is why gradients flow through LSTM much more easily — they don't get repeatedly multiplied by the same matrix.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas for the KTU board exam on this topic. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (instead of $|$) inside tables to avoid markdown breaking.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| RNN Hidden State Update | $h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)$ | Forward pass at time $t$ |
| BPTT Gradient | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial W} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial W}$ | Total gradient across all time steps |
| Gradient w.r.t. $h_t$ | $\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \sum_{k=t}^{T} \dfrac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}$ | Sum of future losses affecting $h_t$ |
| Recursive Gradient Term | $\dfrac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} = V^T(\hat{y}_t - y_t)$ | Output layer contribution |
| Chain Rule Expansion | $\dfrac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ | Repeated Jacobian product |
| Jacobian Form | $\dfrac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = W_{hh}^T \cdot \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot))$ | Per-step gradient factor |
| Vanishing Condition | $\rho(W_{hh}^T) < 1$ | Spectral radius less than 1 |
| Exploding Condition | $\rho(W_{hh}^T) > 1$ | Spectral radius greater than 1 |
| LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Decides what to discard |
| LSTM Input Gate | $i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$ | Decides what to update |
| LSTM Candidate | $\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$ | New candidate values |
| Cell State Update | $C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$ | Additive memory update |
| LSTM Output Gate | $o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$ | Decides what to output |
| Hidden State Output | $h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$ | Filtered cell state |
| Gradient Clipping | $g \leftarrow \dfrac{g \cdot \theta}{\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2}$ if $\vert \vert g \vert \vert_2 > \theta$ | Mitigation for exploding gradients |
| Orthogonal Initialization | $W_{hh}^T W_{hh} = I$ (approx.) | Mitigates both issues |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition Systems** (e.g., Google Assistant, Siri): LSTMs handle long audio sequences where context from several seconds earlier affects current word recognition.
- **Machine Translation** (e.g., Google Translate): LSTMs translated entire sentences while preserving long-distance grammatical context (now largely replaced by Transformers, but foundational).
- **Financial Time-Series Forecasting**: Stock prices depend on patterns from days/months ago.
- **Medical Signal Analysis**: ECG/EEG signals require detection of long-term patterns.
- **Music Generation**: LSTMs learn temporal musical structures spanning entire compositions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** The vanishing gradient problem is **not just a theoretical concern**. In real applications, training a vanilla RNN on sequences longer than ~20-30 time steps becomes practically infeasible without architectural changes like LSTM/GRU.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Vanishing/Exploding Gradient in RNNs

**Setup:** Consider a standard RNN with the forward dynamics defined at time step $t$ as:

$$
h_t = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{t-1} + W_{xh} x_t + b_h)
$$

The output and loss at each time step:

$$
\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(V h_t + b_y), \quad L_t = L(\hat{y}_t, y_t)
$$

The total loss across the sequence is:

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t
$$

**Step 1: Compute gradient of total loss w.r.t. hidden state $h_t$**

Using the chain rule, the loss at any future time $k > t$ depends on $h_t$ through the chain of hidden states. So:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}
$$

**Step 2: Expand the future contribution recursively**

For $k = t$, the term is straightforward (it is the direct gradient from the current loss). For $k > t$, we must propagate through the recurrence:

$$
\frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_{k-1}} \cdot \frac{\partial h_{k-1}}{\partial h_{k-2}} \cdots \frac{\partial h_{t+1}}{\partial h_t}
$$

**Step 3: Express the product as a compact form**

$$
\frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the per-step Jacobian**

From the forward equation $h_i = \tanh(W_{hh} h_{i-1} + W_{xh} x_i + b_h)$, we have:

$$
\frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}} = W_{hh}^T \cdot \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(W_{hh} h_{i-1} + W_{xh} x_i + b_h)\right)
$$

Let us denote this Jacobian as $J_i$ for compactness:

$$
J_i = W_{hh}^T \cdot \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)\right)
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into the total gradient**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} J_i
$$

**Step 6: Analyze the product for vanishing/exploding behavior**

Consider the product $P_{t,k} = \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} J_i$. Since the diagonal matrix $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot))$ has values in $[0, 1]$, its spectral norm is bounded by 1. Therefore, the norm of the product is dominated by $W_{hh}$:

$$
\| P_{t,k} \| \leq \| W_{hh}^T \|^{(k-t)} \cdot 1^{(k-t)} = \rho(W_{hh})^{(k-t)}
$$

where $\rho(\cdot)$ denotes the spectral radius.

**Step 7: Identify the conditions**

- If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$ and $(k-t)$ is large, then $\rho(W_{hh})^{(k-t)} \to 0$ — **Vanishing Gradient**.
- If $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$ and $(k-t)$ is large, then $\rho(W_{hh})^{(k-t)} \to \infty$ — **Exploding Gradient**.
- If $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1$, the product stays bounded — **stable propagation** (theoretical ideal).

**Step 8: Conclude the impact on weight updates**

The gradient of the loss with respect to weight $W_{hh}$ involves the same product:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \left( \prod_{i=t+1}^{k} J_i \right) \cdot h_{t-1}^T
$$

Therefore, early time steps $t$ (where $k - t$ is large) receive updates that are exponentially small or large, making long-term dependency learning fundamentally difficult.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Why LSTM Solves This

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation shows why LSTM's architecture is robust to the vanishing gradient problem.

In LSTM, the cell state update is:

$$
C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t
$$

Computing the gradient of $C_t$ with respect to $C_{t-1}$:

$$
\frac{\partial C_t}{\partial C_{t-1}} = f_t + \text{(small correction terms)}
$$

This is essentially a **scalar product** (element-wise), not a matrix product. The gradient flowing backward through the cell state is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial C_{t-1}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C_t} \cdot f_t + \text{(other terms)}
$$

**Key observation:** If the forget gate $f_t \approx 1$ (preserving memory), the gradient passes through almost unchanged. There is **no repeated matrix multiplication** along the cell state highway. This is the architectural reason LSTM mitigates vanishing gradients.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Vanishing Gradient Magnitude

> [!NOTE]
> **Illustrative numerical example** to demonstrate vanishing gradient magnitude.

Consider $W_{hh}$ with $\rho(W_{hh}) = 0.8$. For a sequence of length $T = 50$:

$$
\rho(W_{hh})^{T-1} = 0.8^{49} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-5}
$$

This is essentially zero — the gradient signal from time step 1 to time step 50 has shrunk to nearly nothing. The model cannot learn from the earliest inputs.

For $\rho(W_{hh}) = 1.2$:

$$
\rho(W_{hh})^{T-1} = 1.2^{49} \approx 1{,}254
$$

This causes numerical overflow. A single weight update could be in the thousands, destabilizing the model.

### 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| **Issue** | **Root Cause** | **Mathematical Signature** | **LSTM's Solution** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vanishing Gradient | $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$, deep unrolling | $\rho(W_{hh})^{(k-t)} \to 0$ | Additive cell state update, gating |
| Exploding Gradient | $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, deep unrolling | $\rho(W_{hh})^{(k-t)} \to \infty$ | Gradient clipping (in practice) |
| Long-term dependency | Combined effect of above | $\partial L / \partial h_t$ decays or blows up | Memory cell with forget gate |
| Sequential bottleneck | Recurrent architecture | Cannot parallelize across $t$ | (Not solved by LSTM; solved by Transformers) |

### 4.2 LSTM Cell Block — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeXt["Xt Input at time t"] --> nodeConcat["Concatenate Ht-1 and Xt"]
    nodeHt1["Ht-1 from previous step"] --> nodeConcat
    nodeConcat --> nodeFg["Forget Gate f_t sigma"]
    nodeConcat --> nodeIg["Input Gate i_t sigma"]
    nodeConcat --> nodeOg["Output Gate o_t sigma"]
    nodeConcat --> nodeCand["Candidate C_t tanh"]
    nodeCt1["Cell State Ct-1"] --> nodeMultF["Multiply with forget gate"]
    nodeFg --> nodeMultF
    nodeMultF --> nodeAdd["Add operation"]
    nodeIg --> nodeMultI["Multiply with candidate"]
    nodeCand --> nodeMultI
    nodeMultI --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeCt["New Cell State Ct"]
    nodeCt --> nodeTanhC["tanh of Ct"]
    nodeOg --> nodeMultO["Multiply with tanh"]
    nodeTanhC --> nodeMultO
    nodeMultO --> nodeHt["New Hidden State Ht"]
    nodeCt --> nodeNextCt1["To next time step as Ct-1"]
    nodeHt --> nodeNextHt1["To next time step as Ht-1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Observe that the **Cell State $C_t$** flows through a near-linear highway (just a $\odot$ multiplication with the forget gate and a $+$ addition with the input gate). This is architecturally distinct from a standard RNN and is the core reason LSTM avoids vanishing gradients.

### 4.3 Gradient Flow Comparison: Vanilla RNN vs LSTM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph vanillaRNN["VANILLA RNN GRADIENT FLOW"]
        direction LR
        nodeVR1["Output L1"] --> nodeVR2["H1"]
        nodeVR2 --> nodeVR3["H2"]
        nodeVR3 --> nodeVR4["H3"]
        nodeVR4 --> nodeVR5["H4"]
        nodeVR2 -. "Multiply by W" .-> nodeVR3
        nodeVR3 -. "Multiply by W" .-> nodeVR4
        nodeVR4 -. "Multiply by W" .-> nodeVR5
    end

    subgraph lstmGrad["LSTM GRADIENT FLOW"]
        direction LR
        nodeLG1["Output L1"] --> nodeLG2["C1 Cell State"]
        nodeLG2 --> nodeLG3["C2 Cell State"]
        nodeLG3 --> nodeLG4["C3 Cell State"]
        nodeLG4 --> nodeLG5["C4 Cell State"]
        nodeLG2 -. "Multiply by forget gate f" .-> nodeLG3
        nodeLG3 -. "Multiply by forget gate f" .-> nodeLG4
        nodeLG4 -. "Multiply by forget gate f" .-> nodeLG5
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical insight for KTU board answers:** In the **vanilla RNN**, the gradient is multiplied by the same weight matrix $W$ at every step. In the **LSTM**, the gradient is multiplied by the **forget gate $f_t$** (a learned scalar value between 0 and 1) at every step. The forget gate can be learned to be close to 1, allowing the gradient to flow nearly unchanged — this is the key architectural innovation.
