## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level VAE Architecture (Functional Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT X"] --> B["ENCODER MLP or CNN"]
    B --> C["MU HEAD K dim"]
    B --> D["LOGVAR HEAD K dim"]
    C --> E["REPARAMETERIZE z mu plus sigma epsilon"]
    D --> E
    R["EPSILON N 0 I"] --> E
    E --> F["DECODER MLP or CNN"]
    F --> G["RECONSTRUCTION XHAT"]
    H["PRIOR pz N 0 I"] -.regularises.-> C
    H -.regularises.-> D
    G --> I["RECONSTRUCTION LOSS"]
    E --> J["KL DIVERGENCE LOSS"]
    I --> K["TOTAL VAE LOSS"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["BACKPROP THRU NETWORKS"]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Reparameterization Sub-Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["MU phi x"] --> C["ADD"]
    B["SIGMA phi x"] --> M["ELEMENTWISE MULTIPLY"]
    E["EPSILON N 0 I"] --> M
    M --> C
    C --> Z["SAMPLE z"]
    Z --> D["DECODER NET"]
    D --> XH["OUTPUT xhat"]
```

### 4.3 Training vs. Generation Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAIN["TRAINING PHASE"]
        T1["Input x"] --> T2["Encoder q phi z given x"]
        T2 --> T3["mu, sigma"]
        T3 --> T4["z via reparameterization"]
        T4 --> T5["Decoder p theta x given z"]
        T5 --> T6["Compare xhat with x"]
        T4 --> T7["KL with prior N 0 I"]
        T6 --> T8["Total VAE Loss"]
        T7 --> T8
        T8 --> T9["Backprop and update phi, theta"]
    end

    subgraph GEN["GENERATION PHASE"]
        G1["Sample z from N 0 I"] --> G2["Decoder p theta x given z"]
        G2 --> G3["Synthetic sample xhat"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Loss Surface Trade-off Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RECONSTRUCTION TERM"] -->|faithful xhat| B["LOW RECON LOSS"]
    C["KL REGULARISATION"] -->|smooth latent| D["LOW KL DIVERGENCE"]
    B --> E["TOTAL ELBO MAX"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["NEW SAMPLES VALID"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram (and not a photorealistic sketch)?** The mermaid-rendering sandbox cannot produce pixel-level image renderings, so we use a *functional block architecture flow* that precisely maps the mathematical data flow: $x \to q_\phi \to (\mu, \sigma) \to z \to p_\theta \to \hat{x}$, with the KL regularisation path acting as a side constraint. This is the standard form used in KTU board-examination answer sheets.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between a standard Autoencoder (AE) and a Variational Autoencoder (VAE). Why is a vanilla AE not considered a generative model?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Autoencoder (AE) | Variational Autoencoder (VAE) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latent code | Deterministic $z = f(x)$ | **Probabilistic** $z \sim q_\phi(z \mid x)$ |
| Latent space | Unstructured, has holes | **Regularised** to follow $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ |
| Loss | $\Vert x - \hat{x}\Vert^2$ only | **ELBO** = reconstruction + KL |
| Generation | Cannot reliably sample | **Can** sample new $x$ from prior |

A standard AE encodes each input as a *point* in the latent space; the decoder has only been trained on these discrete points. Decoding a random point from an arbitrary location in the latent space usually yields a meaningless output because the AE has never seen a code from that region. In contrast, the VAE's KL term forces *every* encoded point to lie near a Gaussian, so any sample from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ is in a region the decoder knows how to invert. Hence, VAE is generative; AE is not. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
State and explain the **reparameterization trick** used in training VAEs. Why is it necessary?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The reparameterization trick rewrites the stochastic sampling of a latent code as a *deterministic* function of the encoder's outputs and an auxiliary noise variable. For a diagonal Gaussian encoder, the sample is expressed as:

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

where $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$ are the encoder's outputs. By isolating the randomness in $\epsilon$ (which is independent of the learnable parameters), the gradient of the reconstruction loss with respect to the encoder's parameters $\phi$ can be back-propagated *through* the sampling node. This is necessary because direct sampling from a Gaussian is a non-differentiable operation, and naive score-function estimators (REINFORCE) suffer from prohibitively high variance for deep networks. The reparameterization provides a **low-variance, unbiased** gradient estimate. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign convention trap:** Many students mix up "maximise ELBO" and "minimise negative ELBO". **State the convention explicitly** at the start of the derivation to avoid losing 1 mark.
> - **Forgetting the prior:** Some derivations skip the term $p(z)$ entirely when using Jensen's inequality. The full expansion $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z)$ is mandatory.
> - **Mixing AE and VAE loss:** The VAE loss has **two** terms. Writing only the reconstruction term costs you 4–5 marks instantly.
> - **Reparameterization without $\epsilon$:** Writing $z = \mu + \sigma$ is a common error; the auxiliary noise $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ must appear.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) objective function for a Variational Autoencoder starting from the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_\theta(x)$. Clearly state each mathematical step and the role of the variational approximation $q_\phi(z \mid x)$. **7 marks**

**(b)** Explain the architecture of a VAE in detail. Show how the encoder and decoder are constructed, and describe the **reparameterization trick** mathematically with a clear block-diagrammatic justification. **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a) — Derivation of ELBO (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Express the marginal likelihood of $x$ by marginalising out the latent variable $z$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z)\, dz$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Multiply and divide the integrand by an arbitrary variational distribution $q_\phi(z \mid x)$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z \mid x)\, \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\, dz$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify the integral as an expectation under $q_\phi(z \mid x)$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log\, \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply Jensen's inequality ($\log$ is concave, so $\log \mathbb{E}[f] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log f]$):

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\right]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Use the chain rule of probability $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \mid z)\, p(z)$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x \mid z) + \log p(z) - \log q_\phi(z \mid x)\right]$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Split the expectation and recognise the second term as the **KL divergence**:

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)\right] - D_{\text{KL}}\!\left(q_\phi(z \mid x) \mid\mid p(z)\right) \equiv \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude: the right-hand side is a **lower bound** on $\log p_\theta(x)$, called the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**. The first term encourages faithful reconstruction, while the second term regularises $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ to match the prior $p(z)$. Training a VAE amounts to maximising this bound with respect to $(\theta, \phi)$.

**Model Solution (b) — Architecture & Reparameterization (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the encoder $q_\phi(z \mid x)$: a neural network (MLP for vectors, CNN for images) whose final layer is split into two heads producing $\mu_\phi(x) \in \mathbb{R}^K$ and $\log \sigma^2_\phi(x) \in \mathbb{R}^K$, parameterising a diagonal Gaussian.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the decoder $p_\theta(x \mid z)$: a neural network that maps the latent $z$ to a distribution over $x$ (e.g., Bernoulli for binary images, Gaussian for continuous). For binary MNIST, the output is a vector of independent sigmoid probabilities.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** State the reparameterization formula:

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Justify the trick: by isolating the noise in $\epsilon$, the gradient w.r.t. $\phi$ can flow through the deterministic mapping, making backpropagation feasible with low variance.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Provide the practical loss function to minimise:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\frac{1}{L}\sum_{l=1}^{L} \log p_\theta(x \mid z^{(l)}) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(\sigma_k^2 + \mu_k^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_k^2)$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for the KL divergence between two univariate Gaussians $q(z) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. State clearly where the moments of the Gaussian are used. **7 marks**

**(b)** Discuss the **posterior collapse** problem in VAEs. Explain its cause, how it manifests during training, and the standard mitigation strategies (KL annealing, free-bits, $\beta$-VAE). **7 marks**

**Model Solution (a) — Closed-Form KL Divergence (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the KL divergence:

$$D_{\text{KL}}(q \mid\mid p) = \int q(z) \log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)}\, dz = \mathbb{E}_{q(z)}\!\left[\log q(z) - \log p(z)\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the log-densities of $q$ and $p$:

$$\log q(z) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

$$\log p(z) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{z^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Form the log-ratio:

$$\log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log(\sigma^2) - \frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{z^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take the expectation under $q(z)$:

$$D_{\text{KL}} = \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}\log \sigma^2 - \frac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{z^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Use the moments $\mathbb{E}_q[z] = \mu$ and $\mathbb{E}_q[(z-\mu)^2] = \sigma^2$, and $\mathbb{E}_q[z^2] = \sigma^2 + \mu^2$:

$$D_{\text{KL}} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\log \sigma^2 - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}(\sigma^2 + \mu^2)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Rearrange to the canonical form:

$$D_{\text{KL}} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\sigma^2 + \mu^2 - 1 - \log \sigma^2\right)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Generalise to $K$ dimensions: $D_{\text{KL}} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(\sigma_k^2 + \mu_k^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_k^2)$, since for diagonal Gaussians the KL decomposes as a sum.

**Model Solution (b) — Posterior Collapse (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition and cause. **Posterior collapse** is a pathological regime in VAE training where the encoder becomes uninformative: $\mu_\phi(x) \to 0$ and $\sigma_\phi(x) \to 1$ for all $x$. Mathematically, $q_\phi(z \mid x) \to p(z)$, so the KL term is minimised trivially (to zero) at the cost of the encoder losing any dependence on $x$. It is caused by an over-strong KL regulariser that the model can "satisfy" by ignoring the latent code altogether, especially when paired with a powerful autoregressive decoder that can model $x$ without $z$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Empirical symptom. The decoder produces realistic-looking samples *unconditionally* on $z$, but reconstructions are nearly independent of the input — i.e., the latent code carries no information.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Mitigation — **KL Annealing.** Introduce a weight $\beta$ on the KL term that is gradually increased from 0 to 1 over training: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{recon}} + \beta\, D_{\text{KL}}$. This gives the encoder time to encode useful information before the regulariser is applied.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mitigation — **Free Bits.** Set a threshold $\lambda$ (e.g., 0.5 nats/dim) below which the KL term is *clipped to zero*, preventing any single latent dimension from being prematurely collapsed.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mitigation — **$\beta$-VAE.** Use $\beta \neq 1$ (typically $\beta < 1$ for image data) to weaken the KL pressure; alternatively, use a tighter bound such as the **IWAE bound** (Importance-Weighted Autoencoder) that is more robust to collapse.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A VAE is a *probabilistic* encoder–decoder model trained by maximising the ELBO; it is a *generative* model.
- **Two distributions:** Encoder $q_\phi(z \mid x)$ (recognition) and decoder $p_\theta(x \mid z)$ (generation).
- **Prior:** Conventionally $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I_K)$.
- **ELBO = Reconstruction + (negative KL):** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] - D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \mid\mid p(z))$.
- **Practical loss to minimise:** $\mathcal{L} = -\text{ELBO} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(x \mid z)] + D_{\text{KL}}(q_\phi(z \mid x) \mid\mid p(z))$.
- **Closed-form KL (diagonal Gaussians):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{K}(\sigma_k^2 + \mu_k^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_k^2)$.
- **Reparameterization:** $z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon,\ \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — enables backprop through stochastic nodes.
- **Jensen's inequality** is the workhorse of the ELBO derivation — be ready to write it on the exam.
- **AE vs. VAE:** AE → point, no generation; VAE → distribution, full generation capability.
- **Failure mode — Posterior collapse:** $\mu \to 0$, $\sigma \to 1$, decoder ignores $z$. Mitigated by KL annealing, free-bits, $\beta$-VAE.
- **VAE vs. GAN:** VAEs explicitly model $p(x)$ (likelihood-based); GANs learn an implicit distribution via adversarial training. VAEs are known to produce *blurry* outputs compared to GANs, but are more stable to train.
- **Assumed decoder likelihoods:** Bernoulli for binary images; Gaussian for continuous; Categorical for discrete tokens.
- **Generation process:** Sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ → pass through decoder → sample $x$ from $p_\theta(x \mid z)$.
- **Inference process:** Pass $x$ through encoder → get $(\mu, \sigma)$ → sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
- **Common exam mistakes to avoid:** forgetting $p(z)$ in the derivation; sign errors; treating $z$ as deterministic; ignoring the prior; using REINFORCE instead of reparameterization.
- **Variants to know:** $\beta$-VAE (controls disentanglement), IWAE (tighter bound), Conditional VAE (CVAE, label-conditioned), VQ-VAE (discrete latent codes).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic — "EAGER":** **E**lbo, **A**rchitecture (encoder/decoder), **G**aussian prior, **E**psilon reparameterization, **R**econstruction + KL loss. Recite before entering the exam hall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variational Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variational Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variational Learning** is a principled probabilistic framework in deep learning that combines **variational inference** with **neural network-based function approximation** to learn complex, high-dimensional data distributions. It provides a tractable optimization objective (the **Evidence Lower Bound — ELBO**) for models that contain unobserved latent variables, enabling end-to-end learning via stochastic gradient descent.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, **Variational Learning** is most commonly studied through the lens of the **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**, proposed by Kingma & Welling (2014). A VAE is a deep latent variable model of the form:

$$p_{\theta}(x) = \int p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\, p(z)\, dz$$

where $x$ is the observed data, $z$ is a continuous latent variable drawn from a prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, and $p_{\theta}(x \vert z)$ is a decoder network parameterized by $\theta$.

Because the marginal likelihood $p_{\theta}(x)$ is **intractable** (it requires integration over a continuous, high-dimensional $z$ space), variational learning introduces an **inference network** $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ (the encoder) that approximates the true posterior $p_{\theta}(z \vert x)$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Compressed Sketchbook Artist"**
>
> Imagine you are an artist trying to learn how to draw every face you have ever seen.
> - **Ordinary Autoencoder:** You compress each face into a tiny code (say, 8 numbers) and try to rebuild the face. You memorize the codes but never learn to *generate* new faces smoothly.
> - **Variational Autoencoder:** Instead of memorizing a single code per face, you learn a **cloud of possible codes** (a probability distribution). When asked to draw a new face, you first *sample* a point from this cloud, then decode it. This forces the codes to be smoothly organized — nearby codes decode to similar faces — and lets you generate an infinite variety of new, realistic faces.
>
> The "cloud" is controlled by a penalty term (**KL Divergence**) that gently pushes the cloud to look like a standard, well-behaved shape (a Gaussian). This is what makes the model **generative**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Latent variable $z$** | Unobserved, low-dimensional, structured representation of $x$ |
| **Encoder $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$** | Inference network that maps data $x$ to a distribution over latent codes |
| **Decoder $p_{\theta}(x \vert z)$** | Generative network that reconstructs $x$ given a latent code $z$ |
| **ELBO** | Evidence Lower Bound — the tractable surrogate objective maximized during training |
| **KL Divergence** | Measures the dissimilarity between the approximate posterior and the prior |
| **Reparameterization Trick** | A low-variance gradient estimator that makes ELBO differentiable through sampling |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects you to know the ELBO objective, the reparameterization trick, and the final two-term loss function (reconstruction + KL) — these are the most frequently tested items.

---

## 1.4 Why Variational Learning Matters in Deep Learning

- It unifies **unsupervised representation learning** and **generative modeling** in one differentiable architecture.
- It provides a mathematically grounded alternative to GANs for **density estimation** and **data generation**.
- It forms the foundation of advanced models: **Conditional VAEs, Beta-VAE, VQ-VAE, Diffusion Models (DDPM via score matching & variational bounds), and Hierarchical VAEs**.
- In engineering applications, it is used for **anomaly detection, image denoising, drug discovery, and Bayesian uncertainty estimation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Problem — Why Marginal Likelihood is Intractable

For a latent variable model, the marginal log-likelihood of a single data point is:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\, p(z)\, dz$$

Direct evaluation is **intractable** because:
1. The integral is over a continuous, high-dimensional space of $z$.
2. The posterior $p_{\theta}(z \vert x) = \frac{p_{\theta}(x \vert z)p(z)}{p_{\theta}(x)}$ requires the same intractable integral in the denominator.

**Variational learning's** central idea: replace the true posterior with a **tractable approximation** $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ and optimize a lower bound on $\log p_{\theta}(x)$.

---

## 2.2 The Variational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The following six logical steps constitute the foundation of variational learning:

1. **Introduce a variational distribution** $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ from a tractable family (e.g., diagonal Gaussian) parameterized by a neural network with weights $\phi$.

2. **Decompose the log-likelihood** using the identity $\log p_{\theta}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$ and Bayes' rule:
   $$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x, z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \vert x)] + D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p_{\theta}(z \vert x)\right)$$

3. **Recognize the bound**: Since $D_{KL} \geq 0$, the first term is a **lower bound** on $\log p_{\theta}(x)$ — this is the **ELBO**.

4. **Rewrite the ELBO** into a more practical two-term form:
   $$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization Term}}$$

5. **Maximize the ELBO** jointly over $\theta$ (decoder weights) and $\phi$ (encoder weights) using stochastic gradient ascent.

6. **Differentiate through the sampling step** using the **Reparameterization Trick**, which expresses $z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, so gradients can flow back through $\mu_{\phi}$ and $\sigma_{\phi}$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Variational Learning |
|---|---|
| **Computer Vision** | Image generation, super-resolution, denoising, inpainting |
| **Medical Imaging** | Anomaly detection in X-rays/MRIs, missing-modality synthesis |
| **Time-Series** | Forecasting, imputation, sequence-to-sequence latent models |
| **NLP** | Latent dialogue models, controllable text generation |
| **Robotics** | World models, planning under uncertainty |
| **Bayesian Deep Learning** | Approximate posterior over neural network weights (Bayesian Neural Nets) |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the boxed expressions below — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log p_{\theta}(x) = \mathcal{L} + D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi} \,\|\, p_{\theta}\right)$ | Log-likelihood decomposition | $\mathcal{L} \leq \log p_{\theta}(x)$ |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}(\theta,\phi;x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$ | ELBO (negative loss form) | Maximize $\mathcal{L}$ |
| 3 | $D_{KL}\!\left(q \,\|\, p\right) = \int q(z) \log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)}\,dz$ | KL Divergence | $D_{KL} \geq 0$, $D_{KL}(q \vert p) \neq D_{KL}(p \vert q)$ |
| 4 | $q_{\phi}(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(z; \mu_{\phi}(x), \sigma_{\phi}^{2}(x) I\right)$ | Diagonal Gaussian encoder | $D_z$-dimensional |
| 5 | $z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon,\;\; \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Reparameterization trick | Element-wise product |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} = -\vert\vert x - \hat{x}\vert\vert^{2} - D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^{2}) \,\|\, \mathcal{N}(0, I)\right)$ | VAE loss (Gaussian decoder) | Minimize this loss |
| 7 | $D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^{2}) \,\|\, \mathcal{N}(0, I)\right) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{D_z}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)$ | Closed-form KL (Gaussian-Gaussian) | Per-dimension sum |
| 8 | $\nabla_{\phi} \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\,f(z)\,] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi} + \sigma_{\phi}\varepsilon)\right]$ | Reparameterized gradient | Unbiased, low-variance |

> **Note on notation:** $\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert$ denotes the $\ell_2$ norm; $\odot$ denotes the Hadamard (element-wise) product; $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I)$ denotes a diagonal multivariate Gaussian with mean $\mu$ and diagonal covariance $\sigma^{2} I$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Marginal Likelihood to the ELBO

**Goal:** Show that the marginal log-likelihood equals ELBO plus a non-negative KL divergence term.

**Step 1 — Write the marginal likelihood of $x$.**
The data $x$ is generated by first sampling $z \sim p(z)$, then sampling $x \sim p_{\theta}(x \vert z)$. Marginalizing over $z$:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x, z)\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Insert the variational distribution as a "probe".**
Multiply and divide inside the log by $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ — this is the standard **importance sampling trick**:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int q_{\phi}(z \vert x)\, \frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integral as an expectation.**
The integral is exactly the expectation of the ratio $\frac{p_{\theta}(x,z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}$ under $q_{\phi}$:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Jensen's inequality.**
Since $\log$ is concave, $\log \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log Y]$. Applying this:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Define the right-hand side as the ELBO.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x, z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \vert x)\right]\;}$$

**Step 6 — Refine the ELBO using Bayes' rule $p_{\theta}(x, z) = p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\, p(z)$ and split the expectation.**

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}\!\left[\log p(z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \vert x)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the second term as the negative KL divergence.**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)] \;-\; D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)\;}$$

This is the canonical **two-term ELBO**: a reconstruction term (faithfulness) and a regularization term (smoothness of latent space).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Kullback–Leibler Divergence

For two continuous distributions $q(z)$ and $p(z)$:

**Step 1 — Definition.**
$$D_{KL}\!\left(q(z) \,\|\, p(z)\right) = \int q(z)\, \log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)}\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Expand the logarithm.**
$$= \int q(z)\, \log q(z)\, dz \;-\; \int q(z)\, \log p(z)\, dz$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integrals as entropies.**
$$= -\mathbb{H}[q(z)] + \mathbb{H}[q(z), p(z)]$$

where $\mathbb{H}[q]$ is the **Shannon entropy** of $q$ and $\mathbb{H}[q, p]$ is the **cross-entropy**.

**Step 4 — Establish key properties.**
- $D_{KL}(q \vert\vert p) \geq 0$ with equality iff $q = p$ almost everywhere (Gibbs' inequality).
- $D_{KL}$ is **asymmetric**: $D_{KL}(q \vert\vert p) \neq D_{KL}(p \vert\vert q)$ in general.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Form KL for Diagonal Gaussian Encoder & Standard Normal Prior

**Setup:** $q_{\phi}(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu, \sigma^{2} I)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

**Step 1 — Write the per-dimension KL for scalar Gaussians.**
Let $q \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_j, \sigma_j^{2})$ and $p \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Then:

$$D_{KL}(q \,\|\, p) = \int q(z)\, \log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)}\, dz$$

**Step 2 — Substitute log-densities.**

$$\log q(z) = -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi \sigma_j^{2}) - \frac{(z - \mu_j)^{2}}{2\sigma_j^{2}}$$

$$\log p(z) = -\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{z^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio inside the log.**

$$\log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)} = \log \frac{\sigma_{j}^{-1}}{\,1\,} - \frac{(z - \mu_j)^{2}}{2\sigma_j^{2}} + \frac{z^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Take the expectation under $q$.**
Using $\mathbb{E}_{q}[(z - \mu_j)^{2}] = \sigma_j^{2}$, $\mathbb{E}_{q}[z] = \mu_j$, $\mathbb{E}_{q}[z^{2}] = \sigma_j^{2} + \mu_j^{2}$:

$$D_{KL}(q \,\|\, p) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)$$

**Step 5 — Sum over all $D_z$ latent dimensions.**

$$\boxed{\;D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I) \,\|\, \mathcal{N}(0, I)\right) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{D_z}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common mistake to avoid:** The sign convention in VAE loss is $D_{KL} \geq 0$ being **subtracted** from reconstruction, so the minimization objective is $\mathcal{J} = -\mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)] + D_{KL}$. The $D_{KL}$ term is therefore a *penalty* that pulls the encoder's posterior toward the prior.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Reparameterization Trick

**The problem:** We must compute $\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[f(z)]$ to optimize the encoder parameters $\phi$.

**Step 1 — Naive (and broken) approach.**
$$\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \nabla_{\phi} \int q_{\phi}(z)\, f(z)\, dz$$
The gradient cannot be moved inside the integral because $q_{\phi}$ depends on $\phi$. One can use the **score-function estimator** (REINFORCE), but it has **very high variance**.

**Step 2 — Reparameterize the random variable.**
Express $z$ as a deterministic, differentiable function of $\phi$, $x$, and an auxiliary noise variable $\varepsilon$ that does **not** depend on $\phi$:

$$z = g_{\phi}(\varepsilon, x) = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the expectation over $\varepsilon$.**

$$\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)}\!\left[f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Move the gradient inside.**

$$\boxed{\;\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon)\right]\;}$$

**Step 5 — Monte Carlo estimate.**
Given a single sample $\varepsilon^{(l)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ per minibatch:

$$\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] \approx \nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon^{(l)})$$

This is **unbiased and has dramatically lower variance** than the score-function estimator — it is the key to making VAEs trainable.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Full VAE Loss (Gaussian Decoder)

Assume $p_{\theta}(x \vert z) = \mathcal{N}(x; \hat{x}, I)$ with $\hat{x}$ = decoder output. Then $\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z) = -\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert x - \hat{x}\vert\vert^{2} + \text{const}$.

For a minibatch of $M$ samples, using $L$ Monte Carlo samples of $z$ per data point:

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M}\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)}\vert\vert^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{D_z}\!\left(\mu_{ij}^{2} + \sigma_{ij}^{2} - \log \sigma_{ij}^{2} - 1\right)\right]\;}$$

The first term is the **reconstruction error**; the second is the **KL regularization**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Variational Learning with Related Concepts

| Aspect | Variational Learning (VAE) | Adversarial Learning (GAN) | Diffusion Models |
|---|---|---|---|
| Objective | Maximize ELBO | Min–Max game | Match score function |
| Latent Space | Explicit, regularized | Implicit, unstructured | Explicit, noisy schedule |
| Likelihood | Tractable lower bound | No explicit likelihood | Tractable via ELBO |
| Sample Quality | Slightly blurry historically | Sharp | State-of-the-art |
| Training Stability | Stable | Mode collapse risk | Very stable |
| Inference | Encoder $q_{\phi}$ | None (no inversion) | Gradual denoising |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VAE Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete forward pass of a Variational Autoencoder, showing the data flow from input through the encoder, sampling step, and decoder, with the loss computation feeding back into both networks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data x] --> B[Encoder Network q_phi]
    B --> C[Mean mu_phi of x]
    B --> D[Log-variance log sigma_sq_phi of x]
    C --> E[Sampling Layer Reparameterization Trick]
    D --> E
    F[Noise epsilon from N0,1] --> E
    E --> G[Latent Code z = mu plus sigma times epsilon]
    G --> H[Decoder Network p_theta]
    H --> I[Reconstruction x_hat]
    I --> J[Reconstruction Loss x minus x_hat squared]
    C --> K[KL Divergence Penalty]
    D --> K
    K --> L[Total VAE Loss equals Recon Loss plus KL]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Backpropagation to update phi and theta]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The encoder produces $(\mu_{\phi}, \log\sigma_{\phi}^{2})$; the reparameterization block combines them with noise $\varepsilon$ to produce $z$; the decoder reconstructs $\hat{x}$; and the loss combines reconstruction + KL penalty to drive gradient updates on both $\phi$ and $\theta$.

---

## 4.2 ELBO Derivation — Conceptual Map

This second diagram visualizes the **decomposition of the marginal log-likelihood** into the ELBO and the KL gap. It is one of the most important conceptual pictures a KTU student can draw in an exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[log p_theta of x] --> B[ELBO L]
    A --> C[KL Divergence between q_phi and p_theta]
    B --> D[Reconstruction Term]
    B --> E[Regularization Term]
    D --> F[Expected log p_theta of x given z]
    E --> G[Negative KL between q_phi and prior p of z]
    C --> H[Always non-negative]
    H --> I[Confirms ELBO is a LOWER bound]
```

---

## 4.3 Training vs. Generation Phase

This diagram distinguishes between the **two operational phases** of a trained VAE.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAINING["Training Phase"]
        T1[Real data x] --> T2[Encode to mu and sigma]
        T2 --> T3[Reparameterized sample z]
        T3 --> T4[Decode to x_hat]
        T4 --> T5[Compute ELBO Loss]
        T5 --> T6[Update encoder and decoder weights]
    end

    subgraph GENERATION["Generation Phase"]
        G1[Sample z from prior N0,1] --> G2[Decode z via trained decoder]
        G2 --> G3[Generated data x_new]
    end
```

> **Key insight:** During training, $z$ is sampled from $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ (data-conditional); during generation, $z$ is sampled from $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ (unconditional). The KL term ensures these two distributions match closely.

---

## 4.4 The Reparameterization Trick — Visual Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[mu_phi of x] --> C[Addition Node]
    B[sigma_phi of x] --> D[Element-wise Multiply]
    F[epsilon from N0,1] --> D
    D --> C
    C --> E[Latent z]
    E --> G[Decoder f_theta of z]
    G --> H[Output x_hat]
    E -.Backprop Path.-> A
    E -.Backprop Path.-> B
```

> **Critical reading:** The dashed arrows show that gradients flow through the sampling node back to $\mu_{\phi}$ and $\sigma_{\phi}$ — this is what makes the encoder trainable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test direct recall of definitions, equations, or properties. Answers should be 3–5 lines.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define *Variational Learning*. State the ELBO objective.

**Model Answer:**
Variational Learning is a probabilistic framework in which a tractable distribution $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ is used to approximate the true posterior $p_{\theta}(z \vert x)$ of a latent variable model. The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) is the optimization objective:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$$

Maximizing the ELBO is equivalent to minimizing the KL divergence between $q_{\phi}$ and the true posterior, which provides a tractable surrogate for the intractable log-likelihood.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the **Reparameterization Trick**? Why is it required in VAEs?

**Model Answer:**
The reparameterization trick expresses the latent sample as a deterministic, differentiable function of the encoder parameters and an auxiliary noise variable:

$$z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

It is required because the expectation $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)]$ cannot be differentiated directly with respect to $\phi$ (the sampling operation is non-differentiable). The reparameterization moves the stochasticity into $\varepsilon$, allowing gradients to flow back through $\mu_{\phi}$ and $\sigma_{\phi}$ with low variance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice is given between **OR** alternatives.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Derive the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** for a latent variable model with latent variable $z$ and observed variable $x$. Clearly identify each term in the final expression.

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the marginal log-likelihood:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x, z)\, dz$$

Multiply and divide by $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$:

$$= \log \int q_{\phi}(z \vert x)\, \frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\, dz$$

Recognize the integral as an expectation:

$$= \log\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\right]$$

Apply **Jensen's inequality** (log is concave):

$$\geq \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\log \frac{p_{\theta}(x, z)}{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\right]$$

Define the right-hand side as the ELBO:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x, z) - \log q_{\phi}(z \vert x)]$$

Applying Bayes' rule $p_{\theta}(x, z) = p_{\theta}(x \vert z)p(z)$ and separating terms:

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{L}(\theta, \phi; x) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularization}}\;}$$

The first term encourages accurate reconstruction of $x$ from $z$; the second regularizes the encoder's posterior to match the prior.

---

#### **Q3(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
For a diagonal Gaussian encoder $q_{\phi}(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2} I)$ and standard normal prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, derive the **closed-form expression for the KL divergence** term.

**Model Answer:**

Per-dimension KL between $\mathcal{N}(\mu_j, \sigma_j^{2})$ and $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$:

$$D_{KL}(q \,\|\, p) = \int q(z)\, \log \frac{q(z)}{p(z)}\, dz$$

Substituting log-densities and taking expectations using $\mathbb{E}_{q}[(z - \mu_j)^{2}] = \sigma_j^{2}$, $\mathbb{E}_{q}[z^{2}] = \sigma_j^{2} + \mu_j^{2}$:

$$= \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\log \sigma_j^{2} - \frac{(z - \mu_j)^{2}}{2\sigma_j^{2}} + \frac{z^{2}}{2}\right]$$

$$= -\frac{1}{2}\log \sigma_j^{2} - \frac{\sigma_j^{2}}{2\sigma_j^{2}} + \frac{\sigma_j^{2} + \mu_j^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^{2} I) \,\|\, \mathcal{N}(0, I)\right) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{D_z}\!\left(\mu_{j}^{2} + \sigma_{j}^{2} - \log \sigma_{j}^{2} - 1\right)\;}$$

This is the analytical KL term that appears in the VAE loss function.

---

### **OR — QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q4(a) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the **Reparameterization Trick** in detail. Show mathematically why it provides a low-variance gradient estimator for training VAEs.

**Model Answer:**

**The Problem:**
The ELBO contains a term $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)]$. Differentiating w.r.t. $\phi$:

$$\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}[f(z)] = \nabla_{\phi} \int q_{\phi}(z \vert x)\, f(z)\, dz$$

The gradient cannot move inside the integral because $q_{\phi}$ depends on $\phi$. The alternative **score-function estimator** gives unbiased but **very high variance** gradients.

**The Trick:**
Reparameterize $z$ as a deterministic function of $\phi$ and an auxiliary noise variable $\varepsilon$:

$$z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

Now:

$$\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)}[f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon)]$$

Gradient passes through the deterministic path:

$$\nabla_{\phi}\, \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[f(z)] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}\!\left[\nabla_{\phi} f(\mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon)\right]$$

**Why low variance?**
- The randomness is decoupled from $\phi$ — only $\mu_{\phi}$ and $\sigma_{\phi}$ carry parameter dependence.
- Gradients are computed on a smooth, differentiable function — Monte Carlo estimates are nearly deterministic for smooth $f$.
- Empirically, variance is reduced by **orders of magnitude** compared to REINFORCE.

---

#### **Q4(b) [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Write the **complete VAE loss function** for a Gaussian decoder $p_{\theta}(x \vert z) = \mathcal{N}(x; \hat{x}, I)$. Explain the role of each term and the trade-off it controls.

**Model Answer:**

The negative ELBO (the loss to minimize) for minibatch of $M$ samples with $L$ Monte Carlo samples per data point is:

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{J}_{\text{VAE}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{l=1}^{L}\log p_{\theta}(x^{(i)} \vert z^{(i,l)}) + \sum_{i=1}^{M} D_{KL}\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x^{(i)}) \,\|\, p(z)\right)\;}$$

For a Gaussian decoder with unit variance:

$$\mathcal{J}_{\text{VAE}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M}\frac{1}{2}\vert\vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)}\vert\vert^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{D_z}\!\left(\mu_{ij}^{2} + \sigma_{ij}^{2} - \log \sigma_{ij}^{2} - 1\right)$$

**Role of each term:**

| Term | Role | Trade-off Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| Reconstruction loss | Forces decoder to reproduce $x$ from $z$ | High weight → sharp reconstructions, but latent space becomes irregular |
| KL divergence | Forces encoder posterior toward $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | High weight → smooth latent space, but reconstructions become blurry |

This is the **fidelity-vs-regularization trade-off** central to VAE design. The parameter $\beta$ in a $\beta$-VAE scales the KL term to control disentanglement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in ELBO** — writing $D_{KL}$ with the wrong sign in the objective. Remember: the **ELBO is MAXIMIZED**; the **VAE loss is MINIMIZED** and equals $-\text{ELBO}$. Many students write $\mathcal{L} = \text{Recon} + D_{KL}$ instead of $\text{Recon} - D_{KL}$.
>
> 2. **Confusing the two KL directions** — $D_{KL}(q \vert\vert p) \neq D_{KL}(p \vert\vert q)$. In VAEs, we always use $D_{KL}(q_{\phi} \vert\vert p)$ (forward KL), which is mode-covering.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the closed-form formula** — when the encoder is Gaussian and the prior is standard normal, the KL is **analytical**. Writing a Monte Carlo estimate is acceptable but slower and noisier; examiners prefer the closed form.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Jensen's inequality step** — this is the heart of the ELBO derivation. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks immediately.
>
> 5. **Not stating non-negativity of KL** — examiners expect you to state $D_{KL} \geq 0$ explicitly to justify why ELBO is a **lower bound**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up VAE with standard autoencoder** — a standard AE has no probabilistic interpretation, no latent distribution, and cannot generate new samples. Always clarify this distinction in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Variational Learning**

- **Core idea:** Approximate the intractable true posterior $p_{\theta}(z \vert x)$ with a tractable variational distribution $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ parameterized by a neural network.
- **ELBO:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}}[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \vert\vert p(z))$. Always **maximize** this.
- **Log-likelihood identity:** $\log p_{\theta}(x) = \mathcal{L} + D_{KL}(q_{\phi} \vert\vert p_{\theta})$, with the KL term $\geq 0$.
- **Reparameterization trick:** $z = \mu_{\phi}(x) + \sigma_{\phi}(x) \odot \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Allows low-variance gradient estimation.
- **Closed-form KL (Gaussian-Gaussian):** $\frac{1}{2}\sum_{j}(\mu_j^{2} + \sigma_j^{2} - \log \sigma_j^{2} - 1)$ — memorized verbatim.
- **VAE architecture:** Encoder $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$, Reparameterization, Decoder $p_{\theta}(x \vert z)$.
- **Two-term loss:** Reconstruction (decoder fidelity) + KL (latent smoothness).
- **Generation:** Sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, decode via trained decoder to produce new $x$.
- **Why VAE blurs images:** The Gaussian prior and Gaussian decoder assumption enforce average-like reconstructions.
- **Variants to know:** $\beta$-VAE, Conditional VAE (CVAE), VQ-VAE, Hierarchical VAE, IWAE.
- **Real-world applications:** Anomaly detection, image generation, semi-supervised learning, latent-space interpolation, Bayesian uncertainty estimation.
- **Key math identities to recall:** Bayes' rule, Jensen's inequality, KL divergence non-negativity, log-derivative trick.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: VAE: Network Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** VAE: Network Architecture # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a generative deep learning model that combines the principles of **autoencoders** with **variational Bayesian inference** to learn a continuous, structured latent representation of input data. Unlike a standard autoencoder that maps an input to a single deterministic point in latent space, a VAE maps each input $x$ to a **probability distribution** (typically a Gaussian) over the latent space, characterized by a mean vector $\mu$ and a variance vector $\sigma^2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT746 - Module 4):** > A Variational Autoencoder is a directed graphical model with a specific approximate inference and learning algorithm, trained with the **Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes (SGVB)** estimator. The architecture consists of an **Encoder (Inference/Recognition Network)** $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ and a **Decoder (Generative Network)** $p_\theta(x \vert z)$, jointly optimized by maximizing the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** on the marginal log-likelihood of the observed data. ### Network Architecture at a Glance | Component | Mathematical Role | Standard Output | |-----------|-------------------|-----------------| | **Input Layer** | Accepts data $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Raw data vector | | **Encoder Network** | Approximates posterior $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ | $\mu$ and $\log \sigma^2$ | | **Sampling Layer** | Reparameterization trick | Latent code $z$ | | **Decoder Network** | Generative model $p_\theta(x \vert z)$ | Reconstructed $\hat{x}$ | | **Output Layer** | Reconstruction | $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Forensic Sketch Artist** > > Imagine a **forensic sketch artist** who interviews a witness (the **encoder**). The witness does not give a single exact description of the suspect. Instead, they describe a *range* of possibilities: "The suspect is *likely* between 25–35 years old, *probably* around 5'10" tall, *possibly* has dark hair." Each of these is a **probability distribution** (a Gaussian) over an attribute. > > The sketch artist (the **decoder**) then takes this *probabilistic description* and draws a face. The artist never sees the real suspect during training — they only learn what the witness's descriptions typically lead to. > > - The **witness = Encoder** (turns a real face into a "cloud" of plausible descriptions). > - The **suspect's description distribution = Latent Space** (a structured probabilistic region). > - The **sketch = Decoded Output** $\hat{x}$. > - **Loss**: How different the sketch is from the *original* real face (reconstruction), plus how well the description's distribution matches a *standard reference distribution* (KL divergence). ## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics - **Latent Dimensionality** $k$: Typically 2, 8, 16, 32, 64, or 128. - **Standard Deviation Prior**: $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — a **standard multivariate Gaussian** with mean $\mathbf{0}$ and identity covariance. - **KL Divergence Weight** $\beta$: Often set to **1** in classical VAE; tuned in $\beta$-VAE variants. - **Reparameterization Constant**: $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — a unit Gaussian noise sample. > [!TIP] > **Why the network outputs $\log \sigma^2$ instead of $\sigma^2$ directly?** > Because $\sigma^2$ must be **strictly positive** ($> 0$). Predicting $\log \sigma^2$ lets the network output *any real number*, which is then exponentiated to guarantee positivity and provides better numerical stability during training. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of VAE Architecture ### Pillar 1: The Encoder (Inference Network) $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ The encoder is a neural network (typically fully-connected MLP or CNN) parameterized by weights $\phi$. For each input $x$, it outputs two vectors: $$q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}\big(z; \mu_\phi(x), \text{diag}(\sigma_\phi^2(x))\big)$$ where $\mu_\phi(x) \in \mathbb{R}^k$ and $\sigma_\phi^2(x) \in \mathbb{R}^k_{>0}$ are produced by the network. **Why a distribution and not a point?** - A point estimate would prevent smooth interpolation between data points. - It enables probabilistic generation: sample a new $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decode a novel $x$. ### Pillar 2: The Reparameterization Trick A naive sampling step $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is **non-differentiable**, breaking backpropagation. The trick: rewrite the sample as a deterministic function of $\mu$, $\sigma$, and an external noise variable $\varepsilon$: $$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ Here, $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**. The randomness is now isolated in $\varepsilon$, so gradients can flow back through $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ### Pillar 3: The Decoder (Generative Network) $p_\theta(x \vert z)$ The decoder takes a latent code $z$ and reconstructs the input. The output distribution depends on the data type: - **Bernoulli** (binary images, e.g., MNIST): $\hat{x} = \sigma(\text{decoder output})$, trained with binary cross-entropy. - **Gaussian** (continuous images, e.g., natural photos): $\hat{x} = \text{decoder output}$, trained with MSE. ## 2.2 The Loss Function: ELBO Derivation (Conceptual Steps) We want to maximize $\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z) \, dz$, but the integral is intractable. 1. **Introduce a variational distribution** $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ to approximate the true posterior. 2. **Apply Jensen's inequality** to obtain a lower bound. 3. **Decompose the bound** into two interpretable terms. 4. **Optimize jointly** over encoder parameters $\phi$ and decoder parameters $\theta$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Type | |---|---------|-------------|--------------| | 1 | $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Standard Gaussian prior on latent code | Probability density | | 2 | $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu, \sigma^2 I)$ | Encoder's approximate posterior | Probability density | | 3 | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ | Reparameterization trick | Vector (dim $k$) | | 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_q[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))$ | Evidence Lower Bound (loss) | Nats (log-likelihood unit) | | 5 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{recon}} = -\sum_i \big[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1 - x_i) \log (1 - \hat{x}_i)\big]$ | Binary cross-entropy reconstruction | Nats | | 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{recon}} = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$ | MSE reconstruction (Gaussian decoder) | Squared units of $x$ | | 7 | $D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left(1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2\right)$ | Analytical KL between two Gaussians | Nats | | 8 | $p_\theta(x \vert z) = \text{Decoder}(z)$ | Generative model output | Probability / vector | | 9 | $\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}$ | Bound relationship (Jensen's inequality) | Inequality | | 10 | $x_{\text{new}} \sim p_\theta(x \vert z), \ z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Generative sampling procedure | Vector | > [!CAUTION] > **Critical LaTeX Note for Exam:** Never write the absolute value bars $ \vert x \vert $ using the pipe symbol `|` inside a markdown table — always use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table formatting. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility VAE network architecture is foundational in: - **Medical Imaging**: Denoising low-dose CT scans, anomaly detection in X-rays. - **Anomaly Detection**: Reconstruction error flags out-of-distribution samples (manufacturing defects, fraud). - **Drug Discovery**: Generating novel molecular structures in latent chemical space. - **Recommendation Systems**: Collaborative filtering with variational inference. - **Speech Synthesis & Audio Generation**: Latent representation of audio spectrograms. - **Data Augmentation**: Generating synthetic training samples for rare classes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the ELBO We want to maximize the log-likelihood of the data under the generative model: $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z) \, dz$$ This integral is **intractable** because $z$ is continuous and high-dimensional, and we have no analytical solution for the posterior $p_\theta(z \vert x)$. **Step 1: Introduce the variational distribution.** Multiply and divide by $q_\phi(z \vert x)$, a tractable approximation of the true posterior: $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z \vert x) \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \, dz$$ This rewriting is a **trick** — it does not change the value, since we have effectively multiplied by $1$. **Step 2: Recognize the form as an expectation.** Since $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ is a probability distribution, the integral is the expectation of the ratio $p_\theta(x, z) / q_\phi(z \vert x)$ under $q_\phi(z \vert x)$: $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \, \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \left[ \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \right]$$ **Step 3: Apply Jensen's Inequality.** The function $\log$ is **concave**, so for any random variable $Y$ and probability distribution $Q$: $$\log \mathbb{E}_Q[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}_Q[\log Y]$$ Applying this with $Q = q_\phi(z \vert x)$ and $Y = p_\theta(x, z) / q_\phi(z \vert x)$: $$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \right]$$ **Step 4: Expand the joint and split the expectation.** Using the chain rule $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \vert z) p(z)$: $$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \big[ \log p_\theta(x \vert z) + \log p(z) - \log q_\phi(z \vert x) \big]$$ **Step 5: Group terms into reconstruction and KL divergence.** Define the **KL Divergence** between two distributions $Q$ and $P$: $$D_{KL}(Q \Vert P) = \mathbb{E}_Q[\log Q - \log P]$$ Rearranging the expectation, we obtain the famous **ELBO**: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}(\theta, \phi; x) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{D_{KL}\big(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\big)}_{\text{Regularization Term}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Each Term:** > - **Reconstruction Term** $\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)]$: How well the decoder reconstructs $x$ from the sampled latent code. Larger = better fidelity. > - **KL Regularization** $D_{KL}$: How far the encoder's posterior deviates from the prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$. Smaller = latent space stays regularized and continuous. ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of the KL Divergence Term For two multivariate Gaussians $q(z) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ with **diagonal covariance**, the KL divergence has a closed form. **Step 1: General formula for two multivariate Gaussians.** $$D_{KL}\big(\mathcal{N}(\mu_q, \Sigma_q) \Vert \mathcal{N}(\mu_p, \Sigma_p)\big) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \text{tr}(\Sigma_p^{-1} \Sigma_q) + (\mu_p - \mu_q)^T \Sigma_p^{-1} (\mu_p - \mu_q) - k + \log \frac{\det \Sigma_p}{\det \Sigma_q} \right]$$ **Step 2: Substitute the prior** $\mu_p = 0$, $\Sigma_p = I$, $\det \Sigma_p = 1$, $\Sigma_p^{-1} = I$: $$D_{KL} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \text{tr}(\Sigma_q) + \mu_q^T \mu_q - k + \log \frac{1}{\det \Sigma_q} \right]$$ **Step 3: Diagonal covariance assumption** $\Sigma_q = \text{diag}(\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_k^2)$: - $\text{tr}(\Sigma_q) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sigma_j^2$ - $\mu_q^T \mu_q = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \mu_j^2$ - $\log \det \Sigma_q = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \log \sigma_j^2$ **Step 4: Combine into the final per-dimension sum:** $$D_{KL}\big(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\big) = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \big( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \big)$$ This is the term that appears in nearly every VAE loss function — **memorize this formula**. ## 3.3 The Full Practical VAE Loss Function Putting it all together for a single sample $x$: $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}(\theta, \phi; x) = \underbrace{-\frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^{L} \log p_\theta(x \vert z^{(l)})}_{\text{Reconstruction Loss}} \ + \ \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \big( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \big)}_{\text{KL Regularization}}$$ where $L$ is the number of Monte Carlo samples (typically $L = 1$ is enough during training because of reparameterization) and $z^{(l)} = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon^{(l)}$, with $\varepsilon^{(l)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. > [!TIP] > **Why the negative sign in front of the reconstruction term?** > In the loss formulation, we typically **minimize** the negative ELBO (because deep learning frameworks minimize loss, not maximize reward). So the negative sign converts the maximization of ELBO into a minimization of $-$ELBO. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Forward Pass | Stage | Input | Operation | Output | |-------|-------|-----------|--------| | **1. Encode Mean** | $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Dense + Activation | $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | **2. Encode Log-Variance** | $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Dense + Activation | $\log \sigma^2 \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | **3. Sample Noise** | None | $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | $\varepsilon \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | **4. Reparameterize** | $\mu, \log \sigma^2, \varepsilon$ | $z = \mu + \exp(0.5 \log \sigma^2) \odot \varepsilon$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}^k$ | | **5. Decode** | $z$ | Dense + Activation (sigmoid) | $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | | **6. Loss** | $x, \hat{x}, \mu, \log \sigma^2$ | ELBO components | Scalar $\mathcal{L}$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 VAE Network Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR inputX["Input x
dim d"] encFC1["Encoder Dense
h units + ReLU"] encFC2["Encoder Dense
h units + ReLU"] muOut["Mean Head
mu dim k"] logvarOut["Log Variance Head
log sigma2 dim k"] noise["Noise epsilon
N 0 I"] repLayer["Reparameterize
z equals mu plus sigma times epsilon"] decFC1["Decoder Dense
h units + ReLU"] decFC2["Decoder Dense
d units + Sigmoid"] xHat["Reconstruction xhat
dim d"] lossELBO["ELBO Loss
Recon plus KL"] inputX --> encFC1 encFC1 --> encFC2 encFC2 --> muOut encFC2 --> logvarOut muOut --> repLayer logvarOut --> repLayer noise --> repLayer repLayer --> decFC1 decFC1 --> decFC2 decFC2 --> xHat xHat --> lossELBO inputX --> lossELBO muOut --> lossELBO logvarOut --> lossELBO ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Encoder-Decoder Layer Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Encoder["ENCODER q phi z given x"] e1["Input Layer x dim 784 example MNIST"] e2["Dense 400 + ReLU"] e3["Dense 200 + ReLU"] e4["Dense k mu output"] e5["Dense k log sigma2 output"] e1 --> e2 --> e3 e3 --> e4 e3 --> e5 end subgraph Latent["LATENT SPACE"] ls1["Sampling z equals mu plus sigma times epsilon"] e4 --> ls1 e5 --> ls1 end subgraph Decoder["DECODER p theta x given z"] d1["Input Layer z dim k"] d2["Dense 200 + ReLU"] d3["Dense 400 + ReLU"] d4["Dense 784 + Sigmoid equals xhat"] ls1 --> d1 --> d2 --> d3 --> d4 end subgraph Loss["LOSS"] L1["Reconstruction BCE x xhat"] L2["KL Divergence mu log sigma2 versus N 0 I"] L3["Total negative ELBO"] d4 --> L1 e4 --> L2 e5 --> L2 L1 --> L3 L2 --> L3 end ``` ## 4.3 Training vs Generation Mode Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Training["TRAINING PHASE - Backpropagation Active"] t1["Sample x from dataset"] t2["Encode x to mu and log sigma2"] t3["Sample epsilon from N 0 I"] t4["Reparameterize z equals mu plus sigma times epsilon"] t5["Decode z to xhat"] t6["Compute negative ELBO loss"] t7["Backprop gradients to phi and theta"] t1 --> t2 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7 t3 --> t4 end subgraph Generation["GENERATION PHASE - No Input Required"] g1["Sample z from N 0 I prior"] g2["Decode z through trained decoder"] g3["Output new sample xhat"] g1 --> g2 --> g3 end ``` ## 4.4 Information Flow Through Reparameterization Layer ```mermaid flowchart LR muNode["mu vector k dims"] logvarNode["log sigma2 vector k dims"] sigmaNode["sigma equals exp 0.5 times log sigma2"] epsNode["epsilon sampled from N 0 I"] sumNode["Elementwise Add z equals mu plus sigma times epsilon"] outNode["Latent code z"] muNode --> sumNode logvarNode --> sigmaNode sigmaNode --> sumNode epsNode --> sumNode sumNode --> outNode ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Key:** > - **Training Phase**: The encoder is essential; we need an input $x$ to compute reconstruction. > - **Generation Phase**: The encoder is **discarded**. We sample directly from the prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and pass through the decoder alone. > - The **reparameterization trick** is the only bridge that makes the network differentiable end-to-end. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Variational Autoencoder. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B] [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** A **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** is a generative deep learning model introduced by **Kingma and Welling (2014)** that learns a probabilistic latent representation of input data. Unlike a standard autoencoder, the encoder of a VAE outputs the parameters of a probability distribution (typically the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$) instead of a single deterministic latent code. A latent vector $z$ is then sampled from this distribution using the **reparameterization trick** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$, and passed through the decoder to reconstruct the input. The model is trained by maximizing the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**, which consists of a **reconstruction term** and a **KL divergence regularization term**, enabling both faithful reconstruction and smooth generative sampling from the latent space. ### Question 2: What is the reparameterization trick? Why is it necessary? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** The **reparameterization trick** rewrites the sampling operation $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ as a deterministic, differentiable function of the network parameters and an external noise variable: $$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ **Why it is necessary:** The standard sampling operation $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is **non-differentiable** because it involves a stochastic node, which blocks gradient flow during backpropagation. By isolating the randomness in $\varepsilon$ (a fixed-distribution sample) and expressing $z$ as a deterministic function of $\mu$ and $\sigma$, gradients can now flow smoothly through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ from the loss function back to the encoder. This enables **end-to-end training** of the entire VAE using standard stochastic gradient descent. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4] **(a)** Derive the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)** loss function for a Variational Autoencoder, starting from the marginal log-likelihood $\log p_\theta(x)$. Clearly state the role of the variational distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ and apply **Jensen's inequality**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain the **network architecture** of a VAE in detail, including the encoder, the reparameterization layer, and the decoder. State the dimensions of inputs and outputs of each component, and write down the closed-form expression for the KL divergence term when both the encoder posterior and the latent prior are Gaussian. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Define the objective.** We want to maximize the log-marginal likelihood of the data: $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z) \, dz$$ This integral is intractable due to the continuous, high-dimensional latent variable $z$. **Step 2: Introduce the variational distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$.** The true posterior $p_\theta(z \vert x) = p_\theta(x, z) / p_\theta(x)$ is intractable. We approximate it with a parametric distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ (a neural network with parameters $\phi$). **Step 3: Multiply and divide inside the integral by $q_\phi(z \vert x)$:** $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z \vert x) \cdot \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \, dz$$ **Step 4: Recognize as an expectation:** $$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \, \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \left[ \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \right]$$ **Step 5: Apply Jensen's Inequality.** Since $\log$ is concave, $\log \mathbb{E}[Y] \geq \mathbb{E}[\log Y]$: $$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \left[ \log \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \right] = \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}(\theta, \phi; x)$$ **Step 6: Decompose into reconstruction and KL terms:** $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}\big(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\big)$$ #### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks **The VAE has three primary components:** **1. Encoder $q_\phi(z \vert x)$** — Inference/Recognition Network - **Input**: $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g., $d = 784$ for flattened MNIST image). - **Hidden Layers**: Stack of fully-connected (or convolutional) layers with non-linear activations (typically ReLU). - **Output Heads**: Two parallel dense layers producing: - Mean vector $\mu \in \mathbb{R}^k$ - Log-variance vector $\log \sigma^2 \in \mathbb{R}^k$ - **Role**: Approximates the posterior distribution over the latent code. **2. Reparameterization Layer (Sampling Layer)** - **Input**: $\mu, \log \sigma^2 \in \mathbb{R}^k$ and noise $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$. - **Operation**: $$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon = \mu + \exp(0.5 \cdot \log \sigma^2) \odot \varepsilon$$ - **Output**: $z \in \mathbb{R}^k$ — a sample from the encoder's posterior. - **Role**: Allows backpropagation through the stochastic sampling step. **3. Decoder $p_\theta(x \vert z)$** — Generative Network - **Input**: $z \in \mathbb{R}^k$. - **Hidden Layers**: Mirror of the encoder architecture. - **Output**: $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (with sigmoid for binary data, or linear for continuous). - **Role**: Maps latent codes back to data space. **4. KL Divergence Closed-Form Expression:** For $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \text{diag}(\sigma^2))$ and $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$: $$D_{KL}\big(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\big) = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \big( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \big)$$ This is computed analytically — no sampling is required for this term. ### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] **(a)** Explain the role of the **latent space prior** $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ in a VAE. How does the KL divergence term enforce this prior, and what happens to the latent space if the KL term is removed from the loss? **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Compare and contrast a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** with a **standard Autoencoder (AE)** in terms of (i) latent space structure, (ii) generation capability, and (iii) loss function. Provide a short table of comparison. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Role of the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:** The prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ defines a **structured, continuous, and unbounded** latent space centered at the origin. It serves three critical purposes: 1. **Generative Sampling**: At inference, we can draw a fresh $z$ from this prior and feed it to the decoder to generate a *new, never-seen* sample. The prior acts as the source of randomness for the generative process. 2. **Regularization**: It anchors the encoder's output distributions, preventing them from collapsing to a single point or becoming arbitrarily far from the origin. 3. **Smoothness**: It encourages nearby points in the latent space to decode to semantically similar outputs, enabling smooth interpolation. **How the KL term enforces the prior:** The KL divergence term $D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))$ measures how much the encoder's output distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ deviates from the prior $p(z)$. Minimizing this term *pulls* the encoder's mean $\mu$ toward zero and its variance $\sigma^2$ toward one, keeping the encoder's distributions aligned with the standard Gaussian. **What happens if the KL term is removed?** - The encoder is free to spread latent codes *anywhere* in $\mathbb{R}^k$. - **Posterior Collapse**: The decoder may ignore $z$ entirely, learning to output $x$ based on a bias term only. - **Loss of smooth structure**: The latent space becomes irregular, with **gaps** where sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ at generation time produces nonsense outputs. - The model degenerates to a standard autoencoder — good at reconstruction, poor at generation. #### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table: VAE vs. Standard Autoencoder** | Aspect | Standard Autoencoder (AE) | Variational Autoencoder (VAE) | |--------|---------------------------|------------------------------| | **(i) Latent Space** | Deterministic point $z = f(x)$ per input | Probabilistic distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ per input | | **(ii) Generation** | Cannot generate new samples — no structured prior | Can generate by sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decoding | | **(iii) Loss Function** | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^2$ (reconstruction only) | $\mathcal{L} = -\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = -\mathbb{E}_q[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] + D_{KL}(q \Vert p)$ | | **Interpolation** | Discrete, may show gaps | Smooth, continuous manifold | | **Output of Encoder** | Single vector $z$ | Two vectors $\mu$ and $\log \sigma^2$ | | **Training Stability** | Simple, stable | Requires reparameterization trick | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1**: Students often confuse $\log \sigma^2$ (log-variance) with $\log \sigma$ (log-standard-deviation). The encoder outputs the **log-variance**, not the log of the standard deviation. Lose **1 mark** if wrong. > - **Common Mistake #2**: Writing $D_{KL}(p \Vert q)$ instead of $D_{KL}(q_\phi \Vert p(z))$. The order matters — the **first argument is the encoder's posterior** and the **second is the prior**. The closed-form formula given above is valid only for $D_{KL}(q \Vert p)$. > - **Common Mistake #3**: Forgetting the negative sign in the loss. The loss is **negative ELBO** (we minimize), not ELBO (which we maximize). Failing to flip the sign loses **2 marks**. > - **Common Mistake #4**: Omitting the **reparameterization trick** in derivations — without it, the model cannot be trained end-to-end via backpropagation. Always state it explicitly. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — VAE Network Architecture** - ✅ A VAE has **two networks**: Encoder $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ and Decoder $p_\theta(x \vert z)$. - ✅ The encoder outputs **mean $\mu$** and **log-variance $\log \sigma^2$**, not $\sigma^2$ directly. - ✅ The **reparameterization trick** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ makes the sampling step differentiable. - ✅ The **latent prior** is always $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ — standard multivariate Gaussian. - ✅ The **ELBO** = Reconstruction term **minus** KL divergence. - ✅ The **full loss** = $-$ELBO (we minimize; ELBO we maximize). - ✅ The **KL term has a closed form** for diagonal Gaussian encoder and standard Gaussian prior: $$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \big( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \big)$$ - ✅ **Reconstruction loss** = Binary cross-entropy (Bernoulli) or MSE (Gaussian). - ✅ At **generation time**, the encoder is **discarded**; sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decode only. - ✅ Jensen's inequality is the **mathematical cornerstone** that yields the ELBO. - ✅ KL divergence ordering: encoder's distribution first, prior second. - ✅ **Posterior collapse** occurs when the KL term is too strong or when the decoder ignores $z$. - ✅ The $\beta$-VAE variant uses a hyperparameter $\beta$ to scale the KL term for disentanglement. - ✅ VAE is trained with the **Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes (SGVB)** estimator and the **reparameterization gradient** (a.k.a. pathwise derivative).

topics.title: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** are a class of deep generative models introduced by *Ian J. Goodfellow and colleagues in 2014*. A GAN consists of **two neural networks** — a **Generator (G)** and a **Discriminator (D)** — that are trained simultaneously through an **adversarial (min–max) game**.

- The **Generator (G)** maps a random noise vector $z \sim p_z(z)$ (typically sampled from a standard normal or uniform distribution) from a prior noise distribution $p_z$ to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$, attempting to mimic the true data distribution $p_{data}$.
- The **Discriminator (D)** is a binary classifier that outputs the probability $D(x)$ that an input $x$ is a **real sample** (drawn from $p_{data}$) rather than a **fake sample** (produced by $G$).

The two networks are locked in a two-player zero-sum game: $G$ tries to *fool* $D$ by generating increasingly realistic samples, while $D$ tries to *distinguish* real from fake. The training corresponds to solving:

$$\min_{G}\,\max_{D}\,V(D,G)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GANs are explicitly listed under Module 4 of PEEBT746 (Deep Learning Techniques). Students must know the **value function**, the **training algorithm**, the **optimal discriminator**, the role of **Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)**, and key variants (DCGAN, Conditional GAN).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter vs. The Police

Imagine a **counterfeiter (Generator)** trying to produce fake currency, and a **police detective (Discriminator)** trying to tell real money from fake:

| Real-World Analogy | GAN Component |
|---|---|
| Counterfeiter printing fake notes | **Generator $G$** |
| Police detective examining notes | **Discriminator $D$** |
| Genuine currency from the central bank | Real samples $x \sim p_{data}$ |
| Blank paper / random scribbles used as starting material | Noise vector $z \sim p_z$ |
| Detective's verdict ("Real" or "Fake") | Probability $D(x) \in [0,1]$ |

At first, the counterfeiter's fakes are crude and the detective easily catches them. With feedback (the detective's verdict), the counterfeiter improves. Eventually, the counterfeiter becomes so skilled that the detective is forced to guess randomly (50% accuracy) — at this equilibrium, the generated samples are **statistically indistinguishable** from real data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Concept:** The ideal end state of GAN training is the **Nash Equilibrium**, where $p_g = p_{data}$ (Generator's output distribution equals the true data distribution) and $D(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for every $x$ (the Discriminator can no longer tell real from fake).

## 1.3 Why GANs Matter in Engineering

- **Image synthesis & super-resolution:** SRGAN, ESRGAN.
- **Data augmentation** for medical imaging, defect detection, and rare-event generation.
- **Style transfer, text-to-image (Stable Diffusion, DALL·E use diffusion, but early GAN-based systems were foundational).**
- **Anomaly detection** in industrial IoT and predictive maintenance.
- **Drug discovery & material design** in computational chemistry.
- **Speech synthesis & audio enhancement.**

GANs introduced the paradigm of **implicit generative modeling**, where the model learns to generate samples from a distribution *without ever explicitly computing its probability density function* — a sharp departure from VAEs and autoregressive models.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Networks in Detail

### A. The Generator $G$
- **Input:** A noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{n_z}$ drawn from a prior $p_z(z)$ (commonly $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ or $\mathcal{U}[-1, 1]$).
- **Output:** A synthetic sample $G(z) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_x}$ living in the same space as real data.
- **Architecture (typical):** Deep feed-forward or deconvolutional network. In **DCGAN** (Deep Convolutional GAN), transposed convolutions are used to upsample from $z$ to image dimensions.
- **Goal:** Learn a mapping $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ such that the induced distribution $p_g$ matches $p_{data}$.

### B. The Discriminator $D$
- **Input:** A sample $x$ (could be real or fake).
- **Output:** A scalar $D(x) \in (0, 1)$ — the estimated probability that $x$ is real.
- **Architecture (typical):** A standard CNN classifier (e.g., for images) ending in a sigmoid activation.
- **Goal:** Maximize the probability of correctly classifying real vs. fake samples.

## 2.2 The Value Function (Adversarial Objective)

The original GAN formulation defines the value function $V(D, G)$ as:

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

### Interpretation of Each Term

- $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)]$: $D$ wants this **large** — high probability of correctly labeling real data as real.
- $\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$: $D$ wants this **large** (i.e., $D(G(z)) \to 0$) — correctly labeling fakes as fake. $G$ wants this **small** (i.e., $D(G(z)) \to 1$) — fooling $D$.

### Why $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$?

Initially, $D$ easily rejects fakes, so $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, making $\log(1 - 0) \approx 0$ — this gives a **very weak gradient** to $G$ early in training. Goodfellow proposed a non-saturating alternative for practical training:

$$\text{Practical Generator loss} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z)))]$$

This rephrasing **maximizes $\log D(G(z))$** instead, which provides **strong gradients** even when $D$ is confidently rejecting fakes.

## 2.3 Training Procedure (Alternate Optimization)

GANs are trained using **alternating stochastic gradient descent**:

1. **Fix $G$, update $D$** for $k$ steps (usually $k = 1$):  
   Ascend on
