   $$\nabla_{\theta_D} \left[\,\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log D(x^{(i)}) + \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\right]$$

2. **Fix $D$, update $G$** for $1$ step:  
   Descend on (non-saturating form)  
   $$-\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\log D(G(z^{(i)}))$$

This is repeated until equilibrium is approached.

## 2.4 Connection to Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theoretical Insight (Frequently asked in KTU exams):** For a **fixed** Generator $G$, the optimal Discriminator $D^*_G$ leads to a loss that equals (up to a constant) the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$.

The optimal $D^*$ satisfies:

$$D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$$

Substituting back, the minimum value of $V$ for the optimal $D$ is:

$$\min_{G} V(D^*_G, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$$

The **global optimum** is $p_g = p_{data}$, where $\text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g) = 0$ and $V = -\log 4$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Value Function | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | The adversarial objective |
| 2 | Min–Max Game | $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$ | Defines the training equilibrium |
| 3 | Optimal Discriminator | $D^*_G(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Derived by setting $\frac{\partial V}{\partial D} = 0$ |
| 4 | Generator Loss at Optimum | $V(D^*_G, G) = -\log(4) + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$ | Minimum value when $D$ is optimal |
| 5 | Global Equilibrium | $p_g = p_{data} \;\Rightarrow\; D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; V = -\log 4$ | Nash equilibrium of the game |
| 6 | Non-Saturating G-loss | $-\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ | Used in practice for stable early gradients |
| 7 | KL Divergence (related) | $D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \int P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} dx$ | Asymmetric; JSD is symmetric version |
| 8 | JSD Definition | $\text{JSD}(P \parallel Q) = \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \parallel M) + \tfrac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \parallel M)$, $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(P+Q)$ | Bounded in $[0, \log 2]$ |
| 9 | Wasserstein Loss (WGAN) | $\min_G \max_{D \in \mathcal{L}-1} \mathbb{E}[D(x)] - \mathbb{E}[D(G(z))]$ | Uses 1-Lipschitz constraint |
| 10 | Mode Collapse | $p_g$ collapses to a few modes of $p_{data}$ | Common failure mode of GANs |

## 2.6 Common Variants (KTU-Frequently Tested)

| Variant | Key Idea | Modification |
|---|---|---|
| **DCGAN** | Uses deep convolutions in $G$ and $D$ | Transposed conv + batch norm |
| **Conditional GAN (cGAN)** | Adds class label $y$ to both $G$ and $D$ | $G(z,y)$, $D(x,y)$ |
| **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** | Uses Earth-Mover distance | Weight clipping or gradient penalty |
| **LSGAN** | Least-squares loss for $D$ | More stable training |
| **CycleGAN** | Unpaired image-to-image translation | Two GANs + cycle consistency |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Synthetic defect generation for training quality-control classifiers when real defects are scarce.
- **Medical Imaging:** Generating pseudo-MRI/CT scans for data augmentation in tumor detection.
- **Autonomous Driving:** Simulating rare hazardous scenarios for training perception models.
- **Cybersecurity:** GAN-based adversarial attack generation to harden intrusion detection systems.
- **Climate Science:** Downscaling low-resolution climate models to high-resolution predictions (super-resolution GANs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **most frequently tested mathematical content** on GANs in KTU End Semester Examinations. Master each step.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^*_G(x)$

### Step 1 — Write the Value Function as an Integral

The expectation form of $V(D, G)$ can be rewritten because $D$ is applied to either real samples (drawn from $p_{data}$) or fake samples (drawn from $p_g$ via $G(z)$):

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x)\, dx \;+\; \int_{z} p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz$$

### Step 2 — Change of Variable

By the change of variable $x = G(z)$, the second integral becomes an integral over $x$ with respect to the generator's distribution $p_g(x)$:

$$\int_{z} p_z(z) \log(1 - D(G(z)))\, dz = \int_{x} p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx$$

### Step 3 — Combine the Two Integrals

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} \Big[\,p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \;+\; p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\,\Big] dx$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Inner Functional

For a **fixed $G$**, the integrand is a pointwise function of $D(x)$. Define for any fixed $x$:

$$f(y) = p_{data}(x) \log y \;+\; p_g(x) \log(1 - y), \quad y = D(x) \in (0, 1)$$

### Step 5 — Maximize $f(y)$ with Respect to $y$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{y} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - y} = 0$$

Solve for $y$:

$$\frac{p_{data}(x)}{y} = \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - y} \;\Rightarrow\; p_{data}(x)(1 - y) = p_g(x) \cdot y$$

$$p_{data}(x) - p_{data}(x)\, y = p_g(x)\, y$$

$$p_{data}(x) = y\,\big(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)\big)$$

$$\boxed{\,D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Verify This Is a Maximum

Second derivative test:

$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial y^2} = -\frac{p_{data}(x)}{y^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1-y)^2} < 0$$

The second derivative is strictly negative (since $p_{data}, p_g \geq 0$ and $y \in (0,1)$), confirming a **maximum**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Generator's Loss at the Optimal Discriminator

### Step 1 — Substitute $D^*_G(x)$ into $V$

$$V(D^*_G, G) = \int_{x} \Big[\,p_{data}(x) \log D^*_G(x) \;+\; p_g(x) \log(1 - D^*_G(x))\,\Big] dx$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Two Logarithms

$$\log D^*_G(x) = \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} = \log p_{data}(x) - \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$$

$$\log(1 - D^*_G(x)) = \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} = \log p_g(x) - \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back

$$V(D^*_G, G) = \int_{x} \Big[\,p_{data}(x) \log p_{data}(x) - p_{data}(x) \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)) + p_g(x) \log p_g(x) - p_g(x) \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))\,\Big] dx$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out Common Terms

Group the two terms containing $\log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$:

$$V(D^*_G, G) = \int_{x} \Big[\,p_{data}(x) \log p_{data}(x) + p_g(x) \log p_g(x) - (p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)) \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))\,\Big] dx$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the JSD

Recall the Kullback–Leibler divergence: $D_{KL}(P \parallel Q) = \int P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} dx$.

Rewrite the integrand by adding and subtracting $-\log 2$ terms to expose KL:

$$V(D^*_G, G) = -\log 4 + \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$$

More precisely, the standard result is:

$$V(D^*_G, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$$

where

$$\text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g) = \frac{1}{2}\Big[D_{KL}(p_{data} \parallel p_m) + D_{KL}(p_g \parallel p_m)\Big], \quad p_m = \frac{p_{data} + p_g}{2}$$

### Step 6 — Conclude the Global Minimum

Since $\text{JSD} \geq 0$, the minimum of $V(D^*_G, G)$ is achieved when $\text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g) = 0$, which occurs **iff** $p_g = p_{data}$. At this point:

$$V = -\log 4, \quad D^*(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{2 p_{data}(x)} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \forall x$$

This is the **Nash Equilibrium** of the GAN game. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Why the Original Generator Loss Saturates (Non-Saturating Trick)

The original Generator objective is:

$$\min_G \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

Near the start of training, $G$'s outputs are poor, so $D$ easily rejects them: $D(G(z)) \approx 0$. Then:

$$\log(1 - D(G(z))) \approx \log(1 - 0) = \log 1 = 0$$

The gradient of $\log(1 - y)$ w.r.t. $y$ is $-\frac{1}{1-y}$, which is small near $y=0$. Hence $G$ receives **vanishing gradients**.

The non-saturating trick replaces this with:

$$\max_G \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$$

Now the gradient of $\log(y)$ w.r.t. $y$ is $\frac{1}{y}$, which is **large** near $y=0$ — strong learning signal.

The dynamic value function using this practical loss is:

$$V'(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$$

This is **no longer the same min–max game** mathematically, but in practice it converges to similar equilibria with much better gradient flow.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Loss Behaviour

| Training Stage | $D$'s Confidence on Fakes | Original $\log(1-D(G(z)))$ Gradient | Non-Saturating $\log D(G(z))$ Gradient |
|---|---|---|---|
| Early (poor $G$) | $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ (vanishing) | $\approx$ large (effective) |
| Mid-training | $D(G(z)) \approx 0.5$ | Moderate | Moderate |
| Near convergence | $D(G(z)) \approx 1$ | $\approx -\infty$ (saturating) | $\approx 0$ (gentle) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Standard GAN

The diagram below maps the data flow between the **noise prior**, the **Generator**, the **Discriminator**, and the **loss computation pipeline** during one training step.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[NOISE PRIOR]:::src -->|z ~ pz| G1
    X1[REAL DATA]:::src -->|x ~ pdata| D1
    G1[GENERATOR NETWORK G]:::gen -->|Gz fake sample| D1
    D1[DISCRIMINATOR NETWORK D]:::disc -->|Dx sigmoid| O1
    O1[OUTPUT PROBABILITY]:::out -->|log Dx| L1
    O1 -->|log 1 minus D Gz| L2
    L1[REAL LOSS TERM]:::loss --> V1
    L2[FAKE LOSS TERM]:::loss --> V1
    V1[VALUE FUNCTION VDG]:::val --> U1
    U1[ALTERNATING OPTIMIZER]:::upd -->|update D| D1
    U1 -->|update G| G1

    classDef src fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef disc fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#F4D4D4,stroke:#D62728,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#E8D4F4,stroke:#9467BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef val fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8C564B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef upd fill:#D0E8FF,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### How to Read the Diagram

- **Noise Prior → Generator:** A latent vector $z$ is sampled (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$) and passed through $G$.
- **Generator → Discriminator:** $G$ produces a fake sample $G(z)$ and feeds it to $D$ alongside real samples $x$.
- **Discriminator → Output:** $D$ emits $D(x) \in (0,1)$ — its belief that $x$ is real.
- **Output → Losses:** Two log-losses are computed: $\log D(x)$ for real samples, $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ for fake samples.
- **Losses → Value Function:** Combined into the scalar $V(D, G)$.
- **Value Function → Optimizer:** Gradients are alternately back-propagated to update $D$'s parameters, then $G$'s parameters.

## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Probability Distributions During Training

The diagram below illustrates the **evolution of $p_g$** relative to $p_{data}$ across training iterations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 INITIAL]
        A1[PG RANDOM NOISE]:::gen --> A2[D EASILY SEPARATES REAL VS FAKE]:::disc
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 INTERMEDIATE]
        B1[PG SHIFTS TOWARD PDATA]:::gen --> B2[D STRUGGLES TO CLASSIFY]:::disc
    end
    subgraph STAGE3[STAGE 3 CONVERGED]
        C1[PG EQUALS PDATA]:::gen --> C2[D OUTPUTS ONE HALF EVERYWHERE]:::disc
    end
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3

    classDef gen fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef disc fill:#D4F4DD,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Interpretation

- **Stage 1 (initial):** $p_g$ is very different from $p_{data}$. The discriminator easily separates the two distributions.
- **Stage 2 (intermediate):** $G$ learns to shift $p_g$ closer to $p_{data}$. $D$'s accuracy drops.
- **Stage 3 (converged):** $p_g = p_{data}$, and $D$ outputs $0.5$ everywhere — Nash equilibrium.

## 4.3 Block-Level Training Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START OF EPOCH]:::step --> S2[SAMPLE MINI BATCH OF M NOISE VECTORS Z]:::step
    S2 --> S3[SAMPLE MINI BATCH OF M REAL SAMPLES X FROM PDATA]:::step
    S3 --> S4[UPDATE DISCRIMINATOR D BY ASCENDING STOCHASTIC GRADIENT OF V]:::step
    S4 --> S5[UPDATE GENERATOR G BY DESCENDING NON SATURATING LOSS]:::step
    S5 --> S6[REPEAT FOR K D STEPS PER G STEP]:::step
    S6 --> S7[END OF EPOCH CHECK CONVERGENCE]:::step

    classDef step fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---
