<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). List its two main components and state their roles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is a deep generative model proposed by Goodfellow et al. (2014) that learns to generate new data samples having the same statistics as the training set, by training two neural networks in opposition.

**Two main components:**

1. **Generator (G):** Takes a random noise vector $z$ from a prior distribution $p_z$ and maps it to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$. Its role is to learn the data distribution $p_{data}$ and produce samples that can fool the Discriminator.

2. **Discriminator (D):** A binary classifier that takes an input $x$ (real or fake) and outputs $D(x) \in (0,1)$, the probability that $x$ is a real sample from $p_{data}$. Its role is to distinguish real samples from generator-produced fakes.

The two networks play a **min–max game** formalized as $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is mode collapse in GANs? Mention one technique used to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mode Collapse** is a common failure mode of GANs in which the Generator learns to produce only a *limited variety* of samples — typically a small subset of the modes of the true data distribution — even though the training set is diverse. In the extreme case, the Generator outputs nearly identical samples for different noise inputs, while the Discriminator's gradient vanishes because all fakes look the same.

**Cause:** The Generator discovers a few samples that consistently fool the Discriminator and exploits them (a local equilibrium).

**Mitigation techniques (any one):**
- **Mini-batch discrimination:** Allow $D$ to compare samples within a batch so it can detect lack of diversity.
- **Unrolled GANs:** Backpropagate through $D$'s future updates to discourage $G$ from overfitting to the current $D$.
- **Wasserstein loss (WGAN):** Smoother gradient flow discourages collapse.
- **Spectral normalization** of $D$ for stable training.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the optimal Discriminator $D^*_G(x)$ for a fixed Generator $G$ in a standard GAN. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result of (a), show that the value function $V(D^*_G, G)$ reduces to $-\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$, and hence conclude the condition for global equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Deriving $D^*_G(x)$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write $V(D,G)$ in integral form.

For a fixed $G$, we can rewrite the value function as an integral over the data space $\mathcal{X}$:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x)\, dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \log(1 - D(x))\, dx$$

after the change of variable $x = G(z)$ in the second term.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Recognize the pointwise structure.

The integral is a sum of pointwise functions of $D(x)$. To find the optimal $D$, we can maximize the integrand **independently at each $x$**.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define and differentiate.

For a fixed $x$, define:

$$f(y) = p_{data}(x) \log y + p_g(x) \log(1 - y), \quad y = D(x)$$

$$\frac{df}{dy} = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{y} - \frac{p_g(x)}{1 - y} = 0$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $y$.

$$p_{data}(x)(1 - y) = p_g(x) y \;\Rightarrow\; p_{data}(x) = y (p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$$

$$\boxed{D^*_G(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Verify the maximum.

$$\frac{d^2 f}{dy^2} = -\frac{p_{data}(x)}{y^2} - \frac{p_g(x)}{(1-y)^2} < 0$$

So the critical point is a **maximum**.

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Interpretation and special cases.

- If $p_{data}(x) \to 0$ and $p_g(x) > 0$: $D^* \to 0$ (correctly flags fake).
- If $p_{data}(x) > 0$ and $p_g(x) \to 0$: $D^* \to 1$ (correctly flags real).
- If $p_{data}(x) = p_g(x)$: $D^* = 1/2$ (cannot distinguish).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — JSD Connection and Equilibrium (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Substitute $D^*_G$ into $V$.

$$V(D^*_G, G) = \int_{x} \left[\,p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} + p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}\,\right] dx$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Expand the logarithms.

\begin{aligned}
V(D^*_G, G) &= \int_{x} \Big[\,p_{data}(x) \log p_{data}(x) + p_g(x) \log p_g(x) \\
&\quad - (p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)) \log(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))\,\Big] dx
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Introduce $M = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{data} + p_g)$ and manipulate.

\begin{aligned}
V(D^*_G, G) &= \int_x \Big[p_{data} \log p_{data} - p_{data} \log(2M) + p_g \log p_g - p_g \log(2M)\Big] dx \\
&= -\log 4 + \int_x \Big[p_{data} \log \tfrac{p_{data}}{M} + p_g \log \tfrac{p_g}{M}\Big] dx \\
&= -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude the global equilibrium.

Since $\text{JSD} \geq 0$ with equality **iff** $p_{data} = p_g$, the global minimum of $V$ is:

$$V_{\min} = -\log 4, \quad \text{achieved when } p_g = p_{data} \text{ and } D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \forall x$$

This is the **Nash Equilibrium** of the adversarial game. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Write down the value function of a standard GAN. Explain the role of the Generator and Discriminator networks and describe the alternating training procedure in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **vanishing gradient problem** of the original GAN objective for the Generator. Show mathematically why the non-saturating heuristic loss is preferred in practice, and briefly mention one advanced GAN variant designed to address training instability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Value Function and Training (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The value function.

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

- The first term encourages $D$ to assign high probability to real samples.
- The second term encourages $D$ to assign low probability to fake samples while simultaneously penalizing $G$ for producing easily detectable fakes.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Roles of $G$ and $D$.

- **Generator $G$:** Maps latent noise $z \sim p_z$ to a synthetic sample $G(z)$. Learns the data distribution $p_g$ by minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$.
- **Discriminator $D$:** A binary classifier. Learns to maximize $\log D(x) + \log(1 - D(G(z)))$, i.e., to correctly label real and fake.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Alternating training algorithm.

For each training iteration:

1. **Sample** a mini-batch of $m$ noise vectors $\{z^{(1)}, \dots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z$.
2. **Sample** a mini-batch of $m$ real examples $\{x^{(1)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{data}$.
3. **Update $D$ (k times, often k=1):** Ascend on  
   $$\nabla_{\theta_D} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\right]$$
4. **Update $G$ (once):** Descend on the non-saturating form  
   $$\nabla_{\theta_G} \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[-\log D(G(z^{(i)}))\right]$$
5. Repeat until $D$'s output approaches $1/2$ on both real and fake samples.

The inner loop trains $D$ to near-optimality, and the outer step gives $G$ useful gradient information.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Vanishing Gradient and the Non-Saturating Loss (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The saturation problem.

The original Generator objective is:

$$L_G^{\text{original}} = \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

Early in training, $G$ produces poor fakes, so $D$ confidently rejects them: $D(G(z)) \approx 0$. Then:

$$\log(1 - D(G(z))) \approx \log 1 = 0$$

The gradient of this w.r.t. $D(G(z))$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \log(1 - y)}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{1 - y}, \quad y = D(G(z))$$

At $y \approx 0$, this gradient is $\approx -1$, which is small. More importantly, $L_G$ itself is **flat near $y=0$**, so $G$ receives negligible learning signal.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The non-saturating loss.

Replace the objective with:

$$L_G^{\text{non-sat}} = -\mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log D(G(z))]$$

The gradient is now:

$$\frac{\partial [-\log y]}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{y}$$

At $y \approx 0$, this gradient is **large**, providing strong updates to $G$ exactly when needed (early training).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Mathematical demonstration.

Near $y = 0$:

- $\log(1 - y) = -y - \tfrac{y^2}{2} - \dots \approx -y$ (first-order small)
- $\log y \to -\infty$ (steep, large negative log)

The $-\log y$ form produces **stronger gradients** in the regime where $D$ is confidently wrong about fakes.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Advanced variant for training stability.

**Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** addresses training instability by replacing the JSD-based loss with the **Earth-Mover (Wasserstein-1) distance**:

$$W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\|x - y\|]$$

Under a **1-Lipschitz constraint** on $D$ (enforced by weight clipping or gradient penalty), this provides meaningful gradients even when the distributions have disjoint support — a situation where JSD saturates to $\log 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the two value functions:** Many students mix up the original $V(D,G)$ with the non-saturating form. Always state clearly which is being used.
> 2. **Forgetting the change of variable:** When deriving $D^*_G(x)$, students often forget to convert the $z$-integral to an $x$-integral via $p_g$. This loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the "fixed $G$" assumption:** $D^*_G$ is optimal *only for a given $G$*. As $G$ updates, $D^*$ changes. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the JSD connection:** Writing $V(D^*_G, G) = -\log 4$ alone (without showing the JSD step) loses 3 marks in derivation questions.
> 5. **Mode Collapse answer:** Just naming "mode collapse" without explaining *why* it happens (Generator finds a narrow set of samples that fool $D$) loses 1 mark.
> 6. **WGAN confusion:** Students often write the WGAN loss without the **1-Lipschitz constraint** — the constraint is what makes the Wasserstein distance usable; omitting it is a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **GAN = Generator + Discriminator** trained in a **two-player zero-sum adversarial game**.
- **Value Function:** $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- **Min–Max formulation:** $\min_G \max_D V(D,G)$.
- **Optimal Discriminator:** $D^*_G(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
- **Optimal $D$ value:** $V(D^*_G, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot \text{JSD}(p_{data} \parallel p_g)$.
- **Global Equilibrium:** $p_g = p_{data}$, $D^*(x) = 1/2$, $V_{\min} = -\log 4 \approx -1.386$.
- **Non-Saturating Generator Loss:** $-\mathbb{E}_z[\log D(G(z))]$ — used in practice to avoid vanishing gradients early in training.
- **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** is symmetric, bounded in $[0, \log 2]$, and equals $0$ iff $P = Q$.
- **Mode Collapse:** $G$ produces a narrow subset of $p_{data}$'s modes. Mitigations: mini-batch discrimination, WGAN, unrolled GANs.
- **WGAN:** Uses Wasserstein-1 distance with a 1-Lipschitz constraint (weight clipping or gradient penalty) to provide stable, meaningful gradients.
- **Conditional GAN (cGAN):** Conditions both $G$ and $D$ on a class label $y$: $G(z,y)$ and $D(x,y)$.
- **DCGAN:** Uses deep convolutional Generator (transposed convolutions) and Discriminator; introduces architectural guidelines (no pooling, batch norm, ReLU/LeakyReLU).
- **Training Procedure:** Alternate — train $D$ for $k$ steps, then $G$ for $1$ step. Use mini-batches. $D$ is updated $k$ (often $1$) times per $G$ update.
- **Failure Modes:** Mode collapse, vanishing gradients, non-convergence, oscillation around equilibrium, sensitivity to hyperparameters.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage (Typical):** Expect **1 Part A question (3 marks)** and **at least 1 sub-part of Part B (7–10 marks)** on GANs in the End Semester Examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boltzmann Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boltzmann Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boltzmann Machine (BM):** A Boltzmann Machine is a type of **stochastic, recurrent, energy-based neural network** (a generative undirected graphical model) in which the network architecture is symmetric (i.e., all connections are bidirectional with equal weights) and learning is governed by the principles of **statistical mechanics**, particularly the **Boltzmann (Gibbs) distribution**.

A Boltzmann Machine is composed of:
- **Visible units** (`v`) – which interface with the environment (input/output data).
- **Hidden units** (`h`) – which capture latent statistical dependencies in the data.
- A **symmetric weight matrix** `W` (no self-loops, typically `w_ii = 0`).
- **Biases** `a` (for visible units) and `b` (for hidden units).

The state of the network is a joint binary vector `x = (v, h)` and the system is assigned a scalar **energy** `E(x)`. The probability of a state follows the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$
P(x) = \frac{e^{-E(x)}}{Z}
$$

where `Z` is the **partition function** (a normalizing constant over all possible states).

### 1.2 Variants of Boltzmann Machines

| Variant | Description |
|---|---|
| **General Boltzmann Machine (BM)** | Fully connected undirected graph; visible and hidden units are inter-mixed. Practically intractable to train. |
| **Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)** | Bipartite graph – no intra-layer connections (visible-visible and hidden-hidden links are removed). This restriction makes Gibbs sampling tractable. |
| **Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM)** | Stack of multiple RBMs where each layer captures higher-order correlations. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy – "Gas Molecules in a Box":** Imagine a sealed box filled with gas molecules. The molecules constantly bump into each other and the walls. According to statistical mechanics, the system tends to **low-energy states** (molecules settling into equilibrium), but it can also fluctuate to higher-energy configurations probabilistically.

A Boltzmann Machine behaves identically:
- Each **unit** is like a molecule that can be `ON (1)` or `OFF (0)`.
- The **weights** between units define how strongly one unit "pushes" another toward being `ON` or `OFF`.
- The network **settles into low-energy configurations** that correspond to patterns it has learned.
- Occasional random flips (thermal noise) allow it to **escape local minima**, which is why Boltzmann Machines can model complex, multimodal data.

The key insight: **learning** = adjusting the weights so that **low-energy states correspond to patterns present in the training data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** The **Boltzmann constant** `k` links energy to probability through the famous factor `e^(-E/kT)`. In machine learning we absorb `kT = 1` for simplicity, yielding the cleaner form `P ∝ e^(-E)`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Function – Heart of the Boltzmann Machine

For binary units `v_i ∈ {0,1}` and `h_j ∈ {0,1}`, the energy of a joint state `(v, h)` is defined as:

$$
E(v, h) = - \sum_{i} a_i v_i \; - \sum_{j} b_j h_j \; - \sum_{i} \sum_{j} v_i \, w_{ij} \, h_j
$$

In compact matrix form:

$$
E(v, h) = -a^{\top} v \; - b^{\top} h \; - v^{\top} W h
$$

**Interpretation of each term:**
- `-a_i v_i`: penalty for activating visible unit `i` without a strong bias.
- `-b_j h_j`: penalty for activating hidden unit `j` without a strong bias.
- `-v_i w_ij h_j`: penalty when visible `i` and hidden `j` are both active and their weight is positive (favors the configuration if `w_ij > 0`).

### 2.2 Joint Probability Distribution

Using the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(v, h) = \frac{e^{-E(v, h)}}{Z} = \frac{e^{a^{\top} v + b^{\top} h + v^{\top} W h}}{Z}
$$

The **partition function** `Z` is intractable (sums over `2^(n_v + n_h)` states):

$$
Z = \sum_{v} \sum_{h} e^{-E(v, h)}
$$

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Why energy?** Energy gives a *scalar* summary of how "compatible" a configuration is with the model. Low energy ⇒ high probability.
- **Why stochastic?** Binary units fire probabilistically as `P(s=1) = σ(ΔE)`, allowing the model to explore diverse configurations and approximate the data distribution.
- **Why symmetric weights?** Symmetry (`w_ij = w_ji`) is required for the Boltzmann distribution to be a well-defined equilibrium distribution (satisfies detailed balance).

### 2.4 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) – The Tractable Cousin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an **RBM**, the bipartite restriction (`W_{vv} = 0` and `W_{hh} = 0`) makes hidden units **conditionally independent given visible units**, and vice versa. This is the key that enables efficient **block Gibbs sampling**.

Conditional activation probabilities:

$$
P(h_j = 1 \mid v) = \sigma\!\left(b_j + \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}\right)
$$

$$
P(v_i = 1 \mid h) = \sigma\!\left(a_i + \sum_{j} h_j w_{ij}\right)
$$

where `σ(·)` is the logistic sigmoid function:

$$
\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Joint energy | `E(v,h) = -aᵀv - bᵀh - vᵀWh` | Symmetric bipartite weights |
| Joint probability | `P(v,h) = e^(-E(v,h)) / Z` | Boltzmann / Gibbs distribution |
| Partition function | `Z = Σ_v Σ_h e^(-E(v,h))` | Intractable for full BM |
| Marginal of data | `P(v) = (1/Z) Σ_h e^(-E(v,h))` | Generator distribution |
| Free energy `F(v)` | `F(v) = -bᵀh - vᵀWh - ln Σ_h e^(bᵀh + vᵀWh)` | Used for tractable RBM gradient |
| Hidden activation | `P(h_j=1 \vert v) = σ(b_j + Σ_i v_i w_ij)` | Conditional independence |
| Visible activation | `P(v_i=1 \vert h) = σ(a_i + Σ_j h_j w_ij)` | Conditional independence |
| Log-likelihood gradient | `∂ ln P(v)/∂w_ij = ⟨v_i h_j⟩_data - ⟨v_i h_j⟩_model` | Positive vs. negative phase |
| CD-k weight update | `Δw_ij = ε (⟨v_i h_j⟩₀ - ⟨v_i h_j⟩_k)` | `ε` = learning rate |
| Bias update (visible) | `Δa_i = ε (⟨v_i⟩_data - ⟨v_i⟩_model)` | |
| Bias update (hidden) | `Δb_j = ε (⟨h_j⟩_data - ⟨h_j⟩_model)` | |
| Effective field | `net_j = b_j + Σ_i v_i w_ij` | Pre-activation |
| Sigmoid function | `σ(x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x))` | Squash to `[0,1]` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Reminder:** `⟨·⟩_data` denotes expectation under the data distribution; `⟨·⟩_model` denotes expectation under the model distribution (sampled via MCMC/Gibbs).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Recommendation engines** (Netflix-style collaborative filtering) – pioneered by RBMs.
- **Feature learning / pre-training** for deep neural networks (Deep Belief Networks).
- **Dimensionality reduction** similar in spirit to PCA but non-linear.
- **Anomaly / novelty detection** in industrial IoT sensor streams.
- **Generative modelling** of images, speech, and molecular structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Energy to Probability

**Step 1 – Start with the Boltzmann (Gibbs) distribution from statistical mechanics.**

$$
P(x) = \frac{1}{Z} \, e^{-E(x)/kT}
$$

Setting `kT = 1` (machine-learning convention):

$$
P(x) = \frac{e^{-E(x)}}{Z}
$$

**Step 2 – Identify `x = (v, h)` and substitute the energy function.**

$$
P(v, h) = \frac{1}{Z} \, e^{- \left[-a^{\top} v - b^{\top} h - v^{\top} W h\right]} = \frac{e^{a^{\top} v + b^{\top} h + v^{\top} W h}}{Z}
$$

**Step 3 – Derive the marginal `P(v)` by summing out the hidden units.**

$$
P(v) = \sum_{h} P(v, h) = \frac{1}{Z} \sum_{h} e^{a^{\top} v + b^{\top} h + v^{\top} W h}
$$

**Step 4 – Define the *free energy* `F(v)` to absorb the dependence on `v`.**

$$
F(v) = -\ln \sum_{h} e^{b^{\top} h + v^{\top} W h} - a^{\top} v
$$

Then by exponentiating:

$$
P(v) = \frac{e^{-F(v)}}{Z}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why free energy matters:** The free energy depends only on the visible units, providing a scalar energy-like value for any input data point. Minimising `F(v)` is equivalent to maximising the data log-likelihood.

### 3.2 Derivation: Conditional Activation Probabilities (RBM)

**Step 1 – Compute `P(h = 1 \mid v)` for a single hidden unit `h_j`.**

By Bayes' rule and the conditional independence in RBMs:

$$
P(h_j = 1 \mid v) = \frac{P(h_j = 1, v)}{P(h_j = 0, v) + P(h_j = 1, v)}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute the joint probability.**

$$
P(h_j = 1, v) = \frac{1}{Z} \, e^{a^{\top} v + b_j + \sum_i v_i w_{ij}} \cdot e^{\sum_{k \neq j} b_k h_k + \sum_i v_i \sum_{l \neq j} w_{il} h_l}
$$

The second exponential factor is the same for `h_j = 0` and `h_j = 1`, so it cancels in the ratio.

**Step 3 – Form the ratio.**

$$
P(h_j = 1 \mid v) = \frac{e^{b_j + \sum_i v_i w_{ij}}}{1 + e^{b_j + \sum_i v_i w_{ij}}}
$$

**Step 4 – Recognise the logistic sigmoid.**

$$
P(h_j = 1 \mid v) = \sigma\!\left(b_j + \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}\right)
$$

**Symmetric argument** gives the visible update:

$$
P(v_i = 1 \mid h) = \sigma\!\left(a_i + \sum_{j} h_j w_{ij}\right)
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum-Likelihood Learning Rule

**Step 1 – Define the log-likelihood of a training sample `v`.**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \ln P(v) = \ln \frac{e^{-F(v)}}{Z} = -F(v) - \ln Z
$$

**Step 2 – Differentiate w.r.t. a weight `w_ij`.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}} = -\frac{\partial F(v)}{\partial w_{ij}} - \frac{1}{Z} \frac{\partial Z}{\partial w_{ij}}
$$

**Step 3 – Evaluate each term using `Z = Σ_{v,h} e^{-E(v,h)}`.**

For the second term:

$$
\frac{1}{Z} \frac{\partial Z}{\partial w_{ij}} = -\sum_{v,h} P(v,h) \, v_i h_j = -\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}
$$

For the first term (after expanding the free-energy derivative):

$$
-\frac{\partial F(v)}{\partial w_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}}
$$

**Step 4 – Combine to obtain the elegant MLE gradient.**

$$
\frac{\partial \ln P(v)}{\partial w_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}
$$

**Step 5 – Stochastic gradient ascent update.**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \varepsilon \left( \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \right)
$$

where `ε` is the learning rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive phase (`⟨v_i h_j⟩_data`):** Push weights up so that the energy of training data decreases.
> **Negative phase (`⟨v_i h_j⟩_model`):** Push weights up so that the energy of model-sampled fantasies increases. The net effect sharpens the energy landscape around training data.

### 3.4 Derivation: Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) Approximation

**Problem:** Computing `⟨v_i h_j⟩_model` requires running a Markov chain to equilibrium – infeasible for large models.

**CD-k trick (Hinton, 2002):** Run the Markov chain for only `k` Gibbs steps (typically `k = 1`).

**Step 1 – Start from a training example `v⁰`.**

Sample hidden units:

$$
h^{0} \sim P(h \mid v^{0})
$$

**Step 2 – Reconstruct visible units.**

$$
v^{1} \sim P(v \mid h^{0})
$$

**Step 3 – Re-sample hidden units once more (for CD-1).**

$$
h^{1} \sim P(h \mid v^{1})
$$

**Step 4 – Use the correlation between the two end-points as the model expectation.**

$$
\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \approx v^{1}_i h^{1}_j
$$

**Step 5 – Final CD-1 weight update.**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \varepsilon \left( v^{0}_i h^{0}_j \; - \; v^{1}_i h^{1}_j \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CD-k works:** The gradient is biased but has very low variance, making it far more practical than full MLE. Empirically, CD-1 produces features nearly as good as much more expensive algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Boltzmann Machine – Architecture

The diagram below depicts a general Boltzmann Machine with three (3) visible units and four (4) hidden units, showing the **fully-connected, symmetric** nature of the network.

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1[v1 visible] <-->|w11| h1[h1 hidden]
    v1 <-->|w12| h2[h2 hidden]
    v1 <-->|w13| h3[h3 hidden]
    v1 <-->|w14| h4[h4 hidden]
    v2[v2 visible] <-->|w21| h1
    v2 <-->|w22| h2
    v2 <-->|w23| h3
    v2 <-->|w24| h4
    v3[v3 visible] <-->|w31| h1
    v3 <-->|w32| h2
    v3 <-->|w33| h3
    v3 <-->|w34| h4
    v1 <-->|intraV| v2
    v2 <-->|intraV| v3
    h1 <-->|intraH| h2
    h2 <-->|intraH| h3
    h3 <-->|intraH| h4
    v1 --- biasA[a visible bias]
    h1 --- biasB[b hidden bias]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Double-headed arrows denote **symmetric weights** (`w_ij = w_ji`). In a full Boltzmann Machine, hidden-hidden (`intraH`) and visible-visible (`intraV`) links exist; in an **RBM**, those would be removed.

### 4.2 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) – Bipartite Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph visibleLayer["VISIBLE LAYER v"]
        v1[v1]
        v2[v2]
        v3[v3]
    end
    subgraph hiddenLayer["HIDDEN LAYER h"]
        h1[h1]
        h2[h2]
        h3[h3]
        h4[h4]
    end
    v1 --- w11((w11)) --- h1
    v1 --- w12((w12)) --- h2
    v1 --- w13((w13)) --- h3
    v1 --- w14((w14)) --- h4
    v2 --- w21((w21)) --- h1
    v2 --- w22((w22)) --- h2
    v2 --- w23((w23)) --- h3
    v2 --- w24((w24)) --- h4
    v3 --- w31((w31)) --- h1
    v3 --- w32((w32)) --- h2
    v3 --- w33((w33)) --- h3
    v3 --- w34((w34)) --- h4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key structural difference:** RBM has **NO** visible-visible or hidden-hidden connections. This single restriction makes block Gibbs sampling feasible: all hidden units can be sampled **in parallel** given the visible layer, and vice versa.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CD-k Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 - Input training sample v0] --> B[Step 2 - Sample hidden layer h0 from P h given v0]
    B --> C[Step 3 - Reconstruct visible v1 from P v given h0]
    C --> D[Step 4 - Re-sample hidden h1 from P h given v1]
    D --> E{Step 5 - More Gibbs steps k greater than 1?}
    E -- yes | k > 1 | F[Repeat v to h alternating sampling until k steps done]
    E -- no | k = 1 | G[Step 6 - Compute positive phase v0h0 and negative phase vk hk]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Step 7 - Compute weight gradient dw_ij = epsilon times v0h0 minus vkhk]
    H --> I[Step 8 - Update weights w and biases a b]
    I --> J[Step 9 - Iterate over mini batch and epochs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flow captures the **full CD-k training loop** used to learn RBM parameters. For large datasets, mini-batches are used for stochastic gradient ascent.

### 4.4 Training-Phase Energy Landscape View

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[High Energy Region - Random Configurations] -->|positive phase lowers E| B[Data Manifold - Low Energy]
    B -->|negative phase raises E| C[Model Fantasy Samples]
    C -->|contrastive step| B
    B -->|gradient descent on F v| D[Converged Energy Function]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`**
**Define a Boltzmann Machine. State the role of the energy function in determining the probability of a state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Boltzmann Machine is a stochastic, undirected, energy-based neural network consisting of binary visible and hidden units connected by symmetric weights, where the probability of a joint state `x = (v, h)` is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(x) = \frac{e^{-E(x)}}{Z}
$$

The energy function `E(x)` plays a **central role** by mapping every configuration of the network to a scalar value. Configurations with **lower energy** are assigned **higher probability**. The energy function therefore shapes the *landscape* the network samples from – learning amounts to sculpting this landscape so that training-data configurations sit in low-energy basins. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`**
**Differentiate between a general Boltzmann Machine (BM) and a Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | General BM | RBM |
|---|---|---|
| Layer connectivity | Fully connected (v–v, h–h, v–h) | Bipartite (only v–h) |
| Conditional independence | Hidden units are NOT independent given visible | Hidden units ARE conditionally independent given visible |
| Inference | Intractable | Tractable via block Gibbs sampling |
| Practical use | Mostly theoretical | Widely used (e.g., DBN, recommender systems) |

The removal of intra-layer connections in the RBM is the single change that makes practical training feasible. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Part-B questions carry 14 marks and typically allow an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Below we present two alternative 14-mark questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) – `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**(a) Derive the energy function and joint probability distribution of a Restricted Boltzmann Machine. Show that the hidden units are conditionally independent given the visible units.**  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Energy function definition.**
For binary units `v_i ∈ {0,1}` and `h_j ∈ {0,1}`, the energy of state `(v, h)` in an RBM is:

$$
E(v, h) = -\sum_{i} a_i v_i - \sum_{j} b_j h_j - \sum_{i} \sum_{j} v_i w_{ij} h_j
$$

Matrix form: `E(v, h) = -aᵀv - bᵀh - vᵀWh`. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 – Joint probability.**
Using the Boltzmann distribution:

$$
P(v, h) = \frac{e^{-E(v, h)}}{Z} = \frac{e^{a^{\top} v + b^{\top} h + v^{\top} W h}}{Z}
$$

with `Z = Σ_{v,h} e^{-E(v,h)}`. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 – Factorization exploiting bipartite structure.**
Because there are no hidden-hidden or visible-visible links, `P(v, h)` factorises as:

$$
P(v, h) = \frac{1}{Z} e^{a^{\top} v} \prod_{j} e^{b_j h_j + h_j \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 – Conditional independence of hidden units.**
The conditional probability of hidden vector `h` given `v` is:

$$
P(h \mid v) = \frac{P(v, h)}{P(v)} = \prod_{j} \frac{e^{b_j h_j + h_j \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}}}{1 + e^{b_j + \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}}}
$$

This is a product of terms involving **only** `h_j` (not `h_k, k ≠ j`). Hence hidden units are **conditionally independent given `v`**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 – Closed-form sigmoid.**
Each factor reduces to a sigmoid:

$$
P(h_j = 1 \mid v) = \sigma\!\left(b_j + \sum_{i} v_i w_{ij}\right)
$$

**(2 marks)** — Total: **7 marks**

---

**(b) Explain the Contrastive Divergence (CD-1) algorithm for training an RBM. State the weight update rule.**  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Motivation.** The exact log-likelihood gradient requires a sample from the model distribution at equilibrium, which is computationally infeasible. Hinton (2002) proposed **Contrastive Divergence (CD)** as a biased-but-cheap approximation. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 – Positive phase.** Start from a training sample `v⁰` and sample hidden states `h⁰` from `P(h \mid v⁰)`. Compute the data correlation `⟨v_i h_j⟩_data = v⁰_i h⁰_j`. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 – Negative phase (one Gibbs step for CD-1).** Reconstruct visible states `v¹` from `P(v \mid h⁰)`, then re-sample `h¹` from `P(h \mid v¹)`. Use `v¹_i h¹_j` as the model expectation `⟨v_i h_j⟩_model`. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 – Weight update rule.**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \varepsilon \left( v^{0}_i h^{0}_j - v^{1}_i h^{1}_j \right)
$$

Similarly, bias updates:

$$
\Delta a_i = \varepsilon (v^{0}_i - v^{1}_i), \quad \Delta b_j = \varepsilon (h^{0}_j - h^{1}_j)
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 – Algorithmic steps.**
1. Initialise `W, a, b` (small random values).
2. For each training example `v⁰`:
   - Sample `h⁰ ~ P(h \mid v⁰)`.
   - Sample `v¹ ~ P(v \mid h⁰)`.
   - Sample `h¹ ~ P(h \mid v¹)`.
3. Apply updates.
4. Repeat over epochs (with mini-batches). **(1 mark)** — Total: **7 marks**

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) – `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` – Alternative Choice

**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of a Restricted Boltzmann Machine. Why is the bipartite restriction important for training?**  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Architecture.** An RBM has two layers: a **visible layer** `v` (`n_v` units) and a **hidden layer** `h` (`n_h` units). Every visible unit is connected to every hidden unit through a symmetric weight `w_ij`. There are **no** visible-to-visible or hidden-to-hidden connections. **(2 marks)** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Step 2 – Energy and probability.**

$$
E(v, h) = -a^{\top} v - b^{\top} h - v^{\top} W h, \quad P(v, h) = \frac{e^{-E(v, h)}}{Z}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 – Importance of bipartite restriction.**
- **Conditional independence:** Given `v`, all hidden units are independent (and vice versa), allowing **parallel/block sampling** in a single matrix multiplication.
- **Tractable inference:** In a full BM, the expectation `⟨v_i h_j⟩_model` requires running a Markov chain to equilibrium over `2^(n_v+n_h)` states – intractable.
- **Exact conditional updates:** Both `P(h \mid v)` and `P(v \mid h)` factorise into closed-form sigmoids. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 – Practical benefit.** This single restriction is what made RBMs trainable on real datasets and enabled the rise of **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)** for unsupervised pre-training of deep networks. **(1 mark)** — Total: **7 marks**

---

**(b) Derive the maximum-likelihood learning rule for a general Boltzmann Machine. Discuss the difficulty of computing the negative-phase term and how Contrastive Divergence overcomes it.**  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Log-likelihood.** For a single training sample `v`:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \ln P(v) = \ln \sum_{h} e^{-E(v,h)} - \ln Z
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 – Gradient w.r.t. `w_ij`.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}} = -\sum_{h} P(h \mid v) \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} + \sum_{v,h} P(v,h) \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}
$$

Using `∂E/∂w_ij = -v_i h_j`:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 – Update rule.**

$$
\Delta w_{ij} = \varepsilon \left( \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}} \right)
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 – Difficulty of the negative phase.** Computing `⟨v_i h_j⟩_model` requires samples from the model distribution, which itself needs the intractable partition function `Z`. In practice, one resorts to MCMC (e.g., Gibbs sampling) and must run the chain to **equilibrium** — a process that is extremely slow and prone to poor mixing in high-dimensional spaces. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 – CD-k solution.** Hinton's Contrastive Divergence approximates the negative phase with a short-run Markov chain (typically `k = 1` step) initialised from the data. Although biased, the estimator has **low variance** and gives useful learning signals. Combined with mini-batching, CD-1 made RBM training practical on datasets like MNIST. **(2 marks)** — Total: **7 marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the energy function. Many students write `+vᵀWh` instead of `-vᵀWh`. The energy function is **always** defined with a leading minus sign so that positively-weighted co-activations lower the energy.
> 2. **Confusing the two phases.** The *positive phase* is computed from the **data**, and the *negative phase* from the **model's fantasy samples**. Inverting these will produce the *opposite* of correct learning.
> 3. **Omitting the partition function `Z`.** Even when writing `P(v,h) = e^(-E)/Z`, students often forget to define `Z`. KTU examiners **expect** the formula `Z = Σ_{v,h} e^(-E(v,h))`.
> 4. **Writing `P(h_j=1|v)` without the sigmoid.** A bare linear expression loses 1 mark. The correct form is the **sigmoid** of the net input.
> 5. **Treating RBM and BM interchangeably.** When the question says "Restricted", do **not** mention visible-visible or hidden-hidden connections. When it says "general" or "Boltzmann Machine" (no qualifier), you **must** mention full connectivity.
> 6. **Stating the MLE gradient without derivation.** KTU's 14-mark questions almost always require you to **derive** `∂ ln P(v)/∂w_ij`, not just write it down.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Points for Boltzmann Machines:**

- **Boltzmann Machine (BM)** = stochastic, symmetric, undirected, energy-based neural network with binary units.
- **Restricted BM (RBM)** = bipartite (no intra-layer connections) ⇒ tractable inference.
- **Energy function:** `E(v, h) = -aᵀv - bᵀh - vᵀWh` — lower energy ⇒ higher probability.
- **Joint probability:** `P(v, h) = e^{-E(v, h)} / Z`, where `Z` is the intractable partition function.
- **Conditional activations (RBM):** `P(h_j = 1 \vert v) = σ(b_j + Σ_i v_i w_ij)` and symmetrically for `P(v_i = 1 \vert h)`.
- **MLE gradient:** `∂ ln P(v)/∂w_ij = ⟨v_i h_j⟩_data - ⟨v_i h_j⟩_model` (positive minus negative phase).
- **Contrastive Divergence (CD-k):** short-run MCMC starting from data — practically, CD-1 with `Δw_ij = ε(v⁰_i h⁰_j - v¹_i h¹_j)`.
- **Symmetric weights are essential** — required for the equilibrium Boltzmann distribution to exist.
- **Hidden units of an RBM are conditionally independent** given the visible layer (and vice versa) — this is the key tractability property.
- **Positive phase** = lower energy of data; **Negative phase** = raise energy of model samples.
- **Applications:** collaborative filtering, Deep Belief Networks, feature learning, generative pre-training, anomaly detection.
- **Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM)** = multiple stacked RBMs; trains via greedy layer-wise pre-training.
- **Practical training trick:** use **mini-batches**, **weight decay**, and a **small learning rate** (e.g., `ε = 0.01`) to stabilise CD-k updates.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Restricted Boltzmann Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Restricted Boltzmann Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM)** is a two-layer, bipartite, stochastic generative neural network that learns a probability distribution over its inputs. It is a restricted variant of the general Boltzmann Machine in which the restriction imposed is that there are **no intra-layer connections** — i.e., no connections between units within the same layer. The two layers are:

1. **Visible Layer ($\mathbf{v}$)** — Represents the observed/input data.
2. **Hidden Layer ($\mathbf{h}$)** — Captures latent features and dependencies in the data.

Connections exist **only** between the visible and hidden layers, forming a bipartite undirected graphical model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> RBMs form the foundational building block of **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)** and **Deep Boltzmann Machines (DBMs)**. They are energy-based models and are trained using the **Contrastive Divergence (CD-k)** algorithm — a key 14-mark exam favorite.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Movie-Rating Orchestra**
> 
> Imagine you are a music streaming service trying to predict whether a user will like a song. You don't know their hidden preferences (e.g., liking upbeat tempos, certain artists, acoustic guitar), but you observe their listening behavior. 
> 
> An **RBM works exactly like this**:
> - The **visible layer** = the songs they listened to (observed data).
> - The **hidden layer** = hidden musical preferences (latent features).
> - The **weights ($W$)** = how strongly each song activates each preference.
> - When you show the RBM a set of songs, the hidden units "vote" based on weighted connections, and the RBM learns to reconstruct the visible layer from hidden activations (and vice versa).
> 
> Unlike a standard Boltzmann Machine where every neuron talks to every other neuron (creating chaotic dependencies), in an RBM, all visible units are **independent given the hidden layer**, and vice versa. This makes learning dramatically faster (parallel sampling, tractable inference).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters
- **$m$** = Number of visible units (dimensionality of input).
- **$n$** = Number of hidden units (chosen by designer).
- **$W \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$** = Symmetric weight matrix between layers.
- **$\mathbf{a} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$** = Visible layer bias vector.
- **$\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$** = Hidden layer bias vector.
- **$Z$** = Partition function (normalizing constant) — usually intractable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from a General Boltzmann Machine:** A general Boltzmann Machine allows connections within layers, making inference exponentially expensive. The RBM's restriction makes **conditional distributions factorize**, enabling efficient **block Gibbs sampling**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an RBM

#### Step 1: Energy Function
Every joint configuration $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ is assigned a scalar **energy**:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$$

- **Why?** Low energy = more probable configuration. The energy function is the core of an **Energy-Based Model (EBM)**.

#### Step 2: Joint Probability Distribution
Using the Boltzmann/Gibbs distribution:

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z} = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{\sum_{\mathbf{v}'}\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}', \mathbf{h}')}}$$

- **Why?** The exponential ensures positivity, and $Z$ ensures probabilities sum to 1.
- **Problem:** $Z$ is **intractable** for non-trivial sizes because it sums over all $2^{m+n}$ configurations.

#### Step 3: Marginal Distribution over Visible Units
The probability of observing an input vector $\mathbf{v}$:

$$P(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

This is what the RBM tries to maximize during training (log-likelihood maximization).

#### Step 4: Conditional Independence (The "Restriction")
The bipartite structure gives us **factorized conditional distributions**:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(h_{j} \mid \mathbf{v})$$

$$P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(v_{i} \mid \mathbf{h})$$

For binary units, each conditional is a **logistic sigmoid**:

$$P(h_{j} = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(b_{j} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} v_{i}W_{ij}\right)$$

$$P(v_{i} = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(a_{i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} h_{j}W_{ij}\right)$$

where $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ is the sigmoid activation.

- **Why?** Because there are no intra-layer connections, all hidden units become independent given $\mathbf{v}$ — enabling **parallel sampling**.

#### Step 5: Training Objective — Maximum Likelihood
The model parameters $\theta = \{W, \mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}\}$ are learned by maximizing:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)} \mid \theta)$$

The gradient of the log-likelihood takes a beautiful form:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{model}}$$

- **$\langle \cdot \rangle_{\text{data}}$** = expectation under the data-driven distribution $P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})\,\delta(\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v}_{\text{train}})$.
- **$\langle \cdot \rangle_{\text{model}}$** = expectation under the model distribution $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ — **intractable** to compute exactly.

#### Step 6: Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) — The KTU Hot Topic
**Contrastive Divergence (CD-k)** is an approximate, efficient learning algorithm proposed by Hinton (2002) that replaces the intractable $\langle \cdot \rangle_{\text{model}}$ with samples obtained by running **k steps of Gibbs sampling** starting from training data.

$$\Delta W_{ij} = \epsilon\!\left(\langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{recon}}\right)$$

$$\Delta a_{i} = \epsilon\!\left(\langle v_{i} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i} \rangle_{\text{recon}}\right)$$

$$\Delta b_{j} = \epsilon\!\left(\langle h_{j} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle h_{j} \rangle_{\text{recon}}\right)$$

- **Why CD-k?** Exact MCMC requires many steps to mix; in practice, **k=1** often works surprisingly well and is computationally cheap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** RBMs are widely used in:
> - **Dimensionality reduction** (nonlinear PCA).
> - **Collaborative filtering** (the original Netflix Prize winner used RBMs).
> - **Feature learning** for deep networks (pre-training).
> - **Topic modelling** and **speech recognition**.
> - **Image reconstruction** and denoising.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Purpose / Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Function $E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})$ | $-\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$ | Defines configuration cost |
| Joint Probability $P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})$ | $e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}/Z$ | Boltzmann distribution |
| Partition Function $Z$ | $\sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}$ | Normalization (intractable) |
| Marginal $P(\mathbf{v})$ | $\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}/Z$ | Input reconstruction probability |
| $P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ | $\sigma(b_{j} + \sum_{i} v_{i}W_{ij})$ | Hidden unit activation (sigmoid) |
| $P(v_{i}=1 \mid \mathbf{h})$ | $\sigma(a_{i} + \sum_{j} h_{j}W_{ij})$ | Visible reconstruction (sigmoid) |
| Free Energy $F(\mathbf{v})$ | $-\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \sum_{j}\log(1+e^{b_{j}+\mathbf{v}^{T}W_{:j}})$ | Tractable surrogate of $-\log P(\mathbf{v})$ |
| Log-Likelihood Gradient | $\langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{\text{model}}$ | Parameter update direction |
| CD-k Update Rule | $\Delta W_{ij} = \epsilon(\langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{k})$ | Approximate MLE training |
| Sigmoid Function $\sigma(x)$ | $1/(1+e^{-x})$ | Activation / probability mapping |
| Contrastive Term | "Positive phase" minus "Negative phase" | Biased gradient estimate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Conditional Probability $P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v})$

We derive the activation probability for a single hidden unit given the visible vector. Recall the energy function:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\sum_{i} a_{i}v_{i} - \sum_{j} b_{j}h_{j} - \sum_{i,j} v_{i}W_{ij}h_{j}$$

**Step 1:** Write the conditional probability using Bayes' rule:

$$P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{P(h_{j}=1, \mathbf{v})}{P(h_{j}=1, \mathbf{v}) + P(h_{j}=0, \mathbf{v})}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute using $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) \propto e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}$. For fixed $\mathbf{v}$ and $h_{j}$, the terms not involving $h_{j}$ cancel in the ratio:

$$P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=1)}}{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=1)} + e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=0)}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the energy difference. Setting $h_{j}=1$ vs $h_{j}=0$ changes only the terms involving $h_{j}$:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=1) = -\sum_{i} a_{i}v_{i} - b_{j} - \sum_{i} v_{i}W_{ij}$$

$$E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=0) = -\sum_{i} a_{i}v_{i} - 0 - 0$$

**Step 4:** The difference in negative energy (logit) is:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=0) - E(\mathbf{v}, h_{j}=1) = b_{j} + \sum_{i} v_{i}W_{ij}$$

**Step 5:** Applying the logistic sigmoid:

$$P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(b_{j} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} v_{i}W_{ij}\right)$$

**Conclusion:** The hidden unit activation is a sigmoid of the weighted sum of visible activations plus bias — analogous to a standard neuron, but with a probabilistic (stochastic) output.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Likelihood Gradient (CD Foundation)

We want to maximize $\log P(\mathbf{v}) = \log\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})} - \log Z$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate the first term (with respect to $W_{ij}$, keeping $\mathbf{v}$ fixed):

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial W_{ij}} \log\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})} = -\frac{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})} \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}}$$

**Step 2:** Since $\frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}} = -v_{i}h_{j}$:

$$= \frac{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})} v_{i}h_{j}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}} = \sum_{\mathbf{h}} h_{j}\,P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})\,v_{i} = \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})}$$

This is the **positive phase** (data-driven) expectation.

**Step 3:** Differentiate $-\log Z$. By the quotient rule:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial W_{ij}} \log Z = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})} v_{i}h_{j} = \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}$$

This is the **negative phase** (model-driven) expectation.

**Step 4:** Combine both phases:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{model}}$$

**Conclusion:** The gradient is the **difference between two correlations** — one from data and one from the model's own imagination. The first term is easy (clamp $\mathbf{v}$ to data, sample $\mathbf{h}$). The second is hard — leading us to **Contrastive Divergence**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Contrastive Divergence (CD-1) Update

**Step 1:** Approximate the intractable $\langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{model}}$ with a sample obtained by one step of Gibbs sampling starting from the training example $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$:

$$\mathbf{h}^{(0)} \sim P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(0)}),\quad \mathbf{v}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}^{(0)}),\quad \mathbf{h}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(1)})$$

**Step 2:** Replace expectations with these samples:

$$\Delta W_{ij} \approx \epsilon\left(v_{i}^{(0)}h_{j}^{(0)} - v_{i}^{(1)}h_{j}^{(1)}\right)$$

**Step 3:** This produces a **biased but low-variance** estimate of the gradient. Hinton showed that the bias becomes negligible in the limit of small $\epsilon$ (learning rate).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: A Toy 2-Visible, 1-Hidden RBM

Consider an RBM with $m=2$ visible units and $n=1$ hidden unit. Let:

$$W = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{a} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},\quad b = 0$$

**Task:** Compute $P(h=1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ for $\mathbf{v} = (1, 0)^{T}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the activation:

$$b + v_{1}W_{11} + v_{2}W_{21} = 0 + (1)(1) + (0)(-1) = 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply sigmoid:

$$P(h=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(1) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-1}} \approx 0.7311$$

**Step 3:** Compute $P(v_{1}=1 \mid h=1)$:

$$a_{1} + hW_{11} = 0 + (1)(1) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma(1) \approx 0.7311$$

**Step 4:** Energy of the configuration $\mathbf{v}=(1,0), h=1$:

$$E = -(0)(1) - (0)(0) - (1)(1)(1) - (0)(-1)(1) = -1$$

**Step 5:** Energy of $\mathbf{v}=(1,0), h=0$:

$$E = 0$$

**Step 6:** The ratio:

$$\frac{P(h=1 \mid \mathbf{v})}{P(h=0 \mid \mathbf{v})} = e^{-(E_{h=1} - E_{h=0})} = e^{1} \approx 2.718$$

This confirms that the configuration with $h=1$ is roughly 2.7× more likely than $h=0$ for this input — consistent with the sigmoid result.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Step** | **Operation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Intuition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample $\mathbf{h}$ from $\mathbf{v}$ | $P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v})=\sigma(b_{j}+\mathbf{v}^{T}W_{:j})$ | Infer hidden features |
| 2 | Reconstruct $\mathbf{v}'$ from $\mathbf{h}$ | $P(v_{i}=1 \mid \mathbf{h})=\sigma(a_{i}+W_{i:}\mathbf{h})$ | Generate visible pattern |
| 3 | Compute positive correlation | $\langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{\text{data}}$ | "Wake" phase |
| 4 | Compute negative correlation | $\langle v_{i}h_{j}\rangle_{\text{model}}$ | "Sleep" phase |
| 5 | Apply CD-k update | $\Delta W = \epsilon(\text{pos} - \text{neg})$ | Learn weights |
| 6 | Iterate over mini-batches | SGD / mini-batch CD | Converge to local optimum |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RBM Architecture (Bipartite Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1[Visible Unit v1] --- W11[Edge W11]
    v2[Visible Unit v2] --- W12[Edge W12]
    v3[Visible Unit v3] --- W13[Edge W13]
    v1 --- W21[Edge W21]
    v2 --- W22[Edge W22]
    v3 --- W23[Edge W23]
    h1[Hidden Unit h1] --- W11
    h1 --- W12
    h1 --- W13
    h2[Hidden Unit h2] --- W21
    h2 --- W22
    h2 --- W23
    a1[Bias a1] -.-> v1
    a2[Bias a2] -.-> v2
    a3[Bias a3] -.-> v3
    b1[Bias b1] -.-> h1
    b2[Bias b2] -.-> h2
```

> **Note:** Notice that there are **no edges between $v_1, v_2, v_3$** and **no edges between $h_1, h_2$** — this is the **restriction** that defines the RBM.

---

### 4.2 Contrastive Divergence (CD-1) Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Input training sample v0] --> B[Positive Phase: Sample h0 from P h given v0]
    B --> C[Compute positive correlation v0 times h0]
    C --> D[Negative Phase: Sample v1 from P v given h0]
    D --> E[Sample h1 from P h given v1]
    E --> F[Compute negative correlation v1 times h1]
    F --> G[Update weights: delta W equals epsilon times v0h0 minus v1h1]
    G --> H[Update biases a and b using sample averages]
    H --> I{Max iterations reached?}
    I -- No --> A
    I -- Yes --> J[Output trained RBM parameters]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Input_Layer
        V[Visible Layer v in R to the m]
    end
    subgraph Hidden_Layer
        H[Hidden Layer h in R to the n]
    end
    subgraph Energy_Module
        E1[Energy Function E of v h]
        P1[Probability P of v h = exp of minus E over Z]
    end
    subgraph Training_Module
        CD[Contrastive Divergence CD-k]
        GD[Gradient Update delta W delta a delta b]
    end
    subgraph Output_Module
        R[Reconstruction of v]
        F[Feature Representation h]
    end
    V --> E1
    H --> E1
    E1 --> P1
    P1 --> CD
    V --> CD
    H --> CD
    CD --> GD
    GD --> H
    GD --> V
    P1 --> R
    H --> F
```

---

### 4.4 RBM vs. General Boltzmann Machine — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph General_BM
        vA[Visible v1] --- hA[Hidden h1]
        vA --- hB[Hidden h2]
        vB[Visible v2] --- hA
        vB --- hB
        vA --- vB
        hA --- hB
    end
    subgraph RBM
        v1R[Visible v1] --- h1R[Hidden h1]
        v1R --- h2R[Hidden h2]
        v2R[Visible v2] --- h1R
        v2R --- h2R
    end
```

> **RBM vs. Boltzmann Machine:** The general Boltzmann Machine (left) has intra-layer connections (red arrows), while the RBM (right) restricts connections to inter-layer only — making inference tractable.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define a Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM). What is the "restriction" that makes it different from a general Boltzmann Machine?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) An **RBM** is a two-layer, stochastic, generative neural network consisting of a visible layer $\mathbf{v}$ and a hidden layer $\mathbf{h}$ that learns a probability distribution over its inputs using an energy-based framework.
- (1 Mark) The "restriction" is that there are **no intra-layer connections** — i.e., no edges between visible-to-visible or hidden-to-hidden units. Connections exist only between the visible and hidden layers (bipartite structure).
- (1 Mark) This restriction makes the **conditional distributions factorize**, enabling efficient block Gibbs sampling and tractable inference. The general Boltzmann Machine lacks this restriction, making inference exponentially expensive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Write the energy function of an RBM and explain each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$$
- (1 Mark) $-\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v}$: contribution from **visible biases** $\mathbf{a}$ that encode the prior over visible units.
- (1 Mark) $-\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h}$: contribution from **hidden biases** $\mathbf{b}$ that encode the prior over hidden units.
- (1 Mark) $-\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$: the **interaction term** encoding the symmetric weights $W$ between visible and hidden units. Lower energy = more probable configuration.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the conditional probability $P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ for a binary RBM. Show clearly how the bipartite restriction simplifies the computation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) algorithm for training an RBM. Why is it preferred over exact maximum likelihood? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ (7 Marks):**

Given the energy function:
$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\sum_{i} a_{i}v_{i} - \sum_{j} b_{j}h_{j} - \sum_{i,j} v_{i}W_{ij}h_{j}$$

The conditional probability is:
$$P(h_{j} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{P(h_{j}, \mathbf{v})}{P(\mathbf{v})} = \frac{e^{-E(h_{j}, \mathbf{v})}}{\sum_{h_{j} \in \{0,1\}} e^{-E(h_{j}, \mathbf{v})}}$$

Since $h_{j}$ is binary, the denominator has only two terms. The terms not involving $h_{j}$ cancel:

$$P(h_{j}=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\left[b_{j} + \sum_{i} v_{i}W_{ij}\right]}} = \sigma\!\left(b_{j} + \sum_{i} v_{i}W_{ij}\right)$$

- **(2 Marks)** The bipartite restriction implies that **all hidden units are conditionally independent given $\mathbf{v}$**:
$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} P(h_{j} \mid \mathbf{v})$$
- **(1 Mark)** This allows **parallel sampling** of all hidden units — a key computational advantage.
- **(2 Marks)** Symmetrically, $P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}) = \prod_{i} P(v_{i} \mid \mathbf{h})$.
- **(1 Mark)** The sigmoid emerges naturally from the exponential of the negative energy, making the RBM a **log-linear model** in the energies.
- **(1 Mark)** Conclusion: Inference becomes a simple weighted sum followed by a sigmoid, just like a standard neural network layer.

---

**(b) Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) Algorithm (7 Marks):**

- **(2 Marks)** The exact log-likelihood gradient is:
$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_{i}h_{j} \rangle_{\text{model}}$$
The **negative phase** expectation requires samples from the model distribution $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$, which is **intractable** (requires summing over $2^{m+n}$ states).
- **(2 Marks)** **CD-k Procedure** (Hinton, 2002):
  1. Take a training sample $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$.
  2. Sample $\mathbf{h}^{(0)} \sim P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(0)})$.
  3. Sample $\mathbf{v}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}^{(0)})$ (reconstruction).
  4. Sample $\mathbf{h}^{(1)} \sim P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(1)})$.
  5. Repeat for $k$ steps (k=1 works in practice).
- **(1 Mark)** Approximate the gradient using these samples:
$$\Delta W_{ij} \approx \epsilon\left(v_{i}^{(0)}h_{j}^{(0)} - v_{i}^{(1)}h_{j}^{(1)}\right)$$
- **(2 Marks)** **Why preferred over exact MLE:**
  - Computationally cheap (single or few Gibbs steps).
  - Produces a **biased but low-variance** estimate.
  - Biasedness vanishes as learning rate $\epsilon \to 0$.
  - Empirically converges to good local optima.
  - Scales to large models (millions of parameters).
  - Eliminates the need to compute the partition function $Z$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **partition function $Z$** in RBMs. Why is it intractable, and what approximation strategies are used? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **RBMs**, **Deep Belief Networks (DBNs)**, and **Deep Boltzmann Machines (DBMs)** in terms of architecture and training procedure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Partition Function and Its Intractability (7 Marks):**
- **(2 Marks)** The partition function $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}$ normalizes the joint distribution so that $P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = e^{-E}/Z$ integrates (sums) to 1.
- **(2 Marks)** It is **intractable** because the sum runs over $2^{m+n}$ possible configurations. For example, an RBM with $m=784$ and $n=500$ has $2^{1284}$ terms — astronomically large.
- **(1 Mark)** $Z$ appears in $P(\mathbf{v})$ and the gradient, making exact maximum likelihood infeasible.
- **(2 Marks)** **Approximation strategies:**
  - **Contrastive Divergence (CD-k)** — uses short MCMC chains.
  - **Persistent Contrastive Divergence (PCD)** — maintains a persistent chain across updates (Tieleman, 2008).
  - **Parallel Tempering (PT)** — runs multiple chains at different temperatures.
  - **Annealed Importance Sampling (AIS)** — for estimating the log-likelihood.
  - **Score Matching** and **Noise-Contrastive Estimation (NCE)** — bypass $Z$ altogether.

---

**(b) RBM vs. DBN vs. DBM (7 Marks):**
- **(2 Marks)** **RBM:** Two-layer bipartite model. Generative joint distribution over $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{h}$. Trained via CD-k.
- **(2 Marks)** **Deep Belief Network (DBN):** Stack of RBMs trained greedily layer-by-layer. The top two layers form an undirected RBM, while lower layers form directed sigmoid belief networks. Trained via **greedy layer-wise pre-training** + fine-tuning.
- **(2 Marks)** **Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM):** Stack of RBMs where **all connections are undirected**. More expressive than DBN but harder to train (requires variational inference + MCMC). Trained via **joint CD** or **mean-field + MCMC** approaches.
- **(1 Mark)** **Key trade-off:** DBNs are computationally efficient but approximate; DBMs capture richer dependencies but are slower; RBMs form the foundational building block of both.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RBM with autoencoders:** RBMs are **stochastic generative models**, not deterministic reconstructive ones. Do not write $\mathbf{v}' = f(\mathbf{v})$.
> 2. **Forgetting the restriction:** Always emphasize that RBMs have **no intra-layer connections**. This is the single most important architectural detail.
> 3. **Missing the energy function:** Examiners love asking for the energy function and its expansion. Memorize $E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$.
> 4. **Confusing CD-k with exact MLE:** CD-k is an **approximation**, not exact maximum likelihood. It is biased but practical.
> 5. **Skipping the Gibbs sampling steps:** When writing CD-k, explicitly show the alternating sampling: $\mathbf{v} \to \mathbf{h} \to \mathbf{v}' \to \mathbf{h}'$.
> 6. **Omitting the partition function $Z$:** Always acknowledge $Z$ and explain its intractability when discussing probability.
> 7. **Not stating conditional independence:** A 14-mark answer without the factorization $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}) = \prod_j P(h_j\mid\mathbf{v})$ will be marked down.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RBM = Restricted Boltzmann Machine** — two-layer (visible + hidden), bipartite, energy-based stochastic network.
- **Restriction:** **No intra-layer connections** — only inter-layer symmetric weights $W$.
- **Energy Function:** $E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$.
- **Joint Probability:** $P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) = e^{-E(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}/Z$.
- **Partition Function $Z$:** Intractable (sums over $2^{m+n}$ states).
- **Conditional Independence:** $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v}) = \prod_j \sigma(b_j + \mathbf{v}^{T}W_{:j})$.
- **Sigmoid Activations:** $P(h_j=1\mid\mathbf{v}) = \sigma(b_j + \sum_i v_iW_{ij})$.
- **Log-Likelihood Gradient:** Positive phase (data) − Negative phase (model).
- **CD-k Algorithm:** Approximate MLE using $k$ steps of Gibbs sampling starting from training data.
- **CD-1 Update Rule:** $\Delta W_{ij} = \epsilon(v_i^{(0)}h_j^{(0)} - v_i^{(k)}h_j^{(k)})$.
- **Why CD-k works:** Biased but low-variance; bias vanishes as $\epsilon \to 0$.
- **Gibbs Sampling:** Alternates between $P(\mathbf{h}\mid\mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v}\mid\mathbf{h})$ due to bipartite structure.
- **Building Block:** RBMs stack to form DBNs (top-down directed) and DBMs (fully undirected).
- **Applications:** Collaborative filtering (Netflix), dimensionality reduction, pre-training deep networks, topic modeling, image reconstruction.
- **Free Energy:** $F(\mathbf{v}) = -\mathbf{a}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \sum_j \log(1 + e^{b_j + \mathbf{v}^{T}W_{:j}})$ — tractable surrogate for $-\log P(\mathbf{v})$.
- **Sigmoid Function:** $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$ — fundamental activation for binary RBMs.
- **Param Count:** $W$ (mn) + $\mathbf{a}$ (m) + $\mathbf{b}$ (n) — total $\mathcal{O}(mn)$ parameters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning: Reinforcement Learning, RL Process, Reward Hypothesis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deep Reinforcement Learning: Reinforcement Learning, RL Process, Reward Hypothesis

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Reinforcement Learning (RL)?

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a sub-field of Machine Learning concerned with how an intelligent **agent** ought to take **actions** in an **environment** to maximize the notion of cumulative **reward**. Unlike supervised learning, RL does not require labelled input/output pairs; instead, it learns from interaction by trial-and-error, using a scalar reward signal as feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Sutton & Barto, KTU Standard):**
> Reinforcement Learning is the science of decision-making — learning the optimal *policy* $\pi(a \vert s)$ that maps states to actions in order to maximize a long-term scalar objective known as the *expected return*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toddler Learning to Walk"

Imagine teaching a toddler to walk. You do not hand the toddler a dataset of "correct leg angles." You simply **let the toddler try**, and:
- **Reward** when the toddler takes a step forward (clap, smile).
- **Penalty** when the toddler falls (no applause, mild discomfort).

Over thousands of falls, the toddler's brain builds an internal **policy** ("if my left leg is bent and tilted, push off with right toes"). This is precisely how an RL agent learns — by interacting with the environment, observing consequences, and gradually refining its action-selection strategy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of RL (Memorize This):**
> 1. **Agent** — the learner / decision-maker (e.g., robot, game AI).
> 2. **Environment** — everything the agent interacts with (e.g., maze, game world).
> 3. **Reward Signal** — the numerical feedback ($r_t \in \mathbb{R}$) received after each action.

---

## 1.3 The Reward Hypothesis

The **Reward Hypothesis** is the foundational assumption upon which the entire field of Reinforcement Learning rests. Without this hypothesis, the mathematical formulation of RL would collapse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reward Hypothesis (Formal Statement):**
> *"All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected cumulative scalar reward signal."*

In simpler terms: **every objective** an agent might pursue — winning a chess match, balancing a pole, flying a drone, or driving a car — can be encoded as a single number (a reward) that the agent must maximize over time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Line:**
> "Intelligence, in the RL paradigm, is defined as the ability to select actions that maximize expected cumulative reward."

### 1.3.1 Why the Reward Hypothesis Matters

- It transforms diverse, high-level objectives into a **single, well-defined optimization problem**.
- It enables a unified mathematical framework (Markov Decision Processes) for ALL decision-making tasks.
- It allows the use of standard optimization techniques (gradient ascent, dynamic programming, etc.).

### 1.3.2 Limitations of the Reward Hypothesis

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often forget that the reward hypothesis is an *assumption*, not a theorem. It does not hold universally. Examples where it struggles:
> - Goals requiring multi-modal reasoning (vision + language).
> - Tasks where the reward is hard to specify (alignment in LLMs).
> - Sparse-reward environments where feedback is rare.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RL Process — Formal Anatomy

The RL process is a **continuous feedback loop** between the agent and environment, formally described by a discrete-time sequence:

$$t = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

At each time step $t$, the following occurs:

1. The agent **observes** the environment's current state $S_t$.
2. Based on $S_t$, the agent **executes an action** $A_t$ drawn from its policy $\pi$.
3. The environment transitions to a **new state** $S_{t+1}$.
4. The environment emits a **reward** $R_{t+1}$.
5. The cycle repeats.

This loop produces a **trajectory** (or *rollout*):

$$S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, R_2, S_2, A_2, R_3, \dots$$

## 2.2 Key Components — Definitions Table

| Component | Symbol | Mathematical Form | Intuitive Meaning |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| State | $S_t$ | $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$ | A snapshot of the environment at time $t$. |
| Action | $A_t$ | $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(s)$ | The agent's choice given state $s$. |
| Reward | $R_t$ | $R_t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Numerical feedback signal. |
| Policy | $\pi(a \vert s)$ | $\pi : \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{A} \to [0,1]$ | Agent's action-selection strategy. |
| Return (Reward-to-Go) | $G_t$ | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Total discounted future reward. |
| State-value function | $V_\pi(s)$ | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$ | How good is state $s$ under policy $\pi$? |
| Action-value function | $Q_\pi(s,a)$ | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$ | How good is action $a$ in state $s$? |

## 2.3 The Discount Factor $\gamma$ — Why Discount Future Rewards?

The discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is one of the most important hyper-parameters in RL.

- **If $\gamma = 0$:** Agent is *myopic* — only cares about immediate reward $R_{t+1}$.
- **If $\gamma = 1$:** Agent is *far-sighted* — treats future and present rewards equally.
- **If $\gamma \to 0$:** Agent becomes greedy / short-termist.
- **If $\gamma \to 1$:** Agent cares deeply about the distant future.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A discount factor $\gamma < 1$ also ensures mathematical convergence of the infinite sum $G_t$ when reward values are bounded. Without discounting, $G_t$ can diverge to infinity in continuing (non-episodic) tasks.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula | Expression | Used For | Unit |
|---------|------------|----------|------|
| Return (Discounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative reward | Dimensionless (scalar) |
| Bellman Equation for $V_\pi$ | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \vert s,a)\,[r + \gamma V_\pi(s')]$ | Value function evaluation | Dimensionless (scalar) |
| Bellman Optimality | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \vert s,a)\,[r + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Optimal policy extraction | Dimensionless (scalar) |
| Q-function Bellman | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \vert s,a)\,[r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \vert s') Q_\pi(s',a')]$ | Action-value recursion | Dimensionless (scalar) |
| Exploration Probability ($\varepsilon$-greedy) | $\pi(a \vert s) = \begin{cases} 1-\varepsilon + \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert}, & a = \arg\max_a Q(s,a) \\ \frac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Action selection | Probability $[0,1]$ |
| Advantage Function | $A_\pi(s,a) = Q_\pi(s,a) - V_\pi(s)$ | Policy gradient methods | Dimensionless (scalar) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** In the table above, $\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert$ denotes the *cardinality* (size) of the action space at state $s$. It is written as `\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Exploration vs Exploitation Dilemma

A fundamental challenge in RL is balancing:
- **Exploitation:** Choose the action known to yield the highest estimated reward (greedy choice).
- **Exploration:** Try new actions to discover potentially better long-term strategies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Why can't we always pick the action with the highest Q-value?" Because the agent might be stuck in a *local optimum*, missing a globally superior strategy that requires taking a seemingly worse action first.

## 2.6 Types of RL Approaches

| Paradigm | Description | Example Algorithm |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Value-Based** | Learns $V(s)$ or $Q(s,a)$; derives policy implicitly. | Q-Learning, DQN |
| **Policy-Based** | Directly parameterizes $\pi_\theta(a \vert s)$; optimizes via gradient. | REINFORCE |
| **Actor-Critic** | Combines both: actor (policy) + critic (value). | A2C, PPO, DDPG |
| **Model-Based** | Learns environment model $p(s',r \vert s,a)$; plans with it. | AlphaZero, Dyna |
| **Model-Free** | No environment model; learns directly from interaction. | DQN, SAC |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Deep RL drives real-world systems. Examples:
> - **Robotics:** Boston Dynamics uses model-free RL for locomotion.
> - **Game AI:** AlphaGo, AlphaZero defeated world champions using DRL + MCTS.
> - **Autonomous Driving:** Waymo uses RL for lane-changing policies.
> - **Recommendation Systems:** YouTube's recommender treats video selection as an RL problem.
> - **Chip Design:** Google's TPU placement was optimized with RL (Nature, 2021).
