# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Return $G_t$ (Cumulative Discounted Reward)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

The return $G_t$ is defined as the sum of discounted rewards from time $t$ onward:

$$G_t \doteq R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1:** Write the definition explicitly.

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 R_{t+4} + \dots$$

**Step 2:** Group the terms after the first one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \bigl( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^2 R_{t+4} + \dots \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the bracketed expression equals $G_{t+1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_t &= R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This is the **recursive form of the return** — a cornerstone used in dynamic programming and bootstrap methods like TD-learning.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$

### 3.2.1 Definition Recap

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi(s) &\doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s] \\
V_\pi(s) &= \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1:** Apply the law of iterated expectations — first over actions, then over next states and rewards.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi(s) &= \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[ r + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $\mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] = V_\pi(s')$ by definition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi(s) &= \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation** for $V_\pi$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*(s)$

The optimal value function is defined as:

$$V^*(s) \doteq \max_\pi V_\pi(s)$$

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Start from Bellman expectation but select the action that maximizes return.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^*(s) &= \max_a \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[ r + \gamma V^*(s') \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding optimal policy $\pi^*$ is greedy w.r.t. $V^*$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^*(a \mid s) &= \begin{cases} 1, & a = \arg\max_a \sum_{s',r} p(s',r \mid s,a)[r + \gamma V^*(s')] \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Q-Learning Update Rule

Q-learning is an off-policy, model-free TD(0) algorithm.

### 3.4.1 Target Construction

The TD target is constructed using the Bellman optimality equation for $Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s,a) &= \mathbb{E}\bigl[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \mid s, a \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 Update Step

At each step, the Q-value is updated toward the TD target:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \Bigl[ \underbrace{r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')}_{\text{TD Target}} - Q(s,a) \Bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\alpha \in (0,1]$ is the learning rate.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Toy MDP:** Consider a 3-state grid world. Agent moves deterministically right with reward structure:
> $R(s_1)=0, R(s_2)=0, R(s_3)=+10$ (terminal), $\gamma=0.9$.

### 3.5.1 Initial Q-values (all zeros)

$$Q(s_1, \text{right}) = Q(s_2, \text{right}) = Q(s_3, \text{right}) = 0$$

### 3.5.2 First Episode: $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3$

**Step 1:** At $s_3$, terminal state: no update.

**Step 2:** Backpropagate from $s_2 \to s_3$ with reward $r=10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_2, \text{right}) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \bigl[10 + 0.9 \cdot \max_a Q(s_3, a) - 0 \bigr] \\
Q(s_2, \text{right}) &\leftarrow 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Backpropagate from $s_1 \to s_2$ with reward $r=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(s_1, \text{right}) &\leftarrow 0 + 0.1 \bigl[0 + 0.9 \cdot \max_a Q(s_2, a) - 0 \bigr] \\
Q(s_1, \text{right}) &\leftarrow 0.1 \times 0.9 \times 1.0 = 0.09
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5.3 After Many Episodes (Convergence)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^*(s_3, \text{right}) &= 0 \\
Q^*(s_2, \text{right}) &= 10 \\
Q^*(s_1, \text{right}) &= 0.9 \times 10 = 9.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition (KTU Insight):** Notice the geometric discount $0.9^1 = 0.9$ multiplying the reward. This is why $Q^*(s_1, \text{right}) = \gamma \cdot Q^*(s_2, \text{right})$ — discounting propagates reward backward through time.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Feedback Type | Labels | None | Scalar reward |
| Data Distribution | i.i.d. samples | i.i.d. samples | Sequential, correlated |
| Goal | Mimic mapping $f(x) \to y$ | Discover hidden structure | Maximize cumulative reward |
| Decision Sequence | Single-shot | Single-shot | Sequential / multi-step |
| Example | Image classification | Clustering | Game playing, robotics |
| Loss Function | Cross-entropy, MSE | Reconstruction error | Negative expected return |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Reinforcement Learning and state the Reward Hypothesis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a paradigm of machine learning in which an *agent* learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an *environment*. At each timestep $t$, the agent observes a state $S_t$, takes an action $A_t$, and receives a scalar reward $R_{t+1}$. The objective is to learn an optimal policy $\pi^*(a \vert s)$ that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted return.

**Reward Hypothesis:** *"All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected cumulative scalar reward signal."*

This hypothesis allows us to convert diverse real-world objectives into a unified mathematical optimization problem.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in RL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The discount factor $\gamma \in [0,1]$ determines the *present value* of future rewards. The return is defined as $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

Roles:
1. **Preference for immediacy:** $\gamma < 1$ makes the agent value near-term rewards more than distant ones.
2. **Mathematical convergence:** When rewards are bounded and $\gamma < 1$, the infinite sum $G_t$ converges to a finite value.
3. **Risk modelling:** Captures uncertainty about the future.

Special cases: $\gamma = 0$ ⇒ greedy agent; $\gamma \to 1$ ⇒ far-sighted agent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

#### **(a) Explain in detail the Reinforcement Learning (RL) Process. Discuss the agent-environment interaction with a proper block diagram.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The RL process is a closed-loop interaction between an agent and its environment. Formally, it is a tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, p, r, \gamma)$ where:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — set of all possible states.
- $\mathcal{A}$ — set of all possible actions.
- $p(s', r \vert s, a)$ — environment dynamics (transition + reward distribution).
- $r$ — reward function.
- $\gamma$ — discount factor.

**Steps of the RL Process:**

1. **Initialization:** The agent starts in initial state $S_0$, sampled from initial-state distribution $p(s_0)$.
2. **Perception:** At time $t$, the agent observes $S_t$.
3. **Action Selection:** Using its current policy $\pi(a \vert s)$, the agent samples action $A_t$.
4. **Environment Step:** The environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$ and emits reward $R_{t+1}$, governed by $p(s', r \vert s, a)$.
5. **Learning Update:** The agent updates its policy/value estimates using observed $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1})$ tuple.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 2–5 repeat until termination or convergence.

[Include the agent-environment block diagram from Section 4.1 here for full marks.]

The cumulative trajectory $S_0, A_0, R_1, S_1, A_1, \dots$ is called an *episode* (in episodic tasks) or *rollout*.

---

#### **(b) Define and derive the Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$. Show how the recursive return relation arises.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The state-value function $V_\pi(s)$ gives the expected return starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$:

$$V_\pi(s) \doteq \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid S_t = s]$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.

$$V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]$$

**Step 2:** Apply law of iterated expectations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi(s) &= \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[ r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the inner expectation as $V_\pi(s')$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi(s) &= \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s', r} p(s', r \mid s, a) \bigl[ r + \gamma V_\pi(s') \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation**. It expresses that the value of a state equals the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the next state — a *bootstrap* relationship fundamental to dynamic programming in RL.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

#### **(a) State and explain the Reward Hypothesis. Why is it central to RL? Discuss its limitations with examples.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** *"All goals can be described by the maximization of the expected cumulative scalar reward signal."*

**Explanation:**

The hypothesis asserts that ANY intelligent goal — winning a chess match, balancing a pole, translating a sentence — can be encoded as the agent maximizing a scalar function $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.

**Why is it central?**

1. **Unification:** Converts diverse objectives into a single optimization problem.
2. **Mathematical tractability:** Allows use of calculus, probability theory, and gradient-based optimization.
3. **Universal framework:** Enables RL algorithms to be applied across any task.
4. **Backbone of MDPs:** The Markov Decision Process framework rests on this assumption.

**Limitations:**

> [!WARNING]
> - **Reward Misspecification:** Poorly designed rewards lead to reward hacking (e.g., a boat-racing RL agent that scored points by looping in circles).
> - **Sparse Rewards:** Many real tasks have infrequent feedback, slowing learning.
> - **Multi-Objective Tasks:** A single scalar may not capture trade-offs between conflicting goals.
> - **Safety-Critical Domains:** Hard to encode "do no harm" as a single number.

---

#### **(b) Differentiate between Exploration and Exploitation. Explain the $\varepsilon$-greedy strategy with the policy equation.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Exploration | Exploitation |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Meaning | Try new actions to discover rewards. | Use known best action to gain reward. |
| Risk | Short-term loss. | Missed long-term gains. |
| Purpose | Reduces uncertainty. | Maximizes immediate reward. |

**$\varepsilon$-greedy strategy:** With probability $\varepsilon$, pick a *random* action (explore); with probability $1-\varepsilon$, pick the action with highest Q-value (exploit).

**Policy Equation:**

$$
\pi(a \mid s) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert}, & a = \arg\max_{a'} Q(s, a') \\
\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The $\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s)\vert}$ correction ensures the policy remains a *valid probability distribution* (sums to 1). The decay schedule $\varepsilon_t = \max(\varepsilon_{\min}, \varepsilon_0 \cdot \delta^t)$ is commonly used to gradually shift from exploration to exploitation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$:** When writing $G_t$, always include the $\gamma^k$ factor. Many students write $G_t = \sum R_{t+k+1}$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $V_\pi$ and $Q_\pi$:** $V_\pi(s)$ is the value of a *state*; $Q_\pi(s,a)$ is the value of a *state-action pair*. Mixed up answers are penalized.
> 3. **Stating the Reward Hypothesis without explanation:** Writing only the one-line statement without explaining *why* it matters yields only 1 of the expected 3 marks.
> 4. **Missing the block diagram in Part B:** RL process questions explicitly demand a diagram — its absence costs at least 2 marks.
> 5. **No step-by-step in derivation:** KTU 2024 values the *process*, not just the final formula. Always show intermediate steps in Bellman equations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deep RL Foundations:**
> - RL is **learning from interaction**, not from labelled data.
> - The **agent-environment loop** is the central abstraction: $S_t \to A_t \to R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}$.
> - **Reward Hypothesis:** Every goal = maximization of expected cumulative scalar reward.
> - **Return:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$, also recursively $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$.
> - **Discount factor $\gamma$** trades off immediacy vs long-term reward; ensures convergence.
> - **Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$** is the agent's behaviour strategy.
> - **Value functions:** $V_\pi(s)$ and $Q_\pi(s,a)$ quantify expected returns.
> - **Bellman Equation** expresses value recursively — fundamental to DP, MC, and TD.
> - **Exploration vs Exploitation** is the central trade-off in action selection.
> - **$\varepsilon$-greedy** is the simplest exploration strategy.
> - **Types of RL:** Model-free (Q-Learning, DQN), Model-based (AlphaZero), Policy-based (REINFORCE), Actor-Critic (PPO).
> - **Deep RL** = RL + deep neural networks to approximate $V$, $Q$, or $\pi$ in high-dimensional state spaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Observations/States Space, Action Space, Rewards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Observations/States Space, Action Space, Rewards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Framework

In **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**, an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment. The mathematical abstraction used to formalize this interaction is the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. The MDP tuple is conventionally written as:

$$
\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle
$$

where each symbol denotes a fundamental component of the learning system. The three components central to KTU Module 4 are:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — **State Space** (set of all possible states the environment can be in)
- $\mathcal{A}$ — **Action Space** (set of all possible actions the agent can take)
- $\mathcal{R}$ — **Reward Signal** (scalar feedback from the environment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The *state* is a complete description of the world, while the *observation* is a partial description that the agent actually perceives. In a **fully observable** environment, observation = state ($O_t = S_t$). In a **partially observable** environment, the observation is a stochastic function of the state, leading to a **Partially Observable MDP (POMDP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Training a Pet Dog"

Imagine you are teaching your pet dog a new trick, such as fetching a ball. Let us map the RL components to this scenario:

| RL Component | Dog Training Analogy |
|--------------|---------------------|
| **Agent** | The dog itself (the learner/decision maker) |
| **Environment** | The living room, garden, and the person throwing the ball |
| **State ($S_t$)** | The dog's current situation: position of the ball, distance from owner, dog's pose |
| **Observation ($O_t$)** | What the dog actually *sees* through its eyes (a noisy/incomplete view of the state) |
| **Action ($A_t$)** | Move forward, sit, bark, run, grab the ball |
| **Reward ($R_t$)** | A treat (+) when fetching correctly; a gentle "no" (-) for wrong behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Just as the dog doesn't have a God's-eye view of the entire room (it sees through its own eyes), an RL agent in a real-world problem (like a self-driving car) doesn't see *everything*. It only receives **observations** sampled from the true **state**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly distinguishes these two concepts.

## 1.3 The Interaction Loop — Step-by-Step Intuition

The fundamental interaction cycle in RL proceeds as follows:

1. At time step $t$, the environment is in some **state** $S_t \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. The agent receives an **observation** $O_t$ (a representation of $S_t$).
3. Based on its policy $\pi$, the agent selects an **action** $A_t \in \mathcal{A}(S_t)$.
4. The environment transitions to a new state $S_{t+1}$ according to transition dynamics $\mathcal{P}$.
5. The agent receives a numerical **reward** $R_{t+1} \in \mathbb{R}$.
6. The cycle repeats.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall Trick:** Remember the cycle using the acronym **SOAR** — **S**tate → **O**bservation → **A**ction → **R**eward.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Episode:** One complete run of the agent-environment interaction from a starting state to a terminal state (e.g., one full game of chess).
> - **Horizon ($T$):** The number of time steps in an episode. It can be **finite** (episodic tasks) or **infinite** (continuing tasks).
> - **Return ($G_t$):** The cumulative discounted reward received from time $t$ onwards.
> - **Policy ($\pi$):** The agent's behavior function mapping states to action probabilities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 State Space ($\mathcal{S}$) — Observations vs. States

The **state** is a *complete specification* of the configuration of the world at a given instant. Formally, a state $S_t$ is **Markovian** if and only if it satisfies the Markov property:

$$
\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_t]
$$

This means: *"The future is independent of the past given the present."* The state captures all relevant history.

### Types of State Spaces:
- **Discrete State Space:** Finite set of states, e.g., $\mathcal{S} = \{\text{sunny}, \text{rainy}, \text{snowy}\}$ or the 64 squares of a chessboard.
- **Continuous State Space:** Infinite uncountable set, e.g., $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{R}^n$ representing the joint angles and velocities of a robotic arm.

### Observation Space ($\mathcal{O}$):
- In a **fully observable** MDP, the agent directly observes the state: $O_t = S_t$.
- In a **POMDP**, observations are drawn from an observation space $\mathcal{O}$ via an observation function: $O_t \sim O(S_{t+1})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly uses the term *"Observations/States"* — this dual phrasing is intentional and reflects the POMDP extension. Always clarify in your exam answer whether the environment is fully or partially observable.

## 2.2 Action Space ($\mathcal{A}$)

The **action space** is the set of all valid moves the agent can execute. Actions are classified along two orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Discrete Actions | Continuous Actions |
|------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Example** | Move Left, Move Right, Jump | Apply torque $\tau \in [-10, 10]$ Nm |
| **Cardinality** | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = N$ (finite) | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert = \infty$ (uncountable) |
| **Algorithm** | DQN, Q-Learning | DDPG, PPO, SAC |

### Policy ($\pi$):
The policy is the agent's strategy. It can be:
- **Deterministic:** $a = \pi(s)$ — a direct mapping.
- **Stochastic:** $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}[A_t = a \mid S_t = s]$ — a probability distribution over actions.

## 2.3 Rewards ($\mathcal{R}$)

The **reward** $R_t$ is a scalar feedback signal at time $t$ that indicates *how well* the agent is doing. The agent's *sole objective* is to maximize the **cumulative reward** over time.

### Reward Function:
$$
R(s, a, s') = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t+1} = s']
$$

### Return (Cumulative Reward):
$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + R_{t+2} + R_{t+3} + \cdots + R_T = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}
$$

### Discounted Return:
$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
$$

where $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the **discount factor**.

## 2.4 The Discount Factor ($\gamma$) — Deep Dive

The discount factor $\gamma$ controls the *present value* of future rewards. It has three critical interpretations:

1. **Mathematical:** Ensures convergence of $G_t$ when $T \to \infty$ (since the geometric series converges iff $\gamma < 1$).
2. **Uncertainty Modeling:** Future rewards are inherently less certain; $\gamma < 1$ reflects this epistemic uncertainty.
3. **Behavioral:** $\gamma \to 0$ produces a *myopic* (short-sighted) agent; $\gamma \to 1$ produces a *far-sighted* agent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Setting $\gamma = 1$ in continuing (infinite-horizon) tasks. This causes the return $G_t$ to diverge in stochastic environments. KTU examiners *will* penalize this.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.** Every formula here has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU ESE papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol/Unit Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | MDP Tuple | $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ | $\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}$ sets; $\mathcal{P}$ transition kernel; $\gamma$ dimensionless |
| 2 | Markov Property | $\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1}\vert S_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1}\vert S_1,\ldots,S_t]$ | Probability in $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | Transition Probability | $\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1}=s'\vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Sums to 1 over $s'$ |
| 4 | Reward Function | $R_s^a = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1}\vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Real number $\mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Policy (Stochastic) | $\pi(a\vert s) = \mathbb{P}[A_t=a\vert S_t=s]$ | Sums to 1 over $a$ |
| 6 | Policy (Deterministic) | $a = \pi(s)$ | Direct mapping |
| 7 | Return (Undiscounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}$ | Finite horizon |
| 8 | Return (Discounted) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1)$ |
| 9 | Recursive Return | $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ | Critical Bellman form |
| 10 | State-Value Function | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \vert S_t=s]$ | Units: same as reward |
| 11 | Action-Value Function | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \vert S_t=s, A_t=a]$ | Units: same as reward |
| 12 | Discount Factor Bound | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ (continuing); $\gamma = 1$ OK if finite | KTU-tested condition |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | State $\mathcal{S}$ | Action $\mathcal{A}$ | Reward $\mathcal{R}$ |
|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Robotics (Locomotion)** | Joint angles, velocities | Motor torques | Forward distance traveled |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Camera pixels, LIDAR, GPS | Steering angle, throttle | Safety + speed efficiency |
| **AlphaGo (DeepMind)** | Board configuration (19$\times$19) | Stone placement | Win (+1) / Loss (-1) / Draw (0) |
| **Inventory Management** | Stock level, demand rate | Order quantity | Profit minus holding cost |
| **Dialogue Systems (LLMs via RLHF)** | Conversation context | Next token | Human preference score |
| **Smart Grid Control** | Power demand, generator states | Generator output levels | Cost reduction |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Recursive Form of the Return

We begin with the definition of the **discounted return** $G_t$:

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \gamma^3 R_{t+4} + \cdots
$$

**Step 1: Factor out $\gamma$ from the terms beyond $R_{t+1}$:**

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^2 R_{t+4} + \cdots \right)
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the bracketed expression as $G_{t+1}$:**

By the definition of return evaluated at time $t+1$:

$$
G_{t+1} = R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \gamma^2 R_{t+4} + \cdots
$$

**Step 3: Substitute back to obtain the recursive relation:**

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \cdot G_{t+1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This recursive identity is the **foundational building block** of *all* Bellman equations in RL. Every value function, every Q-function, and every optimality equation is derived from this single recursive decomposition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Convergence of the Infinite Geometric Series

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Why must $\gamma < 1$ for continuing tasks?"* We provide the formal proof.

**Step 1: Consider the return in the worst case** (constant reward of $R_{\max}$ at every step):

$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \leq R_{\max} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k
$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard infinite geometric series formula:**

$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{1}{1 - \gamma}, \quad \text{for } \vert \gamma \vert < 1
$$

**Step 3: Conclude the bound on the return:**

$$
G_t \leq \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}
$$

This proves that $G_t$ remains finite and well-defined for any $\gamma \in [0, 1)$. If $\gamma = 1$ and the task continues indefinitely with non-zero rewards, $G_t \to \infty$, which is mathematically intractable for value-based learners.

## 3.3 Derivation: The State-Value Function $V_\pi(s)$ from First Principles

**Step 1: Start from the definition of expected return:**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [G_t \mid S_t = s]
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the recursive form $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi [R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]
$$

**Step 3: Apply the law of total expectation. Condition on the action $A_t = a$ and next state $S_{t+1} = s'$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} \left[ R_s^a + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1}=s'] \right]
$$

**Step 4: Recognize that the inner expectation is exactly $V_\pi(s')$:**

$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} \left[ R_s^a + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]
$$

This is the celebrated **Bellman Expectation Equation for $V_\pi$**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to derive $V_\pi(s)$, always start from the recursive return and *show* the law of total expectation step. Marks are awarded for showing the conditioning, not just stating the final formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Discrete vs. Continuous Action Spaces

The choice of action space profoundly affects algorithm design. The KTU board often tests this distinction:

| Criterion | Discrete $\mathcal{A}$ | Continuous $\mathcal{A}$ |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Mathematical Form** | $\mathcal{A} = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_N\}$ | $\mathcal{A} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **Policy Output** | Softmax over Q-values | Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ |
| **Exploration** | $\epsilon$-greedy, Boltzmann | Parameter noise, OU process |
| **DL Algorithm Fit** | DQN (output = N logits) | DDPG, TD3, SAC (output = $\mu$ of policy net) |
| **Loss Function** | Huber loss on TD error | Mean squared TD error + policy gradient |
| **Stability** | Relatively stable | High variance, sensitive to hyperparams |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Reward Shaping Strategies

The reward function $\mathcal{R}$ is the *most engineering-intensive* part of an RL system. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with these design patterns:

| Reward Type | Formula / Idea | Pros | Cons |
|-------------|----------------|------|------|
| **Sparse** | $+1$ at goal, $0$ elsewhere | Easy to define | Credit assignment problem |
| **Dense** | $-d(\text{current, goal})$ per step | Guides learning | Reward hacking risk |
| **Curiosity-Driven** | $R = R_{\text{ext}} + \beta \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{ICM}}$ | Explores novel states | Noisy in stochastic envs |
| **Shaped (Potential-Based)** | $F(s,a,s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ | Preserves optimal policy | Requires good potential $\Phi$ |
| **Multi-Objective** | $R = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2 + \cdots$ | Flexible | Weight tuning hard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** *Reward Hacking* — when a poorly designed reward function causes the agent to maximize the reward signal in unintended ways (e.g., a cleaning robot that hides dirt to "look clean"). Always discuss this in long-answer questions about reward design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop (Mermaid Flow)

This diagram captures the canonical RL interaction at the **core** of every KTU Module 4 problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agent1[Agent]
    env1[Environment]
    policyNode[Policy Pi of A given S]
    valueNode[Value Function V of S]

    agent1 -- Action A_t --> env1
    env1 -- Reward R_t+1 --> agent1
    env1 -- Observation O_t+1 --> agent1
    agent1 -- State S_t --> policyNode
    policyNode -- Selects A_t --> agent1
    valueNode -- Evaluates S_t --> agent1

    style agent1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style env1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style policyNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style valueNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Agent** sends **Action $A_t$** to the **Environment**.
- The **Environment** responds with **Reward $R_{t+1}$** and **Observation $O_{t+1}$**.
- Internally, the agent uses a **Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$** to choose actions and a **Value Function $V(s)$** to evaluate states.

## 4.2 State, Observation, and Action — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    worldState[True State S in World]
    obsFunc[Observation Function O]
    agentEye[Agent Perception O t]
    actionSelect[Action Selector using Policy]
    actionOut[Action A t in Action Space]
    rewardGen[Reward Generator R t+1]

    worldState --> obsFunc
    obsFunc --> agentEye
    agentEye --> actionSelect
    actionSelect --> actionOut
    actionOut --> worldState
    worldState --> rewardGen
    rewardGen --> actionSelect

    style worldState fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style obsFunc fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style agentEye fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style actionSelect fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style actionOut fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rewardGen fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **True State $S$** is the ground truth of the world.
- The **Observation Function $O$** projects this state into the agent's perception (noisy/partial in POMDPs).
- The **Action Selector** uses the policy to choose $A_t$ from the action space.
- The **Reward Generator** produces $R_{t+1}$ as a function of $(S, A, S')$.

## 4.3 MDP Hierarchy — From Fully to Partially Observable

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mdp[MDP Fully Observable]
    pomdp[POMDP Partially Observable]
    beliefMDP[Belief State MDP]

    mdp -- Agent sees full S --> obsEq["O_t equals S_t"]
    pomdp -- Agent sees partial O --> obsIneq["O_t is a function of S_t only"]
    beliefMDP -- Agent maintains belief b --> beliefDef["b of s equals P of S equals s given history"]

    style mdp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style pomdp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style beliefMDP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style obsEq fill:#FFF,stroke:#333
    style obsIneq fill:#FFF,stroke:#333
    style beliefDef fill:#FFF,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The progression MDP $\to$ POMDP $\to$ Belief-MDP reflects the increasing realism of the agent's perception. In a Belief-MDP, the agent maintains a probability distribution over states (the **belief** $b(s)$) and acts on this belief, transforming a POMDP back into a fully observable problem in belief space.

## 4.4 Reward Flow Across an Episode (State-Action-Reward Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0[S sub 0]
    s1[S sub 1]
    s2[S sub 2]
    s3[S sub 3]
    s4[S sub 4 Terminal]

    a0[A sub 0]
    a1[A sub 1]
    a2[A sub 2]
    a3[A sub 3]

    r0[R sub 1]
    r1[R sub 2]
    r2[R sub 3]
    r3[R sub 4]

    s0 -- a0 --> s1
    s1 -- a1 --> s2
    s2 -- a2 --> s3
    s3 -- a3 --> s4

    s0 -. r0 .-> s1
    s1 -. r1 .-> s2
    s2 -. r2 .-> s3
    s3 -. r3 .-> s4

    style s0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style s1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style s2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style s3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style s4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333
    style a0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style a1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style a2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style a3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style r0 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style r1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style r2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style r3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote state transitions driven by actions. Dotted arrows denote the reward received *because of* that action. The terminal state $S_4$ ends the episode.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Differentiate between the State Space and the Observation Space in a Reinforcement Learning setting. When are they equivalent?**

**Model Answer:**

The **State Space** $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of all possible *complete* configurations of the environment at a given time. A state is said to satisfy the *Markov property* if it contains all information necessary to predict the future.

The **Observation Space** $\mathcal{O}$ is the set of all possible *perceptions* the agent can receive from the environment. Observations are what the agent actually *sees*.

**Equivalence Condition:** The state space and observation space are equivalent in a **fully observable MDP**, where the agent's observation equals the true state at every time step, i.e., $O_t = S_t$ for all $t$. In contrast, in a **POMDP**, the observation is a stochastic function of the state, $O_t \sim O(\cdot \mid S_t)$, and the two spaces are *not* equivalent.

(3 marks: 1 mark for state definition, 1 mark for observation definition, 1 mark for equivalence condition.)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**What is the role of the discount factor $\gamma$ in defining the return? Why is $\gamma = 1$ problematic for infinite-horizon tasks?**

**Model Answer:**

The **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is a scalar that multiplies future rewards to compute the discounted return:

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}
$$

Its **three roles** are:
1. **Mathematical Convergence:** When $T \to \infty$ (continuing tasks) and $\gamma < 1$, the geometric series converges, ensuring $G_t$ is finite.
2. **Uncertainty Modeling:** Future rewards are less certain than immediate ones, and $\gamma < 1$ reflects this.
3. **Behavioral Tuning:** A small $\gamma$ produces a *myopic* agent; $\gamma \to 1$ produces a *far-sighted* agent.

**Why $\gamma = 1$ is problematic:** Setting $\gamma = 1$ in an infinite-horizon task yields $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} R_{t+k+1}$. If rewards are non-zero (e.g., $R_t = 1$ for all $t$), the sum diverges to infinity, making value functions $V_\pi(s)$ undefined. This causes numerical instability in learning algorithms.

(3 marks: 1 mark for formula + role, 1 mark for three sub-roles, 1 mark for divergence problem.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, candidates must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives below. Each question carries **14 marks** split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question B1 — Alternative A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define the components of a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**. Explain the Markov property formally and discuss its significance in RL. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between **episodic** and **continuing** tasks. For a continuing task with rewards $R_{t+1} = 1$ for all $t$, compute the expected return $G_t$ when $\gamma = 0.9$ and contrast it with the case $\gamma = 1.0$. Explain the implication. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(a):**

The **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** is a mathematical framework defined by the tuple:

$$
\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle
$$

**Component Definitions:**

1. **$\mathcal{S}$ — State Space:** The set of all valid states the environment can occupy. May be discrete (e.g., $\{$s1, s2, s3, s4$\}$) or continuous (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^n$).

2. **$\mathcal{A}$ — Action Space:** The set of all valid actions. May be discrete ($\{$left, right, up$\}$) or continuous ($[0, 5]$ V applied to a motor).

3. **$\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a}$ — Transition Probability:** Probability of moving to state $s'$ when action $a$ is taken in state $s$:
$$
\mathcal{P}_{ss'}^{a} = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]
$$

4. **$R_s^a$ — Reward Function:** Expected immediate reward:
$$
R_s^a = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]
$$

5. **$\gamma$ — Discount Factor:** Scalar in $[0, 1)$ for computing present value of future rewards.

**The Markov Property (Formal Statement):**
A state $S_t$ is Markov if and only if:

$$
\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_t]
$$

That is, the future is conditionally independent of the entire history given the present state.

**Significance:**
- It permits the agent to make optimal decisions using *only* the current state, with no need to remember history.
- It enables recursive formulations like the Bellman equation.
- Without it, the value function would require conditioning on the entire trajectory, which is intractable.

*(7 marks: 2 for tuple/components, 3 for Markov property with significance, 2 for implications.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B1(b):**

**Episodic vs. Continuing Tasks:**

| Aspect | Episodic Task | Continuing Task |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Horizon $T$** | Finite (e.g., one chess game) | Infinite (e.g., a thermostat controller) |
| **Terminal State** | Exists | Does not exist |
| **Return** | Sum of rewards up to $T$ | Infinite sum, must discount |

**Computation for the Given Problem:**

Given $R_{t+1} = 1$ for all $t$, we compute:

**Case 1: $\gamma = 0.9$**

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (0.9)^k \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

**Case 2: $\gamma = 1.0$**

$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 1^k \cdot 1 = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 1 = \infty
$$

**Implication:**
With $\gamma = 0.9$, the return is finite and equal to **10**. The agent's value function $V_\pi(s) = 10$ for all states under any policy. With $\gamma = 1.0$, the return *diverges to infinity*, rendering value-based learning algorithms (like Q-learning) numerically unstable. This proves the necessity of $\gamma < 1$ for continuing tasks.

*(7 marks: 2 for episodic vs. continuing comparison, 3 for the two $\gamma$ cases with calculations, 2 for the implication.)*

---

### **Question B2 — Alternative B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define **Action Space** and **Policy**. Differentiate between discrete and continuous action spaces with two real-world examples for each. Discuss how the choice of action space affects the choice of deep RL algorithm. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Define the **Reward Signal**. With a suitable example, explain **Reward Shaping**, **Sparse vs. Dense Rewards**, and the concept of **Reward Hacking**. Propose a reward design that mitigates reward hacking for a warehouse robot. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(a):**

**Definitions:**
- **Action Space $\mathcal{A}$:** The set of all valid moves the agent can execute. It is defined as $\mathcal{A}(s) = \{a \mid a \text{ is valid in state } s\}$.
- **Policy $\pi$:** The agent's behavior strategy. A stochastic policy is $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}[A_t = a \mid S_t = s]$. A deterministic policy is $a = \pi(s)$.

**Discrete Action Space:** Finite, countable set of actions.
- *Example 1:* In **Atari Pong**, the actions are $\{$Up, Down, Stay$\}$ (3 actions).
- *Example 2:* In **Chess**, the action space is a finite set of legal moves from each board state.

**Continuous Action Space:** Uncountable infinite set, typically a real-valued vector.
- *Example 1:* A **robotic arm** applying torques $\tau \in [-5, +5]$ Nm to each of 6 joints gives $\mathcal{A} = [-5, 5]^6$.
- *Example 2:* **Autonomous steering** with steering angle $\theta \in [-30°, +30°]$ and throttle $\phi \in [0, 1]$, giving $\mathcal{A} = \mathbb{R}^2$.

**Effect on Algorithm Choice:**

| Action Space Type | Suitable Deep RL Algorithms | Reason |
|-------------------|------------------------------|--------|
| Discrete | DQN, Double DQN, A2C | Output layer = softmax over $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ values |
| Continuous | DDPG, TD3, SAC, PPO | Output layer = mean $\mu$ of Gaussian policy |

Algorithms like **DQN** use a $\max$ operator (e.g., $\max_a Q(s, a)$) which is ill-defined in continuous spaces. Actor-critic methods use a *policy gradient* that can natively output continuous control.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for examples, 3 for algorithm impact.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B2(b):**

**Reward Signal Definition:**
The reward $R_t$ is a scalar numerical feedback signal at time $t$ indicating the immediate desirability of the agent's action. The agent's sole objective is to maximize cumulative reward.

**Reward Shaping:**
Reward shaping is the practice of supplementing the *extrinsic* (environment-provided) reward with an *intrinsic* reward to guide the agent. The classic potential-based shaping formula is:

$$
F(s, a, s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)
$$

where $\Phi(s)$ is a potential function. This formulation (Ng et al., 1999) is guaranteed to preserve the optimal policy.

**Sparse vs. Dense Rewards:**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Sparse** | Non-zero reward only at rare critical events | $+100$ when robot reaches goal, $0$ otherwise |
| **Dense** | Reward provided at every step | $-\vert x_{\text{robot}} - x_{\text{goal}} \vert$ at every step |

Dense rewards accelerate learning but require careful design.

**Reward Hacking — Concept:**
Reward hacking occurs when an agent exploits a poorly specified reward function to maximize the reward *signal* in unintended ways, without achieving the designer's *true* objective.

*Classic Example:* A cleaning robot rewarded for "visible mess detected by camera" learns to *cover the mess* with a cloth rather than clean it, satisfying the camera-based reward while making the floor dirtier.

**Proposed Reward Design for a Warehouse Robot:**

To mitigate reward hacking, the reward should be **multi-component** and *physically grounded*:

$$
R_t = w_1 R_{\text{delivery}} + w_2 R_{\text{collision}} - w_3 R_{\text{energy}} - w_4 R_{\text{time}}
$$

where:
- $R_{\text{delivery}} = +10$ for each successful package drop.
- $R_{\text{collision}} = -50$ for any contact with humans/objects.
- $R_{\text{energy}} = -0.1 \times$ Joules consumed.
- $R_{\text{time}} = -0.01$ per second elapsed.
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ are carefully tuned positive weights.

Additionally, incorporate **human-in-the-loop evaluation** and **adversarial reward testing** before deployment. This makes the reward harder to game because shortcuts (e.g., standing still to save energy) are penalized by the time term.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions + shaping, 2 for sparse/dense + hacking concept, 3 for the proposed reward design.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 4 — MDP Foundations):**
>
> 1. **Conflating State and Observation:** Writing "$S_t$" when the problem clearly describes a *partial* observation (e.g., a single camera frame). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. **Always state the observability assumption first.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Markov Property Check:** When describing a state, the examiner expects you to verify that it contains all information needed to make future predictions. If you propose a state representation that violates Markov (e.g., just the current position without velocity in a moving object task), expect to lose marks.
>
> 3. **Incorrect $\gamma$ in Continuing Tasks:** As shown in Question B1(b), using $\gamma = 1$ in a continuing task leads to divergence. Examiners *love* testing this. Always explicitly check whether the task is *episodic* or *continuing* before applying a discount.
>
> 4. **Confusing Policy Types:** Mixing up deterministic $\pi(s)$ with stochastic $\pi(a \mid s)$. Use the correct notation — KTU expects precision.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Reward Shaping Pitfalls:** Many students discuss reward shaping without mentioning *potential-based* conditions. The Ng et al. theorem is **examiner gold** — quote it whenever discussing reward design.
>
> 6. **No Mention of Exploration–Exploitation:** When discussing action selection, always mention that the agent must *explore* (try new actions) and *exploit* (use known good actions). The $\epsilon$-greedy strategy is the KTU-expected answer.
>
> 7. **Writing Vague Definitions:** "Reward is a feedback" is worth 0 marks. "Reward $R_{t+1}$ is a scalar real-valued signal returned by the environment at time $t+1$, indicating the immediate desirability of the transition $(S_t, A_t) \to S_{t+1}$" is worth full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Observations/States Space, Action Space, Rewards**

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- The **MDP tuple** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma \rangle$ — components and their symbols.
- The **Markov property** formula: $\mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_t] = \mathbb{P}[S_{t+1} \mid S_1, \ldots, S_t]$.
- The distinction: **State** = complete world description; **Observation** = what the agent perceives.
- **Fully observable** = $O_t = S_t$ (MDP); **Partially observable** = $O_t \sim O(\cdot \mid S_t)$ (POMDP).
- **Action space** types: discrete (DQN-friendly) vs. continuous (DDPG/SAC-friendly).
- **Policy** types: deterministic $a = \pi(s)$ vs. stochastic $\pi(a \mid s)$.
- **Return** definition: undiscounted $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{T-t-1} R_{t+k+1}$ vs. discounted $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- **Recursive return:** $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ (Bellman form).
- **Discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ for continuing tasks; $\gamma = 1$ only if horizon is finite.
- **Reward function:** $R_s^a = \mathbb{E}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a]$.
- **Reward shaping** with potential-based form: $F(s,a,s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$ (preserves optimal policy).
- **Reward hacking** = exploitation of poorly specified reward signals.

**Numerical Values to Remember:**

- Convergence of geometric series: $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k = \frac{1}{1 - \gamma}$ for $\vert \gamma \vert < 1$.
- Bound on return: $G_t \leq \frac{R_{\max}}{1 - \gamma}$.

**Diagrams to Redraw Quickly in Exam:**

- The **Agent–Environment loop** (SOAR cycle).
- The **MDP–POMDP–Belief MDP** hierarchy.
- A **state-transition diagram** for any 3-state toy MDP.

**Algorithms to Map to These Concepts:**

- **DQN** → discrete $\mathcal{A}$, dense observation, value-based.
- **DDPG / TD3 / SAC** → continuous $\mathcal{A}$, actor-critic.
- **A3C / PPO** → works for both, on-policy.
- **Recurrent PPO / DRQN** → partial observability, uses history.

**Common Exam Traps to Avoid:**

- Do *not* claim $\gamma$ can equal 1 in continuing tasks — divergent return.
- Do *not* confuse *policy* with *plan* — policy is a reactive mapping, not a sequence.
- Do *not* say "the agent maximizes reward" — it maximizes *expected discounted return* $G_t$.

**Last-Minute Mnemonic:**
> **S.O.A.R.** — **S**tate, **O**bservation, **A**ction, **R**eward — the four pillars of every RL problem. Master these, and Module 4 is yours.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Policy π: The Agent's Brain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT746 - DEEP LEARNING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Policy π: The Agent's Brain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Deep Reinforcement Learning, the **Policy (π)** is formally defined as the **agent's behavioural function or rule-book** that maps the perceived states of the environment to the actions that the agent must take when in those states. It is the core "brain" of the agent that dictates intelligent decision-making.

Mathematically, a policy is a function:

$$\pi : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{A} \quad \text{(Deterministic Policy)}$$

or a probability distribution over actions given a state:

$$\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s) \quad \text{(Stochastic Policy)}$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of all possible **states** in the environment.
- $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all possible **actions** the agent can execute.
- $S_t, A_t$ denote the state and action at time-step $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The policy $\pi$ is the *only* thing we ultimately need to solve an RL problem. A "solved" RL problem means we have found the **optimal policy $\pi^*$** that maximizes the expected cumulative reward. The policy is what makes the agent *intelligent*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Brain of a Self-Driving Car

Imagine a self-driving car navigating a busy Indian city like Kochi or Bengaluru. The car's "brain" is the **policy π**.

- **Sensors (Cameras, LIDAR)** → capture the **state** (a red light, a pedestrian crossing, a narrow lane).
- **The Driver's Rule-Book (Policy π)** → says: *"If the light is red AND a pedestrian is on the zebra crossing, apply brakes with 80% force."*
- **The Car's Actuators** → execute the **action** (brake, accelerate, turn).

Just as your brain subconsciously decides "lift the foot from the accelerator when you see a red light" based on years of learning, the **policy $\pi$** decides the agent's action based on what it has learned from experience (reward signals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Think of the policy as a **habit**. A habit is a mapping from a *situation* (state) to a *behaviour* (action). A "good habit" is an **optimal policy**; a "bad habit" is a **suboptimal policy**. RL is the process of training the agent to form the *best possible habits*.

## 1.3 Why is π Called "The Agent's Brain"?

The policy is the *sole mechanism* through which an RL agent exhibits behaviour. Without $\pi$, the agent is a passive observer. With $\pi$, the agent becomes an active, goal-seeking decision-maker. The function approximator (typically a **Deep Neural Network**) that implements $\pi$ is literally the artificial "brain" — its weights are the "synapses" tuned by gradient-based learning.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Value Function $V(s)$ or $Q(s,a)$** → tells the agent *how good* a state or action is (a critic).
> - **Policy π** → tells the agent *what to actually do* (an actor).
> - Methods like **Actor-Critic** use *both* — the actor (π) acts, the critic (V) evaluates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Policy — The Two Fundamental Variants

### A. Deterministic Policy $\pi(s)$

A **deterministic** policy is a fixed function. For every state $s$, there is exactly *one* action $a$ that the agent will always choose.

$$\pi(s) = a$$

**Use Case:** Best for fully observable, continuous control environments (e.g., robotic arm torque control, autonomous steering angles). Most popular in **Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG)** algorithms.

### B. Stochastic Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$

A **stochastic** policy is a probability distribution over actions. Instead of committing to a single action, the agent *samples* an action from the distribution.

$$\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s) \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) = 1$$

**Use Case:** Essential in **partially observable** or **multi-agent** environments where exploration and randomization are required. Used in **PPO, TRPO, A2C** algorithms.

## 2.2 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) — Where π Lives

The policy $\pi$ operates inside an **MDP** tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{R}, \gamma)$. Let us break down how $\pi$ interacts with each component:

| Component | Symbol | Role in the Policy Loop |
|---|---|---|
| State Space | $\mathcal{S}$ | Input to the policy $\pi$ |
| Action Space | $\mathcal{A}$ | Output of the policy $\pi$ |
| Transition Function | $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ | Defines how the environment changes after the policy's action |
| Reward Function | $\mathcal{R}(s, a)$ | The *teaching signal* used to improve the policy |
| Discount Factor | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | Weights future rewards vs immediate rewards |

## 2.3 Parameterized Policies — The Deep Learning Connection

In Deep RL, the policy is **not** a hand-coded lookup table; it is a **Deep Neural Network** with parameters $\theta$. The neural network's weights and biases form the policy:

$$\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = \text{Softmax}\bigl( f_\theta(s) \bigr) \quad \text{(Discrete Actions)}$$

$$\pi_\theta(s) = \mu_\theta(s) \quad \text{(Continuous Actions, with Gaussian exploration noise)}$$

The **goal of Deep RL** is to tune the parameters $\theta$ such that $\pi_\theta$ becomes the optimal policy $\pi^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive "Why":** A neural network is used because state spaces (e.g., raw images from a video game) are *far too large* for a table. The neural network **generalizes** from seen states to unseen states — a property called *function approximation*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\pi(a \mid s)$ | Probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$ | Policy definition |
| 2 | $\pi(s) = a$ | Deterministic policy mapping | DDPG, TD3 |
| 3 | $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | Parameterized stochastic policy | PPO, A2C, REINFORCE |
| 4 | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Return (cumulative discounted reward) | All RL algorithms |
| 5 | $V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[ G_t \mid S_t = s \bigr]$ | State-value of policy $\pi$ | Policy evaluation |
| 6 | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\bigl[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \bigr]$ | Action-value of policy $\pi$ | Bellman equations |
| 7 | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \, Q^\pi(s, a)$ | Consistency between V and Q | Actor-Critic |
| 8 | $Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) V^\pi(s')$ | Bellman expectation equation | Policy iteration |
| 9 | $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\bigl[ G(\tau) \bigr]$ | Policy objective (expected return) | Policy Gradient theorem |
| 10 | $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\bigl[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \, G_t \bigr]$ | REINFORCE policy gradient | REINFORCE, PPO |
| 11 | $\pi^* = \arg\max_\pi V^\pi(s) \; \forall s$ | Optimal policy definition | RL theory |
| 12 | $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)$ | Optimal state-value | Bellman optimality |
| 13 | $\gamma$ | Discount factor; $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ | All MDPs |
| 14 | $\epsilon$ | Exploration rate (ε-greedy) | ε-greedy policy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Warning to Students:** In KTU answer sheets, always write `\pi(a \vert s)` or `\pi(a \mid s)` for conditional probability — never write `\|` or `|` raw, as it clashes with markdown table parsers in digital answer books.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Policy

| Application Domain | Role of Policy π |
|---|---|
| **Robotics (Manipulation)** | Maps joint angles & object positions to motor torques |
| **Autonomous Driving** | Maps LIDAR/camera frames to steering + throttle + brake |
| **Game AI (AlphaGo, AlphaZero)** | Maps board positions to move probabilities (logits over 361 moves) |
| **Dynamic Resource Allocation in 5G/6G** | Maps network state to resource-block assignment |
| **Industrial Process Control** | Maps sensor readings to actuator setpoints |
| **Algorithmic Trading** | Maps market state to buy / hold / sell signals |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The policy is the *deployable artefact*. Once training is complete, we *discard* the value function and the replay buffer; only $\pi_\theta$ (a saved `.h5` or `.pt` file) ships in the product.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Bellman Expectation Equation for π

The Bellman expectation equation establishes a **recursive consistency** between the value of a state and the values of its successor states under a fixed policy $\pi$.

**Starting Point (Definition of $V^\pi$):**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_t \mid S_t = s \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the Return $G_t$ into its immediate reward and the discounted future return:**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \,\bigr]
$$

> *Logic:* By definition, $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma\, G_{t+1}$. This is the *recursive unrolling* of the return.

**Step 2 — Apply linearity of expectation to split the expectation:**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \,\bigr] \;+\; \gamma\, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\bigl[\, G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the action $A_t = a$ using the law of total expectation over the policy:**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \left(\, \mathcal{R}(s, a) \;+\; \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\, V^\pi(s') \,\right)
$$

> *Logic:* The probability of choosing $a$ in $s$ is $\pi(a \mid s)$ (the policy). The expected immediate reward is $\mathcal{R}(s, a)$. The expected next-state value is $\gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) V^\pi(s')$.

**Step 4 — Final compact form (the Bellman Expectation Equation for $V^\pi$):**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\!\left[\, \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\, V^\pi(s') \right]
$$

This is a **system of linear equations** (one per state) that can be solved directly for $V^\pi$ if $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{R}$ are known — but in Deep RL, both are unknown, so we use **sampling** (Monte Carlo / Temporal Difference).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between $V^\pi$ and $Q^\pi$

This derivation shows the **bridge** between the state-value and the action-value, which is the cornerstone of Actor-Critic methods.

**Starting Point — Definition of $Q^\pi$:**

$$
Q^\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\, V^\pi(s')
$$

> *Logic:* The first step is the immediate reward; the rest is the expected discounted future.

**Step 1 — Substitute the Bellman expectation of $V^\pi(s')$ for the next state:**

$$
Q^\pi(s, a) \;=\; \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s')\, Q^\pi(s', a')
$$

**Step 2 — Marginalize over actions using the policy $\pi$ to recover $V^\pi$:**

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s)\, Q^\pi(s, a)
$$

> *Logic:* $V^\pi(s)$ is the policy-weighted average of all action-values in state $s$. This is the central identity used in **Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C)**: $A^\pi(s, a) = Q^\pi(s, a) - V^\pi(s)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Policy Gradient Theorem (REINFORCE)

This is the **most important derivation** in Deep RL — it shows how to compute the gradient of the expected return with respect to the policy parameters $\theta$.

**Starting Point — The Policy Objective:**

$$
J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\bigl[\, G(\tau) \,\bigr] \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

> *Logic:* $J(\theta)$ is the expected total reward over all possible trajectories $\tau = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots)$ generated by following $\pi_\theta$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \nabla_\theta \int G(\tau)\, p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

> *Logic:* Only $p_\theta(\tau)$ depends on $\theta$, so we bring the gradient inside the integral.

**Step 2 — Apply the Log-Derivative Trick (also called the *REINFORCE trick*):**

$$
\nabla_\theta p_\theta(\tau) \;=\; p_\theta(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(\tau)
$$

> *Logic:* This uses the identity $\nabla \log f = \frac{\nabla f}{f}$, which lets us move the gradient *onto* the log-probability — a Monte-Carlo-friendly form.

**Step 3 — Substitute and rewrite as an expectation:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, p_\theta(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\bigl[\, G(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(\tau) \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Factor the trajectory log-probability into per-step log-probabilities:**

$$
\log p_\theta(\tau) \;=\; \log p(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T} \bigl[\, \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) + \log \mathcal{P}(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) \,\bigr]
$$

> *Logic:* A trajectory's probability factorizes into the policy's log-probability and the environment's transition log-probability. The latter is **independent of $\theta$**, so its gradient vanishes.

**Step 5 — Discard the $\theta$-independent terms and arrive at the final REINFORCE gradient:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\left[\, \sum_{t=0}^{T} G_t\, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \,\right]
$$

> *Logic:* The gradient is the expected sum of (return) × (log-policy gradient) over the trajectory. In practice, we sample a single trajectory and use **stochastic gradient ascent** to update $\theta$.

**Practical Update Rule (Stochastic Gradient Ascent):**

$$
\theta \;\leftarrow\; \theta + \alpha\, G_t\, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)
$$

> *Intuition:* If return $G_t$ is high, we *amplify* the gradient (do more of that action); if $G_t$ is low, we *dampen* it (do less of that action). This is literally the **Hebbian principle** of neural learning — *"neurons that fire together, wire together"*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Policy vs Value-Based Methods

| Feature | **Policy-Based (e.g., REINFORCE, PPO)** | **Value-Based (e.g., DQN)** |
|---|---|---|
| What is learned? | Policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ directly | Action-value $Q(s, a; \theta)$ |
| Action selection | Sample from $\pi_\theta$ | $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$ (greedy) |
| Handles continuous actions? | ✅ **Yes** (native support) | ❌ Difficult (needs discretization) |
| Stochastic policy support? | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ Naturally deterministic |
| Convergence | Often *no* guaranteed local optimum | Converges to $Q^*$ under tabular settings |
| Gradient noise | High variance | Lower variance |
| Sample efficiency | Lower | Higher (with replay buffer) |
| Example algorithms | REINFORCE, PPO, TRPO, A2C, SAC | DQN, Double-DQN, Dueling-DQN |
| KTU exam weightage | **High** (4–7 marks) | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Policy π in the Agent-Environment Interaction Loop

This is the canonical Sutton & Barto RL loop, with the **policy π** explicitly highlighted as the agent's brain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent: Policy pi_theta as Deep Neural Network] -->|Action A_t| B[Environment]
    B -->|Next State S_t+1| A
    B -->|Reward R_t+1| A
    subgraph Agent_Brain [Agent Brain = Policy Network]
        C[Input Layer: State s_t] --> D[Hidden Layers: Dense or CNN or RNN]
        D --> E[Output Layer: Action logits a]
        E --> F[Softmax or Gaussian Sampler]
        F --> G[Action A_t selected]
    end
    A -.contains.-> Agent_Brain
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The outer loop is the **MDP cycle**: agent acts → environment responds → reward is delivered.
- The inner block is the **agent's brain** (a Deep NN). The state flows in, the hidden layers extract features, the output layer produces logits, and the sampler emits an action.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Taxonomy of Policy Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Policy pi] --> D[Deterministic Policy pi of s equals a]
    P --> S[Stochastic Policy pi of a given s]
    D --> D1[pi of s equals argmax a Q of s comma a]
    D --> D2[Used in DDPG and TD3]
    S --> S1[Categorical for Discrete Actions]
    S --> S2[Gaussian for Continuous Actions]
    S1 --> S1a[Softmax output layer]
    S1 --> S1b[Used in PPO and REINFORCE]
    S2 --> S2a[Mean mu of s plus sigma epsilon]
    S2 --> S2b[Used in SAC and DDPG with noise]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The policy tree branches first into **deterministic** vs **stochastic**.
- Stochastic further branches by the **action space type** (discrete → Softmax, continuous → Gaussian).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — How the Policy is Updated via the REINFORCE Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Initialize policy parameters theta] --> S1[Collect trajectory tau using pi_theta]
    S1 --> S2[Compute return G_t for every timestep]
    S2 --> S3[Compute gradient: nabla J equals E of G_t nabla log pi_theta]
    S3 --> S4[Update theta: theta gets theta plus alpha G_t nabla log pi_theta]
    S4 --> S5{Converged?}
    S5 -->|No| S1
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Output optimal policy pi star]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The loop continues until convergence (i.e., the change in $J(\theta)$ falls below a small threshold $\epsilon$).
- In practice, KTU students should note that REINFORCE is **on-policy** — the data used for the update must come from the *current* policy.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** When drawing MDP loop diagrams in the exam, always use **arrows pointing in both directions** between Agent and Environment, and label them clearly. Examiners deduct marks for unlabeled arrows.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Policy Approximation Architectures

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[State s] --> B{Neural Network Architecture}
    B -->|Vector state| C[Multi Layer Perceptron MLP]
    B -->|Image state| D[Convolutional Neural Network CNN]
    B -->|Sequence state| E[Recurrent Neural Network RNN LSTM]
    C --> F[Policy pi_theta]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Action a or Action distribution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A frequent 7-mark question asks *"How is a policy represented in deep reinforcement learning?"* The answer must mention the **choice of architecture** (MLP/CNN/RNN) based on the state modality — exactly what this diagram conveys.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEEBT746]**

### Q1. Define the term "Policy" in the context of Reinforcement Learning. Differentiate between a deterministic policy and a stochastic policy.

**Model Answer:**

A **policy $\pi$** is a mapping from the perceived states of the environment to the actions to be taken by the agent when in those states. It is the agent's behavioural rule-book and is the central decision-making component of any RL system.

- **Deterministic Policy $\pi(s) = a$:** For every state $s$, the policy prescribes *exactly one* action $a$ with certainty. It is a direct function from states to actions. Example: $\pi(s) = \arg\max_a Q(s, a)$ in value-based methods.
- **Stochastic Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$:** For every state $s$, the policy defines a *probability distribution* over actions, and the agent samples an action from this distribution. Formally, $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Deterministic | Stochastic |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single action | Probability distribution |
| Exploration | Needs external ε-greedy | Built-in via sampling |
| Continuous actions | Natural fit | Requires Gaussian parameterization |
| Used in | DDPG, TD3 | PPO, A2C, SAC, REINFORCE |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

### Q2. State the Bellman Expectation Equation for a policy $\pi$ and explain the meaning of each term.

**Model Answer:**

The Bellman Expectation Equation for a policy $\pi$ is:

$$
V^\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \left[\, \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\, V^\pi(s') \,\right]
$$

**Meaning of each term:**

- $V^\pi(s)$ — The expected return (cumulative discounted reward) obtained by starting in state $s$ and then following policy $\pi$ thereafter.
- $\pi(a \mid s)$ — The probability that the policy selects action $a$ when in state $s$.
- $\mathcal{R}(s, a)$ — The expected immediate reward received after taking action $a$ in state $s$.
- $\mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)$ — The probability that the environment transitions to state $s'$ after action $a$ is taken in state $s$.
- $\gamma$ — The discount factor, $\gamma \in [0, 1)$, which weighs the importance of future rewards relative to immediate ones.
- $V^\pi(s')$ — The value of the successor state $s'$, evaluated under the same policy $\pi$.

This equation captures the **recursive consistency** of state values: *the value of where you are equals the value of where you're going plus the reward you get along the way.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT746, Module 4 Choice]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Policy Gradient & REINFORCE

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Past Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Policy Gradient Theorem** from the policy objective $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[G(\tau)]$, clearly explaining the **log-derivative trick** and the role of the return $G_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain how a **policy $\pi_\theta$** is implemented as a **Deep Neural Network**. Discuss the choice of output activation (Softmax for discrete actions, Gaussian for continuous actions) with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define the objective $J(\theta)$:**

$$
J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\bigl[\, G(\tau) \,\bigr] \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $\theta$:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the log-derivative trick** using $\nabla f = f \cdot \nabla \log f$:

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \int G(\tau)\, p_\theta(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite as an expectation (this allows Monte-Carlo sampling):**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\bigl[\, G(\tau)\, \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta(\tau) \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 5 — Factor the trajectory log-probability:**

$$
\log p_\theta(\tau) \;=\; \log p(s_0) + \sum_{t} \bigl[\, \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) + \log \mathcal{P}(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) \,\bigr]
$$

**Step 6 — The $\log p(s_0)$ and $\log \mathcal{P}$ terms have **zero gradient with respect to $\theta$** (they are environment properties), so they vanish. The final REINFORCE gradient is:**

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\!\left[\, \sum_{t=0}^{T} G_t\, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \,\right]
$$

**Step 7 — The parameter update rule (gradient ascent on the objective):**

$$
\theta \;\leftarrow\; \theta + \alpha\, G_t\, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)
$$

**Role of $G_t$:** It acts as a *reinforcement signal*. If $G_t > 0$ (good action), the term $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta$ is amplified, increasing the probability of that action. If $G_t < 0$ (bad action), the probability is suppressed. This implements **trial-and-error learning**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A policy $\pi_\theta$ is implemented as a **Deep Neural Network** whose parameters $\theta$ are the weights and biases. The architecture depends on the state modality:

| State Modality | Recommended Architecture | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| Tabular / low-dim vector | Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) | CartPole, MountainCar |
| Raw image (pixels) | Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) | Atari games, vision-based robotics |
| Time-series / partial observability | Recurrent Neural Network (LSTM/GRU) | Stock trading, POMDPs |

**Output Activations:**

- **Discrete Actions (e.g., {Left, Right, Up, Down}):** The final layer outputs one logit per action, and a **Softmax** converts these logits into a probability distribution: $\pi_\theta(a_i \mid s) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$. Example: In Atari Pong, the network outputs probabilities over 6 possible actions.
- **Continuous Actions (e.g., steering angle $\in [-1, 1]$):** The final layer outputs the **mean $\mu_\theta(s)$** of a Gaussian distribution, and a fixed (or learnable) standard deviation $\sigma$ provides exploration noise: $a \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_\theta(s), \sigma^2)$. Example: A robot arm's joint torques in DDPG.

The block diagram is the same as the **Agent_Brain** subgraph in Section 4.1.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Optimal Policy, Bellman Equations & Comparison

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Past Paper]**

**(a)** Define **optimal policy $\pi^*$** and **optimal value function $V^*(s)$**. State and derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Policy-Based** and **Value-Based** Reinforcement Learning methods. Why are policy-based methods preferred for continuous action spaces? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** The **optimal policy $\pi^*$** is a policy that yields the maximum expected return from every state, compared to all other policies. Formally:

$$
\pi^*(s) \;=\; \arg\max_\pi V^\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

The corresponding **optimal state-value function** is:

$$
V^*(s) \;=\; \max_\pi V^\pi(s) \quad \forall s \in \mathcal{S}
$$

Similarly, the **optimal action-value function** is $Q^*(s, a) = \max_\pi Q^\pi(s, a)$.

**Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation:**

The Bellman optimality equation for $V^*$ is obtained by taking the $\max$ over actions instead of a weighted sum under a fixed policy:

$$
V^*(s) \;=\; \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \left[\, \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a)\, V^*(s') \,\right]
$$

> *Logic:* The optimal policy is greedy with respect to the optimal value function — it picks the action that maximizes the one-step reward plus the discounted optimal value of the next state. There is no $\pi(a \mid s)$ term because the $\max$ operation collapses the distribution.

The action-value version is:

$$
Q^*(s, a) \;=\; \mathcal{R}(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')
$$

The relationship $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)$ connects the two.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The full comparison is captured in the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.4. The key advantages of policy-based methods for continuous action spaces are:

1. **No Action Maximization Required:** Value-based methods like DQN need $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$, which is tractable only if $\mathcal{A}$ is discrete and small. In continuous spaces (e.g., torque in $[-1, 1]^{10}$ for a 10-DOF arm), the $\arg\max$ is an **NP-hard non-convex optimization** in real time.
2. **Native Stochasticity:** Policy-based methods naturally handle stochastic optimal policies — important when multiple actions are equally good or in adversarial multi-agent settings.
3. **Smooth Policy Updates:** Small changes in $\theta$ produce small changes in $\pi_\theta$, enabling stable gradient-based learning. Value-based methods can have sudden discontinuous policy changes when $Q$ rankings flip.
4. **Better Convergence on High-Dimensional Problems:** Algorithms like **PPO, TRPO, and SAC** scale to high-dimensional continuous control (humanoid locomotion, dexterous manipulation) where DQN-style methods fail.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\pi(s)$ with $\pi(a \mid s)$:** A deterministic policy is $\pi(s) = a$; a stochastic policy is $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$. Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor $\gamma$:** When writing the Bellman equation, the future value term must be multiplied by $\gamma$. This is a recurring 0.5-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the log-derivative trick explanation:** When deriving the policy gradient, you *must* explicitly write $\nabla f = f \cdot \nabla \log f$. Simply stating the final formula costs you 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the trajectory log-probability factorization:** The terms $\log p(s_0)$ and $\log \mathcal{P}(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ must be written out and *explicitly discarded* with the reason "they do not depend on $\theta$". Otherwise, the derivation is incomplete.
> 5. **Confusing on-policy vs off-policy:** REINFORCE is **on-policy** (data must come from the current $\pi$). DQN is **off-policy** (uses a replay buffer). Examiners test this frequently.
> 6. **Not mentioning Softmax/Gaussian:** When asked how policies handle discrete vs continuous actions, students often forget to specify the output activation. This costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Quick-Recall Bullet Points (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- ✅ **Policy $\pi$** = Agent's brain = mapping from states to actions.
- ✅ **Deterministic policy:** $\pi(s) = a$ — one action per state.
- ✅ **Stochastic policy:** $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ — a probability distribution.
- ✅ **Deep RL parameterization:** $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ is implemented by a neural network with weights $\theta$.
- ✅ **Bellman Expectation Eqn for $V^\pi$:** $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s)[\mathcal{R}(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \mathcal{P}(s' \mid s, a) V^\pi(s')]$.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality Eqn for $V^*$:** Replace $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s)(\cdot)$ with $\max_a (\cdot)$.
- ✅ **Bridge identity:** $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) Q^\pi(s, a)$ — the link between actor and critic.
- ✅ **Policy Objective:** $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[G(\tau)]$ — what we want to maximize.
- ✅ **REINFORCE Gradient:** $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[G_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)]$.
- ✅ **Update Rule:** $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \cdot G_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ — gradient *ascent*.
- ✅ **Log-Derivative Trick:** $\nabla_\theta p_\theta = p_\theta \cdot \nabla_\theta \log p_\theta$ — converts an *intractable* integral into a *sampleable* expectation.
- ✅ **Discrete actions → Softmax output.** **Continuous actions → Gaussian (mean μθ(s), variance σ²).**
