# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams depict functional architecture flows for the most-exam-relevant applications in Module 4. All node IDs are alphanumeric and label text avoids markdown formatting inside double-quotes, as per V10 compilation safeguards.

---

## 4.1 Image Captioning — CNN–LSTM "See-and-Tell" Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    img[INPUT IMAGE] --> cnn[CNN ENCODER ResNet50]
    cnn -->|feature vector v| emb0[INITIAL LSTM STATE h0 = v]
    emb0 --> lstm1[LSTM DECODER STEP 1]
    w1[WORD EMBEDDING y1] --> lstm1
    lstm1 --> p1[SOFTMAX OVER VOCABULARY]
    p1 -->|argmax| w2[NEXT WORD y2]
    w2 -->|embedding| lstm2[LSTM DECODER STEP 2]
    lstm2 --> p2[SOFTMAX]
    p2 --> w3[NEXT WORD y3]
    w3 -->|embedding| lstm3[LSTM DECODER STEP T]
    lstm3 --> pT[SOFTMAX END OF SENTENCE]
    pT --> out[OUTPUT CAPTION]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The image is encoded **once** by the CNN. The LSTM decoder then unrolls autoregressively — each generated word is fed back as input to predict the next.

---

## 4.2 Object Detection — YOLO Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT IMAGE 448x448x3] --> B[CNN BACKBONE Darknet53]
    B --> C[FEATURE MAP 7x7x1024]
    C --> D[CONV + DETECTION HEAD]
    D --> E[GRID CELL PREDICTIONS]
    E --> F1[BOUNDING BOX COORDINATES x y w h]
    E --> F2[OBJECT CONFIDENCE SCORE]
    E --> F3[CLASS PROBABILITIES]
    F1 --> G[NMS NON MAX SUPPRESSION]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[FINAL DETECTIONS WITH BOXES]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single CNN forward pass produces **all** detections. Non-Max Suppression (NMS) removes overlapping boxes sharing the same class.

---

## 4.3 U-Net for Semantic Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in[INPUT IMAGE] --> e1[CONV BLOCK 1 64]
    e1 --> p1[POOL]
    p1 --> e2[CONV BLOCK 2 128]
    e2 --> p2[POOL]
    p2 --> e3[CONV BLOCK 3 256]
    e3 --> p3[POOL]
    p3 --> e4[CONV BLOCK 4 512]
    e4 --> p4[POOL]
    p4 --> b[BOTTLENECK 1024]
    b --> u1[UPSAMPLE]
    u1 --> s1[SKIP CONCAT FROM E4]
    s1 --> d1[DECONV BLOCK 512]
    d1 --> u2[UPSAMPLE]
    u2 --> s2[SKIP CONCAT FROM E3]
    s2 --> d2[DECONV BLOCK 256]
    d2 --> u3[UPSAMPLE]
    u3 --> s3[SKIP CONCAT FROM E2]
    s3 --> d3[DECONV BLOCK 128]
    d3 --> u4[UPSAMPLE]
    u4 --> s4[SKIP CONCAT FROM E1]
    s4 --> d4[DECONV BLOCK 64]
    d4 --> out[PER PIXEL SOFTMAX MASK]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The left (down-sampling) arm captures *context*; the right (up-sampling) arm captures *localisation*. **Skip connections** fuse fine-grained encoder features into the decoder.

---

## 4.4 Seq2Seq with Attention for Machine Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[SOURCE SENTENCE] --> enc1[ENCODER LSTM STEP 1]
    enc1 --> enc2[ENCODER LSTM STEP 2]
    enc2 --> enc3[ENCODER LSTM STEP T]
    enc3 --> h1[HIDDEN STATES h1 to hT]
    h1 --> att[ATTENTION MODULE]
    dec1[DECODER LSTM STEP 1] --> att
    att --> ctx[CONTEXT VECTOR c1]
    ctx --> out1[WORD y1]
    out1 --> dec2[DECODER LSTM STEP 2]
    dec2 --> att2[ATTENTION MODULE]
    att2 --> ctx2[CONTEXT VECTOR c2]
    ctx2 --> out2[WORD y2]
    out2 --> decT[DECODER LSTM STEP T]
    decT --> attT[ATTENTION MODULE]
    attT --> ctxT[CONTEXT VECTOR cT]
    ctxT --> outT[WORD yT END]
```

**Reading the diagram:** At each decoding step, attention dynamically aligns the current decoder state with all encoder hidden states, producing a step-specific context vector.

---

## 4.5 GAN – Adversarial Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    z[NOISE z N 0 I] --> g[GENERATOR G]
    g --> fake[FAKE IMAGE G z]
    real[REAL IMAGE SAMPLE] --> mix[COMBINE BATCH]
    fake --> mix
    mix --> d[DISCRIMINATOR D]
    d --> prob[PROBABILITY REAL]
    prob --> lossG[G LOSS log 1 D G z]
    prob --> lossD[D LOSS log D x plus log 1 D G z]
    lossG --> optG[UPDATE G WEIGHTS]
    lossD --> optD[UPDATE D WEIGHTS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** $G$ and $D$ are trained alternately. Over time, $G$'s fakes become indistinguishable from real samples.

---

## 4.6 DQN for Reinforcement Learning Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s[STATE s_t] --> q[Q NETWORK CNN OR MLP]
    q --> qvals[Q VALUES FOR ALL ACTIONS]
    qvals --> sel[EPSILON GREEDY ACTION a_t]
    sel --> env[ENVIRONMENT]
    env --> rt[REWARD r_t]
    env --> stp1[NEXT STATE s_t+1]
    rt --> buf[REPLAY BUFFER]
    stp1 --> buf
    stp1 --> target[TARGET NETWORK]
    rt --> target
    buf --> sample[MINIBATCH SAMPLE]
    sample --> q2[Q NETWORK FORWARD PASS]
    target --> q2
    q2 --> tderr[TD ERROR]
    tderr --> backprop[BACKPROP UPDATE Q WEIGHTS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Q-network approximates $Q(s,a)$. A separate target network stabilises bootstrapping. Experience replay breaks temporal correlation.

---

## 4.7 Master Block Diagram: Deep Learning Application Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[PROBLEM DEFINITION] --> q1{DATA MODALITY?}
    q1 -->|IMAGE| vision[VISION PIPELINE CNN]
    q1 -->|TEXT| nlp[NLP PIPELINE LSTM TRANSFORMER]
    q1 -->|AUDIO| speech[SPEECH PIPELINE RNN CTC]
    q1 -->|TIME SERIES| ts[RNN LSTM GRU]
    vision --> q2{TASK?}
    q2 -->|CLASSIFY| cls[CNN CLASSIFIER]
    q2 -->|LOCALISE| det[YOLO RCNN]
    q2 -->|SEGMENT| seg[UNET FCN]
    q2 -->|DESCRIBE| cap[CNN plus LSTM CAPTIONING]
    nlp --> q3{TASK?}
    q3 -->|TRANSLATE| mt[SEQ2SEQ ATTENTION]
    q3 -->|GENERATE| gen[TRANSFORMER]
    q3 -->|CLASSIFY| bert[BERT EMBEDDING plus MLP]
    speech --> asr[CTC SEQ2SEQ]
    ts --> fc[LSTM GRU FORECASTING]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **KTU Exam Decision Tree** — students should be able to flowchart any problem to its correct architecture family.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any three major applications of deep learning in computer vision and state the network architecture most suited for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **No.** | **Application** | **Best-Suited Architecture** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Image Classification | CNN (ResNet, VGG) — uses hierarchical feature extraction. |
| 2 | Object Detection | YOLO / Faster R-CNN — produces bounding boxes + class probabilities. |
| 3 | Semantic Segmentation | U-Net / FCN — encoder-decoder with skip connections for pixel-level output. |

Other acceptable answers: Image Captioning (CNN+LSTM), Pose Estimation (Stacked Hourglass), Style Transfer (VGG-based).

*(1 mark per correct application–architecture pair.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the discriminator in a Generative Adversarial Network? Mention the loss function it minimises.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role (2 marks):** The discriminator $D$ is a binary classifier that distinguishes real samples (drawn from the training data) from fake samples produced by the generator $G$. It outputs $D(x) \in [0,1]$, the probability that input $x$ is real.
- **Loss Function (1 mark):** It maximises

$$
V(D) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]
$$

Equivalently, it minimises the **binary cross-entropy** between its predictions and the "real vs. fake" labels.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:**
> *Answer both Sub-Questions (a) AND (b). Either Sub-Q of Q-A OR Sub-Q of Q-B carries internal choice (full module choice as per KTU norms).*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Encoder–Decoder architecture with Bahdanau Attention for Machine Translation**. Draw the architecture diagram and derive the attention weight formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **U-Net architecture for semantic segmentation**, including its loss function. Explain how the Dice Loss handles class imbalance better than pixel-wise cross-entropy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a): Encoder–Decoder + Bahdanau Attention**

**Step 1 – Problem Context (1 mark):**
Machine Translation (MT) converts a source sentence $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{T_x})$ in language L1 into a target sentence $Y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_{T_y})$ in language L2. Vanilla Seq2Seq compresses $X$ into a fixed vector, which fails for long sentences.

**Step 2 – Encoder (2 marks):**
A bidirectional LSTM reads the source. At each step $j$:

$$
\overrightarrow{h_j} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{fwd}}(x_j, \overrightarrow{h_{j-1}}), \quad
\overleftarrow{h_j} = \text{LSTM}_{\text{bwd}}(x_j, \overleftarrow{h_{j+1}})
$$

The encoder annotation is the concatenation:

$$
h_j = [\overrightarrow{h_j}; \overleftarrow{h_j}]
$$

**Step 3 – Decoder + Attention (3 marks):**
The decoder LSTM maintains state $s_i$. Bahdanau attention computes an alignment score:

$$
e_{ij} = v_a^\top \tanh(W_a s_{i-1} + U_a h_j)
$$

Softmax-normalised weights:

$$
\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\exp(e_{ij})}{\sum_{k=1}^{T_x}\exp(e_{ik})}
$$

Context vector at decoding step $i$:

$$
c_i = \sum_{j=1}^{T_x}\alpha_{ij}\,h_j
$$

Decoder update:

$$
s_i = \text{LSTM}(s_{i-1}, y_{i-1}, c_i), \quad P(y_i \mid y_{<i}, X) = \text{softmax}(W_o\, s_i)
$$

**Step 4 – Loss (1 mark):**
Cross-entropy over target tokens:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{T_y}\log P(y_i \mid y_{<i}, X)
```

**Architecture Diagram (see Section 4.4 of these notes).**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b): U-Net for Semantic Segmentation**

**Step 1 – Task Definition (1 mark):**
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to **every pixel** in the image, producing a mask of size $H \times W \times C$.

**Step 2 – U-Net Architecture (3 marks):**
- **Contracting path (encoder):** repeated $3\times3$ convolutions + ReLU + $2\times2$ max-pooling (downsampling). At each stage, feature channels double.
- **Bottleneck:** deepest feature map.
- **Expansive path (decoder):** $2\times2$ transposed convolution (upsampling) halves the channels.
- **Skip connections:** feature maps from encoder are cropped and concatenated with decoder feature maps — recovers spatial precision lost in pooling.

**Step 3 – Loss Function: Dice Loss (2 marks):**
For ground-truth mask $Y$ and predicted probability mask $\hat{Y}$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2\sum_{i} y_i \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} y_i + \sum_{i}\hat{y}_i + \epsilon}
$$

**Step 4 – Why Dice handles class imbalance (1 mark):**
Pixel-wise cross-entropy computes an average over **all** pixels. If 99% of pixels are background, predicting "background everywhere" achieves 99% CE loss reduction without learning anything useful.
The Dice loss optimises the **overlap ratio** directly — a 1-pixel tumour in a 1000×1000 image still contributes meaningfully to the numerator $2\sum y_i\hat{y}_i$, preventing the model from being biased toward the majority class.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **architecture and training objective of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)** for image synthesis. Derive the optimal discriminator condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** approach for reinforcement learning applications. State the Bellman equation it approximates and the loss function used for training. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a): GAN Architecture and Optimal Discriminator**

**Step 1 – Components (1 mark):**
- **Generator $G_\theta$:** maps noise $z \sim p_z$ to fake sample $G(z)$.
- **Discriminator $D_\phi$:** binary classifier outputting probability that input is real.

**Step 2 – Min–Max Objective (2 marks):**

$$
\min_G \max_D V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]
$$

**Step 3 – Derivation of Optimal Discriminator (3 marks):**
For fixed $G$, the optimal $D^*$ maximises:

$$
V(D) = \int_x p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x)\,dx + \int_z p_z(z)\log(1 - D(G(z)))\,dz
$$

Change of variable $x = G(z)$ in the second integral:

$$
V(D) = \int_x \Big[p_{\text{data}}(x)\log D(x) + p_G(x)\log(1 - D(x))\Big]\,dx
$$

Pointwise optimum (set derivative w.r.t. $D(x)$ to zero):

$$
\frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_G(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
D^*(x) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(x)}{p_{\text{data}}(x) + p_G(x)}
$$

**Step 4 – Interpretation (1 mark):**
At global optimum, $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$, giving $D^*(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere (the discriminator cannot distinguish).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b): Deep Q-Network**

**Step 1 – RL Setting (1 mark):**
An agent interacts with an environment via state $s$, action $a$, reward $r$, next state $s'$. The objective is to learn a policy $\pi$ that maximises cumulative discounted reward:

$$
R_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^k r_{t+k+1}, \quad \gamma \in [0,1)
$$

**Step 2 – Q-Function and Bellman Equation (2 marks):**

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\gamma^k r_{t+k+1}\;\Big\vert\; s_t = s, a_t = a\right]
$$

Bellman optimality:

$$
Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')\;\Big\vert\; s,a\right]
$$

**Step 3 – DQN Approximation (2 marks):**
A neural network $Q_\theta(s,a)$ approximates $Q^*$. Training uses:
- **Experience Replay:** store transitions $(s,a,r,s')$ in a buffer; sample minibatches to break temporal correlation.
- **Target Network:** a periodically updated copy $Q_{\theta^-}$ stabilises bootstrapping.

**Step 4 – Loss Function (2 marks):**
TD-error squared:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{DQN}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s')\sim \mathcal{B}}\Big[\big(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s',a') - Q_\theta(s,a)\big)^2\Big]
$$

Gradient:

$$
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_{\theta^-}(s',a') - Q_\theta(s,a)\big)\nabla_\theta Q_\theta(s,a)\Big]
$$

**Real-World Use Cases:** Atari game playing, robotic manipulation, autonomous driving policy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas**
>
> 1. **Confusing "discriminator loss" with "generator loss":** Many students write a single expression. KTU awards 1 mark **each** for separating $D$'s and $G$'s objectives.
> 2. **Forgetting the square-root in YOLO loss:** Examiners specifically test the $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ term because of its geometric motivation. Losing it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Skipping skip connections in U-Net diagram:** A U-Net without skip connections is just an autoencoder — examiners deduct **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating the role of the target network in DQN:** It is *not* optional; it is the **stabilisation trick** and is worth 1 mark.
> 5. **BLEU without brevity penalty:** Full marks require both $p_k$ *and* BP. Forgetting BP loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **In attention derivation, skipping the softmax normalisation:** The weights must sum to 1; missing softmax costs **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Applications**

### Architecture-Level Recap
- ☐ **CNN** → image classification, feature extraction.
- ☐ **R-CNN family** → high-accuracy object detection (two-stage).
- ☐ **YOLO** → real-time object detection (single-stage, grid-based).
- ☐ **U-Net** → medical/semantic segmentation with skip connections.
- ☐ **CNN + LSTM** → image captioning (See-and-Tell).
- ☐ **Seq2Seq + Attention** → machine translation, summarisation.
- ☐ **CTC** → speech recognition (variable-length alignment).
- ☐ **GAN** → image generation, augmentation, style transfer.
- ☐ **VAE** → smooth latent-space generation.
- ☐ **DQN** → reinforcement-learning control tasks.

### Loss-Function Recap
- ☐ **Categorical Cross-Entropy** — multi-class classification.
- ☐ **Binary Cross-Entropy** — GAN discriminator, binary tasks.
- ☐ **YOLO Loss (5 terms)** — coordinate, size, confidence, no-object, class.
- ☐ **Dice Loss** — imbalanced segmentation.
- ☐ **CTC Loss** — sequence-to-shorter-sequence.
- ☐ **Per-word CE** — captioning, machine translation.
- ☐ **TD-Error²** — DQN.

### Metric Recap
- ☐ **Accuracy, F1** — classification.
- ☐ **mAP, IoU** — detection (threshold 0.5).
- ☐ **mIoU, Dice Coefficient** — segmentation.
- ☐ **BLEU-4, CIDEr** — captioning/translation.
- ☐ **WER, CER** — speech recognition.
- ☐ **Perplexity** — language modelling.
- ☐ **FID, Inception Score** — GAN evaluation (lower FID = better).

### Conceptual Pitfalls
- ☐ Vanilla Seq2Seq fails on long sentences → use attention.
- ☐ Pixel-CE fails on imbalanced segmentation → use Dice.
- ☐ YOLO has high recall but lower precision on small objects → use two-stage for accuracy-critical.
- ☐ GAN training is unstable → use Wasserstein GAN or spectral norm.
- ☐ DQN overestimates Q-values → use Double DQN.
- ☐ Autoencoders compress but do not generate new samples like GANs.

### Numerical Constants to Memorise
- ☐ $\gamma$ (discount factor) = **0.99** (default DQN).
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{coord}}$ (YOLO) = **5.0**.
- ☐ $\lambda_{\text{noobj}}$ (YOLO) = **0.5**.
- ☐ PASCAL VOC IoU threshold = **0.5**.
- ☐ COCO mAP@[.5, .95] averaged over **10 IoU thresholds**.
- ☐ BLEU uses up to **4-grams**.
- ☐ ImageNet input = **224 × 224 × 3**.
- ☐ U-Net channel progression = **64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024**.
